topics.title: Defining Marketing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Defining Marketing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

According to the **American Marketing Association (AMA)**, Marketing is the activity, set of institutions, and processes for creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging offerings that have value for customers, clients, partners, and society at large.

In a more KTU-examinable, structured form:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard)**
> Marketing is the managerial process by which individuals and groups obtain what they need and want through creating, offering, and exchanging products of value with others. The essence of marketing is a **transaction** — an exchange of value between two or more parties.

### 1.2 Marketing — A Dual Perspective

| Perspective | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Marketing as a Social Process** | Focuses on how marketing influences society — needs, wants, standard of living, cultural exchange. |
| **Marketing as a Managerial Process** | Focuses on how managers design and execute marketing programmes (4Ps) to meet organizational objectives. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Coffee Shop" Story**
> Imagine you open a small café in Kochi. You don't just brew coffee and shout at passers-by to buy it (that is **selling**). Instead, you first ask: *What do office-goers near my café actually want?* — Hot, quick, affordable chai-coffee combinations? A calm Wi-Fi zone? Reusable cups for eco-conscious youth? You design the offer, price it reasonably, place it well, and promote it on Instagram. This entire process — from understanding the customer to delivering satisfaction — is **Marketing**.
>
> The coffee itself is the **product**. The ₹30 price is the **price**. The shop location is the **place**. The Instagram post is the **promotion**. Together, they form the **Marketing Mix**.

### 1.4 Core Marketing Terminologies (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Need:** A basic human requirement (food, shelter, clothing). It is *biogenic* (biological) or *psychogenic* (psychological).
> - **Want**: A need shaped by culture, personality, and individual learning. A Keralaite may want *puttu-kadala*; a North Indian may want *paratha*.
> - **Demand**: Wants backed by **purchasing power**. Want + Ability to pay + Willingness to buy = Demand.
> - **Value Proposition**: A bundle of benefits offered to satisfy customer needs.
> - **Customer Satisfaction**: The feeling a customer experiences when a product's perceived performance meets or exceeds expectations.
> - **Exchange:** The act of obtaining a desired object from someone by offering something in return.
> - **Market:** The set of all actual and potential buyers of a product or service.
> - **Marketing Myopia:** A short-sighted, inward-looking approach where a company focuses more on its products than on customer needs (coined by Theodore Levitt, 1960).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Subject of Marketing — A Triad

Marketing ultimately revolves around **three stakeholders**:

1. **The Customer** — whose needs must be understood.
2. **The Company** — which creates value.
3. **Society** — which bears the social and environmental impact.

This triad forms the **Triple Bottom Line** of modern marketing: *People, Planet, Profit*.

### 2.2 The 5-Step Marketing Process (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Understanding the marketplace and customer needs/wants.**
2. **Designing a customer-driven marketing strategy** (segmentation, targeting, positioning — STP).
3. **Constructing an integrated marketing programme** (4Ps / 7Ps).
4. **Building profitable customer relationships** (CRM, loyalty).
5. **Capturing value from customers to create profits and customer equity.**

### 2.3 Evolution of the Marketing Concept (Critical for KTU 14-Mark Answers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Eras of Marketing Thinking**

| Era | Period | Core Philosophy | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Production Concept** | Pre-1930s | Consumers prefer widely available, affordable products. Focus on production efficiency. | Ignores customer preferences beyond price/availability. |
| **Product Concept** | 1930s–1950s | Consumers favour products with the best quality, performance, or features. | **Marketing Myopia** — focuses on product refinement, not evolving customer needs. |
| **Selling Concept** | 1950s–1960s | Consumers will not buy enough unless aggressively sold to. | High-pressure push tactics; ignores long-term relationships. |
| **Marketing Concept** | 1960s–1990s | Achieving organizational goals depends on knowing the needs/wants of target markets and delivering desired satisfaction better than competitors. | Customer-only focus, sometimes at society's cost. |
| **Societal Marketing Concept** | 1990s–present | Marketing should deliver value to customers in a way that maintains or improves both the consumer's and society's well-being. | Balancing short-term demand with long-term welfare. |

### 2.4 Marketing vs. Selling — A KTU Favourite Comparison

| Parameter | Selling | Marketing |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Inside-out (factory → market) | Outside-in (market → factory) |
| **Focus** | Products | Customer needs |
| **Goal** | Profit through sales volume | Profit through customer satisfaction |
| **Horizon** | Short-term transactions | Long-term relationships |
| **Means** | Aggressive persuasion | Integrated value creation |
| **Starting Point** | Existing product | Customer need identification |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Marketing Equation" (Conceptual Form)**

$$
\text{Marketing Effectiveness} = \frac{\text{Value Delivered} \times \text{Customer Satisfaction}}{\text{Customer Cost (Monetary + Time + Effort)}}
$$

| Component | Meaning | Unit / Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | Total revenue a business earns from a single customer over their lifetime. | ₹ (Indian Rupees) |
| **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)** | Cost incurred to acquire a new customer. | ₹ |
| **CLV : CAC Ratio** | Healthy benchmark for sustainable marketing. | Ideal ratio = **3 : 1** |
| **Market Share** | Company's sales as a percentage of total market sales. | Percentage (%) |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | Likelihood that customers recommend the brand. | Score range = **−100 to +100** |
| **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)** | Direct rating of satisfaction post-purchase. | 0–100% |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

- **Product Design Engineering:** Marketing defines specifications — engineers build to *voice of customer*.
- **Industrial Marketing:** B2B marketing of capital goods, machinery, and technology uses the same need–want–demand–value chain.
- **Service Industries:** Banks, hospitals, and IT firms apply the marketing concept to design service blueprints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: Decoding "Defining Marketing"

### 3.1 The Hierarchical Ladder of Human Aspiration

Marketing theory (Maslow-inspired, KTU standard) presents a structured progression from raw need to actualized demand. Each rung adds a layer of complexity.

$$
\text{Need} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Want} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Demand} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Value} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Satisfaction} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Loyalty}
$$

**Step-by-step transition logic:**

| Step | Transition | Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **Step 1** | Need → Want | A universal need (e.g., thirst) gets culturally shaped into a specific want (e.g., tender coconut water). |
| **Step 2** | Want → Demand | A want becomes a demand only when supported by **purchasing power** and **willingness to buy**. |
| **Step 3** | Demand → Value | A customer's evaluation of the offering's perceived benefits vs. perceived cost produces *value*. |
| **Step 4** | Value → Satisfaction | Satisfaction occurs when **perceived performance ≥ expectation**. |
| **Step 5** | Satisfaction → Loyalty | Repeated satisfaction builds trust, advocacy, and long-term equity. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Illustration (Board Style):**
> Suppose a college student in Trivandrum *wants* a new smartphone (Want). The phone costs ₹20,000. If the student has ₹25,000 in savings and is willing to spend → the want becomes a **Demand**.
> The student compares Samsung, Apple, OnePlus on features, price, brand → chooses the one offering **highest perceived value** → experiences **satisfaction** → may repurchase and recommend → **loyalty**.

### 3.2 The Marketing Mix (4Ps) — Decomposition Matrix

For a KTU 14-mark answer, the **4Ps** must be deconstructed as follows:

$$
M = f(P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4)
$$

where $M$ is the integrated marketing offer and each $P_i$ is a controllable marketing variable.

| Element | Sub-components | Example (Smartphone Launch) |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ = Product | Variety, quality, design, features, brand, packaging, services | 256GB storage, 6.7" AMOLED display, eco-recyclable box |
| $P_2$ = Price | List price, discounts, allowances, credit terms | ₹20,000 + ₹2,000 cashback + 6-month no-cost EMI |
| $P_3$ = Place | Channels, coverage, locations, inventory, transport | Croma + Amazon + Brand store in Lulu Mall |
| $P_4$ = Promotion | Advertising, sales promotion, PR, personal selling, digital | Instagram reels + influencer tie-up + 10% Diwali offer |

**Extended 7Ps (for services):** People, Process, Physical Evidence.

### 3.3 The Exchange Equation

For a marketing exchange to occur, **five conditions** must be satisfied:

1. There are at least **two parties**.
2. Each party has something of **value** to the other.
3. Each party is capable of **communication and delivery**.
4. Each party is **free to accept or reject** the offer.
5. Each party believes it is **appropriate or desirable** to deal with the other.

Only when all five conditions hold does a valid **transaction** occur:

$$
\text{Transaction} = \text{Trade of values} \geq \text{2 parties, agreed-upon terms}
$$

If the parties formalize this through a contract, it becomes a **relationship** — the foundation of modern CRM-driven marketing.

### 3.4 Customer Value & Satisfaction — Quantitative Logic

The **Customer Perceived Value (CPV)** can be expressed as:

$$
\text{CPV} = \text{Total Customer Value (TCV)} - \text{Total Customer Cost (TCC)}
$$

where:

$$
\text{TCV} = \text{Product Value} + \text{Service Value} + \text{Image Value} + \text{Personnel Value}
$$

and:

$$
\text{TCC} = \text{Monetary Cost} + \text{Time Cost} + \text{Energy Cost} + \text{Psychological Cost}
$$

**Satisfaction Function:**

$$
S = f(P - E)
$$

where $P$ = perceived performance of the product, $E$ = customer expectation.
- If $P > E$ → **Delighted customer**
- If $P = E$ → **Satisfied customer**
- If $P < E$ → **Dissatisfied customer**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Marketing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Understand Marketplace and Customer Needs] --> B[Design Customer-Driven Marketing Strategy STP]
    B --> C[Construct Integrated Marketing Programme 4Ps]
    C --> D[Build Profitable Customer Relationships CRM]
    D --> E[Capture Value for Customer Equity and Profits]
    E --> F[Reinvest Insights into Customer Understanding]
    F --> A
```

### 4.2 Needs–Wants–Demands Progression

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N[NEED Basic human requirement] --> W[WANT Culturally shaped preference]
    W --> D[DEMAND Want plus purchasing power plus willingness]
    D --> V[VALUE Perceived benefit minus perceived cost]
    V --> S[SATISFACTION Performance meets or exceeds expectation]
    S --> L[LOYALTY Repeat purchase and advocacy]
```

### 4.3 Evolution of the Marketing Concept (Era Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Production Concept Wide availability low cost] --> P2[Product Concept Quality and features]
    P2 --> P3[Selling Concept Aggressive persuasion]
    P3 --> P4[Marketing Concept Customer first satisfaction]
    P4 --> P5[Societal Marketing Concept People Planet Profit]
```

### 4.4 The 4Ps Marketing Mix Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MM[Integrated Marketing Mix] --> P1A[PRODUCT Variety Quality Design Features Brand]
    MM --> P2A[PRICE List Discount Allowance Credit]
    MM --> P3A[PLACE Channels Coverage Location Inventory Transport]
    MM --> P4A[PROMOTION Advertising Sales PR Personal Selling Digital]
```

### 4.5 Marketing vs Selling — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph M[Marketing Paradigm]
        M1[Customer Needs] --> M2[Market Research]
        M2 --> M3[Value Creation]
        M3 --> M4[Customer Satisfaction]
        M4 --> M5[Long Term Relationship]
    end
    subgraph S[Selling Paradigm]
        S1[Existing Product] --> S2[Push Promotion]
        S2 --> S3[Aggressive Persuasion]
        S3 --> S4[One Time Sale]
        S4 --> S5[Short Term Volume]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 1]**
*Define Marketing. Explain the core elements of the AMA definition.*

**Model Answer:**

Marketing, according to the American Marketing Association (AMA), is the activity, set of institutions, and processes for creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging offerings that have value for customers, clients, partners, and society at large.

The core elements are:
- **Activity & Process:** Marketing is not a single act but a continuous cycle.
- **Institutions:** Businesses, NGOs, and governments are all engaged in marketing.
- **Creating, Communicating, Delivering, Exchanging:** The four functional pillars.
- **Value:** The central currency of marketing — benefits exceeding costs.
- **Stakeholders:** Customers, clients, partners, and society — a multi-stakeholder view.

*(3 marks — Board expects the formal definition + identification of 2-3 core elements.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Differentiate between Need, Want, and Demand with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer:**

| Concept | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Need** | A basic, innate human requirement — biological or psychological. | Hunger, thirst, shelter. |
| **Want** | A need shaped by culture, personality, and individual learning. | A Keralite's hunger translated into wanting *appam with stew*. |
| **Demand** | A want backed by purchasing power and willingness to buy. | Wanting *appam* and having ₹150 to spend at a restaurant. |

The hierarchy: **Need → Want → Demand** — each successive concept adds a layer of socio-economic conditioning.

*(3 marks — Concept clarity + example + hierarchy line.)*

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each with Internal Choice)**

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2]**
**(a)** *Explain in detail the evolution of the marketing concept from the Production Concept to the Societal Marketing Concept.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Discuss the concept of Marketing Myopia. How can a company avoid it?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Evolution of the Marketing Concept (7 marks):**

The marketing concept has evolved through five distinct eras, each reflecting a shift in business philosophy:

**1. Production Concept (Pre-1930s):** Assumed consumers prefer products that are widely available and affordable. Companies focused on production efficiency and distribution. *Example:* Henry Ford's Model T — *"any color as long as it is black."* Limitation: ignored customer preferences.

**2. Product Concept (1930s–1950s):** Believed consumers favour products offering the most quality, performance, and features. Companies focused on continuous product improvement. Limitation: often leads to **Marketing Myopia** — improving the product at the cost of understanding the customer.

**3. Selling Concept (1950s–1960s):** Assumed consumers will not buy enough unless persuaded aggressively. Focus on high-pressure sales and promotion. *Example:* Insurance cold-calling in the 1960s. Limitation: short-term thinking, ignores customer satisfaction.

**4. Marketing Concept (1960s–1990s):** A customer-first philosophy. Identifying customer needs better than competitors and delivering superior value. The shift was **outside-in** — from market to factory. *Example:* Toyota's lean approach, Procter & Gamble's market-driven innovation.

**5. Societal Marketing Concept (1990s–Present):** Extends the marketing concept by adding a responsibility to society. Companies must deliver value in a way that maintains or improves consumer and societal well-being. *Example:* Tesla (electric vehicles), Nestlé's fortified baby food, Amul's farmer-first model.

The progression shows a **widening stakeholder lens**: from product → customer → society → planet.

**(b) Marketing Myopia (7 marks):**

Coined by **Theodore Levitt in his 1960 Harvard Business Review article**, Marketing Myopia refers to the short-sighted and inward-looking approach of a company that focuses more on its existing products than on the evolving needs and wants of customers.

**Classic Examples:**
- **Kodak:** Focused on film-based cameras; ignored digital imaging and filed for bankruptcy in 2012.
- **Nokia:** Focused on hardware durability, missed the smartphone OS shift to Apple/Google.
- **Bata:** In India, focused on cheap footwear but missed premium brand positioning.

**How to Avoid Marketing Myopia:**
1. **Customer Orientation:** Continuously conduct market research; treat customer needs as the starting point.
2. **Competitor Orientation:** Benchmark against best-in-class, not just direct rivals.
3. **Innovation Culture:** Invest in R&D and disruptive thinking.
4. **Broad Definition of Business:** Define the *business* (e.g., "transportation" instead of "railways") to capture substitutes.
5. **Long-term Horizon:** Balance short-term sales with long-term customer equity.
6. **Societal Lens:** Adopt the Societal Marketing Concept — consider environmental and social impact.

*(Board-expected: 5 eras + 3 examples + 5 preventive steps for full 14 marks.)*

---

### **OR — Alternative Long-Answer Question**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 3]**
**(a)** *Define the Marketing Mix. Explain the 4Ps of marketing with a relevant example.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the concept of Customer Perceived Value and Satisfaction. How do they lead to customer loyalty?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Marketing Mix and the 4Ps (7 marks):**

The **Marketing Mix** is the set of tactical, controllable marketing tools that a company blends to produce the response it wants from the target market. E. Jerome McCarthy classified them as the **4Ps**:

**1. Product:** The actual offering — tangible good, service, or idea. Sub-elements: variety, quality, design, features, brand name, packaging, services, warranties.
*Example:* Apple iPhone — premium design, iOS ecosystem, 1-year warranty.

**2. Price:** The amount of money customers pay. Sub-elements: list price, discounts, allowances, payment period, credit terms.
*Example:* EMI schemes, student discounts, festive cashback.

**3. Place (Distribution):** Getting the right product to the right place at the right time. Sub-elements: channels, coverage, locations, inventory, transport.
*Example:* Reliance Digital, Amazon India, Apple Store.

**4. Promotion:** Communicating the product's value to the customer. Sub-elements: advertising, sales promotion, public relations, personal selling, direct marketing, digital marketing.
*Example:* Apple's "Shot on iPhone" campaign.

For **services**, three additional Ps apply: **People, Process, Physical Evidence** (Booms & Bitner, 1981).

**(b) Customer Perceived Value and Satisfaction (7 marks):**

**Customer Perceived Value (CPV)** is the difference between the total value a customer gains from a product and the total cost incurred to obtain it.

$$
\text{CPV} = \text{Total Customer Value (TCV)} - \text{Total Customer Cost (TCC)}
$$

- **TCV** includes product value, service value, image value, and personnel value.
- **TCC** includes monetary cost, time cost, energy cost, and psychological cost.

A high CPV creates a competitive edge and is the precondition for satisfaction.

**Customer Satisfaction** is the feeling that results when a product's perceived performance matches or exceeds buyer expectations:

- If $P > E$ → **Delighted customer**
- If $P = E$ → **Satisfied customer**
- If $P < E$ → **Dissatisfied customer**

**Path to Loyalty:** Satisfaction is the gateway to loyalty. Satisfied customers → repeat purchases → increased switching costs → emotional attachment → advocacy → **customer equity**. This is the foundation of modern CRM strategies and the **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** maximization.

*(Board-expected: 4Ps with sub-elements + example + CPV equation + satisfaction logic + loyalty chain.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Definition trap:** Writing only the AMA definition without identifying the *core elements* (activity, value, exchange) costs 1–1.5 marks. Always decompose the definition.
> 2. **Concept confusion:** Many students inter-change "need" and "want" or describe "demand" as mere "desire." Mark loss: 1 mark per wrong pairing.
> 3. **Era overlap:** In evolution questions, students often confuse Selling and Marketing concepts. Selling is *push-based, transaction-focused*; Marketing is *pull-based, relationship-focused*.
> 4. **Marketing Myopia answers:** Citing only Kodak is not enough. Examiners expect *3+ examples* and *preventive steps* for full 7 marks.
> 5. **4Ps superficiality:** Listing the 4Ps without *sub-elements and one real-world example per P* typically fetches only 4–5 out of 7 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the equation:** For CPV and Satisfaction questions, failing to write the **CPV = TCV − TCC** equation or the **P vs. E** logic drops 1 mark.
> 7. **Neglecting 7Ps:** If the question is on services, omitting *People, Process, Physical Evidence* will cost 1.5 marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **AMA Definition** is the universal standard — memorize the **four pillars** (creating, communicating, delivering, exchanging) and the **multi-stakeholder** view.
- ✅ **Need → Want → Demand → Value → Satisfaction → Loyalty** is the master ladder — visualize it before every answer.
- ✅ **Five Eras of Marketing Concept:** Production → Product → Selling → Marketing → Societal. Use the comparative table for any 14-mark evolution question.
- ✅ **Marketing Myopia** is a *guaranteed 7-mark question* in Part B — know Levitt (1960), the Kodak/Nokia/Bata examples, and 5+ preventive strategies.
- ✅ **4Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion)** + **7Ps (add People, Process, Physical Evidence for services)** — memorize with one live brand example per P.
- ✅ **Customer Perceived Value = TCV − TCC** — write the equation and define both terms.
- ✅ **Satisfaction depends on P vs. E (Performance vs. Expectation)** — three cases (P > E, P = E, P < E).
- ✅ **Marketing vs Selling** — always highlight *direction* (inside-out vs. outside-in), *focus* (product vs. customer), and *horizon* (short vs. long term).
- ✅ **KPIs to recall:** CLV, CAC, NPS, CSAT, Market Share — at least one will appear in case-study/numerical applications.
- ✅ **Triple Bottom Line** — People, Planet, Profit — anchors the Societal Marketing Concept in the modern ESG context.
- ✅ **For 14-mark Part B answers:** Structure = *Definition + 5-7 structured points + Real-world example + Conclusion*. Board examiners reward this exact pattern.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Core Concepts in Marketing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Core Concepts in Marketing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Core Concepts in Marketing

## 1.1 Introduction to Marketing

### Formal Academic Definition
**Marketing** is defined by the American Marketing Association (AMA) as the activity, set of institutions, and processes for creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging offerings that have value for customers, clients, partners, and society at large.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, marketing is understood as a **societal process** and a **managerial function** through which individuals and groups obtain what they need and want by creating, offering, and freely exchanging products of value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Marketing is NOT just selling or advertising. It is a comprehensive management philosophy that begins with identifying consumer needs and ends with customer satisfaction and societal welfare.

### Core Marketing Concepts (The Foundation Stones)
The discipline of marketing rests upon the following eight core concepts that every B.Tech management student must internalize:

| # | Concept | Brief Meaning |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Needs** | States of felt deprivation (basic human requirements — food, shelter, safety) |
| 2 | **Wants** | Needs shaped by culture and individual personality (specific forms of needs) |
| 3 | **Demands** | Wants backed by purchasing power |
| 4 | **Products** | Anything that can be offered to satisfy a need or want |
| 5 | **Value** | Customer's perceived benefits versus cost of obtaining the product |
| 6 | **Satisfaction** | The extent to which product performance meets expectations |
| 7 | **Exchange** | The act of obtaining a desired object from someone by offering something in return |
| 8 | **Markets** | The set of actual and potential buyers of a product/service |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> Imagine a thirsty engineering student in Kochi during summer.
> - **Need** = Thirst (a basic physiological state of deprivation).
> - **Want** = A cold Maaza mango drink specifically (shaped by culture/personal preference).
> - **Demand** = Having ₹40 in pocket to actually buy that Maaza.
> - **Product** = The Maaza bottle itself.
> - **Value** = The student perceives the drink as worth ₹40 because the refreshment benefit exceeds the price paid.
> - **Exchange** = The transaction at the campus canteen counter.
> - **Market** = All thirsty students in Kerala who could buy such beverages.

### The Marketing Triad
Marketing operates on three fundamental dimensions:
1. **Customer Orientation** — understanding buyer behavior.
2. **Competitor Orientation** — benchmarking against rivals.
3. **Interfunctional Coordination** — aligning all departments (R&D, production, finance) toward the customer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis: Evolution of Marketing Concepts

### 2.1 The Five Eras of Marketing Thought
Marketing as a discipline has evolved through five distinct phases. This evolution is a **high-yield KTU topic** and frequently appears in Part A questions.

| Era | Time Period | Core Philosophy | Orientation |
|-----|-------------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Production Concept** | Pre-1930s | "Make it and they will buy" | Production efficiency |
| **Product Concept** | 1930s–1950s | "Build a better product" | Product quality & features |
| **Selling Concept** | 1950s–1960s | "Sell what you make" | Aggressive promotion |
| **Marketing Concept** | 1960s–1990s | "Make what they will buy" | Customer needs |
| **Societal Marketing Concept** | 1990s–Present | "Customer welfare + Society welfare" | Triple bottom line |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The difference between the **Selling Concept** and the **Marketing Concept** is the most frequently asked distinction. Selling focuses on the *seller's need to convert the product into cash*. Marketing focuses on *satisfying customer needs through the product and the entire value-delivery system*.

### 2.2 The Marketing Mix — The 4Ps Framework

The **Marketing Mix** is the set of tactical, controllable marketing tools that a company blends to produce the response it wants from the target market. It was originally proposed by **E. Jerome McCarthy** and is the most widely used operational model in marketing.

$$ \text{Marketing Mix} = f(P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4) $$

Where each $P_i$ represents a controllable variable under the marketer's control.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Element | Symbol | Core Question Answered | Key Variables | Strategic Focus |
|---------|--------|------------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Product** | $P_1$ | What do we sell? | Variety, quality, design, features, brand, packaging | Core value proposition |
| **Price** | $P_2$ | At what cost? | List price, discounts, allowances, credit terms | Perceived value capture |
| **Place** | $P_3$ | Where is it available? | Channels, coverage, locations, inventory, transport | Distribution efficiency |
| **Promotion** | $P_4$ | How are we communicating? | Advertising, sales promotion, PR, direct marketing, personal selling | Awareness & persuasion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extended Marketing Mix (For Services — 7Ps):** Booms and Bitner extended the 4Ps to include **People, Process, and Physical Evidence** — critical for service marketing like banking, hospitality, and IT services — a relevant industry context for Kerala's IT sector.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Industry Utility
For engineering firms and B.Tech graduates entering product companies:
- **Product** $\rightarrow$ Defines the technical specifications, features, and quality of the engineered solution.
- **Price** $\rightarrow$ Cost-plus, competitive, or value-based pricing for industrial goods.
- **Place** $\rightarrow$ Industrial distribution channels (B2B) vs. consumer channels (B2C).
- **Promotion** $\rightarrow$ Technical sales, trade shows, content marketing, and digital presence.

The 4Ps framework allows an engineer-turned-manager to systematically analyze any market offering and design a coherent go-to-market strategy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Customer Value Equation (Analytical Derivation)

Customer value is the cornerstone of modern marketing thought. It can be expressed through a structured analytical equation that demonstrates the trade-off between benefits and costs.

**Step 1: Define Total Customer Value (TCV)**

Total Customer Value is the sum of all perceived benefits a customer derives from a market offering.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCV &= V_{\text{product}} + V_{\text{service}} + V_{\text{personnel}} + V_{\text{image}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Define Total Customer Cost (TCC)**

Total Customer Cost is the bundle of costs the customer incurs beyond just the monetary price.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCC &= C_{\text{monetary}} + C_{\text{time}} + C_{\text{energy}} + C_{\text{psychic}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Derive the Customer Value (CV) Relationship**

Customer Value is the difference between Total Customer Value and Total Customer Cost.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= TCV - TCC
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** A customer will choose the offering that maximizes $CV$. Marketers seek to either (a) increase $TCV$ by augmenting benefits, or (b) decrease $TCC$ by reducing non-monetary costs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanatory Insight:** $C_{\text{psychic}}$ refers to mental effort or stress — for example, the anxiety of buying a complicated insurance policy. Reducing this cost is a major value-add in services.

### 3.2 The Satisfaction–Performance Model

Customer satisfaction ($S$) is mathematically modeled as a function of perceived performance ($P$) relative to expectations ($E$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= f(P, E)
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields three outcomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Case 1: } & P > E \Rightarrow \text{Delight (Positive disconfirmation)} \\
\text{Case 2: } & P = E \Rightarrow \text{Satisfaction (Confirmation)} \\
\text{Case 3: } & P < E \Rightarrow \text{Dissatisfaction (Negative disconfirmation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Companies seeking competitive advantage aim for the *Delight* quadrant — exceeding expectations rather than merely meeting them.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Selling vs. Marketing Concepts

| Parameter | Selling Concept | Marketing Concept |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Starting Point | Factory | Target Market |
| Focus | Existing Products | Customer Needs |
| Means | Selling & Promotion | Integrated Marketing |
| End | Profit through sales volume | Profit through customer satisfaction |
| Time Horizon | Short-term (transactional) | Long-term (relationship) |
| Industry Example | Deodorant sales push in 1950s | Apple's iPhone design philosophy |

### 3.4 The Marketing Process — Stepwise Flow

The marketing process follows a structured 5-step model that integrates the analytical concepts above:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1} &\rightarrow \text{Market Research \& Understand Customer Needs} \\
\text{Step 2} &\rightarrow \text{Segment, Target, Position (STP)} \\
\text{Step 3} &\rightarrow \text{Design Marketing Mix (4Ps)} \\
\text{Step 4} &\rightarrow \text{Implement Customer Value \& Satisfaction Strategy} \\
\text{Step 5} &\rightarrow \text{Build Profitable Long-term Relationships (CRM)}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between the Selling Concept and the Marketing Concept of business.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Selling Concept | Marketing Concept |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Orientation | Outward (Factory → Market) | Inward (Market → Factory) |
| Focus | Selling what is produced | Producing what is marketable |
| Goal | Profit through sales volume | Profit through customer satisfaction |
| Scope | Short-term transactions | Long-term relationships |

The **Selling Concept** operates on the assumption that customers will not buy enough without aggressive persuasion, making it transaction-centric. In contrast, the **Marketing Concept** is customer-centric — the company first researches customer needs and then designs offerings to satisfy them. (Marks: 1 for definitions + 2 for tabular comparison)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - July 2024, Sample Paper]**
*Explain the concept of Customer Perceived Value with its components.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Customer Perceived Value is the difference between the total benefits a customer expects from a product and the total costs incurred to obtain it. It has two components:

1. **Total Customer Value (TCV)** — comprising product value, service value, personnel value, and image value.
2. **Total Customer Cost (TCC)** — comprising monetary cost, time cost, energy cost, and psychic cost.

A customer will choose the offering that maximizes the ratio $TCV/TCC$. Marketers must therefore increase perceived benefits while reducing non-monetary costs. (Marks: 1 for definition + 1 for TCV components + 1 for TCC components)

---

### PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU Internal Choice Rule (ESE):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Series]**
*(a)* Define the term "Market" as understood in modern marketing. Differentiate between a physical marketplace and a market in the marketing sense with suitable examples. **[(7 Marks)]**

*(b)* Discuss the Societal Marketing Concept in detail. How does it extend beyond the traditional Marketing Concept? Highlight its relevance in the context of sustainable business practices. **[(7 Marks)]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Definition of Market and its Types**

**Definition (3 Marks):** A market in modern marketing refers to the set of all actual and potential buyers of a product or service. It is not a physical location but a transactional concept representing demand-side aggregation.

**Physical Marketplace vs. Marketing Sense Market (4 Marks):**

A *physical marketplace* is a geographical location where buyers and sellers physically gather (e.g., Broadway Market in Ernakulam). A *market in the marketing sense* is a virtual aggregation of buyers sharing a need/want who could transact with sellers (e.g., the "Indian smartphone market" — comprising millions of buyers across the country). The key distinction is that the latter is defined by **demand homogeneity** rather than physical proximity.

---

**(b) Societal Marketing Concept**

**Definition (2 Marks):** The Societal Marketing Concept holds that the organization's task is to determine the needs, wants, and interests of target markets and to deliver the desired satisfactions more effectively and efficiently than competitors **in a way that preserves or enhances the consumer's and society's long-term well-being**.

**Extension Beyond Marketing Concept (3 Marks):**

While the traditional Marketing Concept focuses on the **three-way linkage** between company profits, customer satisfaction, and **company effort**, the Societal Marketing Concept adds a fourth dimension: **society's welfare**. It addresses conflicts such as:

- Fast food vs. long-term health.
- Fuel-efficient cars vs. environmental sustainability.
- Affordable housing vs. urban sprawl.

**Relevance to Sustainable Business (2 Marks):** In modern Kerala context — with rising environmental consciousness and ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) mandates — firms like Tata, Infosys, and Kerala's own renewable energy startups use the Societal Marketing Concept to balance profitability with planetary welfare. Sustainable packaging, carbon-neutral logistics, and ethical sourcing all stem from this philosophy.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]**
*(a)* Explain the 4Ps of the Marketing Mix. Discuss how the marketing mix is extended for service businesses. **[(7 Marks)]**

*(b)* Illustrate the core marketing concepts of Needs, Wants, and Demands using a real-world example. Explain how these concepts form the foundation of customer-centric marketing. **[(7 Marks)]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) The 4Ps and Extended Marketing Mix**

**The 4Ps (4 Marks):**
1. **Product** — The tangible good or intangible service offered (e.g., a smartphone with specific features).
2. **Price** — The monetary value exchanged (e.g., ₹25,000 for the smartphone).
3. **Place** — Distribution channels making the product accessible (e.g., Amazon, Croma stores).
4. **Promotion** — Communication strategies creating awareness (e.g., digital ads, influencer marketing).

**Extended Marketing Mix for Services (3 Marks):**

Services are intangible, inseparable, variable, and perishable. Booms and Bitner extended the 4Ps to **7Ps**:
- **People** — Service employees are the brand (e.g., Kerala Bank frontline staff).
- **Process** — Procedure for service delivery (e.g., ATM transaction flow).
- **Physical Evidence** — Tangible cues proving service quality (e.g., branch décor, uniform).

This expansion is essential because service quality is judged through the *experience* rather than the product alone.

---

**(b) Needs, Wants, Demands — Real-World Example**

**Example (3 Marks):** Consider a college student in Trivandrum.
- **Need:** Need for transportation between college and home (basic mobility need).
- **Want:** A specific Hero Splendor Plus motorcycle in red color (shaped by culture, peer influence, lifestyle).
- **Demand:** Having ₹85,000 in savings to purchase that specific motorcycle.

**Foundation of Customer-Centric Marketing (4 Marks):**

These three concepts enable marketers to:
1. Identify latent *needs* through market research.
2. Translate needs into *wants* through product design and branding.
3. Convert wants into *demands* by aligning pricing, financing, and distribution.

This hierarchy forms the foundation of STP (Segmentation-Targeting-Positioning) strategy — the next step in the marketing process. Without precise differentiation among Needs, Wants, and Demands, firms cannot design effective value propositions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating Selling and Marketing Concepts** — Many students write that "selling = marketing." This is a critical error. Selling is a *subset activity* of marketing. (Loss: up to 2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the Societal Dimension** — When answering the Marketing Concept, students often omit the *societal welfare* component introduced by Kotler. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 3. **Confusing Needs with Wants** — A need is universal; a want is specific. Examiners look for this distinction. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Listing 4Ps without explanation** — Mere listing without stating the strategic decision involved per P results in partial marks only. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 5. **Writing "Market = Place"** — A common conceptual error. A market is a set of buyers, not a physical location, in modern marketing terminology.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Marketing is a **societal and managerial process** — not synonymous with selling or advertising.
- ✅ The **8 core concepts** are: Needs, Wants, Demands, Products, Value, Satisfaction, Exchange, Markets.
- ✅ **Five eras** of marketing thought: Production → Product → Selling → Marketing → Societal Marketing.
- ✅ The **4Ps Marketing Mix** (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) was proposed by **E. Jerome McCarthy**.
- ✅ The **7Ps** (extended mix) adds People, Process, Physical Evidence for services.
- ✅ Customer Value formula: $CV = TCV - TCC$.
- ✅ Satisfaction depends on **Performance vs. Expectation** — Delight occurs when $P > E$.
- ✅ The **Marketing Concept** is customer-centric; **Selling Concept** is seller-centric.
- ✅ The **Societal Marketing Concept** adds society's welfare to the marketing concept (triple bottom line).
- ✅ KTU keyword watchlist: "Value Delivery System," "Customer Lifetime Value," "Marketing Myopia" (Theodore Levitt).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evolution of Marketing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Evolution of Marketing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Marketing (KTU 2024 Definition):** Marketing is the **management process** responsible for identifying, anticipating, and satisfying customer requirements **profitably**. It is a **societal process** by which individuals and groups obtain what they need and want through creating, offering, and freely exchanging products of value. According to the **American Marketing Association (AMA)**, marketing is the activity, set of institutions, and processes for creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging offerings that have value for customers, clients, partners, and society at large. ### 1.2 Evolution — Conceptual Definition > [!NOTE] > **Evolution of Marketing** refers to the chronological transformation of marketing thought and business orientation, from a narrow focus on **production efficiency** to a broad, **socially responsible**, customer-centric philosophy. It traces how businesses shifted their **strategic priority** in response to changing market conditions, technological progress, and consumer expectations across different eras. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy > [!TIP] > **Think of Marketing Evolution like the growth of a child:** > > - **Toddler Stage (Production Era):** The child only cares about making things — quantity matters, not presentation. > - **Child Stage (Product Era):** The child improves craftsmanship — better toys sell themselves. > - **Teen Stage (Selling Era):** The child learns to push and persuade — even if the other person doesn't want it. > - **Adult Stage (Marketing Era):** The child finally **listens** to what others need before creating anything. > - **Wise Elder Stage (Societal Marketing):** The child considers the **welfare of the entire neighborhood**, not just his friend. Just as the child matures from **self-centered** to **socially conscious**, marketing evolved from **product-centered** to **customer-centered** to **society-centered**. ### 1.4 Why Study the Evolution? Understanding the evolution helps managers: - Diagnose the **current marketing orientation** of an organization. - Avoid **outdated practices** (e.g., over-production, hard-selling). - Anticipate **future trends** (digital, sustainability-driven marketing). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Distinct Eras of Marketing Evolution Marketing scholars (notably **E. Jerome McCarthy** and **Philip Kotler**) generally classify the evolution into **five distinct eras**. Each era represents a shift in the **guiding business philosophy**. #### Era 1 — Production Orientation (Pre-1920s) - **Core Belief:** Customers prefer products that are **widely available and affordable**. - **Focus:** Mass production, distribution efficiency, low cost. - **Mantra:** *"We can sell everything we make."* - **Limitation:** Ignores customer preferences beyond price and availability. #### Era 2 — Product Orientation (1920s–1930s) - **Core Belief:** Customers favor products that offer **superior quality, performance, or features**. - **Focus:** Continuous product improvement. - **Mantra:** *"Build a better mousetrap, and the world will beat a path to your door."* - **Limitation:** Ignores whether customers actually want the product (e.g., **marketing myopia**). #### Era 3 — Selling Orientation (1930s–1950s) - **Core Belief:** Customers will **not buy enough** unless aggressively persuaded. - **Focus:** Heavy advertising, sales promotion, hard-selling tactics. - **Mantra:** *"We must push the product."* - **Limitation:** High-pressure tactics damage long-term relationships. #### Era 4 — Marketing Orientation (1950s–1990s) - **Core Belief:** The **key to achieving organizational goals** is understanding customer needs and delivering superior value. - **Focus:** Customer research, segmentation, targeting, positioning (STP), the **4 Ps**. - **Mantra:** *"The customer is king."* - **Limitation:** May neglect broader societal consequences. #### Era 5 — Societal Marketing Orientation (1990s–Present) - **Core Belief:** Marketing should **deliver value to customers** in a way that **maintains or improves both the consumer's and society's well-being**. - **Focus:** Triple bottom line — **People, Planet, Profit** (sustainability, ethics, CSR). - **Mantra:** *"Sustainable marketing wins long term."* - **Future Direction:** Digital marketing, AI-driven personalization, green marketing. ### 2.2 KTU Cheat Sheet — Marketing Orientations Comparison | S.No. | Era | Time Period | Core Belief | Focus | Tools Used | Limitation | |:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | 1 | Production | Pre-1920s | Availability & affordability | High output | Mass production | Ignores preferences | | 2 | Product | 1920s–1930s | Quality & features | Better products | R\&D, QC | Marketing myopia | | 3 | Selling | 1930s–1950s | Aggressive persuasion | Push strategy | Ads, sales force | Short-term focus | | 4 | Marketing | 1950s–1990s | Customer satisfaction | Pull strategy | STP, 4 Ps | Ignores society | | 5 | Societal | 1990s–Now | Welfare of consumer & society | Sustainable value | CSR, green marketing | Costly, complex | ### 2.3 Key Conceptual Definitions > [!NOTE] > **Marketing Myopia (Theodore Levitt, 1960):** A short-sighted, inward-looking approach where a business defines its purpose too narrowly — focusing on products rather than customer benefits. *Classic example:* Railroad companies thinking they were in the "railroad business" instead of the "transportation business." > [!IMPORTANT] > **Marketing Mix (4 Ps — McCarthy, 1960):** The set of tactical, controllable marketing tools — **Product, Price, Place, Promotion** — that an organization blends to produce the response it wants from the target market. > [!NOTE] > **Customer Value:** The difference between the **total customer benefits** received and the **total customer costs** incurred. *Formula concept:* $CV = B - C$. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility For an engineer-turned-manager: - **Production Era logic** still applies in **manufacturing engineering** — focus on yield optimization. - **Product Era logic** applies in **R\&D and quality engineering** — focus on features and reliability. - **Marketing Era logic** applies in **product design** — focus on **user-centered design** and **customer feedback loops**. - **Societal Era logic** applies in **sustainable product development**, **life-cycle assessment**, and **green engineering**. > **Example:** A smartphone company in the **Product Era** would only focus on camera megapixels; in the **Marketing Era**, it studies what consumers actually value (battery life, durability); in the **Societal Era**, it ensures responsible sourcing of minerals and ethical labour practices. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Logical Derivations ### 3.1 Step-by-Step Logical Transition Between Eras Let us formally trace the **causal transition** from one era to the next. Each transition is triggered by a **market shock** (a major environmental change). **Step 1 — Production to Product Transition** - **Trigger:** Increased competition once products became widely available. - **Logic:** With supply meeting demand, companies could no longer rely on volume alone. Quality became the differentiator. - **Result:** Investment in **R\&D** and **quality control**. **Step 2 — Product to Selling Transition** - **Trigger:** Saturation of "better" products; consumers grew indifferent. - **Logic:** Surplus supply required **active persuasion** to move inventory. - **Result:** Birth of **modern advertising agencies** and **sales departments**. **Step 3 — Selling to Marketing Transition** - **Trigger:** Post-WWII consumer awareness; rise of **mass media**, **research**, and informed buyers. - **Logic:** Hard-sell tactics lost credibility. Companies that **listened** to customers outperformed those that **pushed**. - **Result:** Adoption of **market research**, **STP**, and the **4 Ps framework**. **Step 4 — Marketing to Societal Transition** - **Trigger:** Environmental crises, consumerism backlash, regulatory tightening, and global sustainability concerns. - **Logic:** Customer satisfaction alone was insufficient; **public good** became a competitive necessity. - **Result:** Adoption of **CSR**, **green marketing**, and **triple bottom line** reporting. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Era Characteristics vs. KTU Outcomes | KTU Course Outcome (CO) | Mapped Era | Bloom's Cognitive Level | Reasoning Depth | |:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | CO1 — Define marketing concepts | Production, Product | Remember (L1) | Recall definitions | | CO1 — Explain evolution | All five eras | Understand (L2) | Interpret transition logic | | CO2 — Compare orientations | All five eras | Analyze (L4) | Compare belief systems | | CO3 — Apply in real scenarios | Marketing, Societal | Apply (L3) | Apply 4 Ps framework | | CO4 — Evaluate societal impact | Societal Era | Evaluate (L5) | Judge sustainability | ### 3.3 Conceptual Derivation — Customer Value Equation The transition from Selling to Marketing Era can be mathematically expressed through the **Customer Value** concept: $$ \begin{aligned} CV &= B - C \\ \text{where } B &= \text{Total Customer Benefit (functional + emotional)} \\ C &= \text{Total Customer Cost (monetary + time + psychological)} \end{aligned} $$ **Transition Logic:** - **Selling Era:** $B$ is artificially inflated through persuasion; real $CV$ is negative. - **Marketing Era:** Genuine $B$ is delivered through customer research; $CV$ is positive and sustainable. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Marketing Orientation Condition:} \quad CV &> 0 \\ \text{Societal Orientation Condition:} \quad CV &> 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Net Social Welfare} \geq 0 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.4 Marketing Myopia — Formal Diagnostic Framework > [!NOTE] > **Marketing Myopia Diagnostic Equation:** > A company is *myopic* if its strategic focus satisfies: > $$\begin{aligned} \text{Myopia Index (MI)} = \frac{\text{Focus on Product vs. Customer Benefit}}{1 + \text{Environmental Change Rate}} \end{aligned}$$ > When **MI > 1**, the firm risks extinction (Kotler, after Levitt). **Engineering Interpretation:** This is analogous to designing a product with **high technical specs but no user testing** — a classic failure mode in engineering projects. ### 3.5 Why Each Era Coexists Today > [!IMPORTANT] > Not every business operates in the latest era. The current marketing orientation of any firm depends on: > $$\begin{aligned} \text{Current Orientation} = f(\text{Industry Maturity}, \text{Competition Intensity}, \text{Consumer Awareness}, \text{Regulation}) \end{aligned}$$ > > - **FMCG companies** (e.g., packaged food) often retain **Selling Orientation** elements. > - **Tech startups** operate in **Marketing Orientation**. > - **Renewable energy firms** operate in **Societal Orientation**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Evolution Flow Chart ```mermaid flowchart LR A["PRODUCTION ERA
Pre 1920s
Make and Distribute"] --> B["PRODUCT ERA
1920s 1930s
Improve Quality"] B --> C["SELLING ERA
1930s 1950s
Push and Persuade"] C --> D["MARKETING ERA
1950s 1990s
Customer First"] D --> E["SOCIETAL ERA
1990s Present
People Planet Profit"] E --> F["DIGITAL GREEN ERA
Future
AI Ethics Sustainability"] A1["Belief Availability"] -.-> A B1["Belief Quality"] -.-> B C1["Belief Persuasion"] -.-> C D1["Belief Customer Value"] -.-> D E1["Belief Social Welfare"] -.-> E style A fill:#ffd6d6 style B fill:#ffe7c2 style C fill:#fff5b3 style D fill:#d4f4c2 style E fill:#c2e9f4 style F fill:#d8c2f4 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Transition Triggers ```mermaid flowchart TD T1["Market Saturation"] -->|Trigger| S1["Production to Product"] T2["Consumer Indifference"] -->|Trigger| S2["Product to Selling"] T3["Informed Consumers
Mass Media Rise"] -->|Trigger| S3["Selling to Marketing"] T4["Environmental Crisis
Consumerism Backlash"] -->|Trigger| S4["Marketing to Societal"] T5["AI Big Data
Climate Urgency"] -->|Trigger| S5["Societal to Digital Green"] S1 --> R1["Focus on Quality and R&D"] S2 --> R2["Focus on Advertising and Sales Force"] S3 --> R3["Focus on Market Research and STP"] S4 --> R4["Focus on CSR and Green Marketing"] S5 --> R5["Focus on Personalization and Ethics"] style T1 fill:#ffcccc style T2 fill:#ffddcc style T3 fill:#fff0cc style T4 fill:#d9f2d0 style T5 fill:#cce0ff ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph era1["Production Era Logic"] p1["Inputs Raw Materials"] --> p2["Process Mass Production"] p2 --> p3["Output Standardized Goods"] p3 --> p4["Customer Purchase"] end subgraph era5["Societal Era Logic"] s1["Societal Need Identification"] --> s2["Stakeholder Consultation"] s2 --> s3["Sustainable Design"] s3 --> s4["Ethical Production"] s4 --> s5["Customer Satisfaction"] s5 --> s6["Social Welfare Check"] s6 -->|Feedback Loop| s1 end era1 -.Evolution.-> era5 style era1 fill:#ffe5e5 style era5 fill:#e5f5e5 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* Define marketing. Briefly explain the **Production Orientation** era. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Marketing is the management process responsible for identifying, anticipating, and satisfying customer needs profitably (AMA, 2013). **Production Orientation (1 mark):** Prevailing before 1920s, this era assumed customers prefer products that are **widely available and inexpensive**. The firm's focus was on **mass production and wide distribution**. Henry Ford's famous line *"Any customer can have a car painted any color that he wants, so long as it is black"* exemplifies this era. **(Total: 3 Marks)** --- > **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]* What is **Marketing Myopia**? Give one example. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Marketing Myopia, a term coined by **Theodore Levitt in 1960**, refers to the **short-sighted focus** of a business on its products rather than on the **customer benefits** they deliver (2 marks). **Example (1 mark):** **Kodak** failed because it defined itself as a "film company" instead of a "photography company," missing the digital revolution despite inventing the digital camera itself. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* *(a)* Explain the **five eras in the evolution of marketing** with their core beliefs and limitations. *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** The evolution of marketing is broadly classified into five eras: **1. Production Era (Pre-1920s) — (1.4 marks)** - **Core Belief:** Consumers prefer products that are affordable and available. - **Focus:** Mass production and distribution efficiency. - **Limitation:** Ignores consumer preferences; assumes uniform demand. - **Example:** Ford Model T — only black, single model. **2. Product Era (1920s–1930s) — (1.4 marks)** - **Core Belief:** Consumers favour products with **superior quality, features, and performance**. - **Focus:** Continuous product innovation. - **Limitation:** Leads to **Marketing Myopia** — building products no one wants. - **Example:** Over-engineered gadgets with no market. **3. Selling Era (1930s–1950s) — (1.4 marks)** - **Core Belief:** Aggressive selling is needed to push products. - **Focus:** Advertising, personal selling, sales promotions. - **Limitation:** High-pressure tactics damage trust. - **Example:** Door-to-door encyclopedia salesmen of the 1950s. **4. Marketing Era (1950s–1990s) — (1.4 marks)** - **Core Belief:** Customer satisfaction through understanding **needs, wants, and demands**. - **Focus:** Market research, **STP**, **4 Ps**. - **Limitation:** Ignores broader social/environmental impact. - **Example:** Procter & Gamble's consumer-driven product development. **5. Societal Marketing Era (1990s–Present) — (1.4 marks)** - **Core Belief:** Delivering customer value while preserving **societal well-being**. - **Focus:** **Triple Bottom Line** — People, Planet, Profit. - **Limitation:** Higher costs, complex measurement. - **Example:** Tesla — sells EVs but promotes sustainability. --- > *(b)* Compare the **Selling Orientation** and **Marketing Orientation** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Selling Orientation | Marketing Orientation | |:-:|:-:|:-:| | **Core Belief** | Customers must be persuaded to buy | Customer needs drive the firm | | **Starting Point** | The product | The customer | | **Focus** | Seller's need to convert inventory | Buyer's need satisfaction | | **Time Horizon** | Short-term sales | Long-term relationships | | **Tools** | Advertising, sales promotion | Market research, STP, 4 Ps | | **Customer Role** | Passive recipient | Active co-creator of value | | **Success Metric** | Sales volume | Customer lifetime value | **Examples (2 marks):** - **Selling:** Insurance cold-calling, time-share property push-sales. - **Marketing:** Amazon's recommendation engine, Netflix's personalized content. **Key Difference:** Selling is **transaction-focused** ("push"), while marketing is **relationship-focused** ("pull"). Marketing orientation demands **understanding before action**, whereas selling orientation demands **action despite understanding**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]* *(a)* Discuss the **Societal Marketing Concept** in detail. How is it different from the **Marketing Concept**? *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Societal Marketing Concept (4 marks):** Coined by **Philip Kotler** in the 1970s and formalized in the 1990s, the Societal Marketing Concept asserts that a company's marketing strategy should deliver value to customers in a way that **maintains or improves both the consumer's and society's well-being**. **Three Considerations:** 1. **Consumer Satisfaction** (customer wants). 2. **Company Goals** (profitability). 3. **Societal Welfare** (long-term public interest). **Marketing Concept vs. Societal Marketing Concept (3 marks):** | Aspect | Marketing Concept | Societal Marketing Concept | |:-:|:-:|:-:| | Primary Focus | Customer satisfaction | Customer + Society satisfaction | | Time Frame | Medium-term | Long-term | | Scope | Individual buyer welfare | Collective welfare | | Tools | 4 Ps | 4 Ps + CSR + Sustainability | | Metric | Customer satisfaction score | Triple Bottom Line | **Example:** **The Body Shop** uses natural ingredients, supports fair trade, and avoids animal testing — addressing customer beauty needs *and* societal ethical concerns. --- > *(b)* Explain the concept of **Marketing Myopia** with two real-world examples and discuss how organizations can overcome it. *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Concept (2 marks):** **Marketing Myopia** (Theodore Levitt, 1960) is the **short-sighted, inward-looking error** of defining a business in terms of its products rather than the **customer benefits** it provides. **Example 1 — Kodak (2 marks):** Kodak defined itself as a *"film and camera company"* instead of *"image-capture and memory-keeping company."* When digital photography emerged, Kodak failed to adapt, despite inventing the first digital camera in 1975. It filed for bankruptcy in 2012. **Example 2 — Nokia (1.5 marks):** Nokia defined itself as a *"mobile phone manufacturer"* rather than a *"mobile communication experience provider."* It failed to anticipate the iPhone's touchscreen-and-apps revolution and lost its market leadership. **Strategies to Overcome Marketing Myopia (1.5 marks):** 1. **Define the business by customer benefit**, not product (Levitt's "railroad vs. transportation" lesson). 2. **Continuous environmental scanning** — PESTEL analysis. 3. **Invest in customer research** and emerging trend detection. 4. **Encourage intrapreneurship** to nurture innovation. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Evolution Questions:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse eras with marketing functions.** Students often mix up *Marketing Mix* with *Marketing Eras* — both are different concepts. > 2. **Don't write only definitions.** KTU examiners award marks for **examples and limitations** of each era. Always include both. > 3. **Societal Marketing ≠ Marketing Concept.** This is the **most common confusion** in KTU answer scripts. Societal adds a **third dimension — society**. > 4. **Marketing Myopia is product-centred focus**, NOT lack of advertising. > 5. **For 14-mark questions,** students often miss the **comparison table** which carries **3–4 easy marks.** Always use a tabular comparison when asked to compare two concepts. > 6. **Don't skip timelines.** Examiners expect approximate **time periods** for each era. Writing "old era" loses marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Evolution of Marketing:** - ⭐ Marketing = Identifying, anticipating, and **satisfying customer needs profitably**. - ⭐ **Five Eras:** Production → Product → Selling → Marketing → Societal Marketing. - ⭐ **Production Era:** Focus on availability and low cost (Ford Model T). - ⭐ **Product Era:** Focus on quality and features → risk of **Marketing Myopia**. - ⭐ **Selling Era:** Aggressive persuasion, push strategy, short-term focus. - ⭐ **Marketing Era:** Customer-driven, STP, **4 Ps**, long-term relationships. - ⭐ **Societal Era:** Triple Bottom Line — **People, Planet, Profit**. - ⭐ **Marketing Myopia** = Levitt (1960), short-sighted product focus. - ⭐ **AMA definition (2013)** — current standard, KTU examiners expect this version. - ⭐ **Kodak, Nokia, Kodak** = classic Marketing Myopia case studies. - ⭐ The **Customer Value Equation:** $CV = B - C$ where $B$ is benefit, $C$ is cost. - ⭐ Each transition is triggered by a **market shock** (saturation, informed consumers, environmental crisis). - ⭐ For 14-mark answers, always include **definition + eras + comparison table + examples + limitations**.

topics.title: Marketing Planning Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Marketing Planning Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marketing Planning Process** is the systematic sequence of analytical, strategic, and operational activities undertaken by an organization to identify market opportunities, define marketing objectives, formulate target-market and positioning strategies, allocate resources, execute marketing programs, and continuously monitor performance to achieve long-term competitive advantage and customer satisfaction.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned Outcome-Based Education)**, the marketing planning process is taught as a **strategic management function** that integrates the *4 Ps/7 Ps Marketing Mix*, the *STP Framework (Segmentation – Targeting – Positioning)*, and *SWOT/TOWS analysis* into a unified, time-bound written document called the **Marketing Plan**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — A Road Trip Planner:**
>
> Imagine you are planning a long road trip from **Kochi to Delhi** with friends.
>
> - You do not simply start driving; you **decide the destination** (objective), **check your budget** (resource allocation), **look at the map for the best route** (strategy), **book hotels in advance** (tactics), and **track fuel and time along the way** (control).
> - If the highway is blocked, you take a detour — this is **continuous evaluation and feedback**.
>
> The **Marketing Planning Process is exactly this**, but applied to a product, service, or brand. It transforms an organization from "driving blind" into "driving with a GPS".

## 1.3 Why Marketing Planning Matters (KTU 2024 Emphasis)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the marketing planning process is a **high-weightage topic** because it:
- Translates the **Marketing Mix (4Ps)** from theory into actionable strategy.
- Forms the **bridge between strategic management and operational marketing**.
- Is a direct assessable outcome in **Course Outcome CO1 / CO2** of PEIET752.

**Three Pillars of Marketing Planning (KTU Framework):**
1. **Analytical Foundation** — Situation, customer, competitor analysis.
2. **Strategic Direction** — Objectives, STP, positioning.
3. **Operational Execution** — Tactics, budgets, implementation, control.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Whenever the question asks *"Explain the marketing planning process"*, immediately structure your answer around these three pillars. Examiners award full marks when students demonstrate this logical flow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Decomposition — The 8-Step Marketing Planning Process

The marketing planning process is universally accepted as an **iterative, cyclic, and continuous** workflow. The KTU syllabus emphasizes the following **8 sequential steps**:

### Step 1 — Mission, Vision & Corporate Objectives Review
- Re-examine the organization's **mission statement**, **vision**, and **long-term corporate goals**.
- Ensures marketing plans are **aligned vertically** with corporate strategy.
- **Output:** Refined business scope and marketing scope statement.

### Step 2 — Situation Analysis (Internal + External)
- **Internal Analysis:** Resources, capabilities, past performance, financial health (e.g., **SWOT Strengths & Weaknesses**, **Value Chain**, **Resource-Based View**).
- **External Analysis:** Industry, competitors, customers, regulation, technology (e.g., **SWOT Opportunities & Threats**, **PESTEL**, **Porter's Five Forces**).
- **Output:** A complete **SWOT Matrix** that summarizes the firm's current market position.

### Step 3 — Setting Marketing Objectives (SMART Framework)
- Objectives must be **Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound**.
- Examples: *"Increase market share in Kerala by **15%** within 18 months"* or *"Achieve ₹50 crore revenue from new product launch in FY 2025-26"*.
- **Hierarchy:** Corporate → Business → Marketing → Communication Objectives.

### Step 4 — Strategic Marketing Decisions (STP)
- **Segmentation:** Divide heterogeneous market into homogeneous subgroups (demographic, geographic, psychographic, behavioral).
- **Targeting:** Evaluate segments and select one or more using **STP targeting strategies** — *undifferentiated, differentiated, concentrated, micromarketing*.
- **Positioning:** Design a distinctive image in the customer's mind (**Perceptual Mapping**, **Value Proposition Design**).

### Step 5 — Formulating Marketing Strategies (Marketing Mix 4Ps/7Ps)
- **4Ps (Philip Kotler — Product, Price, Place, Promotion).**
- **7Ps (Booms & Bitner — extended with People, Process, Physical Evidence)** — applicable for services.
- Each P is tailored to the chosen target segment's needs.

### Step 6 — Tactical Marketing Programs / Action Plans
- Defines *who, what, when, where, and how* for each strategy element.
- Example: *"Launch digital ad campaign on Instagram and YouTube between **1st March and 30th April 2025**, targeting urban 18–35 year-olds in Tier-1 cities, with a budget of **₹75 lakhs**."*

### Step 7 — Budget Allocation & Resource Planning
- Common budgeting methods:
  - **Percentage-of-Sales Method**
  - **Objective-and-Task Method** (most preferred academically)
  - **Competitive Parity Method**
  - **Affordable Method**
- Establishes **financial feasibility** of the plan.

### Step 8 — Implementation, Monitoring & Control
- Translates plan into action through **marketing organization**, **schedules**, and **execution**.
- Uses **Marketing Metrics / KPIs** (e.g., CAC, CLV, ROI, NPS) for monitoring.
- Apply the **Marketing Control Process**:
  1. Set performance standards.
  2. Measure actual performance.
  3. Compare actual vs. standard.
  4. Take corrective action (**annual-plan control, profitability control, strategic control via Marketing Audit**).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Framework Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every key formula, framework, and standard metric required for KTU 2024 examinations on this topic.

| **Framework / Formula** | **Formula / Structure** | **Application Context** |
|---|---|---|
| **SWOT Matrix** | $S \times W \times O \times T$ | Internal + External situation analysis |
| **TOWS Matrix** | $SO,\,ST,\,WO,\,WT \rightarrow 4\,\text{strategies}$ | Strategy generation from SWOT |
| **SMART Objectives** | $S,\,M,\,A,\,R,\,T$ | Setting marketing objectives |
| **STP Framework** | $S \rightarrow T \rightarrow P$ | Strategic customer focus |
| **Marketing Mix (4Ps)** | $M = P_1 + P_2 + P_3 + P_4$ | Tactical strategy design |
| **Marketing Mix (7Ps)** | $M_{7P} = 4P + People + Process + Phy.\,Evidence$ | Service marketing |
| **ROI (Marketing)** | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Marketing Profit}}{\text{Marketing Investment}} \times 100$ | Performance evaluation |
| **Market Share %** | $MS = \dfrac{\text{Firm Sales}}{\text{Total Market Sales}} \times 100$ | Competitive performance |
| **Sales Growth %** | $SG = \dfrac{\text{Sales}_{t} - \text{Sales}_{t-1}}{\text{Sales}_{t-1}} \times 100$ | Year-on-year growth tracking |
| **Customer Acquisition Cost** | $CAC = \dfrac{\text{Total Marketing Spend}}{\text{New Customers Acquired}}$ | Digital marketing efficiency |
| **Customer Lifetime Value** | $CLV = \text{Avg.\ Purchase Value} \times \text{Freq.} \times \text{Customer Lifespan}$ | Long-term customer value |
| **Marketing Audit Frequency** | $\text{Strategic Audit} \rightarrow \text{Annually}$ | Strategic control mechanism |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Symbols:** The KTU 2024 scheme uses **₹ (INR)** as the default currency in numerical examples; however, formula structure is **currency-agnostic**. Always express percentages with the % symbol explicitly.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Marketing Planning

In engineering-driven businesses (which is the **KTU student context** — e.g., IT services, manufacturing startups, IoT firms, Kerala-based tech SMEs), the marketing planning process is used for:
- **Launching new tech products** (e.g., a wearable IoT device).
- **Entering export markets** (e.g., Kerala spice exports to GCC countries).
- **B2B service positioning** for engineering consultancies.
- **Government-supported startup schemes** (Kerala Startup Mission — KSUM).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivation Logic

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Each Step to KTU Course Outcomes

| **Step No.** | **Step Name** | **Primary Tool** | **KTU Course Outcome** | **Bloom's Level** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Mission & Vision Review | Corporate documents, Vision Tree | CO1 | Understand |
| **2** | Situation Analysis | SWOT, PESTEL, Porter's 5 Forces | CO1, CO2 | Analyze |
| **3** | Marketing Objectives | SMART Framework, KPI Tree | CO2 | Apply |
| **4** | STP Strategy | Segmentation grid, Perceptual Map | CO2, CO3 | Apply / Analyze |
| **5** | Marketing Mix Design | 4Ps/7Ps matrix | CO3 | Create |
| **6** | Tactical Programs | Action plan Gantt chart | CO3, CO4 | Create |
| **7** | Budget Allocation | Objective-and-Task method | CO4 | Evaluate |
| **8** | Implementation & Control | Marketing audit, Balanced Scorecard | CO4, CO5 | Evaluate |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Applying the Process to a Hypothetical Case

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Context:** A Kerala-based engineering college wants to launch a new **B.Tech programme in Artificial Intelligence & Data Science** for the academic year 2025-26. Apply the 8-step marketing planning process.

### Step 1 — Mission Review
The college's mission is *"To produce industry-ready, ethically grounded engineers through outcome-based education aligned with NEP 2020."*

The new AI programme aligns with this mission because AI is a **national priority** and NEP 2020 emphasizes **multidisciplinary learning**.

### Step 2 — Situation Analysis (SWOT)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Strengths} &: \text{Existing infrastructure, KTU affiliation, experienced faculty} \\
\text{Weaknesses} &: \text{Limited AI-specific labs, low brand recall in AI domain} \\
\text{Opportunities} &: \text{Industry demand for AI engineers (CAGR} \approx 40\%\text{), Kerala IT policy} \\
\text{Threats} &: \text{Competition from IITs, NITs, private AI bootcamps}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — SMART Objectives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Objective 1} &: \text{Achieve an intake of 60 students in AY 2025-26 (Specific, Measurable)} \\
\text{Objective 2} &: \text{Attain 95\% placement by 2027-28 (Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound)} \\
\text{Objective 3} &: \text{Establish 5 industry MoUs within 12 months}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — STP Strategy

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Segmentation} &: \text{Kerala + TN students, JEE/KEAM rank holders, NEET-eligible science stream} \\
\text{Targeting} &: \text{Differentiated targeting — both merit-based and NRI quota} \\
\text{Positioning} &: \text{"Kerala's most practice-oriented AI engineering degree with industry internships"}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Marketing Mix (4Ps)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Product} &: \text{B.Tech AI \& DS, 4 years, with honours track in Generative AI} \\
\text{Price} &: \text{₹1,15,000/year (merit quota), \$4,500/year (NRI)} \\
\text{Place} &: \text{On-campus + digital admission portal + Kerala-wide school outreach} \\
\text{Promotion} &: \text{Educational seminars, YouTube channel, alumni testimonials, SEO ads}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Tactical Action Plan

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tactic 1} &: \text{Conduct 20 school seminars across Kerala between Oct 2024 – Feb 2025} \\
\text{Tactic 2} &: \text{Run targeted Instagram + LinkedIn ads with budget of ₹8 lakhs} \\
\text{Tactic 3} &: \text{Partner with 3 tech companies for live project showcases}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Budget Allocation (Objective-and-Task Method)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Marketing Budget} &= \text{Digital Ads} + \text{School Outreach} + \text{Content Production} \\
&= \text{₹8,00,000} + \text{₹4,00,000} + \text{₹3,00,000} \\
&= \text{₹15,00,000}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Implementation & Control

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Monthly Tracking KPIs} &: \text{Applications received, cost per lead (CPL), inquiry-to-enrolment ratio} \\
\text{Quarterly Review} &: \text{Marketing Audit + Faculty Council review} \\
\text{Annual Strategic Review} &: \text{Update plan based on placement outcomes and emerging AI trends}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of ROI from Marketing Investment

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI}_{\text{Marketing}} &= \frac{\text{Marketing Profit}}{\text{Marketing Investment}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(\text{Revenue from Marketing Activity} - \text{Marketing Cost})}{\text{Marketing Cost}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \left( \frac{\text{Revenue}}{\text{Cost}} - 1 \right) \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:**
> - The numerator captures **net profit attributable** to marketing campaigns.
> - The denominator captures **total marketing spend** (ads, salaries, tools).
> - Multiplying by **100** converts the ratio into a percentage for **standardized comparison** across campaigns, quarters, or competitors.

**Example Calculation:**
If a firm spent ₹20,00,000 on digital marketing and generated incremental revenue of ₹1,20,00,000 with 65% gross margin, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Incremental Profit} &= \text{₹1,20,00,000} \times 0.65 = \text{₹78,00,000} \\[6pt]
\text{ROI} &= \frac{\text{₹78,00,000} - \text{₹20,00,000}}{\text{₹20,00,000}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\text{₹58,00,000}}{\text{₹20,00,000}} \times 100 = 290\%
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The 8-Step Marketing Planning Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Mission and Vision Review"]
    B["Step 2: Situation Analysis - SWOT and PESTEL"]
    C["Step 3: Set SMART Marketing Objectives"]
    D["Step 4: STP - Segment, Target, Position"]
    E["Step 5: Design Marketing Mix - 4Ps or 7Ps"]
    F["Step 6: Tactical Action Plans"]
    G["Step 7: Budget Allocation"]
    H["Step 8: Implementation Monitoring and Control"]
    I["Revised Strategy for Next Cycle"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I -.-> B
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Marketing Control Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Set Performance Standards"]
    P2["Measure Actual Performance"]
    P3["Compare Actual vs Standard"]
    P4["Diagnose Deviations"]
    P5["Take Corrective Action"]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 -.-> P1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Tree — Integration of SWOT, STP and Marketing Mix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
