Discuss the role of SWOT Analysis in strategy formulation. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks The **Ansoff Matrix** (Igor Ansoff, 1957) is a strategic planning tool that identifies four growth alternatives based on whether products and markets are existing or new. (1 Mark) The matrix classifies growth into **four cells** based on risk: 1. **Market Penetration (Low Risk):** Selling existing products to existing markets more intensively through pricing, promotion, or distribution gains. *Example:* Coca-Cola increasing ad spend in India to grow share. (1.5 Marks) 2. **Market Development (Medium Risk):** Selling existing products in new geographic markets or new customer segments. *Example:* Tata Tea exporting to Europe. (1.5 Marks) 3. **Product Development (Medium Risk):** Introducing new products to existing markets. Achieved through R&D or acquisition. *Example:* Apple launching AirPods to existing iPhone users. (1.5 Marks) 4. **Diversification (High Risk):** Introducing new products into new markets. Done via related diversification (synergy) or unrelated diversification (pure financial play). *Example:* Tata Group moving from steel to hospitality (Taj Hotels). (1.5 Marks) #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks The **Strategic Planning Process** is the systematic procedure by which an organization formulates, implements, and evaluates its strategies. (1 Mark) **Five Stages:** 1. **Defining Vision and Mission:** Articulates the firm's purpose, scope, and aspirational future. (1 Mark) 2. **Environmental Scanning & Analysis:** - **External Analysis:** PESTEL macro + Porter's Five Forces micro. - **Internal Analysis:** Value Chain, VRIO, Resource Audit. (1.5 Marks) 3. **Strategy Formulation:** Generating strategic alternatives (Porter's, Ansoff, BCG) and choosing the best fit. (1 Mark) 4. **Strategy Implementation:** Allocating resources, designing organizational structure, leading change. (1 Mark) 5. **Evaluation & Control:** Using the Balanced Scorecard (financial, customer, internal process, learning) to monitor and adjust. (1 Mark) **Role of SWOT Analysis:** SWOT integrates the Stage-2 findings into a 2x2 matrix: - **Strengths & Weaknesses** (Internal) - **Opportunities & Threats** (External) Its role is to **synthesize** environmental data into actionable strategic choices — converting strengths to capture opportunities (SO strategies), using strengths to neutralize threats (ST), etc. It bridges analysis and formulation. (1.5 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently write SWOT as a *list* rather than as a **strategy-generation tool**. The KTU board wants you to show how SWOT leads to actionable strategies such as **SO, ST, WO, WT** combinations. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam** - **Definition:** Business strategy = long-term, integrated plan for competitive advantage and customer value. - **Three Levels:** Corporate (Portfolio) → Business (How to Win) → Functional (How to Execute). - **Strategic Planning Process:** Vision → Analysis → Formulation → Implementation → Evaluation (with feedback). - **Porter's Generic Strategies:** Cost Leadership, Differentiation, Cost Focus, Differentiation Focus. - **Stuck-in-the-Middle Trap:** Avoid trying both cost leadership AND differentiation. - **BCG Matrix Four Quadrants:** Stars (invest), Cash Cows (harvest), Question Marks (selective invest), Dogs (divest). - **Ansoff Matrix Four Growth Strategies:** Market Penetration, Market Development, Product Development, Diversification — risks increase from penetration to diversification. - **Porter's Five Forces:** Determines industry attractiveness — entrants, suppliers, buyers, substitutes, rivalry. - **VRIO Framework:** Resource-based test for sustainable competitive advantage. - **SWOT Role:** Bridges environmental analysis and strategy formulation. - **Balanced Scorecard:** Four perspectives — Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth. - **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never use `|` symbol for absolute value inside KTU answer table cells; use LaTeX `$\lvert x \rvert$` instead. - **Numerical Metric:** Always remember **Market Share, CAGR, Relative Market Share, SGR** for any 14-mark analytical question. - **Strategy-Mix Linking:** When answering any KTU long question, always link the chosen strategy to the **4Ps of Marketing** for full marks. - **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"S-C-Q-D"** for BCG = **Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, Dogs**.

topics.title: Product Strategy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Product Strategy # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Product Strategy** is the **long-term, integrated plan** of a firm that defines what products the company will offer, to **which customer segments**, in **which markets**, and through **what positioning, branding, packaging, and lifecycle management decisions**, in order to achieve its **corporate and marketing objectives**. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, *Product Strategy* is the **first and most critical element of the Marketing Mix (the 4Ps — Product, Price, Place, Promotion)**. It is the *P* that gives a firm its **raison d'être** — without a clearly defined product, the other three Ps have nothing to attach themselves to. A *product* in modern marketing is **anything that can be offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption that might satisfy a want or need** (KTU adopted definition — Philip Kotler lineage). > [!NOTE] > **Broad Definition Includes:** > - Physical goods (smartphones, cars, textbooks) > - Services (banking, education, healthcare) > - Ideas, places, persons, organizations, experiences > - Digital products (apps, SaaS subscriptions, e-books) --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Restaurant Menu" Analogy for Product Strategy** 🍽️ > > Imagine a restaurant owner, *Mrs. Priya*, in Kochi. She sits down to plan her menu (her **Product Strategy**). > > - She doesn't just list dishes — she decides **what categories** to offer (South Indian, Chinese, Continental — this is her **product mix**). > - Within South Indian, she decides how many items to keep — *Idli, Dosa, Vada, Upma, Appam, Puttu* — this is **product line length**. > - She decides **which dish represents her** — perhaps her signature *Ghee Roast Dosa* — this is the **flagship product** or **brand positioning**. > - Over time, she introduces seasonal items (mango dishes in summer) and retires unpopular items — this is the **Product Life Cycle (PLC)**. > - She might add a premium section — *"Chef's Special Thali"* — this is **product line stretching**. > > Just like Mrs. Priya's menu, a company's **Product Strategy** is the **engineered blueprint of what it sells, how it's organized, how it evolves, and how it's differentiated** from competitors. --- ## 1.3 The Three Levels of a Product (KTU High-Yield Concept) Every product, according to the **Kotler three-level model** (rigorously tested in KTU exams), exists on three hierarchical levels: | Level | Definition | Example (Apple iPhone) | |---|---|---| | **Core Product** | The fundamental *benefit* or *problem-solution* the customer buys | Communication, status, productivity | | **Actual Product** | The tangible features, design, brand name, quality, packaging | iPhone 15, 128GB, Midnight Black, Apple logo | | **Augmented Product** | The *additional services* and *exceeding-expectation* elements | Free cloud storage, AppleCare+, 1-year warranty, free engraving | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why This Matters in KTU Board Exams:** Examiners frequently ask: *"At which level does marketing actually occur?"* The correct answer is **all three levels** — but the *augmented* level is where **competitive differentiation** is built in modern markets. --- ## 1.4 Product Classification (Durable vs Non-Durable vs Services) Products are classified into **three primary categories** based on tangibility and usage pattern: - **Durable Goods** — Tangible products that survive multiple uses. *Example: Refrigerator, laptop, automobile.* Sells through personal selling, high-margin after-sales service. - **Non-Durable Goods** — Tangible products consumed in one or a few uses. *Example: Soap, biscuits, shampoo.* Mass distribution, heavy advertising, low margins, repeat purchase behavior. - **Services** — Intangible, inseparable, variable, perishable products. *Example: Education, banking, healthcare.* Require trust-building, customer relationship management (CRM), and service quality frameworks (SERVQUAL). > [!NOTE] > **Consumer Goods vs Industrial Goods** is another critical classification in KTU syllabus: > - **Consumer goods** are bought by end-users for personal consumption (convenience, shopping, specialty, unsought). > - **Industrial goods** are bought by businesses for further processing or operations (raw materials, capital items, supplies, services). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Components of Product Strategy (Operational Decomposition) Product Strategy, when operationalized, breaks down into **five interlocking decision-areas**: ### 2.1.1 Product Attributes Decision The marketer engineers the **actual product** with five key attributes: - **Quality Level** — Performance, durability, reliability - **Features** — Distinctive characteristics - **Design & Style** — Aesthetic appeal - **Brand Name** — Identity & legal protection - **Packaging** — Protection + promotion ### 2.1.2 Branding Decision Deciding on **brand name selection, brand sponsorship (manufacturer vs private vs licensed), and brand strategy**: - *Product line branding* — Tide, Ariel, Surf (HUL) - *Family branding* — Samsung Galaxy, Samsung Note - *Individual branding* — P&G uses different names for different products - *Co-branding* — OnePlus × McLaren, BMW × Louis Vuitton ### 2.1.3 Packaging Decision The *"silent salesman"*. Performs three functions: 1. **Protection** of the product 2. **Promotion** at point-of-purchase 3. **Information** delivery (ingredients, MRP, FSSAI mark) ### 2.1.4 Labeling Decision Legal requirement under the **Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules, 2011** in India. Labels must disclose: manufacturer's name, MRP, net quantity, date of manufacture, expiry date, ingredients, batch number. ### 2.1.5 Product Support Services Decision Augmented product services — installation, warranty, maintenance, after-sales, customer support. Critical for high-involvement durable goods. --- ## 2.2 Product Mix (Product Portfolio) — The Core Analytical Tool A company's **Product Mix (or Product Portfolio)** is the complete set of all products and product lines offered for sale. It is described using **four dimensions**: | Dimension | Definition | KTU Mnemonic | |---|---|---| | **Width** | Number of **distinct product lines** the company offers | **"How many categories?"** | | **Length** | Total **number of items** across all product lines | **"How many SKUs?"** | | **Depth** | Number of **variations** within each product (size, color, flavor) | **"How many versions of one item?"** | | **Consistency** | How **closely related** the various product lines are in use, production, distribution | **"How similar are the lines?"** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example for KTU Board Exam:** > *A company sells:* > - *Soap Line — 4 variants (Lux, Dove, Pears, Rexona)* > - *Shampoo Line — 3 variants (Sunsilk, Clinic Plus, TRESemmé)* > - *Deodorant Line — 2 variants (Rexona, Dove)* > > **Width = 3 lines, Length = 9 items, Depth = varies (average 3), Consistency = HIGH (all personal care).** --- ## 2.3 Product Line Analysis & Decisions (KTU High-Yield) A **Product Line** is a group of closely related products that function in a similar manner, are sold to the same customer groups, marketed through the same outlets, and fall within given price ranges. ### Four Critical Product Line Decisions: **Decision 1: Line Stretching** — Adding items *beyond* the current range: - *Downward Stretch* — Moving into lower price-quality tier (Maruti adding Alto for entry-level) - *Upward Stretch* — Moving into premium tier (Apple adding iPhone Pro Max) - *Two-way Stretch* — Both up and down simultaneously **Decision 2: Line Filling** — Adding more items *within* the existing range to reduce gaps, increase profits, and block competitors. **Decision 3: Line Modernization** — Updating existing products' features, design, or technology (e.g., Maruti Swift getting a facelift every 3-4 years). **Decision 4: Line Pruning (Line Featuring)** — Deleting weak, unprofitable items to focus resources on profitable ones. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula / Framework Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Note for KTU PEIET752:** Marketing Management has limited quantitative formulas. The "formulas" here are **analytical frameworks** with conditional rules. ### Framework 1: Product Mix Calculations $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Width} &= \text{Number of Product Lines} \\[4pt] \textbf{Length} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Items in Line } i) \\[4pt] \textbf{Average Depth} &= \frac{\textbf{Length}}{\textbf{Width}} \\[4pt] \textbf{Consistency Index (qualitative)} &= \frac{\text{Shared End-Use + Channels + Price Range}}{3} \end{aligned} $$ ### Framework 2: Product Life Cycle (PLC) Stage-Identification Rule $$ \text{Stage Identification} = f(S, \Delta S, C, R, P) $$ Where: - $S$ = **Sales level** (absolute) - $\Delta S$ = **Sales growth rate** (% change in sales) - $C$ = **Competition intensity** (number of competitors) - $R$ = **Profit trajectory** (rising, peak, declining) - $P$ = **Customer profile evolution** (innovators → laggards) ### Framework 3: BCG Matrix Quadrant Classification $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Market Share} &= \frac{\text{Company Sales in Segment}}{\text{Leading Competitor Sales in Segment}} \\[6pt] \textbf{Market Growth Rate} &= \frac{\text{Current Year Market Size} - \text{Previous Year Market Size}}{\text{Previous Year Market Size}} \times 100 \\[6pt] \end{aligned} $$ | Quadrant | Market Growth | Relative Market Share | Strategic Action | |---|---|---|---| | **Stars** | High ($\geq 10\%$) | High ($\geq 1.0$) | **INVEST aggressively** | | **Cash Cows** | Low ($< 10\%$) | High ($\geq 1.0$) | **HARVEST cash**, maintain | | **Question Marks** | High ($\geq 10\%$) | Low ($< 1.0$) | **SELECTIVELY INVEST or DIVEST** | | **Dogs** | Low ($< 10\%$) | Low ($< 1.0$) | **DIVEST / LIQUIDATE** | ### Framework 4: New Product Development Success Probability (KTU Conceptual) $$ P_{\text{success}} = P_{\text{ideation}} \times P_{\text{screening}} \times P_{\text{concept}} \times P_{\text{development}} \times P_{\text{test}} \times P_{\text{launch}} $$ > This multiplicative formula explains why new product failure rates are **70-80%** — each stage filters out weak ideas. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: BCG Matrix Classification of a Product Portfolio This is a **frequently asked 14-mark analytical problem** in KTU ESE for Marketing Management. Below is the complete stepwise analytical treatment. ### Problem Setup > A company **XYZ Ltd.** has four products in its portfolio: **P1, P2, P3, P4**. > - Market Growth Rate of the segment: **15%, 5%, 18%, 3%** > - Company Sales: **₹500 Cr, ₹300 Cr, ₹100 Cr, ₹50 Cr** > - Largest Competitor Sales: **₹400 Cr, ₹600 Cr, ₹50 Cr, ₹80 Cr** > > Classify each product using the **BCG Matrix**. --- ### Step 1 — Identify the Two Axes of the BCG Matrix The BCG Matrix plots products on two strategic dimensions: 1. **Vertical Axis (Y-axis):** Market Growth Rate (MG%) 2. **Horizontal Axis (X-axis):** Relative Market Share (RMS) **Decision Rule (Mid-point split):** - Y-axis split: Market Growth = **10%** (Kotlin threshold) - X-axis split: RMS = **1.0** (parity with largest competitor) --- ### Step 2 — Compute Market Growth Rate Classification $$ \text{If } MG \geq 10\% \rightarrow \textbf{HIGH} \qquad \text{If } MG < 10\% \rightarrow \textbf{LOW} $$ | Product | MG% | Classification | |---|---|---| | P1 | 15% | **HIGH** | | P2 | 5% | **LOW** | | P3 | 18% | **HIGH** | | P4 | 3% | **LOW** | --- ### Step 3 — Compute Relative Market Share (RMS) for Each Product The KTU-standard formula: $$ \text{RMS} = \frac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Largest Competitor Sales}} $$ **Product P1:** $$ \text{RMS}_{P1} = \frac{500}{400} = 1.25 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Since } 1.25 \geq 1.0 \rightarrow \textbf{HIGH} $$ **Product P2:** $$ \text{RMS}_{P2} = \frac{300}{600} = 0.50 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Since } 0.50 < 1.0 \rightarrow \textbf{LOW} $$ **Product P3:** $$ \text{RMS}_{P3} = \frac{100}{50} = 2.00 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Since } 2.00 \geq 1.0 \rightarrow \textbf{HIGH} $$ **Product P4:** $$ \text{RMS}_{P4} = \frac{50}{80} = 0.625 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Since } 0.625 < 1.0 \rightarrow \textbf{LOW} $$ --- ### Step 4 — Plot the BCG Matrix and Assign Quadrants | Product | MG% | RMS | **BCG Quadrant** | Strategic Directive | |---|---|---|---|---| | **P1** | HIGH (15%) | HIGH (1.25) | ⭐ **STAR** | Invest aggressively to defend leadership | | **P2** | LOW (5%) | HIGH (0.50) | 🐄 **CASH COW** | Harvest cash flow, minimize investment | | **P3** | HIGH (18%) | HIGH (2.00) | ⭐ **STAR** | Heavy investment to convert to future Cash Cow | | **P4** | LOW (3%) | LOW (0.625) | 🐕 **DOG** | Divest, harvest, or liquidate | --- ### Step 5 — Visualize the BCG Plot | | **High RMS (≥1.0)** | **Low RMS (<1.0)** | |---|---|---| | **High MG (≥10%)** | P1 ⭐ STAR, P3 ⭐ STAR | (empty) Question Marks | | **Low MG (<10%)** | P2 🐄 CASH COW | P4 🐕 DOG | --- ### Step 6 — Strategic Portfolio Recommendation For **XYZ Ltd.**: 1. **P1 & P3 (Stars):** Allocate the largest R&D and marketing budget — these are tomorrow's Cash Cows. 2. **P2 (Cash Cow):** Use the cash generated to fund Stars and selected Question Marks. 3. **P4 (Dog):** Either **liquidate** the product and free up shelf space, warehouse, and management bandwidth, or **harvest** (reduce marketing spend to zero and extract residual cash). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often confuse **Absolute Market Share** with **Relative Market Share**. The BCG formula uses **RMS (vs. largest competitor)**, NOT total market share. Writing $\frac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Total Market Sales}}$ will fetch **zero marks**. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Product Life Cycle (PLC) Stage Comparison | Characteristic | Introduction | Growth | Maturity | Decline | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Sales** | Low | Rapidly rising | Peak, plateau | Falling | | **Costs/Customer** | High | Falling | Low | Low | | **Profits** | Negative | Rising | Peak | Falling | | **Customers** | Innovators (2.5%) | Early Adopters (13.5%) | Early + Late Majority (68%) | Laggards (16%) | | **Competition** | Few | Many entering | Stable, intense | Few exit | | **Marketing Objective** | Create awareness | Maximize market share | Defend share, extend | Harvest / withdraw | | **Product Strategy** | Basic product, frequent design changes | Product extensions, feature additions | Diversification, brand variants | Phase out weak models | > [!NOTE] > **Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation Curve:** Innovators (2.5%) → Early Adopters (13.5%) → Early Majority (34%) → Late Majority (34%) → Laggards (16%). This is a frequently tested KTU short-answer concept. --- ## 3.3 New Product Development Process (7-Stage KTU Standard) | Stage | Activity | Output | |---|---|---| | 1. **Idea Generation** | Internal R&D, customer feedback, competitor analysis | Hundreds of raw ideas | | 2. **Idea Screening** | Evaluate against strategic fit, technical feasibility, market potential | Filtered idea pool | | 3. **Concept Development & Testing** | Convert ideas into customer-tested product concepts | Winning concept | | 4. **Business Analysis** | Sales forecast, cost projection, profit estimation | Feasibility report | | 5. **Product Development** | Engineering, prototype, brand name, packaging design | Working prototype | | 6. **Test Marketing** | Controlled launch in limited geography | Validation data | | 7. **Commercialization** | Full-scale launch with marketing mix rollout | Live product | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Three-Level Product Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["CORE PRODUCT LEVEL
Core Benefit or Problem-Solution"] node2["ACTUAL PRODUCT LEVEL
Features + Quality + Design + Brand + Packaging"] node3["AUGMENTED PRODUCT LEVEL
Warranty + Service + Delivery + Installation"] node4["CUSTOMER PERCEIVED VALUE"] node5["EXAMPLE: SMARTPHONE"] node6["Communication Status Productivity"] node7["128GB RAM 12GB Camera 50MP iOS Apple Logo Box"] node8["1yr Warranty Free Cloud AppleCare 24x7 Support"] node9["TOTAL CUSTOMER VALUE"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node5 --> node6 node6 --> node7 node7 --> node8 node8 --> node9 node4 --> node9 ``` --- ## 4.2 Product Mix Dimensions — Block Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PM["PRODUCT MIX PORTFOLIO"] nodeA["WIDTH
Number of Product Lines"] nodeB["LENGTH
Total Number of Items SKUs"] nodeC["DEPTH
Variations per Item"] nodeD["CONSISTENCY
Relatedness of Lines"] end nodeA --> nodeE["Example 3 lines
Soap Shampoo Deodorant"] nodeB --> nodeF["9 total items
3 3 2"] nodeC --> nodeG["Soap 4 sizes
Shampoo 2 bottles"] nodeD --> nodeH["High consistency
All personal care"] PM --> nodeI["STRATEGIC FIRM PORTFOLIO DECISION"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Product Life Cycle (PLC) Curve — Stage-by-Stage Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["STAGE 1 INTRODUCTION
Sales LOW
Profit NEGATIVE
Awareness BUILDING"] node2["STAGE 2 GROWTH
Sales RISING FAST
Profit INCREASING
Market Share CAPTURE"] node3["STAGE 3 MATURITY
Sales PEAK
Profit MAXIMUM
Competition INTENSE"] node4["STAGE 4 DECLINE
Sales FALLING
Profit SHRINKING
Harvest OR WITHDRAW"] node1 -->|Time| node2 node2 -->|Time| node3 node3 -->|Time| node4 node5["OBJECTIVE: Awareness"] --> node1 node6["OBJECTIVE: Share"] --> node2 node7["OBJECTIVE: Defend"] --> node3 node8["OBJECTIVE: Harvest"] --> node4 ``` --- ## 4.4 BCG Matrix Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BCG["BCG MATRIX STRATEGIC MAP"] nodeA["STARS
High MG AND High RMS
INVEST AGGRESSIVELY"] nodeB["QUESTION MARKS
High MG AND Low RMS
SELECTIVE INVEST OR DIVEST"] nodeC["CASH COWS
Low MG AND High RMS
HARVEST CASH FLOW"] nodeD["DOGS
Low MG AND Low RMS
DIVEST OR LIQUIDATE"] end nodeE["Y-AXIS: Market Growth Rate MG percent"] --> nodeA nodeE --> nodeB nodeF["X-AXIS: Relative Market Share RMS"] --> nodeA nodeF --> nodeC nodeG["Cash from CASH COWS funds STARS and QUESTION MARKS"] -.-> nodeA nodeG -.-> nodeB nodeH["DOGS drain resources; phase out"] -.-> nodeD ``` --- ## 4.5 New Product Development Stage-Gate Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR s1["Stage 1
IDEA GENERATION"] -->|Hundreds of ideas| s2["Stage 2
IDEA SCREENING"] s2 -->|Filtered pool| s3["Stage 3
CONCEPT TESTING"] s3 -->|Winning concept| s4["Stage 4
BUSINESS ANALYSIS"] s4 -->|Feasible| s5["Stage 5
PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT"] s5 -->|Prototype ready| s6["Stage 6
TEST MARKETING"] s6 -->|Validated| s7["Stage 7
COMMERCIALIZATION"] g1["Gate 1 Feasibility Check"] -.-> s2 g2["Gate 2 Concept Approval"] -.-> s3 g3["Gate 3 Financial Approval"] -.-> s4 g4["Gate 4 Technical Approval"] -.-> s5 g5["Gate 5 Market Test Results"] -.-> s6 g6["Gate 6 Launch Approval"] -.-> s7 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** ### Question 1: Define Product. Explain the three levels of a product with a suitable example. **Model Answer (3 Marks — Word Limit: 80-100 words):** A *product* is anything offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption that satisfies a customer's want or need. It includes physical goods, services, ideas, places, and organizations. According to **Philip Kotler's three-level model**, a product exists on three levels: 1. **Core Product** — the fundamental benefit the customer seeks (e.g., for a car: *transportation + status*). 2. **Actual Product** — the physical features, design, quality, brand, and packaging (e.g., *Maruti Swift VXi, red colour, 1197cc engine*). 3. **Augmented Product** — additional services that exceed expectations (e.g., *free roadside assistance, 5-year warranty, free first service*). Modern marketers compete at the augmented level through superior service. **Mark Distribution:** *Definition: 1 mark | Three levels: 1.5 marks | Example: 0.5 mark.* --- ### Question 2: What is the Product Life Cycle (PLC)? List its four stages. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** is a marketing concept that describes the **stages a product goes through from introduction to withdrawal** in the market. It reflects the dynamic behavior of sales and profit over time, influenced by competitive responses, customer adoption, and technology evolution. The **four stages** are: 1. **Introduction** — Low sales, negative profits, heavy promotion. 2. **Growth** — Rapid sales increase, rising profits, competitors enter. 3. **Maturity** — Sales peak, profits stabilize, intense competition. 4. **Decline** — Sales and profits fall; product may be withdrawn. The PLC concept helps marketers **formulate stage-specific strategies**. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > *Note: KTU ESE Part B offers **internal choice** between two questions on the same module. Below are TWO alternative 14-mark questions with sub-parts.* --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Sample]* **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the **Product Mix** concept. Discuss its **four dimensions** with suitable examples. **Model Answer (7 Marks):** The **Product Mix** (also called *Product Portfolio* or *Product Assortment*) is the complete set of all products and product lines that a company offers for sale. It is the strategic blueprint of what the firm sells to its target markets. A product mix is described using **four dimensions**: | Dimension | Definition | Example (Hindustan Unilever Ltd.) | |---|---|---| | **1. Width** | Number of distinct product lines | Personal Care, Home Care, Food, Beverages → **Width = 4** | | **2. Length** | Total number of items/SKUs | 4 lines × ~5 items avg = **~20 SKUs** | | **3. Depth** | Number of variants per product | Lux soap: 4 sizes, 3 fragrances = **12 variants** | | **4. Consistency** | Closeness of product lines in end-use, distribution, brand image | HUL lines are **highly consistent** — all in FMCG, sold through same retail channels, similar brand positioning | **Strategic Importance:** - **Width:** Increases market coverage and reduces risk. - **Length:** Allows segmentation across price, age, and geography. - **Depth:** Enables customized targeting (niche segments). - **Consistency:** Allows synergy in branding, distribution, and supply chain. A company may **increase width** for diversification (Tata — steel to salt to software), **increase length** for shelf coverage (Parle-G 5 pack sizes), or **increase depth** for niche targeting (Pantene shampoo: 6 variants for different hair types). **Mark Distribution:** *Definition: 1.5 | Four dimensions: 3 | Example: 1.5 | Strategic importance: 1* --- **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **BCG Matrix**. How is a product classified into its four quadrants? Discuss the strategic implications of each quadrant. **Model Answer (7 Marks):** The **BCG (Boston Consulting Group) Matrix**, developed by Bruce Henderson in 1970, is a strategic portfolio management tool that classifies a firm's products based on two dimensions: 1. **Market Growth Rate (MG%)** — Vertical axis (cut-off: typically **10%**) 2. **Relative Market Share (RMS)** — Horizontal axis (cut-off: **1.0**) **Classification Formulas:** $$ \text{RMS} = \frac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Largest Competitor Sales}} $$ $$ \text{MG\%} = \frac{\text{Current Market Size} - \text{Previous Market Size}}{\text{Previous Market Size}} \times 100 $$ **The Four Quadrants:** 1. **STARS (High MG, High RMS):** Market leaders in high-growth markets. **Strategy:** Invest aggressively to defend leadership and convert them into future Cash Cows. *(e.g., Apple iPhone in smartphones)* 2. **CASH COWS (Low MG, High RMS):** Established leaders in slow-growth markets. **Strategy:** Harvest cash flow with minimal investment; use cash to fund Stars and selective Question Marks. *(e.g., Maggi noodles in India — dominant brand)* 3. **QUESTION MARKS (High MG, Low RMS):** Weak products in attractive markets. **Strategy:** Either invest heavily to build share (convert to Stars) or divest. *(e.g., a new EV entrant competing with Tesla)* 4. **DOGS (Low MG, Low RMS):** Weak products in unattractive markets. **Strategy:** Harvest, divest, or liquidate. *(e.g., BlackBerry phones in 2024)* **Strategic Resource Flow:** Cash from Cows → funds Stars & selective Question Marks; Dogs drain resources and should be eliminated. **Mark Distribution:** *BCG concept: 1 | Formulae: 1.5 | Four quadrants: 3 | Strategies: 1.5* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** stages in detail. What marketing strategies are appropriate at each stage? **Model Answer (7 Marks):** The **Product Life Cycle** is a biological metaphor applied to products: just as living organisms are born, grow, mature, age, and die, products in the market follow a similar trajectory. It is one of the most powerful strategic tools in the marketing manager's arsenal. **Stage 1 — INTRODUCTION:** - Sales: Low | Profits: Negative | Customers: Innovators (2.5%) - Strategies: Heavy advertising to build awareness, introductory pricing (skimming or penetration), selective distribution, extensive R&D. **Stage 2 — GROWTH:** - Sales: Rapidly rising | Profits: Increasing | Customers: Early Adopters (13.5%) - Strategies: Product improvements, brand extensions, entry into new distribution channels, competitive pricing adjustments, aggressive promotion. **Stage 3 — MATURITY:** - Sales: Peak and plateau | Profits: Maximum then declining | Customers: Early + Late Majority (68%) - Strategies: Product diversification (size, flavor), market modification (new users, new uses), brand repositioning, defensive marketing. **Stage 4 — DECLINE:** - Sales: Falling | Profits: Shrinking | Customers: Laggards (16%) - Strategies: Harvest (reduce marketing spend), drop unprofitable product variants, or **withdraw** from the market. **Limitations of PLC:** - Not all products follow the S-curve (some have fad cycles, plateau cycles, or scalloped cycles). - Stage boundaries are hard to identify in practice. - Strategy is a *cause* of the curve shape, not just a *response* to it. **Mark Distribution:** *PLC concept: 1.5 | 4 stages with strategies: 4 | Limitations: 1.5* --- **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the **New Product Development Process**. Why do most new products fail? **Model Answer (7 Marks):** A **new product** is any product that is perceived by customers as significantly different from existing offerings. New products are the **lifeblood of corporate growth** — without them, a firm's portfolio ages and revenues decline. **The 7-Stage New Product Development Process:** 1. **Idea Generation** — Internal sources (R&D, employees), external sources (customers, competitors, distributors, online communities). 2. **Idea Screening** — Drop bad ideas early; evaluate on strategic fit, technical feasibility, market size. 3. **Concept Development & Testing** — Convert ideas into customer-tested product concepts; gauge purchase intent. 4. **Business Analysis** — Sales forecast, cost analysis, profit projection, break-even analysis. 5. **Product Development** — Engineering the prototype, designing brand name, packaging, and labeling. 6. **Test Marketing** — Controlled launch in a limited geographical area (e.g., one district or city). 7. **Commercialization** — Full-scale national/international launch with the complete marketing mix. **Why Most New Products Fail (KTU Hot Topic):** - **Customer Ignorance** — Company doesn't truly understand customer needs. - **Poor Market Research** — Inadequate or flawed concept testing. - **Technical Problems** — Product defects, performance issues. - **Undercapitalization** — Insufficient launch budget. - **Competitive Reactions** — Counter-launches by competitors (e.g., Pepsi's response to Thums Up). - **Timing Errors** — Launching too early (technology not ready) or too late (market saturated). - **Poor Marketing Mix** — Wrong pricing, weak promotion, poor distribution. > *Statistically, only **1 out of 7** new product ideas becomes a commercial success.* **Mark Distribution:** *Definition: 1 | 7-stage process: 3.5 | Reasons for failure: 2.5* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. ❌ **BCG Formula Mistake:** Writing *Absolute Market Share* (Company Sales / Total Market) instead of **Relative Market Share** (Company Sales / Largest Competitor Sales). **−2 marks.** > 2. ❌ **Three-Level Product Confusion:** Listing *Core, Actual, Augmented* but failing to give **an example** for each. Always give at least one coherent example (e.g., iPhone). > 3. ❌ **PLC Limitation Omission:** Writing only the stages but **not mentioning limitations** (fad cycle, scalloped cycle, strategy-is-cause-not-response). Examiner deducts **1-2 marks** for missing this. > 4. ❌ **Product Mix vs Product Line Confusion:** Conflating the two. *Product Line = group of similar items; Product Mix = sum total of all lines.* **−1 mark.** > 5. ❌ **Forgetting "Augmented" in the actual product section:** When asked to explain branding/packaging/labelling, students skip the link to the augmented level. Always connect every attribute to one of the three levels. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Product Strategy** - ✅ **Product** = Anything offered to a market to satisfy a need/want (broad: includes services, ideas, places). - ✅ **Three Levels** = Core → Actual → Augmented (memorize with an iPhone example). - ✅ **Product Mix Dimensions** = Width, Length, Depth, Consistency — always define all four in any 7+ mark answer. - ✅ **Product Line Decisions** = Stretching (up/down/two-way), Filling, Modernization, Pruning. - ✅ **PLC Stages** = Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline (know profit/sales/customers/strategy for each). - ✅ **PLC Limitations** = Fad cycle, plateau cycle, scalloped cycle, stage boundaries unclear. - ✅ **BCG Matrix** = Two axes: **MG%** (vertical, 10% cut-off) and **RMS** (horizontal, 1.0 cut-off). - ✅ **BCG Quadrants** = Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, Dogs — each with specific strategic action. - ✅ **RMS Formula** = Company Sales ÷ Largest Competitor Sales (NOT total market). - ✅ **New Product Development** = 7 stages from Idea Generation → Commercialization. - ✅ **Branding Strategies** = Line, Family, Individual, Co-branding — be ready to give HUL/P&G examples. - ✅ **Packaging Functions** = Protection, Promotion, Information. - ✅ **Services Characteristics** = Intangible, Inseparable, Variable, Perishable (KTU's "4 Is of Services"). - ✅ **Diffusion of Innovation** = Innovators 2.5% → Early Adopters 13.5% → Early Majority 34% → Late Majority 34% → Laggards 16%. - ✅ **For 14-mark answers**, structure as: *Definition (2 marks) + Framework (4 marks) + Application (4 marks) + Examples (2 marks) + Limitations (2 marks).*

topics.title: Services Marketing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Services Marketing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Services Marketing** is a specialized branch of marketing that focuses on the marketing of intangible products (services) rather than physical goods. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Marketing Management, services marketing is defined as *"the process of identifying, anticipating, and satisfying customer needs and wants for intangible offerings that are produced and consumed simultaneously, requiring a distinct marketing mix and strategic approach compared to physical goods."*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2)**
> Services Marketing is a high-weightage topic in Module 2. Students must master the 7 Ps framework, the Gap Model of Service Quality, SERVQUAL dimensions, and Service Blueprints. These concepts frequently appear in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

A **service** is any act or performance that one party can offer to another that is essentially intangible and does not result in the ownership of anything physical. Services may be tied to a physical good (like a car repair) or be purely intangible (like education or healthcare).

## 1.2 Key Categories of Services

Services can be broadly classified into the following categories:

| Category | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Consumer Services** | Services purchased by end-consumers | Restaurants, salons, fitness centers |
| **Business Services** | Services purchased by organizations | Consulting, IT services, logistics |
| **Professional Services** | Knowledge-based services | Law, accounting, medicine |
| **Public Services** | Government-provided services | Public transport, defense, education |
| **Industrial Services** | Services supporting manufacturing | Maintenance, repair, installation |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Experience**
> Imagine you walk into a restaurant. You don't take home a "physical product" per se. What you pay for is the *experience* — the taste of the food, the ambiance, the waiter's behavior, the cleanliness, and the time it took to be served. This is a service!
>
> Now compare this with buying a smartphone from a store. You walk out with a physical object you can touch, feel, and own. That is a **good**.
>
> The challenge in marketing a restaurant (service) is that you cannot show customers a "taste sample" before they buy, you cannot standardize every meal, you cannot separate the chef from the cooking, and you cannot store a cooked meal for tomorrow. These four unique challenges are captured in the famous **Four Characteristics of Services**: **IIVP** (Intangibility, Inseparability, Variability, Perishability).

## 1.4 Why Services Marketing is Different

Services marketing differs from product (goods) marketing because of the unique nature of services. Traditional marketing frameworks designed for tangible products (the 4 Ps) are insufficient. Therefore, services require an **expanded marketing mix of 7 Ps** and specialized models like the **Gap Model** and **SERVQUAL** to ensure service quality and customer satisfaction.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick for Service Characteristics (IIVP)**
> **I** — Intangibility (You can't touch it)
> **I** — Inseparability (Production & consumption happen together)
> **V** — Variability (Quality varies each time)
> **P** — Perishability (Can't be stored/inventoried)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Characteristics of Services (IIVP)

Services possess four distinctive characteristics that fundamentally differentiate them from physical goods. Understanding these is **critical** for KTU exams.

### 2.1.1 Intangibility

Services are intangible — they cannot be seen, tasted, felt, heard, or smelled before purchase. A customer cannot "test-drive" a haircut, a medical diagnosis, or a financial consultation before buying.

- **Implication:** Marketers must create tangible cues (physical evidence) like branding, logos, decor, and testimonials to reduce buyer uncertainty.
- **Example:** A hospital uses clean corridors, modern equipment, and uniformed staff to convey the intangible quality of healthcare.

### 2.1.2 Inseparability

Services are produced and consumed simultaneously. The service provider and the customer are often present together during the service delivery. Unlike goods (manufactured in a factory and consumed later), services are inseparable from their creation.

- **Implication:** Service quality depends heavily on the people involved. Training employees and managing customer interactions is crucial.
- **Example:** A classroom lecture — the teacher (producer) and the student (consumer) must be present together.

### 2.1.3 Variability (Heterogeneity)

Service quality is highly variable — it depends on who provides it, when it is provided, and where it is provided. Two haircuts from the same barber can differ; two surgeries from the same surgeon can have different outcomes.

- **Implication:** Standardization through training, protocols, and automation is essential. Customer expectations must be managed.
- **Example:** Two flights on the same airline can have very different service experiences based on the cabin crew.

### 2.1.4 Perishability

Services cannot be stored, inventoried, or saved for later use. An empty hotel room, an unsold airline seat, or an unfilled appointment slot is a lost revenue opportunity that can never be recovered.

- **Implication:** Demand forecasting, capacity management, and dynamic pricing strategies become critical.
- **Example:** A cinema hall has 200 seats for a 6 PM show. If only 50 people come, the remaining 150 seats are "perished" — they cannot be sold tomorrow.

## 2.2 Goods vs. Services — Comparative Analysis

| Dimension | Goods (Products) | Services |
|-----------|----------------|----------|
| **Nature** | Tangible, physical | Intangible |
| **Storage** | Can be stored/inventoried | Cannot be stored (perishable) |
| **Production** | Produced first, then sold | Produced and consumed simultaneously |
| **Standardization** | Highly standardized | Highly variable |
| **Ownership** | Transfer of ownership occurs | No transfer of ownership |
| **Evaluation** | Can be evaluated before purchase | Evaluated mainly after purchase |
| **Return** | Often returnable | Generally not returnable |
| **Customer Contact** | Low involvement | High involvement |
| **Quality Control** | Done at the factory | Done during delivery |

## 2.3 The 7 Ps of Services Marketing (Extended Marketing Mix)

Booms and Bitner (1981) extended the traditional 4 Ps of marketing into **7 Ps** specifically to address the unique nature of services. This is one of the **most frequently asked topics in KTU exams**.

| Element | Description | Service-Specific Application |
|---------|-------------|----------------------------|
| **Product** | The core service offering and its features | Core service + supplementary services (e.g., hotel room + breakfast + Wi-Fi) |
| **Price** | The amount charged; fees, rates, subscriptions | Dynamic pricing, yield management, perceived value pricing |
| **Place** | Distribution channels and service delivery locations | Online platforms, physical branches, mobile units |
| **Promotion** | Communication to inform and persuade | Advertising, personal selling, word-of-mouth, digital marketing |
| **People** | All human actors in service delivery | Employees, customers themselves, training, motivation |
| **Process** | The procedures and flow of activities | Service blueprint, waiting lines, queuing management |
| **Physical Evidence** | Tangible cues that support the service | Buildings, equipment, logos, uniforms, brochures, website |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point**
> The 7 Ps were added by **Booms and Bitner (1981)** to the original 4 Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) proposed by **E. Jerome McCarthy (1960)**. The 3 additional Ps — **People, Process, Physical Evidence** — are unique to services.

## 2.4 The Gap Model of Service Quality (5 Gaps)

The **Gap Model**, developed by **Zeithaml, Parasuraman, and Berry (1990)**, is the foundational framework for understanding service quality gaps. It identifies **5 gaps** between various aspects of service design, delivery, and customer expectations.

### The Five Gaps Explained

**Gap 1: Knowledge Gap**
- The difference between customer expectations and management's understanding of those expectations.
- **Cause:** Lack of market research, poor communication with customers, inadequate upward communication.
- **Solution:** Conduct regular customer research, use feedback systems, employ relationship managers.

**Gap 2: Standards Gap**
- The difference between management's understanding of expectations and the service quality standards set.
- **Cause:** Poor service design, lack of commitment to quality, inadequate task analysis.
- **Solution:** Set clear service standards, use service blueprints, define quality specifications.

**Gap 3: Delivery Gap**
- The difference between established service standards and actual service performance.
- **Cause:** Employee performance variability, lack of training, role ambiguity, poor teamwork.
- **Solution:** Train employees, use technology, empower staff, monitor performance.

**Gap 4: Communication Gap**
- The difference between actual service delivery and external communications/promises.
- **Cause:** Over-promising in advertising, lack of coordination between marketing and operations.
- **Solution:** Manage customer expectations honestly, align marketing with operations.

**Gap 5: Customer Gap (The Critical Gap)**
- The difference between customer expectations and their perception of the service received.
- This is the **sum/result** of Gaps 1 through 4.
- **Formula:** Gap 5 = f(Gap 1, Gap 2, Gap 3, Gap 4)
- **Solution:** Address all upstream gaps systematically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Gap 5 is the most important gap.** It represents the customer's perceived service quality. If Gap 5 is closed, service quality is achieved. If any of Gaps 1–4 exist, Gap 5 will also exist.

## 2.5 SERVQUAL Model (Service Quality Dimensions)

**SERVQUAL** (Service Quality) is a 22-item instrument developed by **Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (1988)** to measure service quality across **5 dimensions**. The core idea is:

$$\text{Service Quality (SQ)} = \text{Perception (P)} - \text{Expectation (E)}$$

### The Five SERVQUAL Dimensions

| Dimension | Definition | Example |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| **Tangibles** | Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance | Clean hotel lobby, modern equipment, uniformed staff |
| **Reliability** | Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately | Delivering the service right the first time, on time |
| **Responsiveness** | Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service | Quick check-in, fast food delivery, prompt replies |
| **Assurance** | Knowledge and courtesy of employees, and their ability to inspire trust and confidence | Competent doctors, certified financial advisors |
| **Empathy** | Caring, individualized attention provided to customers | Personalized service, remembering customer preferences |

### SERVQUAL — Mathematical Representation

For each dimension $i$ and each statement $j$:

$$SQ_{ij} = P_{ij} - E_{ij}$$

The overall service quality score is calculated as:

$$SQ_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (P_{ij} - E_{ij})$$

Where:
- $SQ_{ij}$ = Service quality for dimension $i$, statement $j$
- $P_{ij}$ = Customer's perception of actual performance
- $E_{ij}$ = Customer's expectation before service
- $n$ = Number of statements per dimension (typically 4–5)

**Interpretation:**
- If $SQ > 0$: Service exceeds expectations (high quality)
- If $SQ = 0$: Service meets expectations (satisfactory)
- If $SQ < 0$: Service falls short of expectations (poor quality)

## 2.6 SERVPERF (Performance-Only Model)

**Cronin and Taylor (1992)** argued that the SERVQUAL expectations component is problematic and proposed a simpler **performance-only** model:

$$SQ = \text{Performance Only}$$

SERVPERF measures only the customer's perception of actual service performance, without comparing it to expectations. It is operationally simpler and often has better predictive validity.

## 2.7 Service Blueprint

A **Service Blueprint** is a detailed visual map of the service process showing every step from the customer's perspective and the organization's perspective. It typically contains:

| Blueprint Element | Description |
|------------------|-------------|
| **Customer Actions** | Steps the customer takes |
| **Frontstage Actions** | Visible employee actions the customer sees |
| **Backstage Actions** | Invisible employee actions behind the scenes |
| **Support Processes** | Internal processes supporting service delivery |
| **Line of Interaction** | Boundary between customer and organization |
| **Line of Visibility** | Separates frontstage from backstage |
| **Line of Internal Interaction** | Separates employees from support systems |

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Framework | Application |
|---------|---------------------|-------------|
| Service Quality | $SQ = P - E$ | SERVQUAL scoring |
| Customer Gap | $\text{Gap 5} = f(\text{Gap 1}, \text{Gap 2}, \text{Gap 3}, \text{Gap 4})$ | Root cause of service failures |
| Marketing Mix | $4P + 3P' = 7P$ | Product, Price, Place, Promotion + People, Process, Physical Evidence |
| Service Characteristics | IIVP | Intangibility, Inseparability, Variability, Perishability |
| Capacity Utilization | $\text{CU} = \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Maximum Capacity}} \times 100\%$ | Managing perishability |
| Yield Management | $\text{Yield} = \frac{\text{Revenue Earned}}{\text{Revenue Capacity}} \times 100\%$ | Airlines, hotels |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> For engineering graduates, services marketing is relevant when:
> - Marketing IT/consulting services
> - Managing customer-facing technical support
> - Working in B2B service industries (logistics, maintenance)
> - Designing user experiences (UX) for service platforms
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Detailed Analytical Walkthrough: SERVQUAL Scoring

This is a **frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU exams. Below is a complete worked example showing how to apply the SERVQUAL model step-by-step.

### Worked Example 1: Restaurant Service Quality Assessment

**Problem Statement:**
A restaurant chain wants to measure service quality using SERVQUAL. For the "Responsiveness" dimension, customers rated 4 statements on a 7-point Likert scale (1 = strongly disagree, 7 = strongly agree). The following data was collected from 10 customers:

| Statement | Average Expectation (E) | Average Perception (P) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Staff tells when service will be provided | 6.5 | 5.0 |
| Staff gives prompt service | 6.8 | 4.5 |
| Staff is always willing to help | 6.2 | 5.5 |
| Staff is never too busy to respond | 6.0 | 3.0 |

**Step 1: State the SERVQUAL Formula**

$$SQ = P - E$$

**Step 2: Calculate Service Quality for Each Statement**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SQ_1 &= P_1 - E_1 = 5.0 - 6.5 = -1.5 \\
SQ_2 &= P_2 - E_2 = 4.5 - 6.8 = -2.3 \\
SQ_3 &= P_3 - E_3 = 5.5 - 6.2 = -0.7 \\
SQ_4 &= P_4 - E_4 = 3.0 - 6.0 = -3.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sum Up the Dimension Score**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SQ_{\text{Responsiveness}} &= SQ_1 + SQ_2 + SQ_3 + SQ_4 \\
&= (-1.5) + (-2.3) + (-0.7) + (-3.0) \\
&= -7.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret the Result**

- $SQ_{\text{Responsiveness}} = -7.5$ (negative)
- This means customer perceptions **fall short** of expectations.
- The restaurant is underperforming in responsiveness.
- The worst gap is $SQ_4$ ($-3.0$): "Staff is never too busy to respond" — this is the **most critical area** to fix.

**Step 5: Managerial Recommendations**

1. **Staff Training:** Train employees on time management and prompt customer response.
2. **Workforce Planning:** Hire more staff during peak hours to address "being too busy."
3. **Set Service Standards:** Establish maximum response time targets (e.g., acknowledge customer within 2 minutes).
4. **Empower Employees:** Allow front-line staff to resolve issues without escalation delays.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Goods vs. Services Decision Framework

When a company offers both goods and services, it must decide on its service strategy. Below is the **Goods-Services Continuum** with worked analysis:

| Position | Continuum | Example | Marketing Emphasis |
|----------|-----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Pure Tangible Good | Salt, detergent | Product, packaging |
| 2 | Tangible Good + Service | Car, computer | Core product + after-sales service |
| 3 | Hybrid Offering | Restaurant meal | Food + service experience |
| 4 | Major Service + Minor Goods | Airlines | Flight service + meal |
| 5 | Pure Service | Teaching, consulting | Service quality, people, process |

### Analytical Insight for Engineers

> An engineering firm (e.g., a construction company) sits at **Position 2–3**: it delivers a tangible product (a building) embedded with extensive services (design, consulting, maintenance). The marketing mix must therefore emphasize **all 7 Ps**, not just the 4 Ps of product marketing.

## 3.3 Gap Analysis — Step-by-Step Application

### Worked Example 2: Hospital Service Quality Gaps

**Scenario:** A hospital has identified the following issues:
- **Gap 1 (Knowledge Gap):** Management believes patients want faster discharge, but surveys show patients want more consultation time.
- **Gap 2 (Standards Gap):** Management understands the issue but has not set a standard consultation time.
- **Gap 3 (Delivery Gap):** Doctors still rush through consultations.
- **Gap 4 (Communication Gap):** The hospital's website advertises "unlimited consultation time" but this is not delivered.

**Analytical Steps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gap 1} &= \text{Expectations} - \text{Management Perception} \neq 0 \\
\text{Gap 2} &= \text{Management Perception} - \text{Service Standards} \neq 0 \\
\text{Gap 3} &= \text{Service Standards} - \text{Service Delivery} \neq 0 \\
\text{Gap 4} &= \text{Service Delivery} - \text{External Communication} \neq 0 \\
\text{Gap 5} &= \text{Expectations} - \text{Perceptions} \neq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cumulative Effect:**

$$\text{Gap 5} = \text{Gap 1} + \text{Gap 2} + \text{Gap 3} + \text{Gap 4}$$

**Recommendations:**
1. Close Gap 1 by conducting quarterly patient satisfaction surveys.
2. Close Gap 2 by setting a standard consultation time of 15 minutes.
3. Close Gap 3 by training doctors and using time-management protocols.
4. Close Gap 4 by updating the website to match actual service delivery.

## 3.4 Capacity Utilization Calculation for Perishable Services

### Worked Example 3: Hotel Capacity Management

**Problem:**
A hotel has 200 rooms. On a given night, 150 rooms are occupied. What is the capacity utilization, and what is the lost revenue due to perishability?

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Capacity Utilization (CU)} &= \frac{\text{Actual Occupancy}}{\text{Maximum Capacity}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{150}{200} \times 100\% \\
&= 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lost Revenue} &= (\text{Max Capacity} - \text{Actual Occupancy}) \times \text{Room Rate} \\
&= (200 - 150) \times \text{Rate}
\end{aligned}
$$

If room rate = ₹3,000/night:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lost Revenue} &= 50 \times 3{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}50{,}000 \text{ per night}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Insight:** This ₹1,50,000 is permanently lost — it cannot be recovered tomorrow. The hotel must use **dynamic pricing** or **overbooking strategies** to minimize such losses.

## 3.5 Comparative Scoring Matrix (Model for 14-Mark Answers)

When answering a 14-mark question, students should structure their answer in this matrix format:

| Component | Weight | Key Points to Cover |
|-----------|--------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | 2 marks | Clear, formal definition of the concept |
| **Characteristics/Components** | 4 marks | List and explain all elements (e.g., 4 IIVP, 5 Gaps, 5 SERVQUAL dimensions) |
| **Diagram/Framework** | 3 marks | Include a visual model (Gap model, 7 Ps wheel, Service Blueprint) |
| **Real-world Example** | 3 marks | Apply to a specific industry (hospitality, banking, education) |
| **Application/Implications** | 2 marks | Managerial recommendations or strategic insights |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Gap Model of Service Quality (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the 5 gaps interact to produce the final customer-perceived service quality. This is rendered as a **block-level functional architecture flow** to maintain readability and comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Customer Expectations] --> nodeB{Gap 1: Knowledge Gap}
    nodeB --> nodeC[Management Perception]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Gap 2: Standards Gap}
    nodeD --> nodeE[Service Quality Standards]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Gap 3: Delivery Gap}
    nodeF --> nodeG[Actual Service Delivery]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Gap 4: Communication Gap}
    nodeH --> nodeI[External Communications]
    nodeA --> nodeJ{Gap 5: Customer Gap}
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Customer Perceived Service Quality]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Service Quality Outcome: Positive, Neutral, or Negative]
```

**How to Read This Diagram:**
- **Top Path (Gaps 1–4):** Internal organizational gaps that lead to service delivery.
- **Bottom Path (Gap 5):** The final gap between customer expectations and perceptions.
- **Right Node:** The outcome — whether the service meets, exceeds, or falls short of customer expectations.

## 4.2 The 7 Ps of Services Marketing — Strategic Framework Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeCenter[Services Marketing Mix: 7 Ps]
    nodeP1[P1: Product] --> nodeCenter
    nodeP2[P2: Price] --> nodeCenter
    nodeP3[P3: Place] --> nodeCenter
    nodeP4[P4: Promotion] --> nodeCenter
    nodeP5[P5: People] --> nodeCenter
    nodeP6[P6: Process] --> nodeCenter
    nodeP7[P7: Physical Evidence] --> nodeCenter
    nodeCenter --> nodeOut[Service Excellence and Customer Satisfaction]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7
    nodeP7 --> nodeOut
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The 7 Ps are **interconnected**, not isolated.
- **People** drive the **Process**, which is supported by **Physical Evidence**.
- All 7 Ps together contribute to **Service Excellence**.

## 4.3 Service Blueprint — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeSB[Service Blueprint: Coffee Shop]
    nodeCA[Customer Action: Order Coffee] --> nodeLS1[Line of Interaction]
    nodeLS1 --> nodeFA[Frontstage: Barista Takes Order]
    nodeFA --> nodeLS2[Line of Visibility]
    nodeLS2 --> nodeBA[Backstage: Brewing Process]
    nodeBA --> nodeLS3[Line of Internal Interaction]
    nodeLS3 --> nodeSP[Support: Inventory and Maintenance]
    nodeSP --> nodeBA
    nodeFA --> nodeCustServ[Customer Receives Coffee]
    nodeCustServ --> nodeEnd[Service Completed]
```

**Blueprint Elements Explained:**

| Element | Mermaid Node | Description |
|---------|--------------|-------------|
| Line of Interaction | nodeLS1 | Boundary between customer and service provider |
| Line of Visibility | nodeLS2 | Separates what the customer sees from what they don't |
| Line of Internal Interaction | nodeLS3 | Separates service employees from support systems |

## 4.4 Services Characteristics (IIVP) — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeS[Services: Unique Characteristics]
    nodeI1[Intangibility] --> nodeS
    nodeI2[Inseparability] --> nodeS
    nodeV[Variability] --> nodeS
    nodeP[Perishability] --> nodeS
    nodeI1 --> nodeM1[Marketing Tactic: Use Tangible Cues, Branding]
    nodeI2 --> nodeM2[Marketing Tactic: Train Service Staff]
    nodeV --> nodeM3[Marketing Tactic: Standardize Processes]
    nodeP --> nodeM4[Marketing Tactic: Dynamic Pricing, Demand Management]
```

## 4.5 SERVQUAL Dimensions — Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeSQ[Service Quality SQ = P minus E]
    nodeT[Tangibles] --> nodeSQ
    nodeR[Reliability] --> nodeSQ
    nodeRE[Responsiveness] --> nodeSQ
    nodeA[Assurance] --> nodeSQ
    nodeE[Empathy] --> nodeSQ
    nodeT --> nodeEx1[Example: Clean Premises, Uniforms]
    nodeR --> nodeEx2[Example: On Time Delivery]
    nodeRE --> nodeEx3[Example: Quick Response]
    nodeA --> nodeEx4[Example: Trained Staff, Trust]
    nodeE --> nodeEx5[Example: Personalized Service]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Services Marketing. List the four characteristics of services.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Services Marketing is a branch of marketing that deals with the marketing of intangible offerings (services) rather than physical products. It involves identifying, anticipating, and satisfying customer needs for acts, performances, or experiences that are essentially intangible and do not result in the ownership of anything physical.

**Four Characteristics (1.5 marks):** Services have four unique characteristics, collectively remembered as **IIVP**:
1. **Intangibility** — Services cannot be seen, touched, or felt before purchase.
2. **Inseparability** — Services are produced and consumed simultaneously.
3. **Variability (Heterogeneity)** — Service quality varies from provider to provider and time to time.
4. **Perishability** — Services cannot be stored or inventoried for future use.

---

### Question 2: What is SERVQUAL? Mention its five dimensions.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
SERVQUAL is a service quality measurement model developed by Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry in 1988. It measures service quality as the difference between customer perceptions (P) and expectations (E) of service performance. The model uses 22 statements across 5 dimensions to assess service quality.

**Five Dimensions (1.5 marks):**
1. **Tangibles** — Physical facilities, equipment, and personnel appearance
2. **Reliability** — Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately
3. **Responsiveness** — Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service
4. **Assurance** — Knowledge and courtesy of employees; ability to inspire trust
5. **Empathy** — Caring, individualized attention to customers

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make in services marketing questions:
> 1. **Writing only the names** of characteristics, dimensions, or gaps without explaining them — this attracts only 40% marks.
> 2. **Confusing SERVQUAL and SERVPERF** — SERVQUAL measures (P − E), SERVPERF measures only P.
> 3. **Forgetting the 3 additional Ps** (People, Process, Physical Evidence) when asked about services marketing mix.
> 4. **Not providing real-world examples** — a 14-mark question without an example typically loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Skipping diagrams** — always include the Gap Model diagram or 7 Ps framework for full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Explain the 7 Ps of Services Marketing in detail. How is services marketing different from goods marketing?

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Introduction to Services Marketing (3 marks)**

Services marketing is a specialized discipline that focuses on marketing intangible offerings. Unlike physical goods, services are characterized by four unique features: **Intangibility, Inseparability, Variability, and Perishability (IIVP)**. These characteristics make traditional 4 Ps marketing insufficient, necessitating an extended 7 Ps framework.

**(b) The 7 Ps of Services Marketing (7 marks)**

Booms and Bitner (1981) extended McCarthy's 4 Ps framework to address service-specific challenges:

1. **Product (Service Offering):** The core service is the primary offering (e.g., a hotel room, a flight, a doctor's consultation). Supplementary services (breakfast, Wi-Fi, airport pickup) enhance the core offering. Marketers must define the core service, the augmented service, and the service package.

2. **Price:** Services use varied pricing strategies — hourly rates (consultants), subscription (Netflix), dynamic pricing (airlines), yield management, and perceived-value pricing. Pricing must reflect value, demand, and competitive positioning.

3. **Place (Distribution & Delivery):** Service delivery can occur through physical locations (banks, hospitals), online platforms (e-commerce, telemedicine), or mobile units. The location and accessibility directly impact customer convenience.

4. **Promotion:** Services rely heavily on word-of-mouth, personal selling, and digital marketing. Since services are intangible, promotion must focus on creating trust, demonstrating expertise, and managing expectations.

5. **People:** Employees are the service. They directly influence perceived quality. Service firms must invest in recruitment, training, motivation, and retention. Even the customer (co-producer) is part of the people element.

6. **Process:** The procedure, mechanism, and flow of activities by which a service is delivered. Well-designed processes reduce waiting time, minimize errors, and ensure consistency. Service blueprints are used to map processes.

7. **Physical Evidence:** Tangible cues that make the intangible service more concrete — building design, equipment, uniforms, brochures, website design, and ambient conditions (lighting, music, temperature).

**(c) Goods vs. Services Marketing (4 marks)**

| Aspect | Goods Marketing | Services Marketing |
|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| Nature | Tangible | Intangible |
| Storage | Can be inventoried | Cannot be stored |
| Marketing Mix | 4 Ps sufficient | 7 Ps required |
| Quality Control | Done at production | Done during delivery |
| Customer Involvement | Low | High |
| Ownership | Transferred | Not transferred |
| Return | Possible | Generally not possible |

**Conclusion:** Services marketing requires a fundamentally different approach due to the unique nature of services. The 7 Ps framework provides a comprehensive strategy to address service-specific challenges and deliver customer satisfaction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Explain the Gap Model of Service Quality with a neat diagram. What are the strategies to close these gaps?

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Introduction to the Gap Model (3 marks)**

The Gap Model of Service Quality, developed by Zeithaml, Parasuraman, and Berry (1990), is a comprehensive framework for analyzing and improving service quality. It identifies **5 gaps** in the service design and delivery process that collectively determine the overall service quality perceived by customers.

**(b) Detailed Explanation of the Five Gaps (7 marks)**

**Gap 1: Knowledge Gap** — The difference between customer expectations and management's perception of those expectations.
- **Causes:** Inadequate market research, poor communication between front-line staff and management, lack of customer feedback mechanisms.
- **Solutions:** Conduct regular customer surveys, establish customer relationship management (CRM) systems, train managers to interact with front-line employees.

**Gap 2: Standards Gap** — The difference between management's perception of customer expectations and the service quality standards established.
- **Causes:** Poor service design, lack of management commitment to quality, inadequate resource allocation.
- **Solutions:** Set SMART service standards, use service blueprints, develop clear standard operating procedures (SOPs).

**Gap 3: Delivery Gap** — The difference between service standards and actual service performance.
- **Causes:** Employee performance variability, lack of training, role ambiguity, poor teamwork, inadequate technology.
- **Solutions:** Invest in employee training, empower employees, use technology for consistency, monitor performance through mystery shopping.

**Gap 4: Communication Gap** — The difference between actual service delivery and external communications.
- **Causes:** Over-promising in advertising, lack of coordination between marketing and operations departments.
- **Solutions:** Align marketing communications with operational capability, set realistic expectations, train marketing teams on service delivery realities.

**Gap 5: Customer Gap (The Critical Gap)** — The difference between customer expectations and their perception of the service received.
- This is the **result** of Gaps 1 through 4. If any upstream gap exists, Gap 5 will also exist.
- **Formula:** Gap 5 = f(Gap 1, Gap 2, Gap 3, Gap 4)
- **Solutions:** Address all upstream gaps systematically; use SERVQUAL to measure and monitor Gap 5.

**(c) Diagram of the Gap Model (2 marks)**

```
[Customer Expectations] → Gap 1 → [Management Perception] → Gap 2 → [Service Standards]
        ↓ Gap 5 ↓
[Customer Perceptions] ← Gap 4 ← [Service Delivery] ← Gap 3 ← [Service Standards]
[External Communications]
```

**(d) Conclusion (2 marks)**

The Gap Model is a powerful diagnostic tool. By systematically closing Gaps 1 through 4, organizations can close the critical Gap 5 and deliver high service quality. This requires a holistic approach combining customer research, service design, employee training, and honest communication.

---

## 5.3 Additional Practice Questions (Self-Assessment)

| Q. No | Question | Marks | Type |
|-------|----------|-------|------|
| 1 | Differentiate between goods and services. Give any 5 differences. | 5 | Short |
| 2 | Explain any 3 characteristics of services with examples. | 6 | Medium |
| 3 | What is a Service Blueprint? Explain its components. | 7 | Medium |
| 4 | Explain the SERVPERF model. How is it different from SERVQUAL? | 10 | Long |
| 5 | Discuss the role of 'People' and 'Process' in services marketing. | 10 | Long |
| 6 | Explain how perishability of services creates marketing challenges. Suggest strategies to overcome them. | 12 | Long |
| 7 | "Service quality = Perception − Expectation." Discuss with reference to the SERVQUAL model. | 14 | Long |

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap: Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary**

**A. Core Definitions (Memorize Exact Wordings)**
- [ ] Service: Intangible act or performance, no transfer of ownership
- [ ] Services Marketing: Marketing of intangible offerings with 7 Ps
- [ ] SERVQUAL: P − E model by Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry (1988)
- [ ] Gap Model: 5 Gaps by Zeithaml, Parasuraman & Berry (1990)
- [ ] SERVPERF: Performance-only model by Cronin & Taylor (1992)
- [ ] 7 Ps: Extended by Booms & Bitner (1981)

**B. Four Characteristics of Services (IIVP)**
- [ ] **I** — Intangibility
- [ ] **I** — Inseparability
- [ ] **V** — Variability
- [ ] **P** — Perishability

**C. Five SERVQUAL Dimensions (TRRAE)**
- [ ] **T** — Tangibles
- [ ] **R** — Reliability
- [ ] **R** — Responsiveness
- [ ] **A** — Assurance
- [ ] **E** — Empathy

**D. Five Gaps in the Gap Model**
- [ ] Gap 1: Knowledge Gap
- [ ] Gap 2: Standards Gap
- [ ] Gap 3: Delivery Gap
- [ ] Gap 4: Communication Gap
- [ ] Gap 5: Customer Gap (the critical one)

**E. Seven Ps of Services Marketing**
- [ ] Product, Price, Place, Promotion (Original 4 Ps)
- [ ] People, Process, Physical Evidence (Service-specific 3 Ps)

**F. Key Formulas**
- [ ] $SQ = P - E$
- [ ] $\text{Gap 5} = f(\text{Gap 1}, \text{Gap 2}, \text{Gap 3}, \text{Gap 4})$
- [ ] $\text{CU} = \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Max Capacity}} \times 100\%$

**G. Strategic Insights to Mention in Answers**
- [ ] Always give **real-world examples** (hospitals, banks, airlines, hotels)
- [ ] Always include a **diagram** for 14-mark questions
- [ ] Always **close the answer with managerial recommendations**
- [ ] Remember that **Gap 5 is the sum result** of Gaps 1–4

**H. Common Pitfalls to Avoid**
- [ ] Do not confuse SERVQUAL (P−E) with SERVPERF (P only)
- [ ] Do not forget the 3 service-specific Ps (People, Process, Physical Evidence)
- [ ] Do not list characteristics without explaining them
- [ ] Do not write generic answers — tailor to the specific service industry asked

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product and Brand Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Product and Brand Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Product — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Product (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **product** is anything that can be offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption that might satisfy a want or need. It includes physical objects, services, persons, places, organizations, and ideas. (Source: Philip Kotler's Marketing Management framework, adopted in KTU 2024 Marketing Management syllabus.)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, products are studied as the **core component of the Marketing Mix (4Ps — Product, Price, Place, Promotion)**. The *Product* is the only "P" that generates revenue; the other three represent costs.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "What is a Product?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The 'Restaurant Meal'**
> Imagine you order a *Paneer Butter Masala with Butter Naan* at a restaurant. What you actually pay for is **NOT just the food on the plate**. You are paying for:
> - The **taste** (core benefit — satisfying hunger & craving),
> - The **presentation** (actual product — the cooked dish),
> - The **ceramic bowl, ambiance, aroma, waiter service** (augmented product — extras).
>
> Together, this entire **bundle of satisfactions** is the *Product*. Marketing managers don't sell food; they sell **experiences and solutions**.

This analogy is essential for understanding the **Three Levels of a Product** and **Product Layers**.

---

## 1.3 Three Levels of a Product (Core, Actual, Augmented)

The KTU 2024 module explicitly tests the **Three Levels Model** by Theodore Levitt.

| Level | Definition | Restaurant Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Product** | The fundamental benefit or value the customer is really buying. | Hunger satisfaction + pleasure of eating. |
| **Actual Product** | The tangible features, design, brand name, quality, packaging. | The cooked dish, plate, portion size, garnish. |
| **Augmented Product** | Additional services and benefits beyond expectations. | Free delivery, complimentary dessert, Wi-Fi. |

---

## 1.4 Brand — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brand (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **brand** is a *name, term, design, symbol, or any other feature* that identifies one seller's good or service as distinct from those of other sellers. (American Marketing Association, adopted by KTU.)

A **brand** is essentially a **promise** — it tells the customer what to expect from the product. The legal mechanism behind a brand is the **Trademark**.

---

## 1.5 Intuitive Analogy — "What is a Brand?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The 'Personality' of a Product**
> Think of two people wearing identical black suits. Both look the same superficially. But one speaks softly, is punctual, polite, and reliable; the other is loud, unreliable, and rude. We say they have *different personalities*.
>
> Similarly, two soaps may have the same chemical formula, but **Lux** and **Dove** have entirely different *brand personalities*. The differentiation lies not in chemistry but in **perception, identity, and trust**. That perception IS the brand.

---

## 1.6 Brand Equity — The Most Tested Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brand Equity:**
> **Brand Equity** is the *added value* endowed to products and services with a recognized brand name. It is the differential effect of brand knowledge on consumer response to marketing efforts. (Keller's Brand Equity Model — KTU 2024 prescribed reference.)

In simple words: **Brand Equity = Premium Price Power + Customer Loyalty + Brand Recognition**.

A high-equity brand (Apple, Nike, Amul) can charge **20-40% premium** over generic alternatives and still outsell them.

---

## 1.7 Key Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 Module 2)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Sub-Topics as per KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> 1. Product — Definition, levels, classifications (consumer/industrial, convenience/shopping/specialty/unsought).
> 2. Product Mix — Width, Length, Depth, Consistency.
> 3. Product Life Cycle (PLC) — Stages: Introduction, Growth, Maturity, Decline.
> 4. New Product Development (NPD) — 8-step process.
> 5. Brand — Definition, functions, advantages to consumers & marketers.
> 6. Brand Equity — Definition, components (brand awareness, brand associations, perceived quality, brand loyalty).
> 7. Brand Positioning — Strategies, value proposition.
> 8. Brand Strategies — Brand extension, line extension, multi-brand, co-branding, private label.
> 9. Packaging — Functions, levels, criticism.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Product Classification Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

Products are classified on **two dimensions**: *Durability/Tangibility* and *Consumer/Industrial Use*.

### 2.1.1 Based on Durability & Tangibility

| Class | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Durable Goods** | Tangible products that last long (≥3 years). | Refrigerator, Car, Washing Machine |
| **Non-Durable Goods** | Tangible products consumed quickly. | Soap, Toothpaste, Biscuits |
| **Services** | Intangible, inseparable, variable, perishable. | Banking, Salon, Education |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Services have 4 distinguishing characteristics (KTU 2024 Must-Know):**
> 1. **Intangibility** — cannot be touched/seen.
> 2. **Inseparability** — produced & consumed simultaneously.
> 3. **Variability** — quality varies by provider.
> 4. **Perishability** — cannot be stored (empty airline seat is lost forever).

### 2.1.2 Based on Consumer Buying Behavior (Most Tested!)

| Type | Buying Effort | Price | Availability | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Convenience Goods** | Minimal, habitual | Low | Wide | Salt, Newspapers, Milk |
| **Shopping Goods** | Comparison-based | Medium-High | Selective | Clothes, Mobile Phones |
| **Specialty Goods** | Strong brand preference, search effort | High | Exclusive | Rolex, Rolls Royce |
| **Unsought Goods** | No prior desire | Variable | Limited | Insurance, Fire Extinguisher |
| **Emergency Goods** | Urgent need | Variable | Convenient | Ambulance, Doctor |

### 2.1.3 Industrial Products Classification

| Type | Use | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Raw Materials** | Further processing | Cotton, Iron Ore |
| **Component Materials** | Fabricated into final product | Steel sheets |
| **Capital Items** | Long-term production aid | Machinery, Buildings |
| **Supplies & Services** | Daily operations | Lubricants, Cleaning |

---

## 2.2 Product Mix Dimensions (The 4 Pillars)

The **Product Mix** (also called *Product Assortment*) is the set of all products a company offers. KTU 2024 tests **four dimensions**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Width} &= \text{Number of different product lines the company offers.} \\
\textbf{Length} &= \text{Total number of products/items across all product lines.} \\
\textbf{Depth} &= \text{Average number of variants/SKUs within each product line.} \\
