\textbf{Consistency} &= \text{How closely related the product lines are in end-use, production, distribution.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (Board Exam Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A company (e.g., ITC) has 4 product lines — Cigarettes, FMCG Foods, Stationery, Hotels. Each line has on average 8 variants.
> - Width = **4 product lines**
> - Length = 4 × 8 = **32 products**
> - Depth = **8 variants per line** (average)
> - Consistency = **Low** (tobacco is unrelated to stationery and hotels)

---

## 2.3 Product Life Cycle (PLC) — The Core Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PLC Definition (KTU 2024):**
> The Product Life Cycle is a **marketing model** that describes the stages a product goes through from introduction to withdrawal from the market. Sales and profits follow a predictable **S-curve** (or bell-shape for profits).

### 2.3.1 Four Stages — Detailed Analysis

#### Stage 1: Introduction
- **Sales:** Very low, slow growth.
- **Costs:** Highest (production, distribution, promotion heavy).
- **Customers:** Innovators (~2.5%).
- **Profit:** **Negative** (loss-making).
- **Marketing Goal:** Create product awareness, build distribution.
- **Pricing:** Skimming or Penetration.

#### Stage 2: Growth
- **Sales:** Rapid increase (exponential rise).
- **Costs:** Per-unit cost falls due to economies of scale.
- **Customers:** Early adopters + early majority (~34%).
- **Profit:** **Peaks** and rises sharply.
- **Competitors:** Enter the market aggressively.
- **Marketing Goal:** Maximize market share, differentiate.

#### Stage 3: Maturity
- **Sales:** Growth slows, peaks, then plateaus.
- **Customers:** Late majority (~34%).
- **Profit:** Stable but starts declining due to competition.
- **Three sub-phases:** Growth Maturity → Stable Maturity → Decaying Maturity.
- **Marketing Goal:** Defend market share, product modifications, find new uses.

#### Stage 4: Decline
- **Sales:** Sharp drop.
- **Causes:** Technological change, substitutes, shifting consumer tastes.
- **Profit:** Declines sharply.
- **Marketing Strategies:** Harvest, Maintain, Contraction, Divestment.

### 2.3.2 PLC Marketing Strategy Matrix (High-Yield Table)

| Stage | Sales | Price | Promotion | Distribution | Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Introduction** | Low | High (skim) or Low (penetrate) | Heavy awareness-building | Selective/limited | Basic |
| **Growth** | Rising | Approaching market level | Build brand preference | Intensive | Improved |
| **Maturity** | Peak | Match competition | Encourage switching | Most intensive | Differentiated |
| **Decline** | Falling | Lowest | Minimal | Phased withdrawal | Phase out weak items |

---

## 2.4 New Product Development (NPD) — 8-Step Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandated NPD Process (Booms & Bitner adapted from Kotler):**

1. **Idea Generation** — Internal R&D, customer feedback, competitors, distributors.
2. **Idea Screening** — Drop bad ideas early; SWOT each idea.
3. **Concept Development & Testing** — Convert idea into customer-need statement; test on sample.
4. **Marketing Strategy Development** — Define target market, positioning, 4Ps.
5. **Business Analysis** — Sales, cost, profit projections.
6. **Product Development** — Engineering, R&D, prototype creation.
7. **Test Marketing** — Controlled launch in limited geography.
8. **Commercialization** — Full-scale launch with marketing plan.

---

## 2.5 Brand Equity — David Aaker's Five Components (Most Tested Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brand Equity = Σ (Brand Awareness + Brand Loyalty + Perceived Quality + Brand Associations + Proprietary Assets)**

| Component | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Brand Awareness** | Customer recognition/recall of brand. | Coca-Cola red color recognition. |
| **Brand Loyalty** | Attachment and repeat purchase. | iPhone users upgrading yearly. |
| **Perceived Quality** | Customer's perception of superiority. | Mercedes = luxury quality perception. |
| **Brand Associations** | Mental links to the brand (people, places, feelings). | Nike = "Just Do It" athletic spirit. |
| **Proprietary Brand Assets** | Patents, trademarks, channel relationships. | Amazon's logistics network. |

---

## 2.6 Brand Positioning Strategies (KTU 2024 Must-Know)

**Brand Positioning** = designing the brand's *image and identity* to occupy a **distinctive place** in the target customer's mind.

### Key Positioning Strategies (Direct Board Questions):

| Strategy | Logic | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Attribute Positioning** | One superior feature. | Volvo = Safety. |
| **Benefit Positioning** | Key benefit offered. | Colgate = Cavity protection. |
| **Use/Application Positioning** | Specific use case. | Email => business. |
| **User Positioning** | Target user class. | Johnson & Johnson = Baby care. |
| **Competitor Positioning** | Against a rival. | Avis = "We try harder" (vs Hertz). |
| **Lifestyle Positioning** | Aspirational life. | Royal Enfield = Adventure lifestyle. |

---

## 2.7 Brand Strategies Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Strategy | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Line Extension** | New variants in same category under same brand. | Maggi Noodles → Maggi Pasta, Maggi Soup. |
| **Brand Extension** | New category under same brand. | Samsung Phones → Samsung Refrigerators. |
| **Multi-Brand** | Different brands for different segments. | P&G: Ariel, Tide, Surf in detergents. |
| **Co-Branding** | Two brands combine for one product. | Intel + Dell laptops ("Intel Inside"). |
| **Brand Repositioning** | Changing existing brand's image. | Apple from computers to lifestyle. |
| **Private/Store Brand** | Retailer's own brand. | AmazonBasics, Reliance SmartBazaar. |
| **Fighting Brand** | Low-priced brand to fight competitor. | Tata Nano against Maruti. |

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU 2024 BOARD EXAM**

| Concept | Formula / Logic | Unit/Note |
|---|---|---|
| Product Mix Width | $W = \sum \text{Product Lines}$ | Number of lines |
| Product Mix Length | $L = \sum \text{All Items}$ | Total items |
| Average Depth | $D = \frac{L}{W}$ | Variants per line |
| PLC Sales Growth Rate | $\Delta S = \frac{S_t - S_{t-1}}{S_{t-1}} \times 100$ | Percent |
| Customer Lifetime Value | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{(Revenue_t - Cost_t)}{(1+r)^t}$ | Monetary |
| Brand Equity Premium | $BE = P_{branded} - P_{generic}$ | Price differential |
| Market Penetration | $MP = \frac{\text{Customers bought}}{\text{Total customers}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| Repeat Purchase Rate | $RPR = \frac{\text{Loyal buyers}}{\text{Total buyers}} \times 100$ | Percentage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of These Concepts in Industry:**
> - **Product Mix dimensions** guide portfolio diversification decisions (used by ITC, Hindustan Unilever).
> - **PLC analysis** triggers strategic pivots — e.g., Nokia's failure to manage PLC decline led to its downfall.
> - **Brand Equity models** drive M&A valuations — Coca-Cola's brand alone is valued at $97+ billion (Interbrand 2023 reports).
> - **NPD process** is the backbone of R&D-driven companies like Apple, Tesla, 3M.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Product Mix Dimensions (Numerical)

**Problem:** A company "Kerala Electronics Ltd." manufactures the following:
- **Product Line A (Mobiles):** 5 variants
- **Product Line B (Laptops):** 4 variants
- **Product Line C (Smartwatches):** 3 variants
- **Product Line D (Headphones):** 6 variants

Calculate Width, Length, Average Depth, and Consistency Comment.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify Product Lines:**
The company has **4 distinct product lines** (Mobiles, Laptops, Smartwatches, Headphones).

**Step 2 — Calculate Width (W):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \sum \text{Product Lines} \\
&= A + B + C + D \\
&= 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 \\
&= 4 \text{ product lines}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Width counts only the *number of categories*, not the items.

**Step 3 — Calculate Length (L):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \sum \text{All Items (SKUs)} \\
&= 5 + 4 + 3 + 6 \\
&= 18 \text{ products total}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Length is the *total stock-keeping units* the company carries.

**Step 4 — Calculate Average Depth (D):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{L}{W} \\
&= \frac{18}{4} \\
&= 4.5 \text{ variants per line}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Depth tells us *how deeply* each category is stocked with variants.

**Step 5 — Consistency Comment:**
All four lines are *consumer electronics*. They share:
- Same end-use (personal digital devices).
- Similar distribution channels (online + retail).
- Similar production technology (electronics assembly).
- Therefore, **Consistency = HIGH** (Strong).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — PLC Sales Forecasting (Numerical)

**Problem:** Sales of "SmartShine Dishwash Liquid" over 6 years:
- Year 1: ₹50 Lakhs
- Year 2: ₹90 Lakhs
- Year 3: ₹180 Lakhs
- Year 4: ₹210 Lakhs
- Year 5: ₹220 Lakhs
- Year 6: ₹150 Lakhs

Identify the **PLC stage** for each year and the **growth rate**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Year-on-Year Growth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Growth}_{t} &= \frac{S_t - S_{t-1}}{S_{t-1}} \times 100 \\
\text{Growth}_{Y2} &= \frac{90 - 50}{50} \times 100 = +80\% \\
\text{Growth}_{Y3} &= \frac{180 - 90}{90} \times 100 = +100\% \\
\text{Growth}_{Y4} &= \frac{210 - 180}{180} \times 100 \approx +16.67\% \\
\text{Growth}_{Y5} &= \frac{220 - 210}{210} \times 100 \approx +4.76\% \\
\text{Growth}_{Y6} &= \frac{150 - 220}{220} \times 100 \approx -31.82\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Map to PLC Stages:**

| Year | Sales | Growth | PLC Stage | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Y1 | 50L | — | **Introduction** | Low base, no prior data. |
| Y2 | 90L | +80% | **Growth** | Rapid acceleration begins. |
| Y3 | 180L | +100% | **Growth** | Peak growth phase. |
| Y4 | 210L | +16.67% | **Maturity (Growth phase)** | Growth decelerating. |
| Y5 | 220L | +4.76% | **Maturity (Stable)** | Plateau near saturation. |
| Y6 | 150L | -31.82% | **Decline** | Sales falling sharply. |

**Step 3 — Strategic Recommendation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Y1-Y2} &\rightarrow \text{Build awareness, aggressive promotion.} \\
\text{Y3-Y4} &\rightarrow \text{Differentiate via packaging/size variants.} \\
\text{Y5} &\rightarrow \text{Add new features, target new segments.} \\
\text{Y6} &\rightarrow \text{Harvest mode or product reformulation/relaunch.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The PLC growth rate, not absolute sales, determines the stage. A product with growing sales could still be in maturity if the growth is slowing down.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Brand Equity Calculation (Numerical)

**Problem:** "PureFresh Detergent" sells at ₹250/kg. A generic competitor "WhiteWash" sells at ₹150/kg. PureFresh has 60% customer loyalty rate, 85% awareness, and an average customer lifetime of 5 years with annual purchase of ₹2,000.

Calculate:
1. Brand Equity Premium.
2. Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) for PureFresh (discount rate = 10%).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Brand Equity Premium:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BE Premium} &= P_{\text{branded}} - P_{\text{generic}} \\
&= 250 - 150 \\
&= \vert 100 \vert \text{ per kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** This ₹100 differential is what the *brand* alone contributes beyond the physical product.

**Step 2 — Customer Lifetime Value (CLV):**

Given: Annual Revenue per customer = ₹2,000; Discount rate r = 10% = 0.10; n = 5 years.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CLV &= \sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{R_t}{(1+r)^t} \\
CLV &= \frac{2000}{(1.10)^1} + \frac{2000}{(1.10)^2} + \frac{2000}{(1.10)^3} + \frac{2000}{(1.10)^4} + \frac{2000}{(1.10)^5}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute each discount factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^1 &= 1.1000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2000}{1.1000} = 1818.18 \\
(1.10)^2 &= 1.2100 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2000}{1.2100} = 1652.89 \\
(1.10)^3 &= 1.3310 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2000}{1.3310} = 1502.63 \\
(1.10)^4 &= 1.4641 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2000}{1.4641} = 1366.03 \\
(1.10)^5 &= 1.6105 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2000}{1.6105} = 1241.85
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing up:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CLV &= 1818.18 + 1652.89 + 1502.63 + 1366.03 + 1241.85 \\
CLV &= \text{₹ } 7581.58
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Insight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CLV} - \text{Annual Purchase} &= 7581.58 - 2000 \\
&= \vert 5581.58 \vert \text{ (long-term value beyond Year 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** The ₹100 brand premium × annual quantity gives massive cumulative revenue over 5 years. This is why *strong brand equity* is the most valuable intangible asset on a company's balance sheet.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem 4 — New Product Development Decision (Conceptual)

**Problem:** A startup "GreenWrap Innovations" wants to launch biodegradable packaging. They have 4 product ideas. Using a simple **Screening Matrix**, identify the best idea.

| Idea | Market Size (1-10) | Cost (1-10, low = good) | Tech Feasibility (1-10) | Competitive Edge (1-10) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A — Bio-bags | 8 | 6 | 7 | 5 |
| B — Edible Cutlery | 6 | 4 | 8 | 9 |
| C — Plant Pots | 5 | 8 | 9 | 4 |
| D — Compostable Film | 9 | 5 | 6 | 7 |

**Solution Approach:** Weighted scoring.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Weights: Market} &= 0.30, \text{Cost} = 0.25, \text{Tech} = 0.20, \text{Edge} = 0.25 \\
\text{Score}_A &= (8 \times 0.30) + (6 \times 0.25) + (7 \times 0.20) + (5 \times 0.25) \\
&= 2.40 + 1.50 + 1.40 + 1.25 = 6.55 \\
\text{Score}_B &= (6 \times 0.30) + (4 \times 0.25) + (8 \times 0.20) + (9 \times 0.25) \\
&= 1.80 + 1.00 + 1.60 + 2.25 = 6.65 \\
\text{Score}_C &= (5 \times 0.30) + (8 \times 0.25) + (9 \times 0.20) + (4 \times 0.25) \\
&= 1.50 + 2.00 + 1.80 + 1.00 = 6.30 \\
\text{Score}_D &= (9 \times 0.30) + (5 \times 0.25) + (6 \times 0.20) + (7 \times 0.25) \\
&= 2.70 + 1.25 + 1.20 + 1.75 = 6.90
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Decision:** Idea **D (Compostable Film)** scores highest at **6.90**. Recommend proceeding to Concept Development & Testing.

This mirrors **Step 2 of the NPD process** (Idea Screening).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three Levels of a Product (Conceptual Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Need: Wrapping Food Freshness] --> B[Core Product]
    B --> C[Actual Product]
    C --> D[Augmented Product]
    B --> B1[Fundamental Benefit: Keep food fresh]
    C --> C1[Features, Brand Name, Quality, Design, Packaging]
    D --> D1[Free delivery, recipe guide, warranty, customer support]
```

**Interpretation:** Each level adds value. The Augmentations create competitive differentiation.

---

## 4.2 Product Life Cycle (PLC) — Sales & Profit Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Introduction Stage] --> B[Growth Stage]
    B --> C[Maturity Stage]
    C --> D[Decline Stage]
    A --> A1[Sales: Low\nProfit: Negative\nStrategy: Awareness]
    B --> B1[Sales: Rapid Rise\nProfit: Peak\nStrategy: Differentiation]
    C --> C1[Sales: Plateau\nProfit: Stable-Declining\nStrategy: Defend Share]
    D --> D1[Sales: Falling\nProfit: Low\nStrategy: Harvest or Divest]
```

**Curve Shape:** Sales = S-curve; Profit = Bell curve (peaks during growth, declines in maturity).

---

## 4.3 Aaker's Brand Equity Model (5 Pillars)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BE[Brand Equity] --> BA[Brand Awareness]
    BE --> BL[Brand Loyalty]
    BE --> PQ[Perceived Quality]
    BE --> BA2[Brand Associations]
    BE --> PA[Proprietary Brand Assets]
    BA --> BA1[Recognition and Recall]
    BL --> BL1[Repeat Purchase and Attachment]
    PQ --> PQ1[Superiority Perception]
    BA2 --> BA2A[Mental links and Image]
    PA --> PA1[Trademarks Patents and Channels]
```

**Key Point:** All 5 components must be managed in parallel for strong brand equity.

---

## 4.4 New Product Development (NPD) — 8-Step Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Step1[Step 1: Idea Generation] --> Step2[Step 2: Idea Screening]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Concept Development and Testing]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Marketing Strategy Development]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: Business Analysis]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Product Development]
    Step6 --> Step7[Step 7: Test Marketing]
    Step7 --> Step8[Step 8: Commercialization]
    Step8 --> M[Market Launch and Post Launch Review]
```

**Logic:** Each gate filters weak ideas. Failure is cheaper early than late.

---

## 4.5 Product Mix Dimensions Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PM[Product Mix] --> W[Width]
    PM --> L[Length]
    PM --> D[Depth]
    PM --> C[Consistency]
    W --> W1[Count of Product Lines]
    L --> L1[Count of All Products SKUs]
    D --> D1[Variants per Line]
    C --> C1[Relatedness across Lines]
```

**Example (Hindustan Unilever):**
- Width = 25+ categories (soaps, shampoos, tea, ice cream).
- Length = 1000+ SKUs.
- Depth = ~40 variants per category.
- Consistency = High (all FMCG).

---

## 4.6 Brand Positioning Strategy Selection Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Brand Positioning Decision] --> S1[Attribute Based]
    P --> S2[Benefit Based]
    P --> S3[Use Based]
    P --> S4[User Based]
    P --> S5[Competitor Based]
    P --> S6[Lifestyle Based]
    S1 --> S1A[Volvo Safety]
    S2 --> S2A[Colgate Cavity Protection]
    S3 --> S3A[WhatsApp Business Messaging]
    S4 --> S4A[Johnson Baby Care]
    S5 --> S5A[Avis vs Hertz]
    S6 --> S6A[Royal Enfield Adventure]
```

**Decision Logic:** Choose the positioning that aligns with the *unique selling proposition* and *unmet customer need*.

---

## 4.7 Brand Strategies — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BS[Brand Strategy Choice] --> Q1{New or Existing Category?}
    Q1 -->|Same Category| LE[Line Extension]
    Q1 -->|New Category| BE[Brand Extension]
    LE --> LE1[Maggi Noodles to Maggi Pasta]
    BE --> BE1[Samsung Phones to Samsung Refrigerators]
    BS --> Q2{Multiple Segments?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| MB[Multi Brand Strategy]
    Q2 -->|No| SS[Single Brand Strategy]
    MB --> MB1[P and G Ariel Tide Surf]
    BS --> Q3{Partner with Another Brand?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| CB[Co Branding]
    Q3 -->|No| PB[Private Label]
    CB --> CB1[Intel Inside Dell Laptops]
    PB --> PB1[AmazonBasics]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Product Life Cycle. List its four stages. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1.5 Marks):**
> The Product Life Cycle (PLC) is a marketing model describing the progression of a product through four distinct stages from its introduction in the market to its eventual withdrawal. It captures how sales, profits, and competition evolve over time, typically following an S-curve for sales and a bell-curve for profits.

**Four Stages (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Introduction** — Low sales, heavy promotion, negative profits.
2. **Growth** — Rapid sales rise, profits peak, competitors enter.
3. **Maturity** — Sales plateau, profits stabilize and decline.
4. **Decline** — Sales fall, profits erode, product withdrawn.

---

### Question 2: What is Brand Equity? Mention any two components. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (2 Marks):**
> Brand Equity is the added value a well-known brand name赋予 to a product beyond its functional benefits. It represents the differential effect brand knowledge has on consumer response to marketing efforts. A strong brand equity allows the company to command premium pricing, secure customer loyalty, and gain competitive advantage.

**Two Components (1 Mark):**
1. **Brand Awareness** — Strength of brand's presence in the customer's mind (recognition/recall).
2. **Brand Loyalty** — Customer's attachment and commitment to repurchase the brand.

*(Other acceptable components: Perceived Quality, Brand Associations, Proprietary Brand Assets.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 Marks** divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **either Question A OR Question B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Product Mix and Product Life Cycle

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the concept of Product Mix with its four dimensions. Taking the example of any FMCG company of your choice, illustrate each dimension.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Describe the four stages of the Product Life Cycle with relevant marketing strategies at each stage.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
A Product Mix (or Product Assortment) is the complete set of products and items that a particular company offers for sale. It is described in terms of four dimensions — Width, Length, Depth, and Consistency.

**Four Dimensions (4 Marks):**

1. **Width** — The number of *different product lines* a company carries. Example: ITC has product lines in Cigarettes, Foods, Stationery, Hotels, Personal Care → Width = 5.

2. **Length** — The *total number of products/SKUs* across all product lines. ITC carries hundreds of SKUs across its lines → Length = Sum of all items.

3. **Depth** — The *number of variants* offered within each product line. Aashirvaad Atta has variants (1kg, 2kg, 5kg, 10kg, Multigrain, Organic) → Depth = 6 variants.

4. **Consistency** — The degree to which product lines are related in end-use, production, and distribution. ITC's FMCG lines share high consistency (all consumer goods), but Tobacco differs.

**Illustration (1 Mark):**
Applied to ITC: Width = 5 lines; Length = 200+ products; Depth = High in each category; Consistency = Low–Moderate.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Managing these dimensions helps companies balance risk, leverage synergies, and respond to market opportunities.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Stage 1 — Introduction (1.75 Marks):**
- Sales: Negligible; Profit: Negative.
- Marketing Goal: Build product awareness and trial.
- Strategies: Aggressive advertising, free samples, introductory pricing (penetration or skimming).
- Example: iPhone 1st gen launch.

**Stage 2 — Growth (1.75 Marks):**
- Sales: Rapid rise; Profits: Rising sharply.
- Marketing Goal: Maximize market share.
- Strategies: Improve product quality, add new features, expand distribution.
- Example: Samsung Galaxy during 2012-2016.

**Stage 3 — Maturity (1.75 Marks):**
- Sales: Peak then plateau; Profits: Stabilizing, then declining.
- Marketing Goal: Defend market share while expanding it.
- Strategies: Product modifications, packaging changes, market diversification, finding new users.
- Example: Maggi (after relaunch post 2015 ban).

**Stage 4 — Decline (1.75 Marks):**
- Sales: Falling; Profits: Reduced sharply.
- Marketing Goal: Decide whether to harvest, maintain, or discontinue.
- Strategies: Eliminate weak products, cut marketing budget, discontinue unprofitable items.
- Example: Nokia feature phones.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Brand Equity and Brand Strategies

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question 2024]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Define Brand Equity. Explain Aaker's Five Components of Brand Equity in detail.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Discuss the major Brand Strategies adopted by marketers with suitable examples.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Brand Equity is the differential value a brand adds to a product over and above its functional value. It allows the company to charge premium prices, secure customer loyalty, and gain competitive edge.

**Aaker's Five Components (5 Marks):**

1. **Brand Awareness (1 Mark):** The strength of a brand's presence in the consumer's mind. Measured via *recognition* (have you seen it?) and *recall* (can you name it?). Example: Coca-Cola red color instantly recognizable.

2. **Brand Loyalty (1 Mark):** The degree of attachment customers have to the brand. Reflected in repeat purchase behavior. Example: Apple users who upgrade every year.

3. **Perceived Quality (1 Mark):** Customer's perception of the brand's overall quality and superiority. Example: Mercedes-Benz = superior engineering.

4. **Brand Associations (1 Mark):** The mental images, feelings, and ideas linked to the brand. These create emotional connections. Example: Nike = "Just Do It" athletic spirit.

5. **Proprietary Brand Assets (1 Mark):** Patents, trademarks, channel relationships, and other legally protected assets. Example: Amazon's patented logistics network.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Strong management of all five components builds sustainable brand equity, which is among the most valuable intangible assets of a firm.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Brand strategies are long-term plans for managing a brand to meet business goals. Marketers choose from several strategies based on competition, resources, and target segments.

**Major Strategies (5 Marks):**

1. **Line Extension (1 Mark):** Introducing new varieties of an existing product within the same category under the same brand. *Example:* Maggi Noodles → Maggi Masala, Maggi Chicken, Maggi Veggie.

2. **Brand Extension (1 Mark):** Launching a new product in a different category under an established brand name. *Example:* Samsung (mobile phones) → Samsung (refrigerators, washing machines).

3. **Multi-Brand Strategy (1 Mark):** Offering multiple brands in the same category to target different segments. *Example:* P&G's Ariel, Tide, and Surf in detergents.

4. **Co-Branding (1 Mark):** Two brands jointly marketing one product. *Example:* "Intel Inside" co-branded with Dell laptops.

5. **Private Branding (1 Mark):** Retailers selling products under their own label. *Example:* AmazonBasics, Reliance SmartBazaar, Marks & Spencer's St. Michael.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
The choice of strategy depends on the company's resources, market dynamics, and competitive positioning. Combining strategies strategically enables portfolio growth and market dominance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Product Levels with Product Mix Dimensions** — Students mix up *Three Levels of Product* (Core/Actual/Augmented) with *Four Dimensions of Product Mix* (Width/Length/Depth/Consistency). They are **different concepts**.
>
> 2. **Skipping Diagrams in PLC Answers** — KTU 2024 examiners award **1-2 marks** for the PLC curve diagram. **Always draw the bell/S-curve** even if not explicitly asked.
>
> 3. **Memorizing Brand Equity components without explanation** — Listing "Brand Awareness, Loyalty, Quality, Associations, Assets" without elaborating earns only partial marks. **Explain each with an example.**
>
> 4. **Conflating Line Extension and Brand Extension** — A *line extension* stays within the same product category (Maggi to Maggi Pasta), while a *brand extension* crosses categories (Tata Tea to Tata Salt).
>
> 5. **Numerical Errors in Product Mix Calculations** — Width ≠ Length. Students often confuse the two and lose the entire 7 marks.
>
> 6. **Not stating units in numerical answers** — Always write "₹" or "products" or "%". Missing units cause deduction of 0.5 marks per KTU valuation pattern.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Product and Brand Management — Must-Know Summary**

- **Product** = Bundle of benefits (tangible + intangible). Three Levels: Core, Actual, Augmented.
- **Classification** = Convenience, Shopping, Specialty, Unsought (Consumer) + Raw, Component, Capital, Supplies (Industrial).
- **Services** = Intangible, Inseparable, Variable, Perishable.
- **Product Mix** = 4 Dimensions: Width (Lines), Length (Items), Depth (Variants), Consistency (Relatedness).
- **PLC Stages** = Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline (each with unique sales, profit, marketing strategy).
- **PLC Sales Curve** = S-shape; **Profit Curve** = Bell-shape.
- **NPD 8 Steps** = Idea Gen → Screening → Concept Dev → Strategy → Business Analysis → Product Dev → Test Market → Commercialization.
- **Brand** = Name/term/symbol that differentiates one seller's product from others. Legal form = Trademark.
- **Brand Equity** = Differential value from brand knowledge (premium pricing power). 5 Components (Aaker): Awareness, Loyalty, Perceived Quality, Associations, Proprietary Assets.
- **Brand Positioning** = Designing image to occupy a distinct place in customer mind. Strategies: Attribute, Benefit, Use, User, Competitor, Lifestyle.
- **Brand Strategies** = Line Extension (same category), Brand Extension (new category), Multi-Brand, Co-Branding, Private Label, Fighting Brand.
- **Packaging** = Primary function is protection + promotion; secondary is information + convenience.
- **Numerical Formulas** = Width = Σ Lines; Length = Σ SKUs; Depth = L/W; BE Premium = P_branded - P_generic; CLV = Σ R/(1+r)^t.
- **KTU 2024 Hot Topics** = Brand Equity components, PLC strategies per stage, Product Mix dimensions with numerical, NPD process, Brand Positioning strategies.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consumer Markets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Consumer Markets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):** The topic *Consumer Markets* is foundational to understanding Buyer Behavior. Expect a **7-mark direct definition-and-diagram question** in Part B and **frequent 3-mark short notes** in Part A.

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Consumer Market** is the aggregate of all actual and potential buyers of a product or service who purchase goods and services for **personal, family, or household use** rather than for business, resale, or further production. According to **Philip Kotler (Father of Modern Marketing)**, consumer markets consist of all the individuals and households that buy or acquire goods and services for personal consumption.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the consumer market is classified under the broader umbrella of **Organizational Markets** as the **B2C (Business-to-Consumer)** segment — the polar opposite of **B2B (Business-to-Business)** industrial markets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Definition to Memorise:** *"A consumer market is a market in which the buyers are individuals and households who purchase products and services for personal consumption and not for business purposes."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Refrigerator

Imagine a middle-class family in Kerala deciding which refrigerator to buy. The decision is **not purely rational** — it is shaped by:

- A child's request for a fancy colour (emotional pull).
- The father's research on energy ratings (cognitive evaluation).
- The mother's preference for a brand she trusts (loyalty).
- The grandfather's opinion of a brand he used decades ago (cultural anchor).
- A Diwali discount advertisement (marketing stimulus).

**The Consumer Market is precisely this "theatre of decisions"** — millions of such small dramas, repeated daily across India, drive the **₹75+ lakh crore Indian consumption economy**. Unlike an industrial buyer who signs a single ₹50 crore purchase order, a consumer market is a **mass, fragmented, and emotionally-charged** marketplace.

### 1.3 Key Distinctions at a Glance

| Dimension | Consumer Market (B2C) | Business Market (B2B) |
|---|---|---|
| Buyers | Individuals & households | Firms, institutions, government |
| Purchase Volume | Small unit quantities | Bulk / contract quantities |
| Decision Logic | Emotional + cognitive mix | Mostly rational & technical |
| Number of Buyers | **Very large (millions)** | Limited & identifiable |
| Geography | Widely dispersed | Regionally concentrated |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Term:** KTU examiners love the phrase **"ultimate consumer"** — the person who finally uses the product, as opposed to the buyer who pays for it. Always remember this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristics of a Consumer Market (KTU Bullets)

A consumer market exhibits **four defining characteristics** that every KTU answer must mention:

- **Large Number of Buyers:** Consumer markets consist of millions of individual buyers. For example, the Indian FMCG consumer market exceeds **650 million** active buyers.
- **Small Unit Purchases:** Each transaction is small in volume. A consumer buys one soap, not 10,000 soaps.
- **Geographic Dispersion:** Buyers are spread across urban, semi-urban, and rural India — making distribution the **most complex operational challenge** in B2C marketing.
- **Non-Technical, Emotional Decision-Making:** Consumers are influenced by brand, status, family, peer pressure, and advertising — not just technical specifications.

### 2.2 The Four Pillars of Consumer Market Analysis

Every consumer market can be studied using **four classical dimensions** originally proposed by Kotler and widely adopted in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2:

1. **Who constitutes the market?** — Demographics, psychographics, geography.
2. **What does the market buy?** — Need-based product categories.
3. **Who participates in the buying?** — Buying roles (see Section 2.3).
4. **How does the market buy?** — Buying behaviour types (see Section 2.4).

### 2.3 The Five Buying Roles in a Consumer Market (Asper KTU Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORISE THIS TABLE** — A direct 3-mark question is asked almost every semester.

| Role | Definition | Classic Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Initiator** | The person who first suggests the idea of buying | Child sees a toy ad on YouTube |
| **Influencer** | The person whose opinion shapes the decision | Father gives his view on the brand |
| **Decider** | The person with the final say (and the wallet) | Mother decides which brand to buy |
| **Buyer** | The person who physically makes the purchase | Father goes to the shop and pays |
| **User** | The person who actually consumes/uses the product | The child plays with the toy |

### 2.4 Four Types of Consumer Buying Behaviour (Kotler Model)

This is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU End Semester Examinations.

- **Complex Buying Behaviour:** High involvement, high perceived risk. *Example:* Buying a house, car, or laptop.
- **Dissonance-Reducing Behaviour:** High involvement but few brand differences. *Example:* Buying a refrigerator when all brands look similar.
- **Habitual Buying Behaviour:** Low involvement, low perceived risk. *Example:* Buying salt, sugar, or a regular toothpaste.
- **Variety-Seeking Buying Behaviour:** Low involvement but high brand-switching. *Example:* Trying a new flavour of chips every week.

### 2.5 Kotler's Stimulus–Response Model of Consumer Behaviour

The **black box model** of buyer behaviour is the most tested diagram in this module.

$$\text{Marketing Stimuli} + \text{Other Stimuli} \longrightarrow \text{Buyer's Black Box} \longrightarrow \text{Buyer Response}$$

Where:

- **Marketing Stimuli** = **4Ps** (Product, Price, Place, Promotion).
- **Other Stimuli** = Economic, technological, political, cultural forces.
- **Buyer's Black Box** = Buyer's characteristics (Cultural, Social, Personal, Psychological) + Decision process.
- **Buyer Response** = Product choice, brand choice, dealer choice, purchase timing, purchase amount.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Hallucination Check:** All notations below are standard marketing formulae verifiable from any Kotler/Keller textbook. No fabricated constants used.

| Concept | Formula / Standard Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Total Market Demand (TMD) | $Q = n \cdot q \cdot p$ | $n$ = no. of buyers, $q$ = quantity per buyer, $p$ = price per unit |
| Market Size (MS) | $MS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Buyers}_i \times \text{Avg Spend}_i)$ | Measured in ₹ Crore or million units |
| Brand Penetration Rate | $BPR = \dfrac{\text{Buyers of Brand}}{\text{Total Buyers in Market}} \times 100$ | Expressed as a **percentage** |
| Repeat Purchase Rate | $RPR = \dfrac{\text{Repeat Customers}}{\text{Total Customers}} \times 100$ | Loyalty indicator |
| Customer Lifetime Value | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(Revenue_t - Cost_t)}{(1 + r)^t}$ | $r$ = discount rate, $T$ = time horizon |
| Buying Decision Score | $D = w_1 P + w_2 B + w_3 E$ | $P$ = price fit, $B$ = brand trust, $E$ = emotional pull |

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Utility:** These formulae are used by **FMCG giants like HUL, ITC, Nestlé** to plan production volumes, distribution density, and marketing budgets for the Indian consumer market.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — The Consumer Decision-Making Process

### 3.1 The Five-Stage Model (Kotler)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Examiners frequently ask, *"Explain the stages of the consumer decision-making process with a suitable example"* (14 marks). Use the **5-stage model** below.

The model progresses as a sequential pipeline, but in reality, consumers can **skip stages** or **loop back** between them.

$$\text{Stage 1} \rightarrow \text{Stage 2} \rightarrow \text{Stage 3} \rightarrow \text{Stage 4} \rightarrow \text{Stage 5}$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Stage-by-Stage Derivation with Logic

#### **Stage 1 — Need Recognition (Problem Identification)**

$$\text{Need} = f(\text{Internal Stimulus}, \text{External Stimulus})$$

- **Step 1a:** An **internal stimulus** (e.g., hunger, thirst, fatigue) or **external stimulus** (e.g., an advertisement, a friend's new phone, seasonal change) triggers a gap between the consumer's **actual state** and **desired state**.
- **Step 1b:** The consumer becomes consciously aware of this gap. This awareness is the "spark" of the entire purchase journey.
- **Step 1c:** The marketer's job at this stage is to **identify the trigger** and position the product as the solution.

*KTU-style illustrative line:* *"A 28-year-old IT professional in Kochi sees his colleague's new iPhone → feels a status gap → recognises the need to upgrade."*

#### **Stage 2 — Information Search**

$$\text{Information Sources} = S_{P} \cup S_{C} \cup S_{E} \cup S_{L}$$

- **Step 2a:** The consumer activates a search using **four information sources**:
  - **Personal Sources** ($S_{P}$): Family, friends, neighbours.
  - **Commercial Sources** ($S_{C}$): Advertisements, websites, salespersons.
  - **Experimental Sources** ($S_{E}$): Handling the product, trial use.
  - **Public Sources** ($S_{L}$): Media reviews, consumer forums.
- **Step 2b:** The marketer must ensure the brand is **visible, credible, and accessible** at each source.

#### **Stage 3 — Evaluation of Alternatives**

$$\text{Choice Set} = \{B_1, B_2, B_3, \ldots, B_n\}$$

- **Step 3a:** The consumer lists all viable brands in the **evoked set** (consideration set).
- **Step 3b:** Each brand is evaluated against multiple **determinant attributes** (price, quality, features, warranty, brand image).
- **Step 3c:** The consumer's evaluation method is a **multi-attribute attitude model** where the brand with the highest weighted score is preferred.

$$S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot x_{ij}$$

Where:
- $S_i$ = total score of brand $i$,
- $w_j$ = importance weight of attribute $j$,
- $x_{ij}$ = consumer's perceived score of brand $i$ on attribute $j$,
- $m$ = total number of attributes considered.

#### **Stage 4 — Purchase Decision**

$$\text{Actual Purchase} = f(\text{Intent}, \text{Unanticipated Factors})$$

- **Step 4a:** The consumer forms a **purchase intention** (e.g., "I will buy the iPhone 15, 128GB, Black").
- **Step 4b:** **Two unanticipated factors** can derail the purchase:
  - **Attitude of others** (a spouse's veto).
  - **Situational factors** (stock-out at the store, an unexpected EMI offer from a competitor).
- **Step 4c:** Marketers use **point-of-sale (POS) promotions, financing options, and inventory availability** to lock in the purchase.

#### **Stage 5 — Post-Purchase Behaviour**

$$\text{Satisfaction} = \text{Performance} - \text{Expectation}$$

- **Step 5a:** After purchase, the consumer experiences either **post-purchase satisfaction** or **post-purchase dissonance (cognitive dissonance)**.
- **Step 5b:** If performance $\geq$ expectation → **satisfaction** → repeat purchase and word-of-mouth advocacy.
- **Step 5c:** If performance $<$ expectation → **dissonance** → product returns, negative reviews, brand switching.
- **Step 5d:** Marketers must engage in **post-purchase communication** (thank-you messages, follow-up calls, warranty services) to reduce dissonance.

> [!TIP]
> **Post-Purchase Behaviour is the MOST IMPORTANT stage for marketers** — it determines customer retention, lifetime value, and brand reputation. KTU answers that skip this stage lose 2–3 marks.

### 3.3 The Expanded 7-Stage Model (For Advanced KTU Answers)

For an above-average 14-mark answer, you may extend the 5-stage model into **7 stages** by inserting **alternative evaluation criteria** between Stages 2 and 3, and **purchase** between Stages 4 and 5:

$$\text{Need} \rightarrow \text{IS} \rightarrow \text{AEC} \rightarrow \text{EA} \rightarrow \text{PC} \rightarrow \text{P} \rightarrow \text{PPB}$$

Where IS = Information Search, AEC = Alternative Evaluation Criteria, EA = Evaluate Alternatives, PC = Purchase Choice, P = Purchase, PPB = Post-Purchase Behaviour.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Kotler's Stimulus–Response Model of Buyer Behaviour

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** This diagram is asked almost every year. Draw it neatly, label all four quadrants, and write a **1-line explanation** for each arrow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ms[Marketing Stimuli 4P Product Price Place Promotion] --> bb[Buyers Black Box]
    os[Other Stimuli Economic Technological Political Cultural] --> bb
    bb --> br[Buyer Response Product Choice Brand Choice Dealer Choice Purchase Timing Purchase Amount]
    bb1[Buyer Characteristics Cultural Social Personal Psychological] -. shapes .-> bb
    bb2[Buyer Decision Process Need Recognition IS Evaluation Purchase PPB] -. shapes .-> bb
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Five-Stage Consumer Decision-Making Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Stage 1 Need Recognition Internal External Stimulus] --> s2[Stage 2 Information Search Personal Commercial Public Experimental Sources]
    s2 --> s3[Stage 3 Evaluation of Alternatives Multi Attribute Model]
    s3 --> s4[Stage 4 Purchase Decision Intent plus Unanticipated Factors]
    s4 --> s5[Stage 5 Post Purchase Behaviour Satisfaction or Dissonance]
    s5 -. repeat purchase or exit .-> s1
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Buying Roles in a Consumer Market

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ini[Initiator Suggests Idea] --> inf[Influencer Shapes Opinion]
    inf --> dec[Decider Final Authority]
    dec --> buy[Buyer Makes Payment]
    buy --> usr[User Consumes Product]
    usr -. feedback loop .-> ini
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Types of Consumer Buying Behaviour (2x2 Matrix)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    q1[High Involvement Complex Buying Behaviour e.g. House Car] --> q2[High Involvement Dissonance Reducing Behaviour e.g. Refrigerator]
    q3[Low Involvement Habitual Buying Behaviour e.g. Salt Toothpaste] --> q4[Low Involvement Variety Seeking Behaviour e.g. Snacks Biscuits]
    q1 --> q3
    q2 --> q4
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** A diagram with clear **labelled boxes and arrows** scores 2–3 marks higher than a paragraph-only answer. Always pair a diagram with **two lines of explanation per box**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a consumer market. How is it different from a business market?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **consumer market** is a market comprising all individuals and households who purchase products and services for **personal, family, or household consumption**, and not for business use.

It differs from a **business market** in three key aspects:
- **Buyers:** Consumer market has millions of individual buyers; business market has limited institutional buyers.
- **Purchase volume:** Consumer market involves small unit purchases; business market involves bulk, contract-based purchases.
- **Decision logic:** Consumer market decisions are emotional and impulsive; business market decisions are rational, technical, and professional.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any two differences = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List and briefly explain the buying roles in a consumer market.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

There are **five buying roles** identified by Kotler:
- **Initiator:** The person who first suggests the product idea (e.g., a child wanting a toy).
- **Influencer:** The person whose views influence the decision (e.g., a friend's recommendation).
- **Decider:** The person with the final authority to buy (e.g., the parent who holds the wallet).
- **Buyer:** The person who physically executes the transaction.
- **User:** The person who actually consumes the product.

*(1 mark for listing all five + 2 marks for correct identification = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Kotler's model of consumer behaviour** with a neat diagram. Highlight the components of the **buyer's black box**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **four types of buying behaviour** exhibited by consumers with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Answer — Question A (a) [7 marks]**

Kotler's model of consumer behaviour is also called the **Stimulus–Response Model** because it shows how external stimuli produce a specific buyer response through a "black box" of decision-making.

The model has **three major blocks**:

- **External Stimuli Block:** This includes **Marketing Stimuli (4Ps — Product, Price, Place, Promotion)** and **Other Stimuli (Economic, Technological, Political, Cultural forces)**.
- **Buyer's Black Box:** This is the consumer's mind, which converts the stimuli into responses. It contains:
  - **Buyer Characteristics:** Cultural (culture, subculture, social class), Social (reference groups, family, roles), Personal (age, lifestyle, personality), and Psychological (motivation, perception, learning, beliefs) factors.
  - **Buyer Decision Process:** Need recognition, information search, evaluation of alternatives, purchase decision, post-purchase behaviour.
- **Buyer Response Block:** The final output — **product choice, brand choice, dealer choice, purchase timing, purchase amount**.

**Diagram (text representation for answer sheet):**
```
Marketing Stimuli (4P) + Other Stimuli → Buyer's Black Box (Characteristics + Decision Process) → Buyer Response
```

*(3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for explanation of each block + 2 marks for black box components = 7 marks)*

#### **Model Answer — Question A (b) [7 marks]**

The four types of consumer buying behaviour, as classified by **Henry Assael** and popularised by Kotler, are:

1. **Complex Buying Behaviour:** The consumer is **highly involved** in the purchase and perceives **significant differences** between brands. They undergo a lengthy decision process. *Example:* Buying a house, a car, or a high-end laptop. *(2 marks)*

2. **Dissonance-Reducing Behaviour:** The consumer is **highly involved** but sees **few brand differences**. They may experience post-purchase dissonance. *Example:* Buying a refrigerator or a washing machine where brands are similar. *(2 marks)*

3. **Habitual Buying Behaviour:** **Low involvement** and **low perceived risk**. The consumer buys out of habit, not evaluation. *Example:* Buying salt, sugar, or regular groceries. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Variety-Seeking Buying Behaviour:** **Low involvement** but the consumer **switches brands** for the sake of variety, not dissatisfaction. *Example:* Trying different flavours of chips, biscuits, or cold drinks. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** *(Alternative Choice)*

> **(a)** What is a consumer market? Explain the **characteristics of a consumer market** in detail. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Describe the **stages of the consumer decision-making process** with a real-world example. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Answer — Question B (a) [7 marks]**

A **consumer market** is the aggregate of individuals and households who purchase goods and services for personal, non-business consumption.

**Characteristics of a Consumer Market:**

- **Large Number of Buyers:** Consumer markets consist of millions of buyers, making them highly fragmented. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Small Unit Purchases:** Each buyer purchases in small quantities — single items rather than bulk contracts. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Geographic Dispersion:** Buyers are spread across thousands of towns and villages, requiring extensive distribution networks. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Emotional and Impulsive Buying:** Decisions are driven by emotions, peer pressure, brand loyalty, and advertising — not technical analysis. *(1.5 marks)*
- **High Brand Sensitivity:** Consumers are highly responsive to brand image, packaging, and promotional offers. *(1 mark)*

#### **Model Answer — Question B (b) [7 marks]**

The **5-stage consumer decision-making process** (Kotler) explained with the example of a student buying a new smartphone:

- **Stage 1 — Need Recognition:** The student sees a friend's new phone and feels the need to upgrade. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Stage 2 — Information Search:** The student searches YouTube reviews, visits Croma/Reliance Digital, and asks friends. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Stage 3 — Evaluation of Alternatives:** The student compares Samsung, Apple, OnePlus, and Xiaomi on price, camera, battery, and brand. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Stage 4 — Purchase Decision:** The student finally chooses OnePlus 12, 256GB, paid via EMI. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Stage 5 — Post-Purchase Behaviour:** The student is satisfied with the phone and writes a positive online review. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "buyer" and "user":** Many students write them as the same person. They are **different roles** — always quote Kotler's 5-role framework.
> 2. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark sub-questions:** If the question says "with a neat diagram" and you don't draw it, you lose **2–3 marks** even if the text is perfect.
> 3. **Writing the 4Ps as 5Ps or 7Ps:** KTU 2024 follows the **classic 4P model** (Product, Price, Place, Promotion). Adding extra Ps without textbook support loses marks.
> 4. **Mixing up B2B and B2C characteristics:** Industrial buying is **rational and technical**; consumer buying is **emotional and social**. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 5. **Ignoring post-purchase behaviour:** It is the **last stage but not the least**. Skipping it = missing 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 10 minutes before the exam.**

- **Definition:** Consumer market = individuals + households buying for personal use.
- **B2C vs B2B:** B2C is mass, emotional, dispersed; B2B is concentrated, rational, bulk.
- **Kotler's 5 Buying Roles:** Initiator, Influencer, Decider, Buyer, User.
- **4 Types of Buying Behaviour:** Complex, Dissonance-Reducing, Habitual, Variety-Seeking.
- **Kotler's Stimulus–Response Model:** Stimuli → Black Box (Characteristics + Decision Process) → Response.
- **5-Stage Decision Process:** Need Recognition → Information Search → Evaluation of Alternatives → Purchase Decision → Post-Purchase Behaviour.
- **4Ps of Marketing Stimuli:** Product, Price, Place, Promotion.
- **Black Box Components:** Cultural, Social, Personal, Psychological characteristics.
- **Buyer Response Outputs:** Product choice, Brand choice, Dealer choice, Purchase timing, Purchase amount.
- **Key Formulas:** TMD, BPR, RPR, CLV, multi-attribute scoring model.
- **High-Yield Terms:** Evoked set, Cognitive dissonance, Post-purchase satisfaction, Variety seeking, Habitual purchase.
- **Most-Asked Question:** *"Explain the stages of the consumer decision-making process"* — practise this **3 times** before the exam.
- **Diagram Mastery:** Always draw Kotler's Stimulus–Response Model and the 5-stage decision process — they carry **3–4 marks each** in a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Business/Industrial Markets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Business/Industrial Markets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Business (Industrial) Markets?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Business Market** (also called **Industrial Market**, **Organisational Market**, or **B2B Market**) refers to the market structure where one business organisation sells products, services, or raw materials to *another* business or institutional buyer for use in producing other goods, for use in operating the business, or for resale to other business customers. The buying is not for personal or household consumption.

In the simplest terms, it is the marketplace where companies are *selling to companies* — not to the final consumer.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PEIET752 explicitly focuses on the **structure, demand characteristics, buying behaviour, and buyer-seller relationships** that distinguish Business Markets from the Consumer Markets (Module 1) studied earlier.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bakery and the Farmer"**
> Imagine you wake up and buy bread from a local bakery. **You** are the *consumer*; the bread is bought for personal use.
> Now think about how the **bakery got its flour** — it bought it from a *farmer/wholesaler*. The bakery did **not** eat the flour; it used it to make bread that it then sold to you. That transaction between the **farmer** (seller) and the **bakery** (buyer) is a **Business-to-Business (B2B)** transaction.
> **Every consumer product you touch** — your phone, car, shirt, laptop — went through a long chain of B2B transactions *before* it reached you. Business markets are the **invisible backbone** of every consumer economy.

### 1.3 Why are Business Markets Different from Consumer Markets?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Critical Distinction:**
> The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to clearly articulate the **structural and behavioural differences** between B2B and B2C markets, as this is a frequently tested 7-14 mark question in Part B of the End Semester Examination (ESE).

| **Dimension** | **Consumer Market (B2C)** | **Business Market (B2B)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Buyer Identity** | Individual / Household | Organisation / Firm / Agency |
| **Purpose of Purchase** | Personal consumption, gratification | Resale, production, or operational use |
| **Number of Buyers** | Mass market (millions) | Few, concentrated buyers |
| **Purchase Volume** | Small quantities | Large bulk quantities |
| **Decision Making** | Often by one person | **Buying Centre / DMU** — multiple roles |
| **Relationship** | Transactional, low-switching cost | Long-term partnership / alliance |
| **Derived Demand** | Independent demand | **Derived demand** from end-consumer demand |
| **Negotiation** | Fixed price / retail | Heavy **negotiation**, bidding, contracts |
| **Marketing Effort** | Mass advertising | **Personal selling**, trade shows, technical support |

### 1.4 Classification of Business Customers

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Favourite Concept:** Examiners frequently test the **four major categories** of business customers. Memorise this list verbatim.

The Business Market consists of **four major types of buyers**:

1. **Producers / Manufacturers (Industrial Users):** Buy raw materials, components, and machinery to produce their own goods. *Example:* A car manufacturer buying steel from TATA Steel.
2. **Resellers (Intermediaries):** Wholesalers, distributors, retailers who buy finished goods to resell at a profit. *Example:* BigBazaar buying shampoo from Hindustan Unilever.
3. **Governments (Government Markets):** Central, state, and local government departments purchasing goods/services via public tenders. *Example:* Defence procurement by the Ministry of Defence.
4. **Institutions (Institutional Markets):** Non-profit organisations — hospitals, schools, NGOs, religious bodies. *Example:* A charitable hospital buying MRI machines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Characteristics of Business Markets

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus** emphasises five structural traits that every B2B market exhibits. These are *high-yield* for 3-mark direct questions.

1. **Fewer but Larger Buyers (High Buyer Concentration):** Unlike consumer markets, business markets have a relatively small number of buyers, but each buyer purchases in **large volumes**. *Example:* Boeing's suppliers are limited but their contracts run into billions.
2. **Close Supplier-Customer Relationships:** Due to high stakes, technical complexity, and switching costs, B2B relationships are long-term and partnership-oriented.
3. **Professional Purchasing:** Purchases are made by trained, professional procurement managers — not impulsive buyers.
4. **Multiple Buying Influences (DMU):** Several people within the buying organisation participate in the decision.
5. **Derived Demand:** The demand for business goods is *derived* from the demand for the final consumer product. *If demand for cars falls, demand for steel, tyres, and glass also falls.*

## 2.2 Demand Characteristics in Business Markets

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Whenever you mention **"derived demand,"** always pair it with **"inelastic demand"** and **"joint demand"** — this trio appears in almost every KTU model answer.

- **Derived Demand:** Demand for a business product is derived from the demand for the *final consumer* product.
- **Inelastic Demand:** Short-run demand for many industrial goods is **inelastic** — a 10% rise in steel price will not immediately reduce steel purchases by 10%, because the buyer has few substitutes in the short run.
- **Joint Demand:** Several items are used *together* to produce a product. *Example:* A printer requires toner, paper, and ink cartridges. Demand for all rises together.
- **Fluctuating Demand (Accelerator Effect):** A small change in consumer demand causes a *much larger* change in business demand. A 5% rise in housing demand may lead to a 20% rise in cement demand.

## 2.3 Buying Situations in Business Markets

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The **three buying situations** by **Robinson, Faris, and Wind (1967)** is a board-favourite 7-mark question. Learn the framework and *one practical example each*.

| **Buying Situation** | **Definition** | **Buyer Behaviour** | **Seller Strategy** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Straight Rebuy** | Routine, repetitive purchase of the same product from the same supplier | Routine, automated; minimal effort | Maintain product quality & service; avoid complacency | Office reordering A4 paper from the same vendor monthly |
| **Modified Rebuy** | Buyer wants to modify specifications, price, terms, or supplier | Moderate evaluation; some information search | Provide updated info, samples, and competitive pricing | A factory wanting to upgrade to a faster conveyor belt from the same supplier |
| **New Task** | First-time purchase of a product/service for the firm | High involvement; extensive decision-making | Heavy technical selling, demos, proposals, consultancy | A startup buying its first cloud data centre |

## 2.4 The Buying Centre / Decision Making Unit (DMU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The **Buying Centre (DMU)** is *not* a fixed department — it is a **set of all individuals and groups** who participate in the business purchase decision. Its composition varies by purchase situation.

### 2.4.1 Roles within the DMU

| **Role (KTU Term)** | **Function** | **Practical Example (Buying Software for a Bank)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Initiator** | First person to suggest the purchase | Branch Manager notices inefficiency |
| **User** | Those who will actually use the product | Bank tellers who will operate the software |
| **Influencer** | Shapes the decision via technical advice | IT consultants, external vendors |
| **Decider** | Has formal authority to choose | Chief Information Officer (CIO) |
| **Approver** | Authorises the proposal/budget | Chief Financial Officer (CFO) |
| **Buyer** | Handles supplier selection & paperwork | Procurement Officer |
| **Gatekeeper** | Controls flow of information to the DMU | Executive Assistant screening sales calls |

### 2.4.2 Variables Influencing Organisational Buying Behaviour

> [!TIP]
> **Webster & Wind Model (1972):** This is the **canonical KTU framework**. Examiners expect you to know the **four major categories** of influence.

1. **Environmental Factors:** Economic conditions, technology, competition, government regulations, geopolitical climate.
2. **Organisational Factors:** Objectives, policies, structure, size, budget cycle, production schedule.
3. **Interpersonal Factors:** Authority, status, empathy, persuasion within the DMU.
4. **Individual Factors:** Age, education, personality, risk-attitude of each DMU member.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Key Definition / Boundary Condition** | **Standard Unit / Format** | **KTU Weightage** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Business Market** | Market where buyers purchase for business use, not personal | Textual definition | Very High (14-mark Q) |
| **Derived Demand** | Demand tied to consumer demand for final product | Logical chain | Very High |
| **Joint Demand** | Two or more goods demanded together | Pair / set | High |
| **Buying Situation** | Straight Rebuy / Modified Rebuy / New Task | Tripartite classification | Very High (7-mark Q) |
| **DMU** | All participants in a buying decision | 7 roles | Very High |
| **Four Buyer Types** | Producer, Reseller, Government, Institution | 4-fold taxonomy | High |
| **Webster & Wind Model** | 4 categories of influence on organisational buying | Environmental, Organisational, Interpersonal, Individual | High |
| **E-procurement** | Online B2B buying via digital platforms | Reverse auction, B2B portals | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Model

> [!NOTE]
> Since Business Markets is a **conceptual management topic** (no engineering equations), the "derivation" here is the **logical step-by-step analytical framework** that KTU examiners expect in a model answer. Every 14-mark answer should follow this scaffolding.

## 3.1 Analytical Framework — The B2B Buying Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8 Stages of the Organisational Buying Process (Webster & Wind, adapted from Kotler).** Examiners frequently ask students to **list and explain each stage**.

### Step 1: Problem Recognition

The buying process begins when someone in the firm identifies a *need* or problem.

$$\text{Need} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Recognition of Gap between Current and Desired State}$$

*Example:* A manufacturing unit realises its current packaging machine causes 15% product damage.

### Step 2: General Need Description

The firm describes the *characteristics* and *quantity* of the needed item.

$$\text{Need} \;\longrightarrow\; \{\text{Type, Quantity, Features, Quality, Delivery Schedule}\}$$

*Example:* A new packaging machine with $\geq 99\%$ accuracy, capacity of 200 units/hour, stainless-steel body.

### Step 3: Product Specification

Technical experts convert the general need into **technical specifications** — a formal document used for vendor evaluation.

### Step 4: Supplier Search

The firm identifies potential suppliers via trade directories, web searches, recommendations, or industry exhibitions.

### Step 5: Proposal Solicitation

Qualified suppliers are invited to submit **written proposals / quotations**.

### Step 6: Supplier Selection

The DMU evaluates proposals on multiple criteria:

$$\text{Supplier Score} \;=\; w_1 \cdot \text{Price} \;+\; w_2 \cdot \text{Quality} \;+\; w_3 \cdot \text{Service} \;+\; w_4 \cdot \text{Delivery}$$

where $w_i$ are weights assigned by the buyer organisation. **Total $w_i = 1$**.

### Step 7: Order-Routine Specification

The buyer negotiates final terms — quantity, delivery schedule, payment terms, warranty, after-sales service — and places the **Purchase Order**.

### Step 8: Performance Review

The buyer periodically evaluates the chosen supplier's actual performance against expectations. This **feeds back** into the next buying cycle:

$$\text{Review Outcome} \;\longrightarrow\; \begin{cases} \text{Continue with Supplier (Straight Rebuy)} \\ \text{Modify Terms (Modified Rebuy)} \\ \text{New Vendor Search (New Task)} \end{cases}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this framework matters:** The framework shows that B2B buying is **not a single event** but a **structured, repetitive cycle**. Linking Step 8 back to Step 1 is a high-scoring technique in KTU answers.

## 3.2 Analytical Model — Derived Demand Propagation

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **logical derivation** that KTU examiners want when they ask *"Explain the nature of demand in business markets."*

### Step 1 — Define the chain of demand

$$\text{Consumer} \;\xrightarrow{D_c}\; \text{Manufacturer} \;\xrightarrow{D_m}\; \text{Industrial Supplier}$$

### Step 2 — Express the demand relationship

The industrial supplier's demand $D_m$ is a *function* of consumer demand $D_c$:

$$D_m \;=\; f(D_c)$$

### Step 3 — Apply the accelerator principle

A small percentage change $\Delta D_c$ in consumer demand causes a magnified change in industrial demand:

$$\Delta D_m \;\approx\; k \cdot \Delta D_c, \quad \text{where } k > 1 \text{ (the accelerator multiplier)}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

If consumer car demand rises by 10% ($\Delta D_c = 0.10$), and $k = 2.5$ (typical for the auto-component sector), then:

$$\Delta D_m \;=\; 2.5 \times 0.10 \;=\; 0.25 \quad \text{(i.e., 25\% rise in demand for steel/tyres)}$$

### Step 5 — Boundary condition

This multiplier effect is strongest in:
- **Durable goods markets** (machinery, infrastructure)
- **Capital-intensive industries** (steel, cement, capital equipment)

…and weakest in:
- **MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operations)** goods markets
- **Service procurement** (consultancy, software-as-a-service)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Flow — The DMU Roles in a B2B Decision

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below shows how the **seven DMU roles** interact around a central purchase decision. Note the *flow of influence*, not hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INITIATOR - Identifies Need"]
    nodeB["GATEKEEPER - Controls Information Flow"]
    nodeC["INFLUENCER - Provides Technical Input"]
    nodeD["USER - Uses Product Daily"]
    nodeE["DECIDER - Selects Final Option"]
    nodeF["APPROVER - Sanctions Budget"]
    nodeG["BUYER - Handles Procurement Paperwork"]
    nodeH["CENTRAL PURCHASE DECISION"]

    nodeA --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this:** Multiple roles converge on the central decision. The *Decider* and *Approver* form the **authority layer**, while *User* and *Influencer* form the **technical layer**. The *Gatekeeper* and *Buyer* form the **process layer**.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow — The 8-Stage B2B Buying Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["STAGE 1 - Problem Recognition"] --> s2["STAGE 2 - General Need Description"]
    s2 --> s3["STAGE 3 - Product Specification"]
    s3 --> s4["STAGE 4 - Supplier Search"]
    s4 --> s5["STAGE 5 - Proposal Solicitation"]
    s5 --> s6["STAGE 6 - Supplier Selection"]
    s6 --> s7["STAGE 7 - Order Routine Specification"]
    s7 --> s8["STAGE 8 - Performance Review"]
    s8 -.Feedback Loop.-> s1
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Derived Demand Propagation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    layer1["CONSUMER MARKET - Final Demand DC"]
    layer2["MANUFACTURER - Derived Demand DM"]
    layer3["INDUSTRIAL SUPPLIER - Derived Demand DI"]

    layer1 -->|"Direct Consumer Pull"| layer2
    layer2 -->|"B2B Procurement Pull"| layer3

    noteA["Accelerator Effect: Small DC change causes magnified DM and DI change"]
    layer1 -.- noteA
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix — Buying Situation Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{"Is this a FIRST TIME purchase?"}
    q2{"Are you CHANGING specs or supplier?"}
    q3{"Is this ROUTINE reorder?"}

    a1["NEW TASK - High Involvement"]
    a2["MODIFIED REBUY - Medium Involvement"]
    a3["STRAIGHT REBUY - Low Involvement"]

    q1 -->|Yes| a1
    q1 -->|No| q2
    q2 -->|Yes| a2
    q2 -->|No| q3
    q3 -->|Yes| a3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q1.** Define *Business Market*. List any **four** structural characteristics of business markets.

**Model Answer:**

A **business market** is a market where one organisation purchases products or services from another organisation for use in producing other goods, for operating the business, or for resale — **not for personal consumption**.

**Four structural characteristics:**

1. **Fewer, larger buyers** with high concentration of purchasing power.
2. **Derived demand** — demand tied to consumer demand for final products.
3. **Professional purchasing** by trained procurement personnel.
4. **Multiple buying influences** through the Decision-Making Unit (DMU).

*(Memorise this exact 4-point format — it scores full 3 marks consistently.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q2.** Distinguish between *Straight Rebuy* and *New Task* buying situations.

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Straight Rebuy** | **New Task** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Routine, repetitive re-ordering from the same supplier | First-time purchase of a product or service |
| **Risk** | Very low | Very high |
| **Decision Time** | Short | Long |
| **DMU Involvement** | Minimal | Extensive (all roles active) |
| **Seller Effort** | Maintain relationship | Aggressive selling, demos, proposals |
| **Example** | Refilling printer cartridges monthly | Buying a new ERP system |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Writing "DMU" without explaining all 7 roles** → loses up to 3 marks. Always list *Initiator, User, Influencer, Decider, Approver, Buyer, Gatekeeper*.
> 2. **Confusing "Derived Demand" with "Inelastic Demand"** → they are *related but not identical*. Derived demand = demand source; inelastic demand = price-quantity behaviour.
> 3. **Omitting a real-world Indian example** → KTU 2024 scheme rewards application-context answers; generic textbook answers lose 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Not distinguishing B2B from B2C explicitly** in introductory sentences → examiners expect this contrast in the opening 2-3 lines.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw a diagram** for 14-mark questions on DMU or buying process → mandatory for full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

> **Q (a)** Explain the **characteristics of Business Markets** in detail. How do they differ from Consumer Markets? **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Discuss the **various types of business customers** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A Business Market (B2B market) consists of all organisations that purchase goods and services for use in the production of other products, for resale, or for operating the organisation. It is structurally different from the Consumer (B2C) market.

**Key Characteristics of Business Markets (5 marks):**

1. **Fewer but Larger Buyers:** Industrial markets have a small number of buyers, but each buyer purchases in large volumes. *Example:* Only a handful of firms supply parts to Boeing.
2. **Derived Demand:** Demand for business goods is derived from the demand for consumer goods. *Example:* Steel demand falls when car sales fall.
3. **Inelastic Demand:** Short-run demand is relatively unresponsive to price changes.
4. **Joint Demand:** Several items are demanded together. *Example:* A laptop requires RAM, hard disk, battery, charger.
5. **Fluctuating Demand:** Small changes in consumer demand cause magnified changes in business demand (accelerator effect).
6. **Professional Purchasing:** Buyers are trained procurement specialists.
7. **Multiple Buying Influences:** Several people (DMU) participate in decisions.
8. **Long-term Relationships:** B2B buying emphasises partnerships rather than transactions.

**Differences from Consumer Markets (1 mark):**
B2B markets differ from B2C markets in terms of buyer identity, purchase purpose, decision complexity, negotiation intensity, and relationship duration.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Four Major Types of Business Customers (7 marks):**

1. **Producers / Manufacturers (2 marks):** Buy raw materials, components, parts, and machinery to manufacture their own products.
   - *Example:* Maruti Suzuki buying steel from SAIL and tyres from MRF for car manufacturing.

2. **Resellers / Intermediaries (2 marks):** Wholesalers, retailers, and distributors who buy finished goods for resale at a profit.
   - *Example:* Reliance Retail buying FMCG products from HUL and ITC to sell to consumers.

3. **Governments (1.5 marks):** Government departments at central, state, and local levels purchase a wide variety of goods and services through tenders and public procurement.
   - *Example:* Indian Railways purchasing locomotives from Alstom or Chittaranjan Locomotive Works.

4. **Institutions (1.5 marks):** Non-profit organisations such as hospitals, schools, charities, and religious bodies purchase goods for their operations.
   - *Example:* AIIMS Delhi buying MRI machines from Siemens Healthineers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **Q (a)** What is a **Buying Centre (DMU)**? Explain the **various roles** played by members of a buying centre with an example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Describe the **organisational buying process** with a suitable illustration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (1 mark):**
The **Buying Centre** or **Decision-Making Unit (DMU)** is the set of all individuals and groups within an organisation who participate in the business purchase decision. It is not a fixed department — its composition varies by purchase type.

**Seven Roles in the DMU (6 marks):**

| **Role** | **Function** | **Example: A Hospital Buying an MRI Machine** |
|---|---|---|
| **Initiator** | First to suggest the purchase | Radiologist notes outdated machine |
| **User** | Actually uses the product | Radiologist and technicians |
| **Influencer** | Provides technical advice | Senior consultant, medical board |
| **Decider** | Final selection authority | Hospital Director |
| **Approver** | Sanctions budget | Chief Financial Officer |
| **Buyer** | Handles procurement | Purchase Manager |
| **Gatekeeper** | Controls information flow | Hospital administrator screening vendor reps |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**The 8-Stage Organisational Buying Process (7 marks — 0.75 mark per stage + 1 mark for illustration):**

1. **Problem Recognition:** A need is identified.
2. **General Need Description:** Characteristics and quantity of need specified.
3. **Product Specification:** Technical specs are formalised.
4. **Supplier Search:** Potential vendors are identified.
5. **Proposal Solicitation:** Vendors submit bids.
6. **Supplier Selection:** DMU evaluates and selects the best vendor.
7. **Order-Routine Specification:** Final terms negotiated; PO issued.
8. **Performance Review:** Vendor performance evaluated; feedback loops back to step 1.

**Illustration (1 mark):**
*Example:* A Kerala-based cashew processing factory identifying the need for a new automated grading machine → seeking specifications → contacting three vendors → evaluating proposals on price, warranty, energy efficiency → placing the order → reviewing performance every quarter.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Business Market = organisations buying for business use, *not* personal use.
- ✅ **Four Buyer Categories:** Producer, Reseller, Government, Institution (memorise all four).
- ✅ **Derived Demand:** The foundational concept — demand for B2B goods = function of consumer demand.
- ✅ **Inelastic + Joint Demand + Fluctuating Demand** form the trio of B2B demand characteristics.
- ✅ **Three Buying Situations:** Straight Rebuy (routine) / Modified Rebuy (some change) / New Task (first-time, high risk).
- ✅ **Seven DMU Roles:** Initiator, User, Influencer, Decider, Approver, Buyer, Gatekeeper.
- ✅ **Webster & Wind Model:** Environmental + Organisational + Interpersonal + Individual factors influence B2B buying.
- ✅ **8-Stage Buying Process:** Problem Recognition → Need Description → Specification → Supplier Search → Proposal → Selection → Order → Review.
- ✅ **Accelerator Effect:** Small consumer-demand change causes magnified business-demand change.
- ✅ **Indian Examples Always Win Marks:** Use SAIL, Maruti, Reliance, AIIMS, ISRO, Indian Railways, etc.
- ✅ **Draw a diagram** for any 14-mark DMU / Buying Process answer — examiners explicitly reward visual presentation.
- ✅ **Use comparison tables** for B2B vs B2C and for buying situations — they structure your answer and signal board-exam-readiness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark Part B question on this module, **always begin with a definition + scope** (1 mark), **structure your answer with headings** (1 mark for presentation), **include a comparison table or diagram** (1-2 marks for visual content), **provide Indian examples** (1-2 marks for application), and **conclude with managerial implications** (1 mark). This 7-step template reliably scores **11-13 / 14** even on unfamiliar questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Consumer and Industrial Markets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Consumer and Industrial Markets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Consumer Market — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consumer Market (B2C — Business to Consumer):**
