1. Stock Turnover Rate
2. Gross Margin Return on Inventory Investment (GMROI)

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Stock Turnover Rate**

Stock turnover measures how many times average inventory is sold during a year.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stock Turnover} &= \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory at Retail}} \\
&= \dfrac{60{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 6 \text{ times per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** This means the retailer's average inventory cycles through sales 6 times annually.

**Step 2 — GMROI Calculation**

GMROI measures how much gross margin is earned for every rupee invested in inventory.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GMROI} &= \dfrac{\text{Gross Margin}}{\text{Average Inventory at Cost}} \\
&= \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{7{,}00{,}000} \\
&\approx 2.571 \text{ ₹ per ₹ invested}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** For every ₹1 spent on inventory, the retailer earns ₹2.57 in gross margin — a strong performance benchmark.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Retail Strategies

| **Strategy Decision** | **Mass Merchandiser** | **Niche Retailer** | **Value Retailer** |
|:----------------------|:---------------------|:------------------|:-------------------|
| **Target Customer** | Middle-income mass market | Specific demographic / psychographic | Price-sensitive shoppers |
| **Assortment Breadth** | Very wide | Narrow & deep | Moderate |
| **Price Strategy** | Competitive | Premium / specialty pricing | Everyday Low Pricing (EDLP) |
| **Service Level** | Self-service | High-touch, personalized | Self-service |
| **Margin** | Low margin, high volume | High margin, low volume | Very low margin, very high volume |
| **Example** | Big Bazaar, Reliance Fresh | Nykaa Luxe, Croma | DMart, Metro Cash & Carry |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Tip for KTU Exams:** Always align the **strategy** with the **target segment** and the **retail mix**. Mismatches lead to failure (e.g., a value retailer offering high-touch service).

### 3.4 Retail Lifecycle (KDV Model)

Retail formats, like products, follow a lifecycle:

1. **Innovation** — new format gains early adopters.
2. **Accelerated Development** — competitors imitate; rapid growth.
3. **Maturity** — saturation; format becomes a norm.
4. **Decline** — consumer preferences shift to newer formats.

> **Example:** Single-screen cinemas → Multiplex → OTT platforms (where OTT is the new format replacing the previous retail format).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Strategic Retail Planning Process (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Retail Mission Statement"] --> B["Situation Audit SWOT"]
    B --> C["Identify Strategic Opportunities"]
    C --> D["Specify Objectives and Goals"]
    D --> E["Develop Retail Strategy"]
    E --> F["Implement the Strategy"]
    F --> G["Evaluate Performance and Control"]
    G --> A
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows indicate the flow of strategic decisions. The arrow from G back to A represents continuous feedback — evaluation outcomes reshape the mission and planning cycle.

### 4.2 The Retail Marketing Mix — 6 Ps Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Retail Strategy Core"] --> P1["Product Merchandise Assortment"]
    R --> P2["Price Pricing Strategy"]
    R --> P3["Promotion Communication Mix"]
    R --> P4["Place Location and Channels"]
    R --> P5["Presentation Visual Merchandising"]
    R --> P6["Personnel Sales and Service"]
    P1 --> OUT["Customer Value and Store Performance"]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
    P5 --> OUT
    P6 --> OUT
```

> **Interpretation:** All six Ps converge into a single outcome — customer-perceived value and store profitability.

### 4.3 Retail Format Classification (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RT["Retail Outlets"] --> STORE["Store Based Retail"]
    RT --> NONSTORE["Non Store Retail"]
    STORE --> SPEC["Specialty Store"]
    STORE --> DEPT["Department Store"]
    STORE --> SUPER["Supermarket Hypermarket"]
    STORE --> CONV["Convenience Store"]
    STORE --> DISC["Discount Store"]
    STORE --> CATK["Category Killer"]
    STORE --> WHSE["Warehouse Club"]
    NONSTORE --> ONLINE["E Retailer Pure Play"]
    NONSTORE --> OMNI["Omnichannel Retailer"]
    NONSTORE --> DIR["Direct Selling and Catalog"]
    NONSTORE --> VM["Vending Machine and Telemarketing"]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Retail outlets are classified into two major branches — store-based and non-store-based. Each branch contains multiple sub-formats that KTU exams frequently ask about.

### 4.4 Retail Supply Chain & Performance Metrics Linkage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["Suppliers and Vendors"] --> WH["Warehouse and Inventory"]
    WH --> ST["Stores and Online Channels"]
    ST --> C["Customer"]
    C -. feedback .-> ST
    WH --> KPI["Performance Metrics"]
    ST --> KPI
    KPI --> ST
```

> **Insight:** Modern retail is a **closed-loop system** — customer feedback and KPIs continuously improve store and warehouse operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
Define retailing. List any two differences between retailing and wholesaling.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Retailing is the final stage of the distribution channel where goods/services are sold directly to the ultimate consumer for personal, non-business use.
>
> **Differences (2 Marks):**
>
> | **Parameter** | **Retailing** | **Wholesaling** |
> |:--------------|:--------------|:----------------|
> | **Customer** | Ultimate consumer | Business buyers (retailers/institutions) |
> | **Volume** | Small quantities | Large quantities |
> | **Location** | Near residential/commercial clusters | Industrial or transport hubs |
> | **Promotion** | Mass advertising, in-store displays | Personal selling, trade shows |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
What is **scrambled merchandising**? Give a real-world example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Scrambled merchandising occurs when a retailer broadens its assortment by offering goods and services not traditionally associated with its category, to attract more customers and increase sales per visit.
>
> **Example (1.5 Marks):** A traditional bookstore like *Crossword* selling coffee, stationery, toys, and merchandise alongside books — diversifying beyond its core category.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **Strategic Retail Planning Process** in detail. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **6 Ps of Retailing** with relevant examples. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Strategic Retail Planning Process — 7 Marks**

The strategic retail planning process is a structured framework that guides retailers in aligning operations with long-term objectives. The steps are:

1. **Define the Retail Mission Statement (1 Mark):** Specifies the retailer's purpose, target customers, value proposition, and core competencies. Example: DMart's mission — *"Offer good value for money and a wide assortment under one roof."*

2. **Conduct a Situation Audit / SWOT Analysis (1 Mark):** Evaluates internal Strengths (e.g., strong supplier relationships) and Weaknesses (e.g., limited digital presence), and external Opportunities (e.g., rural demand growth) and Threats (e.g., intense e-commerce competition).

3. **Identify Strategic Opportunities (1 Mark):** Uncovers untapped markets, new customer segments, or technological innovations such as AI-driven personalization.

4. **Specify Objectives and Goals (1 Mark):** Defines measurable goals — e.g., "achieve 15% sales growth and 25% gross margin in FY 2024–25."

5. **Develop Retail Strategy (1 Mark):** Decides positioning (mass, niche, or value) and crafts the retail mix (6 Ps).

6. **Implementation (1 Mark):** Translates strategy into action — store layout, hiring, supply chain setup, IT systems, marketing campaigns.

7. **Evaluation and Control (1 Mark):** Tracks KPIs (sales/sq.ft., GMROI, stock turnover) and adjusts strategy based on results.

**(b) The 6 Ps of Retailing — 7 Marks**

| **P** | **Meaning** | **Example** |
|:-----:|:------------|:------------|
| **Product** | Merchandise variety, brands, private labels | Big Bazaar's *Fa* private label |
| **Price** | EDLP, Hi-Lo, psychological pricing, markdowns | DMart — Everyday Low Pricing |
| **Promotion** | Advertising, sales promotions, loyalty programs | Amazon Prime membership |
| **Place** | Location, distribution channels, omnichannel | Tanishq stores + online portal |
| **Presentation** | Visual merchandising, store atmosphere | Apple Store — minimalist, hands-on demo zones |
| **Personnel** | Trained sales force, customer service quality | Tanishq's jewellery consultants |

Each P must align with the chosen positioning. For instance, a **niche luxury retailer** like *Cartier* combines high-pricing, exclusive products, premium presentation, and highly trained personnel, while a **value retailer** like *DMart* focuses on low prices, self-service, and wide assortment.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast between **Specialty Store**, **Department Store**, and **Supermarket** as retail formats. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A retailer purchases goods at ₹600 per unit. He applies a 50% markup on cost and later gives a discount of 20% during clearance. Calculate: (i) original selling price, (ii) final selling price, and (iii) actual gross margin %. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison — 7 Marks**

| **Parameter** | **Specialty Store** | **Department Store** | **Supermarket** |
|:--------------|:--------------------|:---------------------|:----------------|
| **Assortment** | Narrow product line, deep assortment | Wide variety across categories | Wide range of groceries & household items |
| **Examples** | Croma (electronics), Nykaa (beauty) | Shoppers Stop, Lifestyle | Big Bazaar, More |
| **Customer Service** | High-touch, knowledgeable staff | Moderate service | Self-service |
| **Pricing** | Premium | Moderate to high | Competitive / low |
| **Location** | High-street malls | City centers, malls | Residential, suburban |
| **Ambience** | Themed, brand-specific | Elegant, multi-floor layout | Functional, spacious |
| **Target Customer** | Specific enthusiasts | Middle to upper-middle class | Mass market, daily-needs shoppers |

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Original Selling Price (2 Marks)**

Markup = 50% on cost.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original Price} &= \text{Cost} + (0.50 \times \text{Cost}) \\
&= 600 + 300 = 900 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Final Selling Price After 20% Discount (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Discount Amount} &= 0.20 \times 900 = 180 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{Final Selling Price} &= 900 - 180 = 720 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual Gross Margin % (3 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual Gross Margin (in ₹)} &= 720 - 600 = 120 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{GM \%} &= \dfrac{120}{720} \times 100 \\
&= 16.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Even though the planned markup was 50%, after a 20% markdown, the actual realized margin fell to ≈ 16.67% — illustrating the *margin erosion effect* of aggressive discounting.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in 'Managing Retailing' Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "markup" with "margin"** — markup is on *cost*, margin is on *selling price*. Examiners check this carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply discount on the marked-up price** — always compute the discount on the *original retail price*, not on cost.
> 3. **Mixing up formats** — a *Category Killer* is NOT a department store; students often interchange them.
> 4. **Not aligning 6 Ps with positioning** — if the question asks about a niche retailer, don't mention EDLP.
> 5. **Ignoring the retail lifecycle** — many answers forget that retail formats decline; this costs CO4 marks.
> 6. **Missing units and labels in numerical answers** — always write ₹, %, or times/year alongside numbers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Retailing** = selling to end consumers for personal use.
- **6 Ps** = Product, Price, Promotion, Place, Presentation, Personnel.
- **Strategic Planning Process** = Mission → SWOT → Opportunities → Goals → Strategy → Implementation → Control (continuous loop).
- **Retail Formats** = Specialty, Department, Supermarket, Convenience, Discount, Category Killer, Warehouse, E-Retailer, Omnichannel.
- **Positioning Strategies** = Mass Merchandiser, Niche Retailer, Value Retailer.
- **Key Performance Metrics** = Net Sales, GM%, Markup, Markdown, Stock Turnover, GMROI, Sales/sq.ft., DPP.
- **Markup %** is on **cost**; **Margin %** is on **selling price**.
- **Retail Lifecycle** = Innovation → Accelerated Development → Maturity → Decline.
- **Scrambled Merchandising** = selling unrelated categories to expand basket size.
- **Category Management** = treating categories as strategic business units.
- **Omnichannel** = seamless integration of online + offline retailing.
- Always show **formulas with units** and final answers **rounded to 2 decimals**.
- Use **SWOT + retail mix alignment** as the answer backbone for case-style questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wholesaling and Logistics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wholesaling and Logistics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 — Wholesaling and Logistics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Wholesaling — Formal Definition

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framework** (drawn from Philip Kotler’s standard marketing lexicon), **Wholesaling** is defined as:

> *"All activities involved in selling goods and services to those who buy for the purpose of resale, or for use in conducting business operations, but not for final personal consumption."*

In simple words, a **wholesaler** is the **"Middleman of the Marketplace"** — a business entity that sits between the manufacturer (producer) and the retailer (or industrial buyer), buying in **bulk** and breaking it into **smaller, manageable lots** for downstream resellers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams**
> A wholesaler is **not** the final consumer. The buyer of a wholesaler is always a **reseller, an industrial user, an institution, or another wholesaler**.

### 1.2 Logistics — Formal Definition

The **Council of Supply Chain Management Professionals (CSCMP)** defines logistics as:

> *"The process of planning, implementing, and controlling the efficient, cost-effective flow and storage of goods, services, and related information from the point of origin to the point of consumption for the purpose of conforming to customer requirements."*

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogies (Plain English)

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Plain English Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| **Wholesaler** | A **vegetable mandi** (wholesale market) that buys tonnes of tomatoes from farmers and sells 50 kg crates to small vegetable vendors. | The **bulk-breaking bridge** between the producer and the shopkeeper. |
| **Logistics** | The **arteries and veins of a human body** — oxygen (goods) must reach the brain (customer) through a network of vessels (transport) and storage depots (warehouses). | The **art and science of moving things from A to B in the smartest, cheapest way possible**. |
| **Supply Chain** | The **entire human anatomy** — lungs, heart, kidneys, brain, all working together to keep the body alive. | The **whole network** of suppliers, manufacturers, warehouses, transporters, and retailers. |
| **Reverse Logistics** | **Returning an Amazon product** — the seller has to collect it, inspect it, refurbish it, and either resell or recycle. | The **backward journey** of goods from customer back to the source. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams (Mnemonic — "SBFFTWIRMS")**
> The 9 major **functions of a wholesaler**:
> **S** – Selling and Promoting
> **B** – Buying and Assortment Building
> **F** – Financing
> **F** – Risk Bearing
> **T** – Transportation
> **W** – Warehousing
> **I** – Information Provision
> **R** – Re-bulking (Bulk Breaking)
> **M** – Management Services
> **S** – Sampling & Demonstration Support

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following **bolded metrics** are board-exam favourites and appear regularly in KTU numerical/model questions:

- **Wholesaler Margin** (typically **5% – 25%** depending on product category)
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** — measured in **times per year**
- **Order Lead Time (OLT)** — measured in **days**
- **Perfect Order Fulfillment Rate** — measured in **percentage (%)**
- **On-Time Delivery (OTD) Rate** — measured in **percentage (%)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Wholesalers (Exhaustive)

Wholesalers are categorised by the **KTU 2024 syllabus** into the following hierarchical structure:

### 2.1.1 Merchant Wholesalers (Independently owned businesses)

**A. Full-Service Wholesalers**
- **Wholesale Merchants** — deal in a wide product range, take title to goods, perform all marketing-channel functions.
- **Industrial Distributors** — sell to manufacturers rather than retailers; provide specialised services like credit, technical advice.

**B. Limited-Service Wholesalers**
- **Cash-and-Carry Wholesalers** — sell to small retailers who pay cash and transport goods themselves.
- **Truck Wholesalers** — perform selling, delivery, and sometimes warehousing from their trucks.
- **Drop Shippers** — do not handle or carry inventory; forward orders to producers who ship directly to buyers.
- **Mail-Order Wholesalers** — sell via catalogues; primarily to rural small businesses.
- **Rack Jobbers** — own and stock the racks of retail stores; handle inventory replenishment (e.g., magazines, books, health products in supermarkets).

### 2.1.2 Brokers and Agents

- **Brokers** — bring buyers and sellers together; do not take title to goods; receive commission.
- **Agents** — negotiate and may represent a manufacturer on a permanent basis; also do not take title.
- **Commission Merchants** — take possession of goods and negotiate sale in foreign markets.

### 2.1.3 Manufacturers’ and Retailers’ Outlets

- **Manufacturers’ Sales Offices/Branches** — owned by the manufacturer; carry no inventory or limited inventory.
- **Retailers’ Cooperatives** — owned by member retailers; undertake buying and possibly wholesaling activities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Board Distinction**
> - **Brokers vs. Agents**: Brokers are usually *short-term* deal makers; Agents are *long-term representatives*.
> - **Drop Shippers vs. Cash-and-Carry**: Drop shippers *never* see the product; cash-and-carry requires the buyer to physically collect.

## 2.2 Functions of Wholesalers — Detailed "Why" and "How"

| Function | Why It Exists | How It Is Performed |
|---|---|---|
| **Selling & Promotion** | Manufacturers lack reach; wholesalers have local presence. | Sales force, trade shows, direct contact with retailers. |
| **Buying & Assortment Building** | Retailers want variety from one source. | Wholesalers select, mix, and assemble product lines. |
| **Bulk Breaking** | Producers ship truckloads; retailers buy small. | Wholesalers divide into smaller cases, packs, units. |
| **Warehousing** | Inventory must be held close to demand. | Centralised / regional warehouses. |
| **Transportation** | Goods must physically reach the retailer. | Wholesalers’ own fleet or third-party carriers. |
| **Financing** | Retailers and small manufacturers need credit. | Extended credit terms, supplier credit facilitation. |
| **Risk Bearing** | Wholesalers absorb risks of spoilage, obsolescence, theft. | They take *title* to the goods. |
| **Market Information** | Manufacturers need demand intelligence. | Wholesalers provide POS data, sales trends. |
| **Management Services** | Retailers need advice on store operations. | Training, accounting help, store layout advice. |

## 2.3 Logistics Management — Detailed Architecture

### 2.3.1 The Six Pillars of the Logistics System

1. **Transportation** — physical movement via road, rail, air, sea, pipeline, or intermodal.
2. **Warehousing** — storage of raw materials, WIP, and finished goods.
3. **Inventory Management** — balancing stock-out risk vs. carrying cost.
4. **Order Processing** — receiving, transmitting, picking, packing, and shipping orders.
5. **Materials Handling** — internal movement within warehouse/factory.
6. **Packaging** — protective, informative, and promotional functions.

### 2.3.2 Logistics vs. Supply Chain Management

> [!NOTE]
> **Logistics ⊂ Supply Chain** — Logistics is a *subset* of Supply Chain Management (SCM).

| Parameter | Logistics Management | Supply Chain Management |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Movement & storage of goods (one direction). | End-to-end flow from raw material supplier to end customer. |
| **Focus** | Operational — getting the product delivered. | Strategic — coordinating all partners. |
| **Key Decisions** | Transport mode, warehouse location, route planning. | Sourcing, partnerships, network design, demand forecasting. |
| **Direction of Flow** | Mostly forward (and reverse logistics). | Forward + Reverse + Information + Financial flows. |
| **Time Horizon** | Short to medium term. | Medium to long term. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Business Metrics)

| Metric | Formula | Unit | Typical KTU Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** | $\text{ITR} = \dfrac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | times/year | Measures wholesaler efficiency. Higher = better. |
| **Days Sales of Inventory (DSI)** | $\text{DSI} = \dfrac{365}{\text{ITR}}$ | days | How many days inventory lasts. |
| **Gross Margin Return on Investment (GMROI)** | $\text{GMROI} = \dfrac{\text{Gross Margin}}{\text{Average Inventory Cost}}$ | ratio | > 2.0 is considered good retail/wholesale. |
| **Order Lead Time (OLT)** | $\text{OLT} = \text{Order Processing Time} + \text{Production Time} + \text{Transport Time}$ | days | Critical for customer satisfaction. |
| **Total Logistics Cost (TLC)** | $\text{TLC} = C_{T} + C_{W} + C_{I} + C_{OP} + C_{MH} + C_{P} + C_{R}$ | ₹ / $ | Sum of all logistics sub-costs. |
| **Service Level (β)** | $\beta = \dfrac{\text{Orders Filled On-Time}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100$ | % | 95%+ is industry benchmark. |
| **Fill Rate** | $\text{Fill Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Units Shipped}}{\text{Units Ordered}} \times 100$ | % | Stockout indicator. |
| **Safety Stock (SS)** | $\text{SS} = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | units | Buffer against demand variability. |
| **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $\text{ROP} = (D \times L) + \text{SS}$ | units | When to place next order. |
| **Average Inventory (AI)** | $\text{AI} = \dfrac{Q}{2} + \text{SS}$ | units | For warehouse planning. |

Where the variables are:
- $C_{T}$ = Transportation cost
- $C_{W}$ = Warehousing cost
- $C_{I}$ = Inventory carrying cost
- $C_{OP}$ = Order processing cost
- $C_{MH}$ = Materials handling cost
- $C_{P}$ = Packaging cost
- $C_{R}$ = Return / Reverse logistics cost
- $Z$ = Z-score (service-level factor, e.g., 1.65 for 95%)
- $\sigma_{LT}$ = Standard deviation of lead time demand
- $L$ = Lead time
- $D$ = Average daily demand
- $Q$ = Order quantity (EOQ)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner’s Note**
> The vertical pipe `|` symbol is **strictly forbidden** inside markdown tables. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX to denote absolute value or conditional probability.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **FMCG (Fast-Moving Consumer Goods)** — Coca-Cola, Unilever, Nestlé — rely heavily on wholesalers for last-mile reach in rural India.
- **E-commerce Giants** (Amazon, Flipkart) — operate massive logistics networks including fulfilment centres, sort centres, last-mile delivery hubs.
- **Pharmaceuticals** — require *cold-chain logistics* (2–8°C).
- **Automotive** — *JIT (Just-in-Time)* delivery by tier-1 suppliers to assembly plants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Solved Numerical Model 1 — Inventory Turnover Analysis (KTU Style)

### Problem Statement
> *A wholesaler of FMCG goods reports an opening inventory of **₹ 8,00,000** and a closing inventory of **₹ 12,00,000**. The annual Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) is **₹ 1,00,00,000**. Calculate:*
> *(a) Average Inventory*
> *(b) Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)*
> *(c) Days Sales of Inventory (DSI)*

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify given parameters**

Opening inventory = ₹ 8,00,000
Closing inventory = ₹ 12,00,000
COGS = ₹ 1,00,00,000
Time period = 1 year (365 days)

**Step 2 — Calculate Average Inventory**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average Inventory (AI)} &= \dfrac{\text{Opening Inventory} + \text{Closing Inventory}}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{20{,}00{,}000}{2} \\
&= \text{₹ 10,00,000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ITR} &= \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}} \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}00{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 10 \ \text{times per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation** — The wholesaler’s entire stock is sold and replaced **10 times** in a year. For FMCG, an ITR of **8–12** is considered healthy.

**Step 4 — Calculate Days Sales of Inventory (DSI)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DSI} &= \dfrac{365}{\text{ITR}} \\
&= \dfrac{365}{10} \\
&= 36.5 \ \text{days}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation** — On average, goods stay in the wholesaler’s warehouse for about **36.5 days** before being sold.

---

## 3.2 Solved Numerical Model 2 — Reorder Point & Safety Stock

### Problem Statement
> *A retail warehouse handles an SKU with:*
> *Average daily demand (D) = **200 units** *
> *Lead time (L) = **6 days** *
> *Standard deviation of daily demand (σ) = **25 units** *
> *Required service level = **95%** (Z = 1.65)*
> *Compute:*
> *(a) Safety Stock (SS)*
> *(b) Reorder Point (ROP)*
> *(c) Average Inventory (if order quantity Q = 4,000 units)*

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Safety Stock (SS)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SS} &= Z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{L} \\
&= 1.65 \times 25 \times \sqrt{6} \\
&= 1.65 \times 25 \times 2.4495 \\
&\approx 101.04 \ \text{units}
\end{aligned}
$$

(We round up to **102 units** in practice to avoid stockouts.)

**Step 2 — Compute Reorder Point (ROP)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROP} &= (D \times L) + \text{SS} \\
&= (200 \times 6) + 102 \\
&= 1200 + 102 \\
&= 1302 \ \text{units}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation** — When inventory falls to **1,302 units**, a new order must be placed.

**Step 3 — Compute Average Inventory (AI)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AI} &= \dfrac{Q}{2} + \text{SS} \\
&= \dfrac{4000}{2} + 102 \\
&= 2000 + 102 \\
&= 2102 \ \text{units}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation** — The average stock holding in the warehouse will be **2,102 units**, balancing replenishment frequency and safety buffer.

---

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Model 3 — Total Logistics Cost (TLC)

### Problem Statement
> *A FMCG company in Kerala reports the following annual logistics costs:*
> *Transportation (Cₜ) = ₹ 25,00,000; Warehousing (C_w) = ₹ 15,00,000; Inventory Carrying (Cᵢ) = ₹ 10,00,000; Order Processing (Cₒₚ) = ₹ 5,00,000; Materials Handling (C_mh) = ₹ 3,00,000; Packaging (C_p) = ₹ 2,00,000; Reverse Logistics (C_r) = ₹ 2,50,000. The annual revenue is ₹ 3 Cr. Compute: (a) Total Logistics Cost (b) Logistics Cost as a % of Revenue.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TLC} &= C_{T} + C_{W} + C_{I} + C_{OP} + C_{MH} + C_{P} + C_{R} \\
&= 25{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 \\
&\quad + 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ 62,50,000} \\
\\
\text{Logistics \% of Revenue} &= \dfrac{\text{TLC}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{62{,}50{,}000}{3{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&\approx 2.083\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation** — Logistics consumes about **2.08%** of revenue. For FMCG, an industry benchmark of **3–5%** is typical; this firm is operationally efficient.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance Metrics

| Wholesaler Type | Inventory Carried? | Title to Goods? | Provides Credit? | Performs Delivery? | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wholesale Merchant | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | High |
| Cash-and-Carry | Yes | Yes | No | No | High |
| Drop Shipper | No | Yes (briefly) | No | No | Medium |
| Broker | No | No | No | No | Medium |
| Agent | No | No | No | No | Medium |
| Rack Jobber | Yes | Yes | Mixed | Yes | Low |
| Manufacturer’s Sales Branch | Sometimes | Yes | Yes | Yes | High |

> **Mnemonic for KTU Diagram Memory** — "**T**itle, **D**elivery, **C**redit, **I**nventory" — check these 4 to identify any wholesaler type.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
