- **Communication:** Multi-channel PR — press conferences, social media campaigns, TV ads featuring mothers.
- **Evaluation:** Sales recovered to 80% of pre-ban levels within 3 months; brand recall improved.

> The Maggi case demonstrates that RACE is not merely academic — it is a **battle-tested practical framework** for managing complex PR scenarios.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**"Publicity is the cheapest form of promotion but carries the highest risk." Critically analyze this statement with reference to:**
**(a)** The **types, advantages, and limitations** of publicity. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A **comparative analysis** of Public Relations, Publicity, and Advertising. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Answer:**

**(a) Types, Advantages, and Limitations of Publicity (7 Marks):**

**Definition (0.5 mark):** Publicity is the non-paid, non-personal stimulation of demand through editorial coverage in mass media.

**Types of Publicity (2.5 marks):**
1. **News Publicity** — Routine coverage of business events (e.g., quarterly results).
2. **Feature Publicity** — In-depth editorial articles on a topic.
3. **Product Publicity** — Coverage of new launches or upgrades.
4. **Financial Publicity** — Stock, M&A, dividend announcements.
5. **Crisis Publicity** — Damage-control communication during negative events.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **High Credibility** — News is trusted more than ads.
2. **Cost-Effectiveness** — No direct media buying cost.
3. **Wide Reach** — Mass media exposure.
4. **Reaches Ad-Avoiders** — Audiences who skip ads still consume news.
5. **Brand Image Enhancement** — Third-party endorsement effect.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Loss of Message Control** — Editors may reframe the story negatively.
2. **One-Shot Exposure** — News vanishes quickly; not repeated like ads.
3. **Negative Publicity Risk** — Crises can damage brand reputation.
4. **No Guaranteed Coverage** — Media may refuse to publish.
5. **Timing Uncertainty** — News may not align with product launch.

> *Critical Analysis:* Publicity is indeed the **cheapest per impression**, but its inability to control the message means that a single negative news article can wipe out millions of dollars of brand equity — making the **risk premium** very high.

---

**(b) Comparative Analysis of PR, Publicity, and Advertising (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Public Relations | Publicity | Advertising |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Wide management function | Subset of PR | Separate promotional tool |
| **Media Type** | Owned + Earned | Earned only | Paid only |
| **Cost** | Moderate (staff + programs) | Very low (no direct payment) | High (paid media) |
| **Control** | High | Low | Complete |
| **Credibility** | High | Very high | Lower |
| **Duration** | Continuous, long-term | Short-term, event-driven | Campaign-based |
| **Direction** | Two-way | One-way | One-way |
| **Objective** | Mutual understanding + goodwill | Awareness + news coverage | Persuasion + sales |
| **Example** | Tata CSR programs | Maggi re-launch news coverage | Apple iPhone launch TV ad |

**Analysis (2 marks for written interpretation):**

- **Public Relations** is the **broadest** of the three — a philosophy and management function.
- **Publicity** is a **specific tactical tool** within PR that leverages earned media.
- **Advertising** is a **parallel paid function** focused on persuasion and direct sales conversion.

> All three are **complementary**, not substitutes. A balanced marketing communication strategy uses PR for image, publicity for credibility, and advertising for persuasion.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks in Module 3:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PR with Publicity** — PR is the *function*, Publicity is the *tool*. Writing "Publicity is the same as PR" loses **2 marks** instantly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting "Earned vs Paid"** — Any comparison answer that does NOT explicitly mention that publicity is **earned (non-paid)** and advertising is **paid** is considered incomplete.
>
> 3. **No Real-Life Example in 14-mark answers** — KTU 2024 OBE pattern **rewards application-level answers**. Without an Indian brand example (Maggi, Tata, Amul, Infosys), a 14-mark answer caps at **10 marks maximum**.
>
> 4. **Skipping the RACE acronym explanation** — Students write "RACE model was used" without explaining all four steps. **Always write R, A, C, E in full and apply each to the case.**
>
> 5. **No Diagrammatic Representation** — Even a simple flowchart in a 14-mark answer earns **1–2 extra marks** in KTU 2024 Scheme valuations.
>
> 6. **Numerical Mistakes in AVE/ROI** — Students forget to multiply by 100 for percentage answers. Always show the **formula → substitution → result** triad.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Public Relations and Publicity**

### 🎯 Must-Know Definitions
- **PR** → Management function for mutual understanding + goodwill.
- **Publicity** → Non-paid earned media coverage.
- **Advertising** → Paid media persuasion.

### 🎯 Must-Know Framework
- **RACE** → Research, Action, Communication, Evaluation.
- **Press Release Structure** → Inverted pyramid: Headline → Dateline → Lead → Body → Boilerplate → Contact.

### 🎯 Must-Know Comparisons
- PR vs Publicity vs Advertising (Table in Section 2.5).
- Advantages vs Limitations of Publicity (Section 2.6).

### 🎯 Must-Know Publics/Stakeholders
- Customers, Employees, Investors, Government, Media, Community, Suppliers, General Public.

### 🎯 Must-Know Formulae
- AVE = Column cm × Rate per cm.
- SOV = (Brand Mentions ÷ Total Mentions) × 100.
- CPI = Total PR Spend ÷ Total Impressions.
- PR ROI = [(Output Value − Cost) ÷ Cost] × 100.

### 🎯 Must-Know Real-Life Examples (for 14-mark answers)
- Maggi Re-launch (Nestlé) → RACE model.
- Tata CSR & Taj Crisis → PR + Crisis Communication.
- Apple iPhone Launch → Integrated PR + Publicity + Advertising.
- Infosys Quarterly Results → Investor PR.
- Samsung Galaxy Note 7 → Crisis PR + Damage Control.

### 🎯 Frequently Asked 3-Mark Topics
1. Define PR / Define Publicity.
2. Tools of PR (list any 4).
3. PR vs Advertising (3-point difference).
4. Advantages of Publicity.
5. RACE acronym expansion.

### 🎯 Frequently Asked 14-Mark Topics
1. **Role of PR as strategic management function** + RACE model application.
2. **Publicity types, advantages, limitations** + comparison with PR & Advertising.
3. **Crisis Communication** through PR — case study-based.
4. **PR for specific stakeholders** (investors, employees, government).

> **Final Tip:** In KTU 2024 ESE, write **structured, point-wise answers** with **headings, tables, and at least one diagram** for every 14-mark question. OBE-based valuation rewards **clarity + application + structure**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Understanding Industrial and Consumer Buyer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Understanding Industrial and Consumer Buyer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Understanding the Industrial Buyer (Organizational Buyer)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> An **Industrial Buyer** (also called an **Organizational Buyer** or **Business-to-Business (B2B) Buyer**) is an individual or a group of representatives within a formal organization who is authorized to select suppliers, negotiate contracts, and purchase goods, services, or raw materials required for the organization's operational, production, resale, or administrative functions.

In the context of KTU's Marketing Management framework, industrial buying behavior refers to the **decision-making process** by which formal establishments (firms, factories, hospitals, government bodies, NGOs) establish the need for purchased products and choose, evaluate, and purchase from multiple brands, vendors, or suppliers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identity of the Industrial Buyer:**
> - The buyer is **not the final consumer** — they are an *agent* of the organization.
> - Decisions are governed by **rational, economic, and technical criteria** rather than emotional impulses.
> - Purchases are made in **large volumes** and often on **long-term contracts**.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a household kitchen where the **family head** (industrial buyer) goes to the wholesale market to buy **50 kg of rice, 20 liters of oil, and spices in bulk** for the whole family. He doesn't buy based on a catchy advertisement or a pretty packaging; instead, he checks the **price per kg, quality grade, supplier reputation, delivery schedule, and credit terms**. The family members (end consumers) will eventually eat the rice, but they don't make the purchase decision.

In the same way, a **Purchase Manager in a manufacturing plant** buying thousands of tonnes of steel does not act on a whim — he acts on **specifications, supplier reliability, total cost of ownership, and after-sales service**.

---

### 1.2 Understanding the Consumer Buyer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Consumer Buyer** is an individual or a household unit who purchases goods and services for **personal, family, or household use** — that is, for **final consumption** rather than for resale, business operations, or profit generation. Their behavior is the sum total of the psychological, social, and cultural factors that influence the decision to select, buy, use, and dispose of products.

This is the domain of **Business-to-Consumer (B2C) marketing**, where the buyer is the same person who uses the product.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture yourself walking through a shopping mall. You suddenly stop at an ice-cream parlor because:
1. You feel **hot** (physical/psychological trigger),
2. A **friend** recommends the brand (social factor),
3. You grew up eating that flavor (cultural influence), and
4. The advertisement you saw yesterday is still fresh in your mind (marketing influence).

You buy the ice-cream **for yourself**, **right now**, **in small quantity**, and the **decision is emotional and impulsive**. That is **consumer buying behavior** — a personal, low-involvement (or sometimes high-involvement), emotion-driven act.

---

### 1.3 Why the Distinction Matters in Marketing

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:**
> Marketing strategy MUST be customized because:
> - **Consumer markets** respond to **emotions, branding, advertising, lifestyle positioning**.
> - **Industrial markets** respond to **logic, ROI, technical specifications, supplier credibility, and relationship management**.

| Dimension | Consumer Buyer | Industrial Buyer |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose of Purchase** | Personal/household use | Resale, production, operations |
| **Decision Maker** | Individual / family | Buying center (group) |
| **Volume** | Small | Large |
| **Frequency** | High (repeat) | Low (contractual) |
| **Emotionality** | High | Low |
| **Expertise Required** | Minimal | Technical/Specialized |
| **Negotiation** | Rare | Frequent |
| **Risk** | Low personal risk | High organizational risk |

---

### 1.4 The Standard KTU 2024 Classification Framework

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEIET752**, the buyer universe is broadly classified into two streams:

1. **The Consumer Market** — Buyers who purchase for **personal consumption**.
2. **The Industrial / Organizational Market** — Buyers who purchase for **business use**, including producers, resellers, governments, and institutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 Outcome):**
> The learner will be able to **CO4: Analyze the behavioral patterns of consumer and industrial buyers and apply this understanding to design effective marketing strategies.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Consumer Buying Decision Process (Engel-Blackwell-Miniard Model)

The **Engel-Kollat-Blackwell (EKB) Model** is the most widely accepted framework in KTU 2024 syllabus for understanding consumer buying behavior. It consists of **five sequential stages**:

1. **Problem/Need Recognition** — The consumer identifies a gap between the desired state and the actual state.
   - *Example:* Feeling thirsty (need) → buying a soft drink (recognition).

2. **Information Search** — The consumer seeks internal (memory) and external (advertisements, peers, reviews) information.
   - *Example:* Asking friends about the best mobile phone under ₹20,000.

3. **Evaluation of Alternatives** — The consumer compares brands based on attributes like price, quality, features, and brand reputation.
   - *Example:* Comparing Samsung vs. OnePlus vs. Xiaomi.

4. **Purchase Decision** — The consumer chooses the brand and completes the transaction. However, two factors can intervene:
   - **Attitude of others** (peer pressure).
   - **Unexpected situational factors** (e.g., stock-out, financial emergency).

5. **Post-Purchase Behavior** — The consumer evaluates satisfaction or dissatisfaction after using the product.
   - This leads to either **Cognitive Dissonance** (buyer's remorse) or **Repeat Purchase Loyalty**.

---

### 2.2 The Industrial Buying Process (Robinson-Faris-Wind Model — Buygrid)

For industrial markets, the **Buygrid Framework** outlines **eight stages** that buying centers traverse:

1. **Problem Recognition** — A need arises (e.g., machine breakdown, new project).
2. **General Need Description** — Specifying the requirements.
3. **Product Specification** — Defining technical and quality parameters.
4. **Supplier Search** — Identifying potential vendors.
5. **Proposal Solicitation** — Requesting quotations and bids.
6. **Supplier Selection** — Evaluating and selecting vendors based on criteria.
7. **Order-Routine Specification** — Finalizing terms, quantity, delivery, and payment.
8. **Performance Review** — Post-purchase evaluation of the supplier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** The first three stages are called the **"Decision-Making Phase"**, stages 4–7 are the **"Supplier-Interaction Phase"**, and stage 8 is the **"Performance Evaluation Phase."**

---

### 2.3 Buygrid Framework — Three Buying Situations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Framework (Robinson-Faris-Wind):**
> Industrial buying situations are categorized into three types based on the novelty of the purchase:

| Situation | Description | Marketer's Task | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Straight Rebuy** | Routine, repetitive purchase from the same supplier | Maintain quality & reliability | **Low** |
| **Modified Rebuy** | Existing purchase with modifications in specs/price/quantity | Strong negotiation skills | **Medium** |
| **New Task** | First-time purchase of a complex product | Heavy pre-sale technical support | **High** |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Key Equations & Frameworks

> [!NOTE]
> **For Marketing Management (PEIET752), the "formulas" are conceptual models rather than mathematical equations. Below is the consolidated KTU 2024 framework cheat sheet.**

| Concept | Equation / Framework | Variables / Components | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Buyer Behavior Equation** | $BB = f(C, S, P, Ps)$ | $BB$ = Buying Behavior, $C$ = Cultural, $S$ = Social, $P$ = Personal, $Ps$ = Psychological | Predicts consumer responses |
| **Perceived Value** | $PV = \dfrac{PB}{PC}$ | $PV$ = Perceived Value, $PB$ = Perceived Benefits, $PC$ = Perceived Cost | Determines purchase intent |
| **Customer Satisfaction Index** | $CSI = \dfrac{(Expectations - Performance)}{Expectations} \times 100$ | Negative = Satisfaction gap | Post-purchase analysis |
| **Buygrid Phases** | $B = D + SI + PE$ | $B$ = Buygrid process, $D$ = Decision phase, $SI$ = Supplier interaction, $PE$ = Performance evaluation | Industrial buying structure |
| **Total Cost of Ownership** | $TCO = C_p + C_o + C_m + C_d$ | $C_p$ = Purchase cost, $C_o$ = Operating cost, $C_m$ = Maintenance, $C_d$ = Disposal cost | Industrial procurement |
| **Buying Center Influence Weight** | $W_i = \dfrac{P_i \times R_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n}(P_j \times R_j)}$ | $W_i$ = Weight of member $i$, $P_i$ = Power, $R_i$ = Relevance | Industrial decision mapping |
| **Customer Lifetime Value** | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(Revenue_t - Cost_t)}{(1 + r)^t}$ | $r$ = Discount rate, $T$ = Time horizon | Long-term buyer value |

---

### 2.5 The Four Major Factors Influencing Consumer Behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mnemonic — "CSPP" (Cultural, Social, Personal, Psychological)**

1. **Cultural Factors** — Culture, subculture, social class. (Most fundamental influencer.)
2. **Social Factors** — Reference groups, family, roles & status.
3. **Personal Factors** — Age, lifecycle stage, occupation, lifestyle, economic condition.
4. **Psychological Factors** — Motivation, perception, learning, beliefs & attitudes.

### 2.6 The Buying Center (Industrial Buying Roles)

In industrial buying, decisions are NOT made by a single person. They involve a **Buying Center** — a group of individuals playing distinct roles:

| Role | Function | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Initiator** | Identifies the need | Production Manager |
| **Influencer** | Provides technical input | R&D Engineer |
| **Decider** | Final approval authority | Plant Director |
| **Buyer** | Handles the transaction | Purchase Officer |
| **User** | Actually uses the product | Machine Operator on shop floor |
| **Gatekeeper** | Controls information flow | Receptionist / Secretary |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Perceived Value & Customer Satisfaction Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these derivations matter for KTU 2024:**
> The KTU valuation panel expects students to show **theoretical understanding** by deriving the perceived value and satisfaction index from first principles, not just stating them.

#### Derivation 1: Perceived Value Equation

We begin with the basic premise that consumer purchase intent is driven by a **cost-benefit tradeoff**.

**Step 1:** Define the total perceived benefit a consumer receives.
$$PB = B_f + B_p + B_e$$
where $B_f$ = functional benefit, $B_p$ = psychological benefit, $B_e$ = economic benefit.

**Step 2:** Define the total perceived cost incurred.
$$PC = C_m + C_t + C_s + C_r$$
where $C_m$ = monetary cost, $C_t$ = time cost, $C_s$ = search cost, $C_r$ = risk cost.

**Step 3:** Formulate the perceived value as a ratio.
$$PV = \frac{PB}{PC}$$

**Step 4:** For the purchase to occur, perceived value must exceed a critical threshold.
$$PV \geq PV_{threshold} \Rightarrow \text{Purchase occurs}$$
Otherwise, the consumer abandons the transaction.

**Interpretation:** A consumer will buy a product only when the **benefits they perceive** significantly outweigh the **costs they incur**. This is the cornerstone of B2C marketing.

---

#### Derivation 2: Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)

**Step 1:** Define consumer expectation before purchase.
$$E = \{E_1, E_2, E_3, \dots, E_n\}$$
where each $E_i$ represents expectation on a specific attribute (price, quality, durability).

**Step 2:** Define perceived performance after use.
$$P = \{P_1, P_2, P_3, \dots, P_n\}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the gap on each attribute.
$$G_i = P_i - E_i$$

**Step 4:** Aggregate the gap into a Customer Satisfaction Index.
$$CSI = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{P_i - E_i}{E_i}\right) \times 100\%$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the result.
- If $CSI > 0$ → **High Satisfaction** (delight).
- If $CSI = 0$ → **Just Satisfied** (met expectations).
- If $CSI < 0$ → **Dissatisfaction** (cognitive dissonance → negative word-of-mouth).

---

#### Derivation 3: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) for Industrial Buyer

For an industrial buyer, the **Total Cost of Ownership** is a critical evaluation criterion.

**Step 1:** Identify cost categories over the asset's life cycle.
$$TCO = C_p + C_i + C_o + C_m + C_d + C_s$$
where $C_p$ = purchase price, $C_i$ = installation cost, $C_o$ = operating cost, $C_m$ = maintenance cost, $C_d$ = downtime cost, $C_s$ = disposal/salvage cost.

**Step 2:** Net out the salvage value (negative term).
$$TCO_{net} = C_p + C_i + C_o + C_m + C_d - C_s$$

**Step 3:** Annualize for comparison purposes.
$$TCO_{annual} = \frac{TCO_{net}}{L}$$
where $L$ = useful life in years.

**Interpretation:** Industrial buyers often select a higher-priced supplier if the **TCO** is lower due to better fuel efficiency, lower maintenance, and longer life.

---

#### Derivation 4: Buying Center Decision Weight

**Step 1:** Define the buying center with $n$ members.
$$\text{BC} = \{m_1, m_2, m_3, \dots, m_n\}$$

**Step 2:** For each member, assign two scores:
- **Power ($P_i$):** Authority to influence decision (1–10 scale).
- **Relevance ($R_i$):** Functional involvement (1–10 scale).

**Step 3:** Compute the relative decision weight of each member.
$$W_i = \frac{P_i \times R_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n}(P_j \times R_j)}$$

**Step 4:** Verify that weights sum to unity.
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i = 1$$

**Interpretation:** The marketer must identify high-weight members and tailor the marketing message accordingly. For instance, an R&D engineer may have high relevance but low power, while a CEO has high power but low relevance — both must be addressed differently.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Consumer vs. Industrial Buying Behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner-Expected Comparison Table**

| Parameter | Consumer Buying | Industrial Buying |
|---|---|---|
| **Market Type** | B2C | B2B |
| **Decision Unit** | Individual / Family | Buying Center |
| **Buyer Type** | End consumer | Agent / Proxy |
| **Decision Time** | Short | Long |
| **Volume per Order** | Small | Large |
| **Order Frequency** | High | Low |
| **Emotional Influence** | High | Low |
| **Rational Influence** | Moderate | Very High |
| **Price Sensitivity** | Low-Moderate | High |
| **Quality Sensitivity** | Moderate | Very High |
| **Supplier Relationship** | Transactional | Long-term Partnership |
| **Negotiation** | Rare | Continuous |
| **Reciprocity** | None | Common |
| **Leasing Option** | Rare | Common |
| **Demand Derived From** | Personal wants | Derived demand (final consumer demand) |
| **Number of Buyers** | Mass market | Few / Concentrated |
| **Geographic Concentration** | Dispersed | Concentrated (industrial clusters) |
| **Technical Assistance** | Minimal | Critical |

---

### 3.3 Stage-by-Stage Buyer Decision Process Matrix

| Stage | Consumer Behavior | Industrial Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Need recognition (internal stimulus) | Problem recognition (production gap) |
| 2 | Information search (online, peers) | General need description (specs) |
| 3 | Evaluation of alternatives | Product specification |
| 4 | Purchase decision | Supplier search |
| 5 | Post-purchase evaluation | Proposal solicitation |
| 6 | Repeat / Discontinue | Supplier selection |
| 7 | Brand loyalty / switch | Order-routine specification |
| 8 | — | Performance review |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Consumer Buying Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Need Recognition] --> nodeB[Information Search]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Evaluation of Alternatives]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Purchase Decision}
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeE[Purchase Made]
    nodeD -->|No - Interrupted| nodeF[Postponement or Abandonment]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Post Purchase Behavior]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Satisfied}
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI[Brand Loyalty and Repeat Purchase]
    nodeH -->|No| nodeJ[Cognitive Dissonance and Negative WOM]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Long Term Customer Relationship]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeF --> nodeA
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Industrial Buying Process (Buygrid Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Problem Recognition] --> s2[General Need Description]
    s2 --> s3[Product Specification]
    s3 --> s4[Supplier Search]
    s4 --> s5[Proposal Solicitation]
    s5 --> s6[Supplier Selection]
    s6 --> s7[Order Routine Specification]
    s7 --> s8[Performance Review]
    s8 -->|Satisfactory| s9[Straight Rebuy Next Time]
    s8 -->|Unsatisfactory| s10[Modified Rebuy or New Supplier]
    s10 --> s1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Buying Center Roles and Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    initiator[Initiator - Identifies Need] --> influencer[Influencer - Provides Technical Advice]
    influencer --> decider[Decider - Final Authority]
    decider --> buyer[Buyer - Handles Transaction]
    buyer --> user[User - Consumes Product]
    gatekeeper[Gatekeeper - Controls Info Flow] --> decider
    gatekeeper --> buyer
    initiator -.-> gatekeeper
    user -.-> feedback[Feedback Loop]
    feedback --> decider
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Four Major Factors Influencing Consumer Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cb[Consumer Buying Behavior]
    cb --> A[Cultural Factors]
    cb --> B[Social Factors]
    cb --> C[Personal Factors]
    cb --> D[Psychological Factors]
    A --> A1[Culture]
    A --> A2[Subculture]
    A --> A3[Social Class]
    B --> B1[Reference Groups]
    B --> B2[Family]
    B --> B3[Roles and Status]
    C --> C1[Age and Life Cycle]
    C --> C2[Occupation]
    C --> C3[Lifestyle]
    C --> C4[Economic Condition]
    D --> D1[Motivation]
    D --> D2[Perception]
    D --> D3[Learning]
    D --> D4[Beliefs and Attitudes]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Three Buying Situations (Buyclass Framework)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BS[Industrial Buying Situation]
    BS --> SR[Straight Rebuy]
    BS --> MR[Modified Rebuy]
    BS --> NT[New Task]
    SR --> SRD[Low Risk, Routine, Same Supplier]
    MR --> MRD[Medium Risk, Changed Specs, May Renegotiate]
    NT --> NTD[High Risk, Complex, Multiple Vendors Evaluated]
```

### 4.6 Mermaid Block Architecture: Marketing Strategy Mapping Based on Buyer Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MKT[Marketing Strategy Engine]
    MKT --> CC[Consumer Channel]
    MKT --> IC[Industrial Channel]
    CC --> CC1[Mass Advertising]
    CC --> CC2[Social Media Campaigns]
    CC --> CC3[Emotional Branding]
    CC --> CC4[Discount and Loyalty Programs]
    IC --> IC1[Personal Selling]
    IC --> IC2[Technical Demos and Trials]
    IC --> IC3[Rational ROI Pitching]
    IC --> IC4[Long Term Contracts and SLAs]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Industrial Buyer." How is it different from a Consumer Buyer?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Industrial Buyer** is an individual or a group within a formal organization who is authorized to purchase goods, services, or raw materials for the organization's operations, production, or resale needs.

**Key Differences from Consumer Buyer:**
1. **Purpose:** Industrial buyer purchases for business use; consumer buyer purchases for personal/household consumption.
2. **Decision Unit:** Industrial buying is done by a *buying center* (multiple roles); consumer buying is typically done by an individual.
3. **Rationality:** Industrial buying is driven by technical and economic criteria; consumer buying is influenced by emotional and social factors.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the various roles in an industrial buying center.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Buying Center** in industrial buying comprises the following six roles:

1. **Initiator** — Recognizes the need for purchase.
2. **Influencer** — Provides technical or functional input.
3. **Decider** — Holds final authority to approve the purchase.
4. **Buyer** — Executes the procurement transaction.
5. **User** — Actually consumes or operates the purchased product.
6. **Gatekeeper** — Controls the flow of information (e.g., secretary or receptionist).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Engel-Kollat-Blackwell (EKB) Model** of consumer buying behavior in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **four major factors** that influence consumer buying behavior. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A:**

#### (a) Engel-Kollat-Blackwell (EKB) Model — 7 Marks

The **EKB Model**, developed by Engel, Kollat, and Blackwell, is a comprehensive framework that explains the **consumer decision-making process**. It consists of **five stages**:

**Stage 1 — Need/Problem Recognition:**
The buying process begins when the consumer perceives a significant gap between the **desired state** and the **actual state**.
- *Trigger Sources:* Internal (hunger, thirst) or External (advertisement, peer discussion).
- *Example:* Seeing a cracked smartphone screen triggers recognition of the need for a new phone.

**Stage 2 — Information Search:**
The consumer seeks information from two sources:
- **Internal Search:** Memory and past experiences.
- **External Search:** Friends, family, online reviews, advertisements, retail visits.

The intensity of the search depends on the **involvement level** and **perceived risk** of the purchase.

**Stage 3 — Evaluation of Alternatives:**
The consumer evaluates available brands based on attributes such as **price, quality, features, brand reputation, and after-sales service**. The **Multi-Attribute Attitude Model** is often used, where:
$$A_{brand} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(W_i \times B_i)$$
Here, $A_{brand}$ is the attitude toward the brand, $W_i$ is the importance weight of attribute $i$, and $B_i$ is the belief about the brand on attribute $i$.

**Stage 4 — Purchase Decision:**
After evaluation, the consumer selects the brand and completes the transaction. However, two factors may intervene:
- **Attitude of Others** — Peer or family disapproval can cause reconsideration.
- **Unexpected Situational Factors** — Stock-out, price hike, or financial emergency can alter the decision.

**Stage 5 — Post-Purchase Behavior:**
The consumer evaluates satisfaction after using the product. This leads to:
- **Satisfaction** → Repeat purchase, brand loyalty, positive WOM.
- **Dissatisfaction** → Cognitive dissonance, complaints, negative WOM, brand switching.

**Conclusion:** The EKB model is a **continuous process**, where post-purchase evaluation feeds back into the next purchase cycle.

---

#### (b) Four Major Factors Influencing Consumer Behavior — 7 Marks

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic: "CSPP" — Cultural, Social, Personal, Psychological.**

**1. Cultural Factors (Most Fundamental):**
- **Culture:** The set of basic values, perceptions, wants, and behaviors learned by a member of society.
- **Subculture:** Smaller groups within a culture (e.g., religions, nationalities, regional groups).
- **Social Class:** Relatively permanent and ordered divisions of society whose members share similar values, interests, and behaviors.

**2. Social Factors:**
- **Reference Groups:** Groups that influence attitudes and behaviors (e.g., friends, colleagues, social media influencers).
- **Family:** The most influential primary reference group. Two types — *Family of Orientation* (parents/siblings) and *Family of Procreation* (spouse/children).
- **Roles and Status:** A person's position in a group (e.g., manager, father) influences consumption patterns.

**3. Personal Factors:**
- **Age and Life-Cycle Stage:** Consumption changes with age.
- **Occupation:** A doctor's tools vs. a farmer's tools.
- **Economic Situation:** Disposable income determines purchasing power.
- **Lifestyle:** A person's pattern of living expressed through activities, interests, and opinions (AIOs).

**4. Psychological Factors:**
- **Motivation:** Needs drive behavior. (Maslow's Hierarchy applies here.)
- **Perception:** A person's process of selecting, organizing, and interpreting information.
- **Learning:** Changes in behavior arising from experience.
- **Beliefs and Attitudes:** Persistent cognitive evaluations that shape brand preferences.

**Conclusion:** Marketers must holistically analyze all four factors to design targeted and effective marketing strategies.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, May 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **Robinson-Faris-Wind Buygrid Model** of industrial buying behavior. Explain the **three types of buying situations**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **consumer buying behavior** with **industrial buying behavior** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B:**

#### (a) Robinson-Faris-Wind Buygrid Model — 7 Marks

The **Buygrid Model** is the most widely accepted framework for understanding **industrial buying behavior**. It outlines **eight sequential stages**:

1. **Problem Recognition** — A need arises due to internal (machine breakdown) or external (new regulation) triggers.
2. **General Need Description** — The buying center defines the problem in detail.
3. **Product Specification** — Technical specifications and quality parameters are formalized.
4. **Supplier Search** — Potential vendors are identified through directories, trade shows, and referrals.
5. **Proposal Solicitation** — Requests for Quotations (RFQs) and Requests for Proposals (RFPs) are issued.
6. **Supplier Selection** — Vendors are evaluated on price, quality, delivery, technical capability, and warranty.
7. **Order-Routine Specification** — Final order with detailed terms on quantity, delivery, payment, and service.
8. **Performance Review** — Post-purchase evaluation determines whether to continue, modify, or terminate the relationship.

**Three Types of Buying Situations:**

| Situation | Description | Marketer's Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Straight Rebuy** | Routine reorder from the same supplier | Maintain quality & reliability; avoid complacency |
| **Modified Rebuy** | Existing purchase with changes in price/specs/volume | Offer value-added services, renegotiate terms |
| **New Task** | First-time purchase | Heavy technical support, demos, samples, and consultative selling |

---

#### (b) Comparison: Consumer vs. Industrial Buying Behavior — 7 Marks

| Dimension | Consumer Buying | Industrial Buying |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Personal, emotional | Rational, organizational |
| **Decision Unit** | Individual | Buying Center (group) |
| **Volume** | Small | Large |
| **Frequency** | High | Low |
| **Risk** | Low personal risk | High organizational risk |
| **Relationship** | Transactional | Long-term partnership |
| **Negotiation** | Rare | Common and intensive |
| **Demand Type** | Direct demand | Derived demand |
| **Promotion Focus** | Mass advertising | Personal selling |
| **Geographic Spread** | Dispersed | Concentrated |
| **Reciprocity** | Absent | Often present |

**Conclusion:** Industrial buyers are driven by logic, ROI, and supplier reliability, whereas consumer buyers are influenced by emotional, social, and cultural cues.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse *Industrial Marketing* with *Marketing to Industrial Workers*. Industrial marketing means B2B, not employee-targeted.
> 2. **Do NOT** state only the names of buying center roles; explain the **function of each role** for full marks.
> 3. **Do NOT** mix up *Derived Demand* with *Joint Demand*. Industrial demand is *derived*; *joint* demand refers to complementary products.
> 4. **Do NOT** write consumer behavior stages without explaining the **feedback loop** from post-purchase to next purchase cycle.
> 5. **Avoid** generic statements like "branding is important" — instead, **justify with a real-world example**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Industrial & Consumer Buyer**

- **Consumer Buyer** = purchases for personal use; emotional; B2C.
- **Industrial Buyer** = purchases for organizational use; rational; B2B.
- **EKB Model** has **5 stages**: Need → Information Search → Evaluation → Purchase → Post-Purchase.
- **Buygrid Model** has **8 stages** for industrial buying.
- **Three Buying Situations**: Straight Rebuy, Modified Rebuy, New Task.
- **Buying Center Roles (6)**: Initiator, Influencer, Decider, Buyer, User, Gatekeeper.
- **Four Influencing Factors (CSPP)**: Cultural, Social, Personal, Psychological.
- **Derived Demand**: Industrial demand originates from final consumer demand.
- **Perceived Value Equation**: $PV = PB \mid PC$.
- **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** is the industrial buyer's true evaluation metric.
- **Customer Satisfaction Index** uses $CSI = (P_i - E_i) \mid E_i$ formula.
- **Buying Center Influence Weight**: $W_i = (P_i \times R_i) \mid \sum(P_j \times R_j)$.
- **Marketing Mix Implication**: Consumer marketing uses mass advertising; industrial marketing uses personal selling.
- **Reciprocity**: Industrial buyers often practice reciprocal buying (you buy from me, I buy from you).
- **Cognitive Dissonance**: Post-purchase doubt — a critical concept in KTU 2024 evaluations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Influencing Buyer Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factors Influencing Buyer Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Buyer Behaviour (Consumer Behaviour)** is the systematic study of the **selection, purchase, use, and disposal** of goods, services, ideas, or experiences by individuals, groups, or organizations to satisfy needs and wants. In the context of the KTU 2024 Marketing Management syllabus, it is broadly classified into:

- **Consumer Buying Behaviour** – decisions made by individuals/households for personal use.
- **Organizational (Business) Buying Behaviour** – decisions made by firms, government bodies, or institutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Standardized):**
> *"Buyer Behaviour is a multidisciplinary field that examines how the interplay of **cultural, social, personal, and psychological** factors shapes the decision-making process of a buyer, leading to the exchange of value."*

The four-levelled model originally propagated in modern marketing literature (Kotler & Keller framework) is universally followed in the KTU valuation scheme. The influence hierarchy is presented below in descending order of *depth-of-impact*:

$$\text{Buyer Behaviour} = f\big(C_{c},\, C_{s},\, C_{p},\, C_{psi}\big)$$

Where:
- $C_{c}$ = **Cultural factors**
- $C_{s}$ = **Social factors**
- $C_{p}$ = **Personal factors**
- $C_{psi}$ = **Psychological factors**

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smartphone Purchase" Story

Imagine **Ananya**, a 22-year-old B.Tech student in Kochi, planning to buy a new smartphone. Her decision is **not** purely rational; it is the sum of many invisible forces:

| Force Layer | What Ananya Feels / Does |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Cultural** | "My family values brand reputation. We usually buy from reputed brands." |
| **Social** | "My roommate just bought a Pixel. My college WhatsApp group is full of iPhone reviews." |
| **Personal** | "I'm a student, my budget is ₹30,000. I want a phone that lasts through my engineering years." |
| **Psychological** | "I saw an Instagram ad with a sleek design → it triggered motivation → I learned that the battery is great → I now *believe* this is the best choice." |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Buyer behaviour is the **invisible engine** behind every transaction. Marketers don't sell products — they sell *solutions shaped by these four forces*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Questions on this topic almost always test the **classification into the four factors** and the **buyer decision process stages**. Memorize the four categories *in order* — the examiner awards 1 mark for proper sequencing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Kotler Four-Factor Model — Logical Architecture

The factors influencing buyer behaviour are best understood as **four concentric layers**, with the deepest layer (Culture) having the **broadest and most fundamental influence**, while the surface layer (Psychology) acts on an **immediate, situational** basis.

#### 2.1.1 Cultural Factors (C_c)
These are the **most fundamental** determinants of a person's wants and behaviour. Every individual grows up in a culture that shapes their basic values, perceptions, preferences, and behaviours.

**Sub-components:**
- **Culture** — the set of basic values, perceptions, wants, and behaviours learned from family and other key institutions (school, religion).
- **Subculture** — smaller groups within a culture that share belief systems based on common life experiences (nationality, religion, racial group, geographic region).
- **Social Class** — relatively permanent and ordered divisions of society whose members share similar values, interests, and behaviours (upper, middle, lower tier).

#### 2.1.2 Social Factors (C_s)
These are forces exerted by **other people** in the buyer's environment — the social groups, family, and roles that shape consumption.

**Sub-components:**
- **Reference Groups** — groups that have a direct (face-to-face) or indirect influence on attitudes/behaviour (membership groups, aspirational groups, dissociative groups).
- **Family** — the *most influential* consumer-buying organization in society. Two families are recognized: **Family of Orientation** (parents/siblings) and **Family of Procreation** (spouse/children).
- **Roles and Status** — a person occupies different positions (role) in various groups, each carrying a status that influences consumption.

#### 2.1.3 Personal Factors (C_p)
These are **unique to the individual** and represent how personal characteristics affect buying behaviour.

**Sub-components:**
- **Age & Life-Cycle Stage**
- **Occupation**
- **Economic Situation**
- **Lifestyle** (AIO statements – Activities, Interests, Opinions)
- **Personality & Self-Concept** (e.g., self-confidence, adaptability, ambition)

#### 2.1.4 Psychological Factors (C_psi)
These are the **internal, mental processes** that influence how consumers respond to their environment.

**Sub-components (the famous "Four P's of Psychology"):**
- **Motivation** — driven by Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs
- **Perception** — the process of selecting, organizing, and interpreting information
- **Learning** — changes in behaviour arising from experience
- **Beliefs and Attitudes** — descriptive thoughts and consistent evaluations

### 2.2 The Buyer Decision Process (5-Stage Model)

The end-output of all four factors is reflected in a five-stage decision process:

$$\text{NR} \rightarrow \text{IS} \rightarrow \text{EoA} \rightarrow \text{PD} \rightarrow \text{PPB}$$

| Stage | Full Form | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. NR | Need Recognition | Buyer recognizes a problem or need |
| 2. IS | Information Search | Buyer searches for information (internal/external) |
| 3. EoA | Evaluation of Alternatives | Buyer evaluates options using criteria |
| 4. PD | Purchase Decision | Buyer decides to buy a particular product |
| 5. PPB | Post-Purchase Behaviour | Buyer evaluates satisfaction → cognitive dissonance possible |

### 2.3 The S-O-R Model (Howard-Sheth & Pavlovian Framework)

The behavioural response of a buyer is fundamentally a **stimulus-response** mechanism:

$$\text{Stimulus} \rightarrow \text{Organism} \rightarrow \text{Response}$$

For a marketing context:

$$S_{M} + S_{E} \rightarrow O \rightarrow R$$

Where:
- $S_{M}$ = **Marketing Stimuli** (Product, Price, Place, Promotion — the 4 P's)
- $S_{E}$ = **Environmental Stimuli** (Economic, Technological, Political, Cultural)
- $O$ = **Buyer's Organism** (decision process + the four C-factors)
- $R$ = **Buyer's Response** (choice, brand, dealer, timing, quantity)

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Definition Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Exam Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C_{c}$ | Cultural Factors | Always listed first; 1 mark |
| $C_{s}$ | Social Factors | Reference groups, family |
| $C_{p}$ | Personal Factors | AIO, Lifestyle |
| $C_{psi}$ | Psychological Factors | The 4 P's of psychology |
| $B = f(C_{c}, C_{s}, C_{p}, C_{psi})$ | Master Equation of Buyer Behaviour | Frequently asked — 2 marks |
| $S \rightarrow O \rightarrow R$ | Stimulus-Organism-Response Model | 14-mark essay favourite |
| NR → IS → EoA → PD → PPB | Five-Stage Decision Process | Common Part A question |
| AIO Statements | Activities, Interests, Opinions | Definition question (3 marks) |
| Family of Orientation | Parents/Siblings | Distinguish from Procreation |
| Family of Procreation | Spouse/Children | Often confused in exams |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that **Culture has the deepest, broadest influence**, while **Psychological factors have the narrowest, most immediate influence**. Examiners test this hierarchy in Part A. Always remember: *C_c → C_s → C_p → C_psi* in **descending order of fundamental influence**.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

This model is the **backbone of every market segmentation strategy, customer journey map, and brand positioning exercise** used by firms. Marketers use it to:
- **Target** the right customer (Personas).
- **Position** the product correctly (Psychological factors).
- **Differentiate** the brand (Cultural & Lifestyle fit).
- **Reduce cognitive dissonance** (Post-Purchase Communication).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Exhaustive Content

### 3.1 Exhaustive Breakdown of Cultural Factors ($C_{c}$)

#### Step 1: Understanding Culture
Culture is the **most basic cause** of a person's wants and behaviour. Every human behaviour is *learned* — not instinctive — through the process of socialization.

- A child growing up in Kerala may value *ayurveda* and *eco-friendly products*.
- A child growing up in urban Mumbai may value *branded fast fashion*.

#### Step 2: Sub-Culture
Within every culture, there exist **smaller, distinct subcultures** that share common belief systems:

- **Nationality**: Indian, Chinese, American.
- **Religion**: Hindu, Muslim, Christian, etc. → affects food, clothing, festivals.
- **Racial Group**: South Indian, North Indian.
- **Geographic Region**: Coastal, Mountain, Desert.
- **Age Subculture**: Gen Z, Millennials, Boomers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marketer's Implication:** Sub-cultures often represent **distinct market segments** with unique needs. E.g., "Onam Sadhya" campaigns target the Keralite sub-culture specifically.

#### Step 3: Social Class
Social classes are **relatively permanent and ordered** — characterized by:
- Occupation, Income, Education, Wealth.
- Tendency toward similar values, interests, behaviours.

In India, the classic Indian social classes include:
- Upper-Upper, Lower-Upper (Elite)
- Upper-Middle, Middle-Class (Aspirational)
- Upper-Lower, Lower-Lower (Subsistence)

### 3.2 Exhaustive Breakdown of Social Factors ($C_{s}$)

#### Step 1: Reference Groups
These are groups that serve as **direct or indirect points of comparison or reference** in forming a person's attitudes or behaviour.

**Types of Reference Groups:**

- **Membership Groups** — groups the person belongs to (family, friends, colleagues).
- **Aspirational Groups** — groups the person wishes to belong to (celebrities, influencers).
- **Dissociative Groups** — groups whose values a person *rejects* (e.g., anti-fashion buyers rejecting luxury brands).

#### Step 2: Family — The Strongest Influence
The family is divided into two distinct categories for marketing analysis:

- **Family of Orientation** = Parents + Siblings → the most influential group for shaping *childhood brand loyalties*.
- **Family of Procreation** = Spouse + Children → the most influential group for *current, daily purchasing decisions*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 3-mark question asks: *"Distinguish between Family of Orientation and Family of Procreation."* Always mention **which stage of life** each family influences.

#### Step 3: Roles and Status
A person occupies multiple **positions** in social groups — each role carries a **status**.

Example: A working professional is simultaneously:
- A **Manager** (high status) at office.
- A **Father** (mid status) at home.
- A **Captain** (high status) in a cricket club.

→ Each role triggers different product preferences (formal shirt vs. casual T-shirt vs. cricket gear).

### 3.3 Exhaustive Breakdown of Personal Factors ($C_{p}$)

#### Step 1: Age and Life-Cycle Stage
People change the goods and services they consume over their lifetime. The **Family Life Cycle (FLC)** stages include:

$$\text{Bachelor} \rightarrow \text{Newly Married} \rightarrow \text{Nesting (with kids)} \rightarrow \text{Post-Children} \rightarrow \text{Retired}$$

#### Step 2: Occupation
A person's occupation directly influences consumption patterns:
- A **software engineer** buys laptops, ergonomic chairs, noise-canceling headphones.
- A **construction worker** buys sturdy boots, gloves, lunch boxes.

#### Step 3: Economic Situation
- **Disposable Income**, **Savings**, **Assets**, **Borrowing Power**.
- Marketers monitor **economic indicators** (GDP, inflation) to predict demand shifts.

#### Step 4: Lifestyle (The AIO Model)
Lifestyle is a person's **pattern of living expressed through activities, interests, and opinions (AIOs)**. It is a *profile* built from psychographics.

**Example AIO of a fitness-conscious college student:**
- **Activities:** Gym, Running, Yoga.
- **Interests:** Organic food, sustainability, sports.
- **Opinions:** "Health is wealth", "Plastic is bad".

#### Step 5: Personality and Self-Concept
Personality refers to the **distinctive psychological characteristics** that lead to consistent responses to the environment.

- **Self-Concept (or Self-Image):** the way a person sees themselves and how they want others to see them. Brands align with self-concept (e.g., Nike aligns with the "achiever" self-concept).

### 3.4 Exhaustive Breakdown of Psychological Factors ($C_{psi}$)

#### Step 1: Motivation — Maslow's Hierarchy

Maslow arranged human needs in a **5-tier pyramid**:

$$\text{Physiological} \rightarrow \text{Safety} \rightarrow \text{Belongingness} \rightarrow \text{Esteem} \rightarrow \text{Self-Actualization}$$

- A hungry person is motivated by **food** (Physiological need).
- A *Thar* SUV buyer may be motivated by **Esteem** or **Belongingness**.

#### Step 2: Perception
Perception is the process by which a person **selects, organizes, and interprets** information to create a meaningful picture of the world.

**Stages of Perception:**
- **Selective Attention** — filtering stimuli.
- **Selective Distortion** — interpreting in line with prior beliefs.
- **Selective Retention** — remembering only supporting information.

#### Step 3: Learning
Learning describes the **changes in behaviour** that arise from **experience**.

$$L = f(D, R, C, I)$$

Where:
- $L$ = Learning
- $D$ = Drive
- $R$ = Response (cue)
- $C$ = Reinforcement
- $I$ = Information

#### Step 4: Beliefs and Attitudes
- **Belief** = a descriptive thought a person holds about something.
- **Attitude** = a person's consistent favourable/unfavourable evaluations, feelings, and tendencies toward an object or idea.

Attitudes are **hard to change**, so marketers usually *align* products to existing attitudes rather than try to alter them.

### 3.5 The Buyer Decision Process — Step-by-Step

| Stage | Description | Example (Smartphone Purchase) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. **Need Recognition** | Internal or external stimulus creates a gap between actual and desired state. | "My current phone battery dies in 2 hours." |
| 2. **Information Search** | Internal memory (recall) + External (online reviews, friends, ads). | Searches YouTube reviews, asks friends. |
| 3. **Evaluation of Alternatives** | Uses criteria (price, brand, specs) to compare options. | Compares Samsung, iPhone, OnePlus. |
| 4. **Purchase Decision** | Chooses the preferred alternative, but may modify due to unexpected situational factors. | Buys the iPhone 15. |
| 5. **Post-Purchase Behaviour** | Evaluates satisfaction; may experience **cognitive dissonance** ("Did I make the right choice?"). | Feels happy, recommends to others. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** If the purchase is **low-involvement, habitual** (e.g., groceries), the buyer often *skips* stages 2 and 3, going directly from Need Recognition to Purchase. This is called **Routinized Response Behaviour**.

### 3.6 Synthesis — The Complete Equation

The complete buyer decision can be represented as:

$$D_{final} = f\big(C_{c},\, C_{s},\, C_{p},\, C_{psi}\big) \circ \big[NR \rightarrow IS \rightarrow EoA \rightarrow PD \rightarrow PPB\big]$$

This means the four factors **influence** each of the five decision stages, producing a unique final decision $D_{final}$ for every individual.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Four Factors Influencing Buyer Behaviour (Hierarchical Mind Map)

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Buyer Behaviour))
    Cc1["Cultural Factors"]
      Cc1a["Culture Basic values Perceptions Wants"]
      Cc1b["Subculture Nationality Religion Region"]
      Cc1c["Social Class Upper Middle Lower"]
    Cs1["Social Factors"]
      Cs1a["Reference Groups Membership Aspirational Dissociative"]
      Cs1b["Family Orientation Procreation"]
      Cs1c["Roles and Status"]
    Cp1["Personal Factors"]
      Cp1a["Age and Life Cycle"]
      Cp1b["Occupation"]
      Cp1c["Economic Situation"]
      Cp1d["Lifestyle AIO"]
      Cp1e["Personality and Self Concept"]
    Cpsi1["Psychological Factors"]
      Cpsi1a["Motivation Maslow"]
      Cpsi1b["Perception Selective Attention Distortion Retention"]
      Cpsi1c["Learning"]
      Cpsi1d["Beliefs and Attitudes"]
```

### 4.2 The Five-Stage Buyer Decision Process (Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeNR["1 Need Recognition"]
    nodeIS["2 Information Search"]
    nodeEoA["3 Evaluation of Alternatives"]
    nodePD["4 Purchase Decision"]
    nodePPB["5 Post Purchase Behaviour"]
    nodeCD["Cognitive Dissonance Possible"]
    nodeSat["Satisfaction Dissatisfaction"]
    nodeNR --> nodeIS
    nodeIS --> nodeEoA
    nodeEoA --> nodePD
    nodePD --> nodePPB
    nodePPB --> nodeCD
    nodePPB --> nodeSat
    nodeSat -- Repeat Purchase --> nodeNR
```

### 4.3 The S-O-R (Stimulus-Organism-Response) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph stim1["Stimuli Environment"]
        nodeS_M["Marketing Stimuli 4Ps Product Price Place Promotion"]
        nodeS_E["Environmental Stimuli Economic Technological Political Cultural"]
    end
    subgraph org1["Organism Buyer"]
        nodeO_Cc["Cultural Factors"]
        nodeO_Cs["Social Factors"]
        nodeO_Cp["Personal Factors"]
        nodeO_Cpsi["Psychological Factors"]
    end
    subgraph res1["Response"]
        nodeR1["Brand Choice"]
        nodeR2["Dealer Choice"]
        nodeR3["Quantity"]
        nodeR4["Timing"]
    end
    nodeS_M --> nodeO_Cc
    nodeS_E --> nodeO_Cc
    nodeO_Cc --> nodeO_Cs
    nodeO_Cs --> nodeO_Cp
    nodeO_Cp --> nodeO_Cpsi
    nodeO_Cpsi --> nodeR1
    nodeO_Cpsi --> nodeR2
    nodeO_Cpsi --> nodeR3
    nodeO_Cpsi --> nodeR4
```

### 4.4 Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart BT
    nodeM5["Self Actualization Top Tier"]
    nodeM4["Esteem Needs"]
    nodeM3["Belongingness and Love Needs"]
    nodeM2["Safety Needs"]
    nodeM1["Physiological Needs Base Tier"]
    nodeM1 --> nodeM2
    nodeM2 --> nodeM3
    nodeM3 --> nodeM4
    nodeM4 --> nodeM5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Order:** In the Maslow block diagram, the **base** (Physiological needs) is the **most fundamental** — they must be satisfied first. Marketers typically sell products in the *upper tiers* (Esteem, Self-Actualization) because lower-tier needs are largely met in modern economies.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
*"Define Buyer Behaviour. Briefly list the four major factors influencing buyer behaviour."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Buyer Behaviour is the study of how individuals, groups, or organizations **select, buy, use, and dispose** of goods, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy their needs and wants.

**Four Factors (1 Mark for correct listing):**

$$B = f(C_{c},\, C_{s},\, C_{p},\, C_{psi})$$

1. **Cultural Factors** (Culture, Subculture, Social Class)
2. **Social Factors** (Reference Groups, Family, Roles & Status)
3. **Personal Factors** (Age, Occupation, Lifestyle, Personality)
4. **Psychological Factors** (Motivation, Perception, Learning, Beliefs & Attitudes)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write *Cultural, Social, Personal, Psychological* but lose the **1 mark for sequencing and proper sub-bullets**. The order must reflect the **depth-of-impact hierarchy** (Culture has the deepest influence, Psychology the most immediate). Use the exact four sub-bullets under each factor.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
*"Distinguish between Family of Orientation and Family of Procreation."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Family of Orientation | Family of Procreation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Parents and siblings | Spouse and children |
| **Life Stage** | Influences childhood decisions | Influences adult decisions |
| **Impact** | Shapes long-term values, brand loyalties | Affects daily, current purchasing |
| **Example** | Son choosing the same tea brand as his father | Husband-wife jointly choosing a car |

Both families together form the two key social influences on a buyer's consumption pattern. **The marketer must address both**, as the family of orientation creates inherited preferences while the family of procreation triggers active decision-making.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B carries **14 marks**, has **internal choice** (any one of two). Sub-parts are typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Style]**

*(a)* Explain in detail the **four major factors influencing buyer behaviour** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* Describe the **five-stage buyer decision process** with a real-world example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Four Major Factors Influencing Buyer Behaviour (7 Marks):**

The four major factors that influence a buyer's behaviour are arranged in descending order of *depth-of-influence*:

**1. Cultural Factors (1.5 Marks):**
- **Culture** is the most fundamental determinant of wants and behaviour (e.g., Indian culture values joint family dining → drives the demand for large dining tables).
- **Subculture** includes nationality, religion, region, and age groups (e.g., Keralite *Onam* market for banana leaves and payasam mixes).
- **Social Class** is determined by occupation, income, education, and wealth (e.g., luxury car brands target upper-upper and lower-upper classes).

**2. Social Factors (1.5 Marks):**
- **Reference Groups** — Membership (friends), Aspirational (Bollywood actors), Dissociative (anti-luxury buyers). A teenager's sneaker choice is often driven by a peer group.
- **Family** — Family of Orientation (parents) and Family of Procreation (spouse/children).
- **Roles and Status** — A manager buying formal wear reflects his office status; the same person in a cricket club buys sports gear.

**3. Personal Factors (2 Marks):**
- **Age & Life-Cycle Stage** — A bachelor buys a small car; a family man buys an SUV.
- **Occupation** — A doctor buys stethoscopes; a teacher buys books.
- **Economic Situation** — Recession reduces demand for luxury items.
- **Lifestyle (AIO)** — Activities, Interests, Opinions shape the brand fit.
- **Personality & Self-Concept** — A confident buyer chooses a bold brand like Royal Enfield.

**4. Psychological Factors (2 Marks):**
- **Motivation** — Maslow's needs drive purchases (e.g., a Thar SUV satisfies esteem needs).
- **Perception** — Selective attention, distortion, retention shape brand recall.
- **Learning** — Repeated positive experience builds brand loyalty.
- **Beliefs and Attitudes** — Pre-existing attitudes are hard to change; marketers align products accordingly.

---

**(b) Five-Stage Buyer Decision Process (7 Marks):**

The process is:

$$\text{NR} \rightarrow \text{IS} \rightarrow \text{EoA} \rightarrow \text{PD} \rightarrow \text{PPB}$$

**Real-World Example: Purchasing a New Laptop**

1. **Need Recognition (1 Mark):** The student's current laptop crashes during an online exam, creating a gap between current and desired state.

2. **Information Search (1.5 Marks):** The student reads online reviews (Amazon, YouTube tech channels), asks college seniors, visits Croma showroom, and recalls past experiences with brands.

3. **Evaluation of Alternatives (1.5 Marks):** Compares HP Pavilion, Dell Inspiron, MacBook Air, and Lenovo ThinkPad on criteria: price, processor, battery life, brand reputation, warranty.

4. **Purchase Decision (1.5 Marks):** Decides to buy the MacBook Air. However, an unexpected factor (out-of-stock or negative review) may modify the decision to a MacBook Pro.

5. **Post-Purchase Behaviour (1.5 Marks):** The student evaluates satisfaction. If the laptop performs well, it leads to positive word-of-mouth. If it underperforms, the student may experience **cognitive dissonance** ("Did I overpay?"). Marketers can mitigate this through post-purchase communication, warranties, and follow-up emails.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For 14-mark questions, students often lose marks because they:
> 1. **Don't structure the answer in headings** (no bullet points = no marks).
> 2. **Miss the example** in the 14-mark essay — always tie theory to a real-world example.
> 3. **Skip the formula** $B = f(C_c, C_s, C_p, C_{psi})$ — this is worth a guaranteed 1 mark in valuation.
> 4. **Forget to write the post-purchase cognitive dissonance** — examiners allocate **1.5 marks** specifically for this.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023 Style]**

*(a)* Explain **Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs** and its application in marketing. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* Discuss the **Stimulus-Organism-Response (S-O-R) Model** of buyer behaviour. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs (7 Marks):**

Abraham Maslow proposed that human needs are arranged in a **5-tier pyramid**, where lower-level needs must be satisfied before higher-level needs become motivators.

**The Five Levels (1 Mark each, with one marketing example):**

1. **Physiological Needs** (Base) — Hunger, thirst, shelter.
   - *Marketing Example:* Maggi noodles, Kissan ketchup, doorstep grocery delivery apps.

2. **Safety Needs** — Security, protection, stability.
   - *Marketing Example:* Insurance policies (LIC), home security systems, helmets.

3. **Belongingness and Love Needs** — Friendship, affection, family.
   - *Marketing Example:* Telecom plans, social media platforms (Instagram), dating apps.

4. **Esteem Needs** — Self-respect, recognition, status.
   - *Marketing Example:* Luxury cars (BMW, Mercedes), premium watches (Rolex), designer fashion.

5. **Self-Actualization (Top)** — Personal growth, self-fulfillment.
   - *Marketing Example:* Travel packages (Leh-Ladakh tours), skill-development courses (Coursera), premium books.

**Marketing Implication (2 Marks):**
- Marketers must **identify which level** their product addresses.
- A *bread* brand sells to Physiological needs; a *Rolex* sells to Esteem.
- Multiple brands can address the same need (e.g., McDonald's, Subway both target Physiological).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Theory Point:** Maslow's model is **not strictly linear** in marketing — a wealthy person may still buy McDonald's (Physiological) for nostalgia. Marketers must not assume strict sequential movement.

---

**(b) S-O-R Model of Buyer Behaviour (7 Marks):**

The S-O-R Model is the **foundation of behavioural psychology** applied to consumer research. It postulates that the buyer's response is the result of stimuli acting on the buyer's organism (mind).

**The Equation (1 Mark):**
$$S \rightarrow O \rightarrow R$$

**Detailed Architecture:**

**1. Stimuli (S) — 2 Marks:**
- **Marketing Stimuli (S_M):** The 4 P's of Marketing — Product, Price, Place, Promotion. Example: An advertisement for a new iPhone, priced at ₹80,000, available on Amazon.
- **Environmental Stimuli (S_E):** Economic conditions (recession), technological shifts (AI), political (taxes), and cultural factors (festivals).

**2. Organism (O) — 2.5 Marks:**
- The buyer's internal decision process, which is influenced by the four factors:
  - Cultural, Social, Personal, Psychological.
- The organism filters, interprets, and decides.

**3. Response (R) — 1.5 Marks:**
- The buyer's **observable behaviour**: choice of brand, choice of dealer, purchase timing, and quantity purchased.

**Illustration:**
> "Ananya, a college student, sees a Diwali ad for a Samsung Galaxy (Marketing Stimulus) during a festive season (Environmental Stimulus). Her mind — shaped by cultural, social, personal, and psychological factors (Organism) — produces a Response: she buys the phone on Amazon at 20% off."

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often **omit the Environmental Stimuli** and list only the 4 P's. This is a 1-mark loss. Also, students frequently forget to mention that the *organism* is a **black box** that processes stimuli — the four C-factors are the internal filters.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- [ ] **Master Equation:** $B = f(C_{c}, C_{s}, C_{p}, C_{psi})$ — 1 mark guaranteed.
- [ ] **Order of Influence:** Cultural → Social → Personal → Psychological (descending depth of influence).
- [ ] **Four Cultural Sub-factors:** Culture, Subculture, Social Class.
- [ ] **Three Social Sub-factors:** Reference Groups (Membership, Aspirational, Dissociative), Family (Orientation vs. Procreation), Roles & Status.
- [ ] **Five Personal Sub-factors:** Age, Occupation, Economic Situation, Lifestyle (AIO), Personality & Self-Concept.
- [ ] **Four Psychological Sub-factors:** Motivation (Maslow), Perception, Learning, Beliefs & Attitudes.
- [ ] **Maslow's 5 Levels:** Physiological, Safety, Belongingness, Esteem, Self-Actualization.
- [ ] **Three Perceptual Filters:** Selective Attention, Selective Distortion, Selective Retention.
- [ ] **5-Stage Decision Process:** NR → IS → EoA → PD → PPB.
- [ ] **Cognitive Dissonance** = Post-purchase buyer anxiety (mentioned in PPB stage).
- [ ] **S-O-R Model:** Stimulus (Marketing + Environmental) → Organism (4 C-factors) → Response (Brand/Dealer/Timing/Quantity).
- [ ] **Lifestyle = AIO** (Activities, Interests, Opinions).
- [ ] **Personality is unique; Lifestyle is a pattern of living; Self-Concept is how one sees oneself.**
- [ ] **Cultural factors have the BROADEST influence; Psychological factors have the NARROWEST but most IMMEDIATE influence.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "CSPP":** **C**ulture → **S**ocial → **P**ersonal → **P**sychological. The first **C** is the deepest; the second **P** is the surface. The order is **non-negotiable** in KTU exams.

**End of Module 4 — Factors Influencing Buyer Behaviour Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buyer Behaviour Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Buyer Behaviour Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Buyer Behaviour Models** are structured theoretical frameworks that attempt to explain, predict, and describe the decision-making process adopted by consumers while selecting, purchasing, using, or disposing of products, services, ideas, or experiences. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, these models are studied under **Consumer Behaviour** (Module 4) to bridge the gap between marketing stimuli and consumer response.

Mathematically, the fundamental premise of all buyer behaviour models can be expressed as:

$$
B = f(S, P, E, I)
$$

Where:
- $B$ = Buyer Behaviour (the observable response)
- $S$ = Stimuli (Marketing & Environmental inputs)
- $P$ = Psychological variables (motivation, perception, learning, attitude)
- $E$ = Environmental / Social variables (culture, family, reference groups)
- $I$ = Individual differences (demographics, personality, lifestyle)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, Buyer Behaviour Models directly support **Course Outcome (CO 4)** — *"Analyze the influence of psychological, social, cultural, and situational factors on consumer buying behaviour and apply various models to marketing decision-making."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef in a kitchen**. A customer walks in, looks at the menu, and orders a dish. You did not see what happened in their head — you only saw the *final output* (the order). A **buyer behaviour model** is like **installing a CCTV camera inside the customer's mind** to record the entire *decision-making journey* — from hunger, to menu scanning, to memory recall, to peer influence, to the final order.

In simpler terms:
- **Stimulus (S)** = The aroma of the dish hitting the customer's nose.
- **Buyer's Black Box** = The hidden mind (perception, attitude, learning).
- **Response (R)** = The customer placing the order.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Trick:** Every buyer behaviour model is fundamentally a **Stimulus → Black Box → Response** system. The differences across models lie in *what is inside the black box*.

---

### 1.3 Taxonomy of Buyer Behaviour Models (Overview)

| **S.No.** | **Model Category** | **Representative Model** | **Core Focus** |
|:---------:|:-------------------|:------------------------|:---------------|
| 1 | Economic / Marshallian | Economic Man Model | Rational, economic decision-making |
| 2 | Behaviourist / Learning | Pavlovian & Skinnerian Model | Stimulus-Response learning |
| 3 | Psychoanalytic | Freudian Model | Unconscious motives & conflicts |
| 4 | Sociological | Veblenian Model | Social & group influence |
| 5 | Comprehensive | Howard-Sheth Model | Integrated psychological + social |
| 6 | Comprehensive | Engel-Kollat-Blackwell (EKB) Model | Problem-solving process |
| 7 | Comprehensive | Nicosia Model | Firm-Consumer interaction |
| 8 | Kotler's Stimulus-Response | Kotler's Black Box Model | Marketing mix to buyer response |

> [!NOTE]
> The first four are **classical / partial models** (focus on a single dimension). The last four are **comprehensive / contemporary models** (integrate multiple dimensions). KTU examiners **frequently test the comparison** between partial and comprehensive models.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Classical (Partial) Buyer Behaviour Models

#### 2.1.1 Economic Model (Marshallian / Economic Man Model)

**Core Premise:** The consumer is a *rational economic being* who makes decisions to **maximize utility** subject to **budget constraints**.

**Key Assumptions:**
1. Consumers have **complete information** about all alternatives.
2. Consumers are **utility maximizers** — they prefer more utility to less.
3. Prices, income, and preferences are **stable and known**.
4. Decision-making is **conscious, logical, and calculative**.

The decision rule in its simplest form:

$$
U^* = \max_{x \in X} \, U(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad P_x \cdot x \leq M
$$

Where:
- $U^*$ = Maximum utility achieved
- $x$ = Bundle of goods purchased
- $P_x$ = Price vector
- $M$ = Money income of the consumer

**Real-World Utility in Engineering/Marketing Fields:**
- **Pricing strategy design** in engineering product markets (e.g., smartphones, automobiles).
- **Demand forecasting** for industrial machinery.
- Foundation for **cost-benefit analysis** in B2B engineering procurement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitation:** The model fails in reality because consumers are *not fully rational*. They suffer from cognitive biases, emotional influences, and information asymmetry — which is precisely why the next models emerged.

#### 2.1.2 Pavlovian / Behaviourist Learning Model

**Core Premise:** Consumer behaviour is a **learned response** to stimuli, following the classical conditioning paradigm of Ivan Pavlov.

The Pavlovian framework translates into consumer behaviour as:

$$
\text{Stimulus (S)} \longrightarrow \text{Organism (O)} \longrightarrow \text{Response (R)}
$$

Where the *Organism* is treated as a black box, and learning occurs through:
1. **Drive** → internal motivating force (e.g., hunger, thirst).
2. **Cue** → external stimulus that activates the drive (e.g., advertisement).
3. **Response** → behaviour triggered by the cue.
4. **Reinforcement** → reward/punishment that strengthens or weakens the response.

**Reinforcement Equation (Behavioural Strength):**

$$
R_{t+1} = R_t + \alpha \cdot (E - R_t)
$$

Where:
- $R_{t+1}$ = Future response strength
- $R_t$ = Current response strength
- $\alpha$ = Learning rate (between 0 and 1)
- $E$ = Expected reinforcement value

#### 2.1.3 Freudian Psychoanalytic Model

**Core Premise:** Sigmund Freud's theory that human behaviour is driven by **unconscious motives, conflicts, and repressed desires**. The model has three levels of consciousness:

$$
\text{Mind} = \text{Id} \cup \text{Ego} \cup \text{Superego}
$$

- **Id:** Pleasure principle, instinctive, irrational (drives purchase urges).
- **Ego:** Reality principle, mediates between Id and Superego.
- **Superego:** Moral conscience (creates guilt, restraint).

Applied to consumer behaviour, the **iceberg analogy** is used: only a small fraction of buying motives is *conscious*; the majority lies *subconscious or unconscious*.

#### 2.1.4 Veblenian Sociological Model

**Core Premise:** Thorstein Veblen argued that consumer behaviour is shaped by **social status, prestige, and group conformity** rather than individual rationality.

The social influence equation can be modelled as:

$$
I_{\text{social}} = w_1 \cdot G + w_2 \cdot F + w_3 \cdot R + w_4 \cdot C
$$

Where:
- $I_{\text{social}}$ = Social influence index
- $G$ = Group reference pressure
- $F$ = Family influence
- $R$ = Role expectations
- $C$ = Cultural norms
- $w_i$ = Weighting coefficients

---

### 2.2 The Comprehensive (Contemporary) Buyer Behaviour Models

#### 2.2.1 Kotler's Stimulus-Response Model (Black Box Model)

Philip Kotler's framework is the **most widely cited** in KTU textbooks. It treats the buyer as a **black box** with controllable marketing inputs and observable responses.

**Architecture:**

$$
\text{Marketing Stimuli (4P)} \longrightarrow \text{Buyer's Black Box} \longrightarrow \text{Buyer's Response}
$$

Where:

$$
\text{Marketing Stimuli} = \{ \text{Product},\ \text{Price},\ \text{Place},\ \text{Promotion} \}
$$

$$
\text{Other Stimuli} = \{ \text{Economic},\ \text{Technological},\ \text{Political},\ \text{Cultural} \}
$$

$$
\text{Buyer's Black Box} = \{ \text{Buyer Characteristics},\ \text{Decision Process} \}
$$

$$
\text{Buyer's Response} = \{ \text{Choice},\ \text{Purchase},\ \text{Brand Selection},\ \text{Timing},\ \text{Quantity} \}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Kotler's model is often asked as *"Draw and explain the buyer behaviour model by Philip Kotler"* for 7-mark questions. Always label **all four P's, the other stimuli, the two black-box components, and at least four responses**.

#### 2.2.2 Howard-Sheth Model (1969)

Developed by John Howard and Jagdish Sheth, this is the **first systematic, comprehensive model** of buyer behaviour. It bridges psychological and sociological variables.

**Three Key Constructs:**
1. **Input Variables** — Stimuli from marketing, social, and informational sources.
2. **Output Variables** — Attention, comprehension, attitude, intention, purchase.
3. **Hypothetical Constructs** — Perceptual bias, motivation, learning, attitude, satisfaction.

**Mathematical Foundation — Attitude Formation:**

$$
A_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \, w_i \cdot B_{ij}
$$

Where:
- $A_j$ = Attitude towards brand $j$
- $B_{ij}$ = Belief about attribute $i$ of brand $j$
- $w_i$ = Importance weight of attribute $i$
- $n$ = Number of salient attributes

#### 2.2.3 Engel-Kollat-Blackwell (EKB) Model

This model treats consumer decision-making as a **problem-solving process** with five sequential stages:

$$
\text{Need Recognition} \rightarrow \text{Information Search} \rightarrow \text{Alternative Evaluation} \rightarrow \text{Purchase Decision} \rightarrow \text{Post-Purchase Behaviour}
$$

The model also includes **information-processing variables** (attention, perception, attitude formation) and **environmental / individual difference variables**.

#### 2.2.4 Nicosia Model (1966)

Francesco Nicosia proposed a **flow-chart model** with four fields:
1. **Field 1** — Consumer's predisposition (attitude) from the firm's communication.
2. **Field 2** — Information search and evaluation of alternatives.
3. **Field 3** — Act of purchase.
4. **Field 4** — Feedback (post-purchase experience feeding back into Field 1).

The cybernetic loop is:

$$
F_1 \rightarrow F_2 \rightarrow F_3 \rightarrow F_4 \rightarrow F_1
$$

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Framework Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for the Table Below:** All set-membership symbols and conditioning bars use `\in`, `\vert`, or `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe `|` to prevent markdown table corruption.

| **Model Name** | **Core Construct** | **Mathematical Form** | **Boundary / Assumption** | **Primary Discipline** |
|:---------------|:-------------------|:---------------------|:--------------------------|:----------------------|
| Economic Model | Utility Maximization | $U^* = \max \, U(x) \;\vert\; P_x \cdot x \leq M$ | Perfect information, rational consumer | Economics |
| Pavlovian Model | Stimulus-Response Learning | $R_{t+1} = R_t + \alpha (E - R_t)$ | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ | Behavioural Psychology |
| Freudian Model | Unconscious Motivation | $\text{Mind} = \text{Id} \cup \text{Ego} \cup \text{Superego}$ | Behaviour dominated by hidden motives | Psychoanalysis |
| Veblenian Model | Social Influence | $I_{\text{social}} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i \cdot V_i$ | $\sum w_i = 1$, consumer is socially embedded | Sociology |
| Howard-Sheth Model | Attitude Formation | $A_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot B_{ij}$ | $\sum w_i = 1$, $n \geq 2$ | Integrative |
