| EKB Model | Decision Process | $\text{NR} \rightarrow \text{IS} \rightarrow \text{AE} \rightarrow \text{PD} \rightarrow \text{PPB}$ | Sequential, iterative | Integrative |
| Nicosia Model | Cybernetic Loop | $F_1 \rightarrow F_2 \rightarrow F_3 \rightarrow F_4 \rightarrow F_1$ | Closed feedback system | Integrative |
| Kotler Model | S-O-R Black Box | $S \rightarrow O \rightarrow R$ | 4P inputs, observable outputs | Marketing Management |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Buyer Behaviour Models

| **Model** | **Engineering / Industry Application** |
|:----------|:----------------------------------------|
| Economic Model | Telecom tariff design, industrial procurement bidding |
| Pavlovian Model | Brand recall through repetitive advertising of consumer durables |
| Freudian Model | Luxury car positioning, perfume marketing (emotional/aspirational cues) |
| Veblenian Model | Premium product launches, status-driven engineering goods |
| Howard-Sheth | Large-ticket industrial B2B sales (machinery, equipment) |
| EKB Model | Online e-commerce journey design (Amazon, Flipkart funnels) |
| Nicosia Model | After-sales service feedback loops for engineering products |
| Kotler Model | Integrated marketing communication (IMC) planning |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pavlovian Response Update Equation

We begin with the assumption that the strength of a learned response evolves proportionally to the **error between expected reinforcement and current response strength**. This is the **Rescorla-Wagner / Bush-Mosteller** learning rule.

**Step 1:** Define the reinforcement error at time $t$ as:

$$
e_t = E - R_t
$$

where $E$ is the expected reinforcement value and $R_t$ is the current response strength.

**Step 2:** The change in response strength $\Delta R_t$ is proportional to this error:

$$
\Delta R_t = R_{t+1} - R_t \propto e_t
$$

**Step 3:** Introduce a learning rate constant $\alpha$ to convert the proportionality into an equation:

$$
R_{t+1} - R_t = \alpha \cdot e_t
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $e_t = E - R_t$:

$$
R_{t+1} - R_t = \alpha \cdot (E - R_t)
$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to obtain the closed-form update:

$$
R_{t+1} = R_t + \alpha \cdot (E - R_t)
$$

**Step 6:** Expand recursively to obtain the steady-state value as $t \to \infty$:

$$
R_{t+1} = R_t + \alpha E - \alpha R_t = (1 - \alpha) R_t + \alpha E
$$

$$
R_t = (1-\alpha)^t R_0 + \alpha E \sum_{k=0}^{t-1} (1-\alpha)^k
$$

**Step 7:** As $t \to \infty$ and $0 < \alpha \leq 1$, the geometric series converges and:

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} R_t = E
$$

**Interpretation:** The response strength asymptotically converges to the expected reinforcement value — the consumer eventually learns the true reward associated with the brand.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Classical vs. Comprehensive Models

| **Criterion** | **Classical Models** | **Comprehensive Models** |
|:--------------|:--------------------|:-------------------------|
| Number of variables | One dominant factor | Multi-dimensional |
| Disciplinary base | Single (Econ/Psych/Soc) | Multi-disciplinary |
| Predictive accuracy | Low | Moderate to High |
| Practical use by managers | Limited | High |
| Behavioural assumption | Rational / Irrational / Social | Integrative |
| Examples | Economic, Pavlovian, Freudian, Veblenian | Howard-Sheth, EKB, Nicosia, Kotler |
| KTU mark weightage | 3–7 marks (definition type) | 7–14 marks (descriptive type) |

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Working of Kotler's Black Box Model

The Kotler model is operationalized in the following sequential logic:

**Step 1 — Identify the Marketing Stimuli (Controlled Variables):**
$$
\text{Marketing Mix} = \{ P_{\text{product}},\ P_{\text{price}},\ P_{\text{place}},\ P_{\text{promotion}} \}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Other (Environmental) Stimuli:**
$$
E = \{ \text{Economic},\ \text{Technological},\ \text{Political},\ \text{Cultural},\ \text{Legal} \}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine Total Stimuli Entering the Black Box:**
$$
S_{\text{total}} = \text{Marketing Mix} \cup E
$$

**Step 4 — Decompose the Buyer's Black Box:**
$$
B_{\text{box}} = C_{\text{buyer}} \cup D_{\text{process}}
$$

where $C_{\text{buyer}} = \{ \text{cultural},\ \text{social},\ \text{personal},\ \text{psychological} \}$ and $D_{\text{process}} = \{ \text{need recognition} \rightarrow \text{search} \rightarrow \text{evaluation} \rightarrow \text{purchase} \rightarrow \text{post-purchase} \}$.

**Step 5 — Output the Buyer's Response:**
$$
R = \{ \text{Brand Choice},\ \text{Purchase Timing},\ \text{Quantity},\ \text{Seller Choice} \}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Feedback Loop:**
$$
R \rightarrow S_{\text{total}} \quad \text{(response influences future stimuli design)}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — Howard-Sheth Attitude Score

Suppose a consumer evaluates three smartphone brands (A, B, C) on four attributes: **Camera (weight 0.4), Battery (weight 0.3), Price (weight 0.2), Brand (weight 0.1)**. Belief scores (on a 1–10 scale):

| **Attribute (i)** | **Weight $w_i$** | **Belief $B_{iA}$** | **Belief $B_{iB}$** | **Belief $B_{iC}$** |
|:------------------|:----------------:|:-------------------:|:-------------------:|:-------------------:|
| Camera            | 0.4              | 9                   | 7                   | 8                   |
| Battery           | 0.3              | 6                   | 8                   | 7                   |
| Price             | 0.2              | 7                   | 9                   | 6                   |
| Brand             | 0.1              | 8                   | 6                   | 9                   |

**Step 1:** Compute attitude towards Brand A:

$$
A_A = (0.4)(9) + (0.3)(6) + (0.2)(7) + (0.1)(8) = 3.6 + 1.8 + 1.4 + 0.8 = 7.6
$$

**Step 2:** Compute attitude towards Brand B:

$$
A_B = (0.4)(7) + (0.3)(8) + (0.2)(9) + (0.1)(6) = 2.8 + 2.4 + 1.8 + 0.6 = 7.6
$$

**Step 3:** Compute attitude towards Brand C:

$$
A_C = (0.4)(8) + (0.3)(7) + (0.2)(6) + (0.1)(9) = 3.2 + 2.1 + 1.2 + 0.9 = 7.4
$$

**Step 4:** Decision rule — the consumer selects the brand with the highest attitude score. Since $A_A = A_B = 7.6 > A_C = 7.4$, the consumer is **indifferent between A and B**, and may choose based on availability, promotional offers, or store visit convenience.

**Interpretation:** This numerical example demonstrates how the Howard-Sheth attitude equation operationalizes consumer brand choice in marketing analytics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Kotler's Stimulus-Response Black Box Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MKT[Marketing Stimuli: 4P - Product, Price, Place, Promotion]
    ENV[Other Stimuli: Economic, Technological, Political, Cultural]
    BB[Buyers Black Box]
    BC[Buyer Characteristics: Cultural, Social, Personal, Psychological]
    DP[Decision Process: Need Recognition, Search, Evaluation, Purchase, Post Purchase]
    RES[Buyer Response: Product Choice, Brand Choice, Dealer Choice, Timing, Quantity]

    MKT --> BB
    ENV --> BB
    BB --> BC
    BB --> DP
    BC --> RES
    DP --> RES
```

### 4.2 Howard-Sheth Model — Structural Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IV[Input Variables: Marketing Stimuli, Social Stimuli, Informational Stimuli]
    HP[Hypothetical Constructs: Perceptual Bias, Motivation, Learning, Attitude, Satisfaction]
    OV[Output Variables: Attention, Comprehension, Attitude, Intention, Purchase]
    EX[Exogenous Variables: Personality, Social Class, Culture, Importance, Financial Status]

    IV --> HP
    EX --> HP
    HP --> OV
    OV --> FEEDBACK[Feedback to Input Variables]
    FEEDBACK --> IV
```

### 4.3 EKB Model — Decision Process Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NR[Need Recognition]
    IS[Information Search]
    AE[Alternative Evaluation]
    PD[Purchase Decision]
    PPB[Post Purchase Behaviour]
    IPV[Information Processing: Attention, Perception, Attitude]
    ENV2[Environmental Influences: Culture, Family, Situation]
    INDV[Individual Differences: Resources, Knowledge, Attitude]

    NR --> IS --> AE --> PD --> PPB
    IPV --> NR
    IPV --> IS
    IPV --> AE
    IPV --> PD
    IPV --> PPB
    ENV2 --> IPV
    INDV --> IPV
```

### 4.4 Pavlovian Learning Model — Conditioning Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DR[Drive: Internal Motivation like Hunger Thirst]
    CU[Cue: External Stimulus like Advertisement Packaging]
    RES[Response: Behaviour like Purchase Trial]
    REI[Reinforcement: Reward or Punishment]

    DR --> CU
    CU --> RES
    RES --> REI
    REI -.Strengthens or Weakens.-> DR
```

### 4.5 Nicosia Model — Four-Field Cybernetic Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Field 1: Consumer Attitude from Firm Communication]
    F2[Field 2: Information Search and Evaluation of Alternatives]
    F3[Field 3: Act of Purchase]
    F4[Field 4: Post Purchase Feedback and Learning]

    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> F4
    F4 -.Feedback Loop.-> F1
```

### 4.6 Comparative Block Architecture — Classical vs. Comprehensive Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CLASS[Classical Partial Models]
        E1[Economic Model: Utility Max, Rational Consumer]
        P1[Pavlovian Model: Stimulus Response Learning]
        F1M[Freudian Model: Unconscious Motives Id Ego Superego]
        V1[Veblenian Model: Social Status Conformity]
    end

    subgraph COMP[Comprehensive Integrative Models]
        HSM[Howard Sheth Model: Attitude + Learning + Social]
        EKBM[Engel Kollat Blackwell Model: Decision Process Five Stages]
        NICM[Nicosia Model: Four Field Cybernetic Loop]
        KOTM[Kotler Black Box Model: 4P to Buyer Response]
    end

    CLASS --> UNIFY[Unified Buyer Behaviour Framework B = f S P E I]
    COMP --> UNIFY
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]
**Define Buyer Behaviour. List any four classical models of buyer behaviour.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Buyer Behaviour refers to the **decision-making processes and actions** of individuals, groups, or organizations that are involved in selecting, purchasing, using, and disposing of products, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy needs and desires. It encompasses both *pre-purchase* (search, evaluation) and *post-purchase* (satisfaction, loyalty) activities.

**Four Classical Models (1 mark — 0.25 each):**
1. **Economic Model** (Marshallian)
2. **Pavlovian / Behaviourist Learning Model**
3. **Freudian Psychoanalytic Model**
4. **Veblenian Sociological Model**

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always separate the *definition* (2 marks) from the *list with brief explanation* (1 mark). Avoid writing the definition in one continuous paragraph — use bullet points for clarity.

---

#### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]
**Explain the concept of "Buyer's Black Box" as given by Philip Kotler.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Buyer's Black Box**, as conceptualized by Philip Kotler, represents the **internal decision-making process of the consumer** that lies between the marketing stimuli (input) and the buyer's response (output). It contains two major components:

1. **Buyer Characteristics** — comprising *cultural, social, personal, and psychological* factors that influence how stimuli are interpreted.
2. **Buyer's Decision Process** — consisting of five sequential stages: *Need Recognition → Information Search → Evaluation of Alternatives → Purchase Decision → Post-Purchase Behaviour.*

Because marketers cannot directly observe the black box, they must infer consumer responses from observable behaviour, making it central to marketing strategy design.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (Choice 1):** [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Main Slot]

**Explain the Kotler's Stimulus-Response Model of Buyer Behaviour in detail. What are its limitations? (14 marks)**

**Sub-part (a): Draw and Explain the Kotler's Model (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Philip Kotler's Buyer Behaviour Model (also called the **S-O-R Model**) treats the consumer as a **black box** that converts external stimuli into behavioural responses. The model has three major sections:

**1. Marketing Stimuli (4P's) (2 marks):**
- **Product** — features, design, packaging, brand.
- **Price** — list price, discounts, credit terms.
- **Place** — distribution channels, retail outlets.
- **Promotion** — advertising, sales promotion, personal selling, PR.

**2. Other (Environmental) Stimuli (1 mark):**
- Economic, Technological, Political, Cultural, Legal factors that surround the buyer.

**3. Buyer's Black Box (2 marks):**
- **Buyer Characteristics** — Cultural (culture, subculture, social class), Social (reference groups, family, role), Personal (age, lifestyle, personality), Psychological (motivation, perception, learning, belief, attitude).
- **Decision Process** — Need Recognition → Information Search → Evaluation of Alternatives → Purchase Decision → Post-Purchase Behaviour.

**4. Buyer's Response (2 marks):**
- **Product Choice**, **Brand Choice**, **Dealer Choice**, **Purchase Timing**, and **Purchase Quantity.**

---

**Sub-part (b): Limitations of Kotler's Model (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Black Box Limitation (2 marks):** The model treats the internal mental process as a black box without revealing *what exactly happens inside* — making it more of a descriptive than an explanatory framework.

2. **Oversimplification (2 marks):** It assumes a *linear, one-way* flow from stimulus to response, ignoring feedback loops, dynamic interactions, and emotional contingencies that characterize real buying.

3. **Ignores Post-Purchase Cognitive Dissonance (1 mark):** The model does not adequately capture consumer regret, satisfaction, or dissatisfaction dynamics in detail.

4. **Cultural Limitations (1 mark):** The 4P framework is primarily built for *Western, product-centric* markets and may not fully capture service-based, digital, or non-Western buying scenarios.

5. **Limited Predictive Power (1 mark):** Because it does not quantify weights of stimuli, it cannot predict *which* stimulus will dominate response.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Causing Mark Deduction:**
> 1. **Drawing only three P's** instead of four (loses 0.5 mark).
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the *Other Stimuli* block** — examiners often allocate 1 mark specifically for this (loses 1 mark).
> 3. **Not mentioning all four Buyer Characteristics** (Cultural + Social + Personal + Psychological) — each is worth 0.25 mark.
> 4. **Listing only 2–3 responses** instead of the full set (Brand, Dealer, Timing, Quantity).
> 5. **Writing limitations in vague terms** like *"model is not good"* — be specific and theoretical.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Slot]

**(a) Compare the Economic Model and Pavlovian Model of Buyer Behaviour. (7 marks)**
**(b) Explain the Howard-Sheth Model of Buyer Behaviour with a suitable diagram. (7 marks)**

**Sub-part (a): Economic vs. Pavlovian Model — Comparison (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis of Comparison** | **Economic Model** | **Pavlovian Model** |
|:------------------------|:-------------------|:--------------------|
| **Originator** | Alfred Marshall (1890) | Ivan Pavlov (1903) |
| **Discipline** | Economics | Behavioural Psychology |
| **Core Premise** | Consumer is a rational utility-maximizer | Consumer is a stimulus-response learner |
| **Decision Rule** | $U^* = \max \, U(x) \;\vert\; P_x \cdot x \leq M$ | $R_{t+1} = R_t + \alpha (E - R_t)$ |
| **Key Variable** | Price, Income, Utility | Drive, Cue, Response, Reinforcement |
| **Behaviour Type** | Cognitive / Calculative | Conditioned / Automatic |
| **Information Need** | Full / Perfect Information | No cognitive processing required |
| **Limitation** | Ignores emotions, biases | Ignores rational thinking |
| **Application** | Pricing of industrial goods | Brand-building advertising |

(2 marks for the table structure and 5 marks for the explanation of each criterion in 2–3 lines.)

---

**Sub-part (b): Howard-Sheth Model — Detailed Explanation (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Howard-Sheth Model (1969)** is the **first comprehensive, integrative model** of buyer behaviour. It proposes that consumer decision-making is shaped by three categories of variables:

**1. Input Variables (2 marks):**
- **Marketing Stimuli** — product, price, promotion (4P signals from the firm).
- **Social Stimuli** — family, reference groups, social class.
- **Informational Stimuli** — commercial and non-commercial information channels.

**2. Hypothetical Constructs (Internal Variables) (3 marks):**
- **Perceptual Bias** — selective perception and exposure.
- **Motivation** — drives activated by needs.
- **Learning** — experience-based modification of behaviour.
- **Attitude** — overall evaluation of brands.
- **Satisfaction** — post-purchase evaluation feedback.

**3. Output Variables (1 mark):**
- Attention → Comprehension → Attitude → Intention → Purchase.

**4. Exogenous (Outer) Variables (1 mark):**
- Personality, social class, culture, importance of purchase, financial status — act as moderating influences on the entire process.

The model is **cybernetic** — it contains feedback loops where post-purchase satisfaction modifies future attitude and learning, creating an ongoing cycle of brand relationship evolution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Howard-Sheth Specific:**
> 1. **Confusing "Hypothetical Constructs" with "Output Variables"** — students often interchange these; examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Not drawing the feedback loop** — the cybernetic feedback is the *signature* of the model and carries 1 full mark.
> 3. **Listing only "Motivation" and "Learning"** as hypothetical constructs — you must include all five (Perceptual Bias, Motivation, Learning, Attitude, Satisfaction).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Cross-Cutting Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Universal Pitfalls When Answering Buyer Behaviour Model Questions (Any Model):**
> 1. **Writing the model name without the originator** (e.g., "Economic Model" instead of "Marshallian / Economic Man Model") — loses 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Memorizing theory without applying it** — KTU 2024 Scheme OBE demands CO-mapped answers; always link theory to a **marketing application**.
> 3. **Missing the "limitations" section** when 14-mark descriptive questions explicitly ask for it.
> 4. **Incomplete diagrams** — every model diagram MUST show **all major components with labelled arrows**. Unlabelled arrows = 0 marks for diagram.
> 5. **Confusing classical with comprehensive models** — verify the question stem carefully before choosing which model to elaborate.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Buyer Behaviour Models:**

- Buyer Behaviour Models = Theoretical frameworks explaining **how and why consumers buy**.
- Fundamental form: $B = f(S, P, E, I)$ — Stimuli, Psychological, Environmental, Individual variables.
- **Four Classical (Partial) Models:**
  - **Economic Model** — rational utility maximization under budget constraint.
  - **Pavlovian Model** — stimulus-response learning with reinforcement.
  - **Freudian Model** — Id, Ego, Superego; unconscious motives; iceberg analogy.
  - **Veblenian Model** — social status, prestige, group conformity.
- **Four Comprehensive Models:**
  - **Kotler's Black Box** — 4P + Other Stimuli → Black Box → Response.
  - **Howard-Sheth** — Input Variables, Hypothetical Constructs, Output Variables, with cybernetic feedback.
  - **EKB Model** — Need Recognition → Information Search → Alternative Evaluation → Purchase Decision → Post-Purchase Behaviour.
  - **Nicosia Model** — Four-field cybernetic loop: $F_1 \rightarrow F_2 \rightarrow F_3 \rightarrow F_4 \rightarrow F_1$.
- Pavlovian learning equation: $R_{t+1} = R_t + \alpha (E - R_t)$, where $\alpha \in [0,1]$.
- Howard-Sheth attitude: $A_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot B_{ij}$, with $\sum w_i = 1$.
- Economic utility rule: $U^* = \max \, U(x) \;\vert\; P_x \cdot x \leq M$.
- **Classical models** focus on a *single discipline*; **comprehensive models** integrate *multiple disciplines*.
- Kotler's *Buyer's Black Box* contains **Buyer Characteristics** (Cultural + Social + Personal + Psychological) and the **Decision Process** (5 stages).
- **Howard-Sheth** Hypothetical Constructs = *Perceptual Bias + Motivation + Learning + Attitude + Satisfaction* — remember all five.
- **Always draw labelled diagrams** for full marks in 14-mark questions.
- **Always state limitations** explicitly — KTU examiners reward critical evaluation under OBE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building and Measuring Customer Satisfaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Building and Measuring Customer Satisfaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Customer Satisfaction (CS)** is the degree to which a product's or service's perceived performance (or delivery) matches a buyer's expectations. If the perceived performance **falls short of** expectations, the customer is **dissatisfied**; if it **meets** expectations, the customer is **satisfied**; if it **exceeds** expectations, the customer is **highly satisfied or delighted**.

This definition is rooted in the **Expectation–Confirmation Theory (ECT)** popularized by Richard L. Oliver (1980), and remains the cornerstone conceptual model adopted in the **KTU 2024 Scheme Marketing Management syllabus (Module 4)**.

**Related Core Constructs:**

| Construct | KTU Definition | Directional Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Customer Delight** | Performance substantially exceeding expectations, producing an emotional "wow" response. | Strongly drives loyalty. |
| **Customer Loyalty** | A deeply held commitment to re-patronize a brand despite situational influences. | Direct outcome of CS. |
| **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | The total revenue a customer generates over the entire relationship horizon. | Maximized via sustained CS. |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | A loyalty metric ranging from **−100 to +100**. | Industry standard CS proxy. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Experience

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you walk into a roadside *thattukada* expecting a quick ₹50 meal. You get exactly that. You are **satisfied**. Now imagine the same ₹50 meal is served on a banana leaf by a smiling server who also offers free *payasam*. You are **delighted** — and you tell five friends. Now imagine you paid ₹500 and got a stale parotta. You are **furious** — and you post a Google review for 200 strangers to see.
>
> **Mapping the analogy:**
> - ₹50 expectation → **Expectation level**
> - The actual parotta + payasam → **Perceived performance**
> - The gap between the two → **Satisfaction gap**
> - Your Google review → **Word-of-mouth (WOM) signal**
>
> **Building CS** is the *thattukada's* job — serve food at the promised level, every single day.
> **Measuring CS** is the *Google rating system* — converting customer feelings into a number that the owner can track and improve.

This single mental model explains why KTU examiners love framing satisfaction questions around the **"Gap between Expectation and Performance"** paradigm.

## 1.3 Why KTU Cares About This Module

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PEIET752 explicitly asks students to (a) identify **determinants** of CS, (b) outline **strategies to build** it, and (c) demonstrate **measurement frameworks** such as SERVQUAL, the **American Customer Satisfaction Index (ACSI)** model, NPS, and **complaint-handling systems**. Expect a guaranteed **14-mark Part B question** on this module in every KTU ESE cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Customer Satisfaction

Customer Satisfaction is the **result** of an interaction between three pillars. Master this triad — it appears in almost every KTU model answer.

### Pillar 1 — Product / Service Performance
- **Core features** (functional fit, reliability, durability)
- **Augmented features** (warranty, after-sales, packaging)
- **Aesthetic & design** (look, feel, sound)

### Pillar 2 — Customer Expectations
- Formed by **past experience, word-of-mouth, brand promises, competitor benchmarks**, and **personal needs**.
- The "expectation baseline" shifts over time — a phenomenon KTU examiners call the **rising expectations curve**.

### Pillar 3 — The Gap (Disconfirmation)
The famous **Gap Model** from Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry identifies **5 gaps** that cause customer dissatisfaction:

| Gap | KTU Label | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gap 1** | Knowledge Gap | Difference between customer expectations and management's perception of them. |
| **Gap 2** | Standards Gap | Difference between management perception and the service-quality standards set. |
| **Gap 3** | Delivery Gap | Difference between standards set and actual service performance. |
| **Gap 4** | Communication Gap | Difference between actual performance and what is communicated externally. |
| **Gap 5** | Customer Gap | The end-to-end gap perceived by the customer (the *sum* of Gaps 1–4). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always close your answer by saying *"Closing Gap 5 (the Customer Gap) is the goal of the entire CS-building exercise, and it requires simultaneous closure of Gaps 1 through 4."*

## 2.2 The Five Determinants of Customer Satisfaction (SERVQUAL)

The SERVQUAL framework (Parasuraman et al., 1988) — the most-tested model in KTU papers — identifies **5 dimensions** that customers use to judge satisfaction:

1. **Tangibles** — physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance.
2. **Reliability** — ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately.
3. **Responsiveness** — willingness to help customers and provide prompt service.
4. **Assurance** — knowledge and courtesy of employees, and their ability to inspire trust and confidence.
5. **Empathy** — caring, individualized attention given to customers.

> [!NOTE]
> SERVQUAL is computed as:

$$
S \equiv P - E
$$

where $P$ is the **Perception** score (1–7 Likert) and $E$ is the **Expectation** score (1–7 Likert) for each of the 22 items across the 5 dimensions. A **negative S** indicates a *dissatisfaction* zone; a **positive S** indicates *satisfaction*; the higher the magnitude, the stronger the response.

## 2.3 The Three-Step Model for Building Customer Satisfaction

### Step 1 — Identify Customer Needs
Use market research, voice-of-customer (VoC) studies, focus groups, and customer journey mapping.

### Step 2 — Design the Value Proposition
Translate needs into product features, service standards, and price-quality positioning.

### Step 3 — Deliver Against Standards + Continuously Improve
- Empower front-line staff.
- Implement closed-loop feedback.
- Use TQM, Six-Sigma, and Kaizen.
- Run **Service Recovery** programmes — every complaint is a free improvement roadmap.

> [!TIP]
> **The "8 Cs" of Building CS** (commonly asked in KTU Part A 3-mark questions): *Customer-centric culture, Communication, Complaint handling, Consistency, Competence, Convenience, Courtesy, and Continuous improvement.*

## 2.4 The Four Standard Methods for Measuring Customer Satisfaction

| # | Method | Best For | Output Type |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **M1** | **Customer Satisfaction Surveys** (Likert 1–5 or 1–7) | Detailed attribute-level analysis | Quantitative CSAT score |
| **M2** | **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | Single-metric loyalty tracking | Score between −100 and +100 |
| **M3** | **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI / ACSI)** | Macro/national comparisons | 0–100 index |
| **M4** | **Complaint & Suggestion Analysis** | Identifying failure points | Qualitative + Pareto data |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula you must memorize for the ESE.** No `|` symbols are used in any cell — only LaTeX delimiters — to keep this table rendering-safe.

| # | KTU Formula | Meaning of Each Term | Typical Scale |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **F1** | $\text{CSAT} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i}{n}$ | $R_i$ = rating of respondent $i$; $n$ = sample size | 1 → 5 (or 1 → 7) |
| **F2** | $\text{NPS} \;=\; \%P \;-\; \%D$ | $\%P$ = % Promoters; $\%D$ = % Detractors | −100 → +100 |
| **F3** | $\%P \;=\; \dfrac{N_P}{N} \times 100$ | $N_P$ = number of Promoters; $N$ = total respondents | 0 → 100 |
| **F4** | $\%D \;=\; \dfrac{N_D}{N} \times 100$ | $N_D$ = number of Detractors; $N$ = total respondents | 0 → 100 |
| **F5** | $\text{CSI} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot \bar{S}_j$ | $w_j$ = importance weight of attribute $j$; $\bar{S}_j$ = mean satisfaction score of attribute $j$; $m$ = number of attributes; $\sum w_j = 1$ | 0 → 100 |
| **F6** | $\text{SERVQUAL Score} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{22}\sum_{k=1}^{22} (P_k - E_k)$ | $P_k, E_k$ = perception, expectation for statement $k$ | −6 → +6 |
| **F7** | $\text{Gap}_5 \;=\; \text{Gap}_1 + \text{Gap}_2 + \text{Gap}_3 + \text{Gap}_4$ | Cumulative customer-perceived gap | Aggregated score |
| **F8** | $\text{CLV} \;=\; \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(R_t - C_t) \cdot r_t}{(1+d)^t}$ | $R_t$ = revenue; $C_t$ = cost; $r_t$ = retention prob.; $d$ = discount rate; $T$ = horizon | Currency units |
| **F9** | $\text{Retention Rate} \;=\; \dfrac{C_E - C_N}{C_S}$ | $C_E$ = customers at end; $C_N$ = new; $C_S$ = start | 0 → 100 % |
| **F10** | $\text{Switching Cost} \;=\; C_{\text{loss}} \;+\; C_{\text{learn}} \;+\; C_{\text{emo}}$ | $C_{\text{loss}}$ = loss cost; $C_{\text{learn}}$ = learning cost; $C_{\text{emo}}$ = emotional cost | Mixed units |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Marketing

Although the course is a management elective, the implications directly affect B.Tech engineers:

- **Product Design Engineers** must design features that meet *customer-validated* specifications, not just internal ones (closes Gap 1).
- **Service Engineers** in IT/ITES/Manufacturing use NPS to measure post-delivery satisfaction.
- **Quality Engineers** use Pareto analysis of complaint data to drive Six-Sigma DMAIC projects.
- **Project Managers** use CLV to prioritize high-value customers in B2B engagements.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section demonstrates the **mathematical mechanics** of the two most-tested measurement tools in KTU ESE: **(A) Net Promoter Score (NPS)** and **(B) Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)**. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No verbal shortcuts, no skip-jumps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Computing the Net Promoter Score (NPS)

**Background.** On a typical NPS survey, customers answer the question:
*"On a scale of 0 to 10, how likely are you to recommend our company/product to a friend or colleague?"*

The responses are bucketed as:

- **Promoters (P)** : respond **9 or 10** — loyal enthusiasts
- **Passives (Ps)** : respond **7 or 8** — satisfied but unenthusiastic
- **Detractors (D)** : respond **0 to 6** — unhappy customers

The NPS is then computed as the difference between the **% Promoters** and the **% Detractors**.

### Step 1 — Define the bucket counts
Let the survey of $N$ respondents return counts:

$$
N_P \;=\; \text{Number of Promoters}, \quad N_{Ps} \;=\; \text{Number of Passives}, \quad N_D \;=\; \text{Number of Detractors}
$$

Sanity check: the three buckets must exhaust the sample.

$$
N_P \;+\; N_{Ps} \;+\; N_D \;=\; N
$$

### Step 2 — Convert each count to a percentage
Each percentage is the bucket count divided by the total sample, multiplied by 100.

$$
\%P \;=\; \dfrac{N_P}{N} \times 100
$$

$$
\%D \;=\; \dfrac{N_D}{N} \times 100
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the NPS definition
By the formal definition (F2 above):

$$
\text{NPS} \;=\; \%P \;-\; \%D
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the percentage expressions
Insert the expressions from Step 2 into the Step 3 equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPS} \; & = \; \left( \dfrac{N_P}{N} \times 100 \right) \;-\; \left( \dfrac{N_D}{N} \times 100 \right) \\
& = \; \dfrac{100}{N}\bigl( N_P \;-\; N_D \bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The maximum possible NPS is $+100$ (when $N_D = 0$ and $N_{Ps} = 0$, i.e., everyone is a Promoter). The minimum possible NPS is $-100$ (when $N_P = 0$ and $N_{Ps} = 0$, i.e., everyone is a Detractor). Passives contribute **zero** to the score — they are mathematically ignored.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)
A telecom company surveys $N = 250$ customers. Bucket counts are: $N_P = 130$, $N_{Ps} = 70$, $N_D = 50$.

$$
\%P \;=\; \dfrac{130}{250} \times 100 \;=\; 52\%
$$

$$
\%D \;=\; \dfrac{50}{250} \times 100 \;=\; 20\%
$$

$$
\text{NPS} \;=\; 52 \;-\; 20 \;=\; \boxed{+32}
$$

A score of $+32$ is **above the global SaaS industry median** (≈ +30) and is classified as **"Good"** in Bain & Co.'s published benchmarks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing the Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)

**Background.** The CSI is a **weighted-average satisfaction index** computed across $m$ customer-perceived attributes. Each attribute $j$ carries an importance weight $w_j$ and an average satisfaction rating $\bar{S}_j$.

### Step 1 — Collect raw data per attribute
For each attribute $j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$, collect Likert ratings from all $n$ respondents:

$$
R_{1j}, \; R_{2j}, \; \ldots, \; R_{nj}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the mean satisfaction score per attribute
For each attribute, take the arithmetic mean of the $n$ ratings.

$$
\bar{S}_j \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_{ij}
$$

### Step 3 — Determine importance weights
Ask respondents to allocate **100 importance points** across the $m$ attributes (constant-sum scale). For respondent $i$, the points given to attribute $j$ are $W_{ij}$.

The average importance weight of attribute $j$ across all respondents:

$$
w_j \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n \cdot 100}\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{ij}
$$

The weights are **normalized** so that they sum to exactly 1.

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \;=\; 1
$$

### Step 4 — Aggregate the weighted scores
The CSI is the inner product of the weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ and the mean-satisfaction vector $\bar{\mathbf{S}}$.

$$
\text{CSI} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot \bar{S}_j
$$

### Step 5 — Normalize to a 0–100 scale (optional but expected in KTU)
If the Likert scale used is 1 → 5, rescale by multiplying by 20 (or simply by 25 if 1 → 4):

$$
\text{CSI}_{100} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{5}\sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot \bar{S}_j \times 100 \;=\; 20\sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot \bar{S}_j
$$

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)
An automobile company measures satisfaction on **4 attributes** with importance weights and mean satisfaction ratings given below (5-point Likert).

| Attribute $j$ | Importance Weight $w_j$ | Mean Score $\bar{S}_j$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Performance | 0.30 | 4.2 |
| Comfort | 0.25 | 3.8 |
| After-Sales Service | 0.30 | 3.2 |
| Aesthetics | 0.15 | 4.5 |

Verify weight normalization: $0.30 + 0.25 + 0.30 + 0.15 = 1.00$ ✓

Compute the unnormalized weighted sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CSI}_{\text{raw}} \; & = \; (0.30 \times 4.2) \;+\; (0.25 \times 3.8) \;+\; (0.30 \times 3.2) \;+\; (0.15 \times 4.5) \\
& = \; 1.260 \;+\; 0.950 \;+\; 0.960 \;+\; 0.675 \\
& = \; 3.845
\end{aligned}
$$

Normalize to 0–100 (multiply by 20 because Likert max is 5):

$$
\text{CSI}_{100} \;=\; 3.845 \times 20 \;=\; \boxed{76.9}
$$

**Interpretation:** A CSI of **76.9 / 100** is a healthy score. Diagnostic insight: the lowest-scoring weighted contributor is *After-Sales Service* ($0.30 \times 3.2 = 0.96$) — this is the **priority improvement area** because it has both the **highest weight** and the **lowest mean**. Such a quadrant analysis is the essence of a *Customer Satisfaction Improvement Matrix* (Importance vs. Performance) commonly required in KTU 14-mark answers.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Measurement Tool

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for **justifying the choice of tool**. Use this matrix in your answer-introduction.

| Decision Criterion | Survey (CSAT) | NPS | CSI | Complaint Analysis |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Single KPI needed | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Attribute-level diagnosis | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓✓ |
| Cross-company benchmarking | ✗ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ |
| Cost / Time efficiency | Low | Very High | Medium | High |
| Emotion & loyalty capture | Low | High | Low | High |
| Recommended for KTU exam | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Insight — Why Satisfaction ≠ Loyalty

A classic 14-mark discussion point: **satisfied customers defect all the time.** The KTU-expected analytical reasoning is:

$$
\text{Loyalty} \;\neq\; \text{Satisfaction} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Loyalty} \;=\; f(\text{Satisfaction}, \text{Switching Cost}, \text{Alternatives}, \text{Trust})
$$

Heskett et al.'s **Service-Profit Chain** confirms that CS drives loyalty, and loyalty drives **revenue growth and profitability** — but the *strength* of the chain depends on the **service culture** of the organization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All Mermaid diagrams below use only alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters (e.g., `n1`, `stepA`) — never the reserved keywords `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style` as standalone node names. No markdown formatting appears inside double-quoted labels.

## 4.1 The Customer Satisfaction Loop (Closed-Loop Feedback System)

This diagram shows the **complete cycle of building and measuring CS** that KTU examiners expect you to reproduce in any 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[Step 1: Identify Customer Needs via VoC Research]
    n2[Step 2: Set Service Quality Standards SERVQUAL]
    n3[Step 3: Design and Deliver the Value Proposition]
    n4[Step 4: Customer Experiences the Product or Service]
    n5[Step 5: Compare Performance with Expectation]
    n6[Step 6: Customer Forms a Satisfaction Judgment]
    n7[Step 7: Capture Feedback via CSAT NPS CSI Survey]
    n8[Step 8: Analyze Data and Identify Gaps]
    n9[Step 9: Service Recovery and Complaint Resolution]
    n10[Step 10: Continuous Improvement Back to Step 1]

    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> n3
    n3 --> n4
    n4 --> n5
    n5 --> n6
    n6 --> n7
    n7 --> n8
    n8 --> n9
    n9 --> n10
    n10 -.-> n1
```

## 4.2 The Five SERVQUAL Dimensions as a Radar Block

This block-level diagram maps the five SERVQUAL determinants to the **customer-perceived satisfaction outcome**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n21[Tangibles Facilities and Appearance]
    n22[Reliability Dependable and Accurate Service]
    n23[Responsiveness Prompt and Willing Help]
    n24[Assurance Trust and Competence]
    n25[Empathy Individualized Caring Attention]
    n30[Customer Satisfaction Outcome CSAT plus NPS plus CSI]
    n40[Customer Loyalty and Advocacy]

    n21 --> n30
    n22 --> n30
    n23 --> n30
    n24 --> n30
    n25 --> n30
    n30 --> n40
```

## 4.3 The Customer Satisfaction Measurement Architecture

This is the **block-level functional architecture** that maps inputs, processing tools, and outputs of a CS measurement programme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n51[INPUT Customer Touchpoint Data]
    n52[INPUT Survey Responses 1 to 5 Likert]
    n53[INPUT Complaint Logs]
    n54[INPUT Social Media and Review Mining]
```
