```mermaid
    n61[PROCESS NPS Bucketing and Scoring]
    n62[PROCESS Weighted CSI Aggregation]
    n63[PROCESS SERVQUAL Gap Computation]
    n64[PROCESS Complaint Pareto and Root Cause]
    n70[OUTPUT Customer Satisfaction Report]
    n71[OUTPUT Improvement Action Plan]
    n72[OUTPUT Strategic Marketing Decisions]

    n51 --> n60
    n51 --> n61
    n52 --> n60
    n52 --> n61
    n52 --> n62
    n52 --> n63
    n53 --> n64
    n54 --> n64
    n60 --> n70
    n61 --> n70
    n62 --> n70
    n63 --> n70
    n64 --> n70
    n70 --> n71
    n71 --> n72
```

## 4.4 The Importance–Performance Matrix (Improvement Quadrants)

This is the **most exam-frequently demanded chart** for any CSI-style 14-mark question. It is rendered as a block flow that maps attribute position to action.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n80[Attributes Plotted by Importance and Performance]
    n81[Q1 High Importance Low Performance CONCENTRATE HERE]
    n82[Q2 High Importance High Performance MAINTAIN]
    n83[Q3 Low Importance Low Performance DEPRIORITIZE]
    n84[Q4 Low Importance High Performance POSSIBLE OVERKILL]

    n80 --> n81
    n80 --> n82
    n80 --> n83
    n80 --> n84
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Smart Move:** If the question is on the *practical* application of CS measurement, redraw this matrix with the 4 automobile attributes from Section 3.2 (Performance, Comfort, After-Sales, Aesthetics). After-Sales falls in **Q1 (Concentrate Here)** — perfect for a 7-mark sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are aligned to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Bloom's Taxonomy** cognitive levels. They are tagged with simulated past-year identifiers to mimic the actual question paper format.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define customer satisfaction. Explain the Expectation–Confirmation Theory.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEIET752]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Customer satisfaction is the customer's evaluation of a product or service in terms of whether it has met their needs and expectations. According to Oliver's **Expectation–Confirmation Theory (ECT)**, customers form an *expectation* about a product before purchase, observe its *perceived performance* after consumption, and then compare the two. **Confirmation** occurs when performance meets expectations, **positive disconfirmation** when performance exceeds expectations (leading to delight), and **negative disconfirmation** when performance falls short (leading to dissatisfaction). (3 marks)

### Q2. List any three determinants of customer satisfaction with one-line examples.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEIET752]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Product Quality** – reliability, durability, and feature richness of the offering. *Example:* A Samsung phone lasting 4 years without battery issues.
2. **Service Quality** – responsiveness, assurance, and empathy of staff. *Example:* Bank resolving a card issue in under 10 minutes.
3. **Price–Value Perception** – perceived fairness of price relative to benefits. *Example:* Zepto's 10-minute delivery perceived as fair at ₹30.
*(Alternate valid determinants: brand reputation, after-sales support, convenience.)* (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Avoid one-word answers.** Each 7-mark sub-part requires *at least* 3 paragraphs and *one* diagram/formula.
> - **Do not confuse CSAT and CSI.** CSAT is the *raw average rating*; CSI is the *importance-weighted index*.
> - **Do not skip the "building" half.** A 14-mark question on "Building AND Measuring CS" will deduct up to 4 marks if you only cover measurement.
> - **Always close with a Service-Profit Chain reference** — it is the examiner's favourite one-line closer.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

**Q3 (a)** Explain the SERVQUAL model of service quality. Discuss its **five dimensions** with one practical example for each. **(7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEIET752]**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The **SERVQUAL model**, developed by Parasuraman, Zeithaml and Berry (1988), is a 22-item instrument that measures service quality across five dimensions by computing the **gap between customer expectations (E) and perceptions (P)** of actual service delivered.

The five dimensions are:
1. **Tangibles** — Physical evidence such as equipment, personnel appearance, and physical facilities. *Example:* Clean, air-conditioned hospital lobbies with uniformed staff reassure patients.
2. **Reliability** — Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately. *Example:* Amazon delivering on the exact promised delivery date.
3. **Responsiveness** — Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service. *Example:* Zomato chat support replying within 60 seconds.
4. **Assurance** — Employee knowledge and courtesy that inspire trust. *Example:* A chartered accountant explaining complex tax filings in plain English.
5. **Empathy** — Caring, individualized attention. *Example:* A boutique remembering a regular customer's name and preferred size.

**Application:** Many Indian banks (HDFC, SBI) use SERVQUAL to audit their branch service quality annually. The aggregated score across all 22 items gives a single **SERVQUAL gap** which is benchmarked across branches to identify training needs.

(7 marks)

---

**Q3 (b)** Describe the **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** methodology. A company surveyed 400 customers: 200 Promoters, 120 Passives, 80 Detractors. Compute and interpret the NPS. **(7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEIET752]**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** is a single-item loyalty metric introduced by Frederick F. Reichheld (2003). It is derived from the survey question: *"On a scale of 0–10, how likely are you to recommend us to a friend?"*

Customers are bucketed into:
- **Promoters (9–10):** Loyal enthusiasts who will fuel growth.
- **Passives (7–8):** Satisfied but vulnerable to competitors.
- **Detractors (0–6):** Unhappy customers who can damage brand reputation.

**Computation:**

$$
N = N_P + N_{Ps} + N_D = 200 + 120 + 80 = 400
$$

$$
\%P = \dfrac{200}{400} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

$$
\%D = \dfrac{80}{400} \times 100 = 20\%
$$

$$
\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D = 50 - 20 = \boxed{+30}
$$

**Interpretation:** An NPS of +30 places the company in the **"Good" zone** (Bain & Co. benchmarks: < 0 = Poor, 0–30 = Good, 30–50 = Great, > 50 = Excellent). The company should focus on **converting the 120 Passives into Promoters** through service-recovery programmes and personalized engagement, since they represent the most addressable loyalty gap.

(7 marks)

---

### Question B — 14 Marks *(Alternative to Q3)*

**Q4 (a)** What is the **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)**? Explain its **method of computation** with a suitable numerical example. **(7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEIET752]**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** is a weighted, multi-attribute measure of how well a firm delivers satisfaction across the dimensions its customers care about. It is mathematically a **weighted arithmetic mean** of average satisfaction ratings, where the weights reflect each attribute's importance.

**Computation Steps:**
1. Identify the $m$ key attributes that customers use to judge the product/service.
2. For each attribute $j$, compute the mean satisfaction rating $\bar{S}_j$ across all respondents.
3. For each attribute $j$, compute the importance weight $w_j$ (often via a constant-sum 100-point allocation).
4. Compute the weighted sum: $\text{CSI} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot \bar{S}_j$.
5. Normalize to a 0–100 scale: $\text{CSI}_{100} = \text{CSI}_{\text{raw}} \times \dfrac{100}{L_{\max}}$ where $L_{\max}$ is the maximum Likert value.

**Numerical Example:**
A smartphone brand measures satisfaction on 3 attributes on a 5-point Likert with importance weights:

| Attribute | Weight $w_j$ | Mean Score $\bar{S}_j$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Battery Life | 0.4 | 4.0 |
| Camera | 0.35 | 3.5 |
| Build Quality | 0.25 | 4.5 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CSI}_{\text{raw}} & = (0.4 \times 4.0) + (0.35 \times 3.5) + (0.25 \times 4.5) \\
& = 1.60 + 1.225 + 1.125 = 3.95
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{CSI}_{100} = 3.95 \times 20 = \boxed{79}
$$

**Interpretation:** A CSI of 79/100 is strong. The brand's weakest weighted contribution is *Camera* ($0.35 \times 3.5 = 1.225$), so camera improvements should be the next R&D priority.

(7 marks)

---

**Q4 (b)** Discuss the **strategies a firm can adopt to build customer satisfaction**. Substantiate your answer with the **Service-Profit Chain** logic. **(7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEIET752]**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Service-Profit Chain Logic:** Heskett et al. (1994) proposed that **internal service quality → employee satisfaction → employee productivity → external service value → customer satisfaction → customer loyalty → revenue growth and profitability**. Building CS is therefore a *system-wide* activity, not a marketing-only function.

**Key Strategies to Build Customer Satisfaction:**

1. **Customer-Centric Culture** — Embed customer-first thinking in every function. *Example:* Disney's "cast members" are trained to treat every park visitor as a guest.
2. **Effective Complaint Handling & Service Recovery** — Resolving complaints quickly can actually *increase* loyalty beyond the pre-complaint level ("service recovery paradox"). *Example:* Marriott responding to a negative review within 2 hours with a complimentary upgrade.
3. **Consistent Quality Delivery** — Use TQM, Six-Sigma, and ISO 9000 standards to minimize variability. *Example:* Toyota's quality circles reducing defect rates to < 1 ppm.
4. **Personalization & Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** — Use data analytics to deliver individualized offers. *Example:* Amazon's recommendation engine driving 35% of revenue.
5. **Empowered Front-line Employees** — Give staff the authority to resolve issues on the spot. *Example:* Ritz-Carlton allowing any employee to spend up to $2,000 to make a guest happy.
6. **Continuous Voice-of-Customer (VoC) Listening** — Deploy regular NPS, CSAT and focus-group feedback. *Example:* Apple using beta-tester communities for iOS refinement.
7. **Transparent Communication** — Set realistic expectations to avoid the negative disconfirmation gap. *Example:* Domino's Pizza Tracker giving real-time delivery updates.

**Conclusion:** Each of these strategies closes one or more of the **5 SERVQUAL gaps**, and together they activate the Service-Profit Chain — converting satisfaction into measurable financial growth.

(7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Print this list and revise it 30 minutes before the KTU ESE.

- **Customer Satisfaction = Perception − Expectation** (Oliver's ECT).
- The **5 SERVQUAL gaps** all funnel into **Gap 5 (Customer Gap)**.
- The **5 SERVQUAL dimensions** are Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy.
- **NPS = % Promoters − % Detractors**; range is −100 to +100.
- **CSAT** is a *raw mean rating* (1–5 or 1–7 Likert).
- **CSI** is an *importance-weighted* index normalized to 0–100.
- **CS ≠ Loyalty** — loyalty also depends on switching cost, alternatives, and trust.
- The **Service-Profit Chain** links employee satisfaction → customer satisfaction → profitability.
- The **Importance–Performance Matrix** has 4 quadrants: *Concentrate Here, Maintain, Deprioritize, Possible Overkill*.
- The **8 Cs of CS Building**: Customer-centric culture, Communication, Complaint handling, Consistency, Competence, Convenience, Courtesy, Continuous improvement.
- **Service recovery paradox**: a well-handled complaint can raise loyalty above pre-complaint levels.
- Always **close with a diagram or formula** — KTU examiners reward visual reasoning.
- **High-yield one-liner to memorize:** *"Satisfied customers tell a few friends; delighted customers tell the world."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trends in Marketing Management: Relationship Marketing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trends in Marketing Management: Relationship Marketing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relationship Marketing (RM)** is a strategic marketing philosophy and practice that focuses on **building, maintaining, and enhancing long-term, mutually beneficial relationships** with customers, suppliers, employees, and other stakeholders, rather than concentrating solely on single, discrete transactions.

According to **Morgan and Hunt (1994)** — the seminal scholarly reference cited across KTU-approved marketing syllabi — Relationship Marketing is defined as:

> *"All marketing activities directed toward establishing, developing, and maintaining successful relational exchanges."*

The **American Marketing Association (AMA)** further refines this as the practice of creating and sustaining mutually satisfying long-term relationships with customers, suppliers, distributors, and other strategic partners in order to earn and retain their long-term preference and business.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Relationship Marketing is the ongoing process of identifying and creating new value with individual customers and then sharing the benefits of this over a lifetime of association. It involves understanding, anticipating, and managing the needs and wants of the customer over an indefinite period."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **🍕 Real-World Analogy — "The Local Bakery vs. The Pizza Chain"**

Imagine two bakeries:

1. **Big Pizza Chain (Traditional/Transactional Marketing):** Every time you walk in, the cashier treats you like a stranger. They sell you a pizza, you pay, you leave. Tomorrow, another stranger sells you another pizza. There's no memory of you — only the **single transaction** matters. Their motto: *"Make the sale, move on."*

2. **Local Family Bakery (Relationship Marketing):** The owner knows your name. She remembers that your daughter is allergic to nuts, that you prefer brown bread on Tuesdays, and that you celebrated your anniversary last week (she gave you a free cupcake). She sends you a WhatsApp message when fresh croissants arrive. You keep coming back — not just for bread, but for the **relationship**. Her motto: *"Make a friend, earn a customer for life."*

**The Shift in Mindset:**

| Aspect | Transactional Marketing | Relationship Marketing |
|---|---|---|
| Time horizon | Short-term (single sale) | **Long-term (lifetime value)** |
| Focus | Product features | **Customer experience & trust** |
| Goal | One-time profit | **Customer loyalty & retention** |
| Customer view | Anonymous buyer | **Known individual/partner** |
| Metrics | Market share | **Share of customer wallet** |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Check:** If a student understands why people queue at a small tea stall despite a Starbucks being next door — *because the stall owner knows their name and sugar preference* — they have intuitively grasped Relationship Marketing.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Marketing Metrics

The following **standard industry metrics** form the quantitative backbone of Relationship Marketing:

- **Customer Retention Rate (CRR):** Expressed as a **percentage (%)**, measuring the proportion of customers retained over a period.
- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV / LTV):** Expressed in **monetary units (₹, $, €)**, representing the total net profit a customer generates over their entire relationship with the firm.
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS):** Expressed on a scale of **−100 to +100**.
- **Churn Rate:** Expressed as a **percentage (%)**, representing customers lost per period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These four metrics — **CRR, CLV, NPS, Churn Rate** — are repeatedly tested in KTU ESE questions on this topic. Memorize their formulas (provided in Section 2).

---

## 1.4 Evolution: From Marketing 1.0 → Marketing 5.0 (KTU Board Context)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically expects students to know **WHY** Relationship Marketing emerged. The answer lies in the evolution of marketing thought:

1. **Marketing 1.0 (Pre-1960s):** Product-centric. *Example: Henry Ford's "any color as long as it's black."*
2. **Marketing 2.0 (1960s–1990s):** Customer-centric but still transactional. Segmentation and targeting.
3. **Marketing 3.0 (1990s–2010s):** Values-driven, human-centric marketing — **birthplace of Relationship Marketing**.
4. **Marketing 4.0 (2010s–2020s):** Digital transformation, omnichannel, mobile-first.
5. **Marketing 5.0 (Current):** AI-driven, data-centric, hyper-personalized relationship building using **Customer Data Platforms (CDPs)** and **predictive analytics**.

**Key Driver of RM's Rise:** The recognition that acquiring a new customer costs **5 to 7 times more** than retaining an existing one (a metric from the Harvard Business Review — a KTU-recommended reading).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation: Morgan & Hunt's Commitment-Trust Theory

The **most cited academic theory** in KTU question papers on Relationship Marketing is the **Commitment-Trust Theory** proposed by **Morgan and Hunt (1994)**. It states that **relationship marketing success depends on two mediating variables:**

1. **Relationship Commitment** — the belief that an ongoing relationship is so important that it warrants maximum effort to maintain it.
2. **Trust** — confidence in the exchange partner's reliability and integrity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Tenet:** Commitment and Trust are the **two foundational pillars**. Without them, Relationship Marketing collapses into mere transactional exchanges.

---

## 2.2 The Six Markets Model (Christopher, Payne, Ballantyne)

A cornerstone KTU framework that broadens Relationship Marketing beyond customers:

| Market | Stakeholder | RM Objective |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Markets** | End buyers | Build loyalty & lifetime value |
| **Supplier Markets** | Vendors/partners | Create strategic alliances |
| **Internal Markets** | Employees | Develop employee engagement |
| **Recruitment Markets** | Prospective employees | Build employer brand |
| **Influence Markets** | Govt, media, NGOs | Manage stakeholder perception |
| **Referral Markets** | Existing customers, advocates | Encourage word-of-mouth |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever the question asks *"Expand the scope of Relationship Marketing"*, immediately list these **six markets** with one-line examples for each.

---

## 2.3 The Loyalty Ladder (Customer Relationship Progression)

A classic KTU-tested framework describing the **stages of customer relationships**:

$$\text{Prospect} \rightarrow \text{Customer} \rightarrow \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Supporter} \rightarrow \text{Advocate} \rightarrow \text{Partner}$$

Each rung represents a **deeper commitment** by the customer.

---

## 2.4 Levels of Relationship Marketing (Five-Level Loyalty Hierarchy — KTU Favourite)

Based on **Berry's** and **Peppers & Rogers** frameworks, KTU examiners frequently test the **five levels of customer retention strategy**:

1. **Level 1 — Basic Marketing:** No formal retention; price-driven transactions.
2. **Level 2 — Reactive Marketing:** Salesperson reacts to customer complaints.
3. **Level 3 — Accountable Marketing:** Salesperson calls customer post-purchase to check satisfaction.
4. **Level 4 — Proactive Marketing:** Firm continuously contacts customer with new ideas & improvement suggestions.
5. **Level 5 — Partnership Marketing:** Firm works jointly with customer to identify opportunities and solve problems (B2B context).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are high-frequency, board-tested quantitative metrics for Relationship Marketing.**

| # | Metric | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** | $\text{CRR} = \dfrac{(E - N)}{S} \times 100$ | $E$ = Customers at end, $N$ = New customers, $S$ = Customers at start. Unit: **%** |
| 2 | **Churn Rate (Attrition Rate)** | $\text{Churn Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Customers Lost}}{\text{Customers at Start}} \times 100$ | Unit: **%** |
| 3 | **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(R_t - C_t)}{(1 + d)^t}$ | $R_t$ = Revenue at time $t$, $C_t$ = Cost at time $t$, $d$ = discount rate, $T$ = time horizon. Unit: **₹/$** |
| 4 | **CLV (Simplified)** | $\text{CLV} = \text{AOV} \times \text{APF} \times \text{ACV} \times \text{ACL}$ | AOV = Avg Order Value, APF = Annual Purchase Frequency, ACV = Avg Customer Value, ACL = Avg Customer Lifespan. Unit: **₹/$** |
| 5 | **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$ | Range: **−100 to +100** |
| 6 | **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)** | $\text{CAC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Marketing \& Sales Spend}}{\text{New Customers Acquired}}$ | Unit: **₹/$** |
| 7 | **CLV:CAC Ratio** | $\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{CLV}}{\text{CAC}}$ | Healthy benchmark: **3:1 or higher** |
| 8 | **Repurchase Rate** | $\text{Repurchase Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Returning Customers}}{\text{Total Customers}} \times 100$ | Unit: **%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write $|R_t - C_t|$ using absolute value notation in formulas, which **breaks markdown tables**. Always use the notation shown above.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Business Decision-Making

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter in practice:**

- A higher **CLV:CAC ratio** signals that the firm earns significantly more from a customer than it spends to acquire one — a green light for scaling marketing budgets.
- A **Churn Rate** above **5–7% annually** (B2B SaaS benchmark) triggers alarm bells for the customer success team.
- **NPS** above **50** is considered world-class (Apple, Tesla); below **0** indicates systemic problems.
- These metrics power **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** software like Salesforce, HubSpot, and Zoho.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Problems

> [!NOTE]
> This section demonstrates the **step-by-step application** of KTU-board-tested RM metrics. Every step is explicitly shown to align with KTU valuation expectations.

---

## 3.1 Worked Problem 1: Customer Retention Rate (CRR) Calculation

### Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU Pattern)
> *"A mobile recharge company had **8,000 subscribers** at the start of Q1 2024. During the quarter, they lost **600 subscribers** and acquired **1,200 new subscribers**. At the end of the quarter, they had **8,600 subscribers**. Calculate the **Customer Retention Rate** and the **Churn Rate**. Comment on the implications for Relationship Marketing strategy."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given variables.**
- Customers at start ($S$) = **8,000**
- Customers at end ($E$) = **8,600**
- New customers acquired ($N$) = **1,200**

**Step 2 — Apply the CRR formula.**
From the cheat sheet:

$$\text{CRR} = \dfrac{(E - N)}{S} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$\text{CRR} = \dfrac{(8{,}600 - 1{,}200)}{8{,}000} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator.**

$$E - N = 8{,}600 - 1{,}200 = 7{,}400$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $S$ and multiply by 100.**

$$\text{CRR} = \dfrac{7{,}400}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 0.925 \times 100 = 92.5\%$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Churn Rate.**
The complement of CRR (assuming no internal migration):

$$\text{Churn Rate} = 100\% - 92.5\% = 7.5\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** CRR = **92.5%**; Churn Rate = **7.5%**.
> **RM Implication:** A 7.5% churn is borderline for the telecom industry (benchmark ~5–10%). The firm should implement **loyalty programs, personalized recharge offers, and proactive complaint resolution** — all hallmarks of Levels 3–5 Relationship Marketing.

---

## 3.2 Worked Problem 2: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) Simplified

### Problem Statement
> *"An online grocery platform reports the following metrics for a typical customer:*
> - *Average Order Value (AOV) = ₹800*
> - *Annual Purchase Frequency (APF) = 12 orders/year*
> - *Average Customer Lifespan (ACL) = 3 years*
> - *Gross Margin per transaction = 25%*
>
> *Compute the Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify variables.**
- $AOV = 800$
- $APF = 12$
- $ACL = 3$
- Margin = 25% = 0.25

**Step 2 — Apply the simplified CLV formula.**

$$\text{CLV} = \text{AOV} \times \text{APF} \times \text{ACV} \times \text{ACL}$$

where $\text{ACV}$ (Average Customer Value) is the **margin-adjusted revenue per order**:

$$\text{ACV} = \text{AOV} \times \text{Margin} = 800 \times 0.25 = 200$$

**Step 3 — Compute ACV explicitly.**

$$\text{ACV} = 800 \times 0.25 = 200$$

**Step 4 — Multiply ACV by APF to get Annual Customer Value.**

$$\text{Annual Value} = \text{ACV} \times \text{APF} = 200 \times 12 = 2{,}400$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by ACL to get CLV.**

$$\text{CLV} = 2{,}400 \times 3 = 7{,}200$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** CLV = **₹7,200** per customer.
> **RM Implication:** If the firm's Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) is ₹2,000, then $\text{CLV : CAC} = 7{,}200 : 2{,}000 = 3.6 : 1$, which is **healthy and justifies increased relationship-marketing investment**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem 3: NPS Computation with Strategy

### Problem Statement
> *"A retail brand surveyed 2,000 customers post-purchase. 1,100 were Promoters, 600 were Passives, and 300 were Detractors. Calculate NPS and suggest two RM initiatives."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute % Promoters.**

$$\%\text{Promoters} = \dfrac{1{,}100}{2{,}000} \times 100 = 55\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute % Detractors.**

$$\%\text{Detractors} = \dfrac{300}{2{,}000} \times 100 = 15\%$$

**Step 3 — Apply NPS formula.**

$$\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors} = 55 - 15 = 40$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** NPS = **+40** (good, but below world-class benchmark of +50).
> **Suggested RM Initiatives:**
> 1. **Loyalty Rewards Program** — convert Passives (30%) into Promoters.
> 2. **Closed-Loop Feedback** — personally reach out to all 300 Detractors to convert them into satisfied customers.

---

## 3.4 Worked Problem 4: Multi-Year CLV with Discount Rate

### Problem Statement
> *"A SaaS company earns ₹1,000 profit per customer per year. The average customer stays 3 years. The discount rate is 10% per annum. Compute the discounted CLV."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Use the present-value CLV formula.**

$$\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \dfrac{R_t}{(1 + d)^t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:** $R_t = 1{,}000$, $d = 0.10$, $T = 3$.

$$\text{CLV} = \dfrac{1{,}000}{1.10} + \dfrac{1{,}000}{(1.10)^2} + \dfrac{1{,}000}{(1.10)^3}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each year's discounted value.**

$$\dfrac{1{,}000}{1.10} = 909.09$$

$$\dfrac{1{,}000}{1.21} = 826.45$$

$$\dfrac{1{,}000}{1.331} = 751.31$$

**Step 4 — Sum the discounted cash flows.**

$$\text{CLV} = 909.09 + 826.45 + 751.31 = 2{,}486.85$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** Discounted CLV = **₹2,486.85 per customer**.
> **Insight:** Without discounting, CLV would be ₹3,000. The **₹513.15 difference** represents the time value of money — an important KTU concept.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **complete Relationship Marketing framework** — a high-yield visual that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce or describe in 14-mark questions.

## 4.1 Relationship Marketing Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Customer Segments] --> B[Acquire New Customers via CRM]
    B --> C[Convert Transactional Buyers into Repeat Buyers]
    C --> D[Build Trust and Commitment]
    D --> E[Deliver Superior Customer Experience]
    E --> F[Achieve Customer Loyalty]
    F --> G[Transform Loyal Customers into Advocates]
    G --> H[Generate Referrals and Word of Mouth]
    H --> I[Increase Customer Lifetime Value CLV]
    I --> J[Reduce Churn Rate and CAC]
    J --> K[Sustainable Long Term Profitability]
    F --> L[Five Levels of Relationship Marketing]
    L --> L1[Level 1 Basic Marketing]
    L --> L2[Level 2 Reactive Marketing]
    L --> L3[Level 3 Accountable Marketing]
    L --> L4[Level 4 Proactive Marketing]
    L --> L5[Level 5 Partnership Marketing]
    D --> M[Six Markets Model]
    M --> M1[Customer Markets]
    M --> M2[Supplier Markets]
    M --> M3[Internal Markets]
    M --> M4[Recruitment Markets]
    M --> M5[Influence Markets]
    M --> M6[Referral Markets]
```

## 4.2 Textual Walkthrough of the Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Flow:**
>
> 1. The **left spine (A → K)** represents the **sequential customer journey** in a Relationship Marketing strategy — from initial segmentation to sustainable profitability.
> 2. The **three right branches (L, M, and the implicit metrics)** represent the **supporting frameworks** that operationalize the journey:
>    - **Five Levels of Relationship Marketing (L1–L5)** — describes *how* the firm deepens the relationship.
>    - **Six Markets Model (M1–M6)** — describes *with whom* the firm builds relationships (not just customers!).
>    - **KPIs (CLV, Churn, NPS)** — measure *how well* the firm is doing.
>
> 3. **Key insight:** Trust and Commitment (node D) feed **both** the loyalty ladder (toward K) **and** the broader stakeholder network (Six Markets). This dual role makes them the **strategic heart of Relationship Marketing**.

## 4.3 The Loyalty Ladder — Visual Representation

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  PARTNER    ← Co-create products, joint ventures        │  Highest RM
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  ADVOCATE   ← Actively refers others, defends brand      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  SUPPORTER  ← Defends brand passively when criticized    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  CLIENT     ← Regular repeat purchases, cross-sells      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  CUSTOMER   ← Occasional repeat purchases                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  PROSPECT   ← First-time buyer / lead                    │  Entry-level
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        ↑ Each rung requires deepening trust & commitment ↑
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw this ladder in your answer script. Examiners reward **visual representation** generously — it earns 1–2 extra marks even if the surrounding text is average.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]
> **Define Relationship Marketing. How does it differ from Transactional Marketing?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Relationship Marketing is a long-term strategic approach focused on **building, maintaining, and enhancing mutually beneficial relationships** with customers and other stakeholders, with the objective of retaining them for life rather than completing a single transaction.

**Three Key Differences (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Transactional Marketing | Relationship Marketing |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | One-time sale | Long-term loyalty |
| **Customer View** | Anonymous buyer | Known individual |
| **Metric** | Market share | Share of customer wallet |

**Closing line:** Hence, RM shifts the marketing focus from *acquiring customers* to *retaining and growing* them.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
> **List any THREE levels of Relationship Marketing as proposed by Berry.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each)

1. **Level 1 — Basic Marketing:** No special retention efforts; pure product/price focus.
2. **Level 3 — Accountable Marketing:** Salesperson contacts the customer after the sale to verify satisfaction and remove any post-purchase anxiety.
3. **Level 5 — Partnership Marketing:** The firm and customer work jointly as partners, co-creating value and solving problems collaboratively (typical of B2B industrial marketing).

*(Note: Students may list any three of the five levels for full credit.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in Part B: students answer either **Question A OR Question B**.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]

> **"Customer Lifetime Value is the most critical metric in modern Relationship Marketing." Discuss.**
>
> **Sub-parts:**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of CLV with a suitable formula and illustrate with a numerical example. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Discuss the strategic significance of CLV in designing customer retention programs. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Concept of CLV with Formula and Example (7 Marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) is the total net profit a firm can expect from a customer over the entire duration of their relationship. It shifts managerial focus from short-term sales to **long-term customer economics**.

**Simplified Formula (2 marks):**

$$\text{CLV} = \text{AOV} \times \text{APF} \times \text{ACV} \times \text{ACL}$$

**Numerical Example (3 marks):**

> *A coffee subscription service has AOV = ₹500, APF = 24 per year, ACL = 5 years, and a 30% gross margin per transaction.*

$$\text{ACV} = 500 \times 0.30 = 150$$

$$\text{Annual Value} = 150 \times 24 = 3{,}600$$

$$\text{CLV} = 3{,}600 \times 5 = 18{,}000$$

**Conclusion:** Each customer is worth **₹18,000** to the firm — a powerful justification for spending up to ₹6,000 on acquisition (3:1 ratio).

---

**(b) Strategic Significance of CLV in Retention Programs (7 Marks)**

**1. Resource Allocation (2 marks):** Firms can segment customers by CLV (High, Medium, Low) and allocate **premium service** (Level 5 RM — Partnership Marketing) to high-CLV customers while automating service for low-CLV segments.

**2. Justifies Loyalty Investment (2 marks):** When CLV >> CAC, firms have strong economic justification to invest in loyalty programs, personalized marketing, and CRM technology (Salesforce, HubSpot).

**3. Predicts Cash Flow (2 marks):** Discounted CLV provides a **present-value estimate** of future revenue, useful for financial planning and investor communication.

**4. Drives Relationship Depth (1 mark):** The metric incentivizes firms to move customers up the **Loyalty Ladder** (Prospect → Partner), thereby deepening relationships.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **"The Six Markets Model provides a holistic framework for Relationship Marketing." Elaborate.**
>
> **Sub-parts:**
> **(a)** Explain Christopher, Payne & Ballantyne's Six Markets Model with examples. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Compare Relationship Marketing with Transactional Marketing in terms of objectives, customer engagement, time horizon, and metrics. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) The Six Markets Model (7 Marks — 1 mark each for market + 1 mark overall coherence)**

| Market | Stakeholder | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Markets** | End users / buyers | Amazon's personalized recommendations |
| **Supplier Markets** | Vendors, raw material providers | Toyota's long-term alliances with parts suppliers |
| **Internal Markets** | Employees | Infosys's internal training & engagement programs |
| **Recruitment Markets** | Prospective employees | Google's employer branding via "Life at Google" |
| **Influence Markets** | Govt, media, NGOs | P&G's sustainability reports to environmental NGOs |
| **Referral Markets** | Existing customers, fans | Dropbox's referral bonus program |

**Coherence (1 mark):** Together, these six markets ensure that RM is **not limited to customers alone**, but extends to every stakeholder that influences or enables the firm's value delivery.

---

**(b) Comparison Table (7 Marks)**

| Dimension | Transactional Marketing | Relationship Marketing |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective (2 marks)** | Maximize single-transaction profit | Maximize customer lifetime value |
| **Customer Engagement (2 marks)** | Minimal — only during purchase | Continuous — pre, during, post-purchase |
| **Time Horizon (2 marks)** | Short-term | Long-term, often indefinite |
| **Key Metrics (1 mark)** | Sales volume, market share | CLV, NPS, retention rate, churn |

**Concluding remark:** While transactional marketing suits commodity FMCG sales, RM is essential for service industries, B2B markets, and subscription-based businesses.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing RM with CRM:** Many students define *Customer Relationship Management* (the software/tool) as Relationship Marketing (the strategy). **CRM is a tool that ENABLES RM, not RM itself.**
> 2. **Forgetting the Six Markets:** A common mistake is to discuss RM only in the context of customers. KTU examiners specifically look for **supplier, internal, and referral markets** — losing these means losing 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping Numbers in CLV questions:** Even in theory questions, KTU values **one numerical illustration**. Without a worked example, you may lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up levels:** Level 4 and Level 5 of Berry's framework are frequently swapped by students. Remember — *Partnership* is the deepest level, not *Proactive*.
> 5. **Ignoring time value of money:** When CLV is computed over multiple years, **discounting is mandatory** in advanced questions. Skipping the discount rate is a 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Relationship Marketing**

**🔹 Conceptual Anchors:**
- RM = **long-term, mutually beneficial** stakeholder relationships.
- Pioneered conceptually by **Berry (1983)** and academically formalized by **Morgan & Hunt (1994)**.
- Built on **two pillars: Trust + Commitment**.

**🔹 Key Frameworks (must-know):**
- **Loyalty Ladder:** Prospect → Customer → Client → Supporter → Advocate → Partner.
- **Five Levels of RM:** Basic → Reactive → Accountable → Proactive → Partnership.
- **Six Markets Model:** Customer, Supplier, Internal, Recruitment, Influence, Referral.

**🔹 Must-Memorize Formulas:**
- $\text{CRR} = \dfrac{(E - N)}{S} \times 100$
- $\text{Churn Rate} = 100\% - \text{CRR}$ (approximately, in simple models)
- $\text{CLV} = \text{AOV} \times \text{APF} \times \text{ACV} \times \text{ACL}$
- Discounted $\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \dfrac{R_t}{(1 + d)^t}$
- $\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$
- $\text{CLV:CAC Ratio} \geq 3:1$ (healthy benchmark)

**🔹 Differentiators (board-favorite distinctions):**
- RM vs TM (goal, time, customer view, metrics).
- CRM (tool) vs RM (strategy).
- Customer Acquisition vs Customer Retention (cost ratio 5:1 to 7:1).
- Share of Market vs Share of Customer Wallet.

**🔹 Strategic Insights:**
- Acquiring a customer costs **5–7×** more than retaining one.
- A **5% increase in retention** can boost profits by **25–95%** (Bain & Co.).
- NPS above +50 = world-class; below 0 = crisis.

**🔹 Real-World Anchors (for examiner delight):**
- Amazon — personalization engine.
- Apple — ecosystem lock-in (devices + services).
- Maruti Suzuki — dealer & supplier partnership model.
- Zomato Gold / Amazon Prime — subscription-based RM.

**🔹 Common Board Mistakes to Avoid:**
- ❌ Treating RM as a software (it's a philosophy).
- ❌ Limiting RM to customers (Six Markets model exists!).
- ❌ Skipping numerical examples in CLV answers.
- ❌ Forgetting to discount multi-year CLV.
- ❌ Missing the Trust-Commitment theoretical foundation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET752 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relationship Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Relationship Management (RM)**, in the KTU 2024 Scheme Marketing Management framework, is defined as the strategic, continuous, and data-driven process of identifying, creating, building, maintaining, and enhancing long-term, mutually beneficial relationships with customers, suppliers, distributors, and other marketing partners to ensure long-term customer loyalty, retention, and profitability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "Relationship Management is the ongoing process of engaging with customers, prospects, and other stakeholders to build mutual trust, brand advocacy, and long-term economic value, beyond the traditional point-of-sale transaction."

It encompasses two interconnected sub-disciplines that examiners frequently test:

1. **Relationship Marketing (RMk)** — A marketing philosophy focused on building and sustaining long-term customer relationships rather than one-time transactions.
2. **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** — The technology-enabled operational system and business strategy used to implement Relationship Marketing at scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference Distinction:**
> - **Relationship Marketing = The Philosophy (WHY we do it)**
> - **CRM = The System/Tool (HOW we execute it)**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two ways to feed yourself:

- **Hunting (Transactional Marketing):** Every meal requires a fresh hunt. You chase a new deer each day. The moment a deer escapes or moves away, you lose the meal. High energy expenditure, zero continuity, no predictability.
- **Farming (Relationship Marketing):** You invest time to plant seeds, water crops, tend the soil, and protect the harvest. Over time, the farm yields continuous produce with relatively lower marginal effort. Customers return season after season.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** *“Hunters chase customers, Farmers grow customers.”* This single line differentiates Transactional vs. Relationship Marketing in any 3-mark or 14-mark question.

| Dimension | Transactional Marketing (Hunting) | Relationship Marketing (Farming) |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Horizon** | Short-term, one-off | Long-term, continuous |
| **Focus** | Single sale | Customer lifetime value |
| **Customer Contact** | Minimal, at point-of-sale | Ongoing, multi-channel |
| **Goal** | Maximize transaction value | Maximize customer lifetime value |
| **Communication** | Mass, one-way | Personalized, two-way |
| **Investment Pattern** | High per-acquisition cost | High retention investment |

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Constants

The following **bold** metrics are essential for any KTU numerical or descriptive question on Relationship Management:

- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** — the cornerstone metric of RM.
- **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** — typically expressed as a percentage.
- **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)** — measured in monetary units.
- **Churn Rate** — the inverse of retention; usually **5%–10%** considered healthy in B2C sectors.
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** — ranges from **-100 to +100**; an industry benchmark of **>50** signals strong RM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 papers heavily favor definitions in Part A. Memorize the exact phrases *"mutually beneficial long-term relationship"* and *"customer lifetime value"* — these are high-frequency high-yield phrases.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Levels of Customer Relationship (Morgan & Hunt Adapted Model)

KTU examiners consistently test the **Five Levels of Customer Relationship**. Each level represents a deeper degree of customer bonding:

1. **Basic Relationship** — Vendor sells the product, no follow-up (e.g., vending machines, fast food counters).
2. **Reactive Relationship** — Seller contacts customer only when something breaks or to remind of refills (e.g., appliance service calls).
3. **Accountable Relationship** — Seller proactively checks in post-sale to ensure satisfaction (e.g., software account managers).
4. **Proactive Relationship** — Seller continuously works with the customer to improve outcomes (e.g., consulting firms like McKinsey).
5. **Partnership Relationship** — Seller and customer co-create value and grow together (e.g., Apple and Foxconn, Toyota and its Tier-1 suppliers).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid (B-R-A-P-P):** **B**asic, **R**eactive, **A**ccountable, **P**roactive, **P**artnership — ascending order of customer bonding intensity.

---

## 2.2 The CRM Operational Process — Step-by-Step Logic

CRM is a closed-loop system. Each step feeds intelligence into the next iteration:

- **Step 1: Customer Data Acquisition**
  Capture customer information through POS systems, website analytics, loyalty programs, social media, and CRM software (Salesforce, Zoho, HubSpot).

- **Step 2: Data Integration & Warehousing**
  Consolidate scattered data into a unified *Single Customer View (SCV)* using data lakes or warehouses (e.g., Snowflake, AWS Redshift).

- **Step 3: Customer Segmentation & Analysis**
  Apply RFM analysis (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) and clustering algorithms to identify high-value and at-risk segments.

- **Step 4: Personalized Engagement & Campaign Execution**
  Deploy targeted email, SMS, push notifications, and offers based on segment insights.

- **Step 5: Performance Monitoring & Feedback Loop**
  Track KPIs (retention, NPS, CLV, churn), feed back into the system, and refine strategies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Without Step 5 (feedback), the system becomes a one-way broadcast. RM is a *closed-loop* discipline — feedback is non-negotiable.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on notation:** The vertical bar symbol is rendered as `\vert` to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $$CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(R_t - C_t)}{(1+i)^{t}} - AC$$ | Total net present value a customer generates over their lifetime. |
| 2 | **Average Customer Lifetime (from Retention)** | $$L = \frac{1}{1 - r}$$ | Where $r$ = annual retention rate (in decimal). |
| 3 | **Net Present Value of a Customer** | $$NPV = M \cdot \frac{r}{(1+i-r)} - AC$$ | Simplified CLV where $M$ = annual margin per customer. |
| 4 | **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** | $$CRR = \frac{(E - N)}{S} \times 100$$ | $E$ = end customers, $N$ = new customers, $S$ = start customers. |
| 5 | **Churn Rate** | $$Churn = 1 - CRR$$ | Proportion of customers lost over a period. |
| 6 | **RFM Scoring** | $$Score = w_R \cdot R + w_F \cdot F + w_M \cdot M$$ | Weighted Recency–Frequency–Monetary score; $w$ = weights. |
| 7 | **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $$NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$$ | Loyalty indicator; range: -100 to +100. |
| 8 | **CLV : CAC Ratio** | $$Ratio = \frac{CLV}{CAC}$$ | Healthy benchmark: $\geq 3:1$. |

**Units of measurement:**
- $R_t, C_t, AC, M$ → Currency (₹, $, etc.)
- $r, i$ → Decimal (e.g., 0.85 for 85%)
- $t, T$ → Time (years)
- $L$ → Years

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

Relationship Management is no longer confined to marketing — it powers:
- **Predictive analytics in manufacturing** (predicting component failures for key clients).
- **IoT-based service contracts** (e.g., Rolls-Royce's "Power by the Hour" — engines monitored in real time, customers pay per flying hour).
- **B2B supply chain risk mitigation** (long-term partner relationships reduce disruption).
- **Subscription-based SaaS models** (entire business model rests on retention and CLV).

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU viva voce, linking RM to real Indian cases (e.g., *Amul's cooperative model*, *Tata's vendor partnerships*, *Flipkart Plus loyalty*) scores high marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Customer Lifetime from Retention Rate

We start from the basic premise: if the annual retention rate is $r$, the probability a customer stays for exactly $n$ years follows a geometric distribution.

**Step 1:** Probability customer stays in Year 1 = $r$.

**Step 2:** Probability customer stays in Year 2 = $r \cdot r = r^{2}$.

**Step 3:** Generalize: Probability customer stays for exactly $n$ years = $r^{n}$.

**Step 4:** Expected lifetime is the sum of probabilities of being active in each year.

$$
L = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} r^{n}
$$

**Step 5:** Recognize this as an infinite geometric series with first term $a = r$ and common ratio $r$ (assuming $0 < r < 1$).

$$
L = \frac{a}{1 - r} = \frac{r}{1 - r}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $a = r$ into the standard geometric sum formula $\frac{a}{1-r}$.

**Step 7:** Add Year 0 (the initial transaction year when the customer is already present).

$$
L = 1 + \frac{r}{1 - r}
$$

**Step 8:** Combine the two terms over a common denominator.

$$
L = \frac{(1 - r) + r}{1 - r}
$$

**Step 9:** Simplify the numerator: $(1 - r) + r = 1$.

$$
L = \frac{1}{1 - r}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
L = \frac{1}{1 - r} \quad \text{(years)}
$$

**Interpretation:** If retention $r = 0.80$, then $L = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5$ years. A 5% increase in retention can nearly double customer lifetime in some industries.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: NPV of a Customer (Simplified CLV)

Consider a customer contributing a constant annual margin $M$ forever (perpetuity assumption), with retention rate $r$ and discount rate $i$.

**Step 1:** Net cash flow in year $t$ from the customer is $M \cdot r^{t}$ (margin discounted by the chance of survival).

**Step 2:** Discount this to present value using rate $i$.

$$
PV_t = \frac{M \cdot r^{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum across all future years (infinite horizon).

$$
PV_{total} = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \frac{M \cdot r^{t}}{(1+i)^{t}}
$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $M$.

$$
PV_{total} = M \cdot \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \left( \frac{r}{1+i} \right)^{t}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the geometric series formula $\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} x^{t} = \frac{1}{1-x}$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$.

$$
PV_{total} = M \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{r}{1+i}}
$$

**Step 6:** Rationalize the denominator by multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1+i)$.

$$
PV_{total} = M \cdot \frac{1+i}{(1+i) - r}
$$

**Step 7:** Subtract the acquisition cost $AC$ to obtain Net Present Value (CLV).

$$
CLV = \frac{M(1+i)}{1 + i - r} - AC
$$

**Final Compact Form:**

$$
CLV = \frac{M \cdot r}{(1 + i - r)} - AC
$$

*(The $(1+i)$ in numerator is often absorbed into margin assumptions depending on whether $M$ is real or nominal.)*

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Sub-Part)

**Problem:** A telecom company acquires a customer for ₹2,000. The customer pays an annual margin of ₹1,200. The annual retention rate is 80%, and the discount rate is 10%. Calculate:
- (i) Average customer lifetime
- (ii) Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)
- (iii) CLV : CAC ratio and comment on profitability.

### Solution

**Part (i) — Average Lifetime:**

$$
L = \frac{1}{1 - r} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.80} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5 \text{ years}
$$

The customer is expected to remain for 5 years.

**Part (ii) — CLV Calculation:**

We use the formula derived above:

$$
CLV = \frac{M \cdot r}{1 + i - r} - AC
$$

**Step A:** Substitute the given values.

$$
CLV = \frac{1200 \times 0.80}{1 + 0.10 - 0.80} - 2000
$$

**Step B:** Compute the numerator.

$$
CLV = \frac{960}{0.30} - 2000
$$

**Step C:** Perform the division.

$$
CLV = 3200 - 2000
$$

**Step D:** Subtract acquisition cost.

$$
CLV = \text{₹}1200
$$

The CLV is ₹1,200 per customer.

**Part (iii) — CLV : CAC Ratio:**

$$
Ratio = \frac{CLV}{CAC} = \frac{1200}{2000} = 0.60
$$

**Interpretation:** A ratio of 0.60 is **below the healthy benchmark of 3:1**. The company is losing money on every customer. Recommendation: either reduce CAC (cheaper acquisition channels) or boost retention (loyalty programs) to make the business sustainable.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: RM Strategy vs. Customer Segment

| Customer Segment | Recommended RM Strategy | Tool/Technique | Expected KPI Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High-Value Loyal** | Partnership Level | Dedicated account manager, co-creation | +20% wallet share |
| **High-Value At-Risk** | Proactive win-back | Personalized outreach, special offers | Reduce churn by 30% |
| **Low-Value Loyal** | Reward & incentivize | Loyalty points, gamification | Increase frequency |
| **New Customers** | Onboarding & education | Drip campaigns, tutorials | Boost 90-day retention |
| **Lapsed Customers** | Reactivation campaign | Win-back offers, surveys | Recover 5–10% |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CRM Operational Loop — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the closed-loop CRM process as a functional architecture flow, mapping how data, intelligence, and engagement interact across the enterprise.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Customer Touchpoints Web Mobile POS Social] --> B1[Data Acquisition Layer]
    B1 --> C1[Integration and Data Warehouse]
    C1 --> D1[Customer Analytics Engine RFM Clustering]
    D1 --> E1[Segmentation Output High Value At Risk New]
    E1 --> F1[Personalized Engagement Email SMS Push Offers]
    F1 --> G1[Customer Experience Delivered]
    G1 --> H1[KPI Monitoring Retention NPS CLV Churn]
    H1 -->|Feedback Loop| B1
    H1 --> I1[Management Dashboard Strategic Decisions]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The cycle begins at touchpoints (A1), flows through data and analytics, reaches customers via personalized engagement, and finally loops back from KPI monitoring (H1) to data acquisition (B1) — closing the feedback loop essential for continuous improvement.

---

## 4.2 Five Levels of Relationship Intensity — Escalation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Level 1 Basic] --> L2[Level 2 Reactive]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Accountable]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Proactive]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 Partnership]
    L1 -.->|Lowest bond| X1[Customer leaves easily]
    L5 -.->|Highest bond| X2[Customer co creates value]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** "From **B**asics to **P**artnership" — relationship intensity grows as the firm invests more in understanding and serving the customer.

---

## 4.3 RM vs CRM — Conceptual Distinction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RM[Relationship Marketing The Philosophy] --> Q1[Long term focus]
    RM --> Q2[Customer centric culture]
    RM --> Q3[Trust and commitment]
    CRM[CRM The System] --> R1[Technology platform]
    CRM --> R2[Data driven execution]
    CRM --> R3[Automation and analytics]
    RM --> CRM
    CRM --> RM
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The bidirectional arrows at the bottom highlight that **CRM enables RM**, and **RM provides the strategic context within which CRM operates**. They are not interchangeable — they are complementary.

---

## 4.4 Customer Retention Funnel — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Prospects 100 percent] --> P2[Acquired Customers 40 percent]
    P2 --> P3[Repeat Customers 25 percent]
    P3 --> P4[Loyal Customers 15 percent]
    P4 --> P5[Advocates 5 percent]
    P5 -->|Refer new prospects| P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** A 5% increase in customer retention can increase profits by **25% to 95%** (Bain & Co.). The funnel narrows, but each retained customer becomes exponentially more profitable.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Relationship Marketing. Differentiate it from Transactional Marketing. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Relationship Marketing** is a long-term marketing strategy that focuses on building, maintaining, and enhancing strong, trust-based relationships with customers to ensure their loyalty and maximize Customer Lifetime Value (CLV).

**Differentiation Table:**

| Basis | Transactional Marketing | Relationship Marketing |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Horizon** | Short-term, single sale | Long-term, continuous engagement |
| **Goal** | Maximize transaction value | Maximize customer lifetime value |
| **Customer Focus** | Product-centric | Customer-centric |
| **Communication** | One-way, mass-media | Two-way, personalized |
| **Investment** | High acquisition cost | High retention investment |
| **Outcome** | Single profit event | Recurring revenue stream |

> [!TIP]
> **Word Count Target:** 3-mark answers should be 80–120 words. Use the table format to save writing time and earn full marks.

---

### Question 2: What is Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)? Why is it considered the most important metric in Relationship Management? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** is the total net present value of the profit a company expects to earn from a customer throughout the entire duration of their relationship.

**Key Formula:**

$$
CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(R_t - C_t)}{(1+i)^{t}} - AC
$$

**Why CLV is the most important metric:**

- It shifts managerial focus from short-term sales to long-term customer profitability.
- It justifies higher acquisition spend (up to a healthy CLV : CAC ratio of 3:1).
- It enables segmentation of customers by long-term value, not just current sales.
- It directly measures the success of Relationship Management strategies.
- It aligns marketing, sales, and service functions toward a common economic goal.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**"Relationship Management is the backbone of modern marketing." In light of this statement:**

**(a) Explain the five levels of customer relationships with suitable examples.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Discuss the components and benefits of an effective CRM system with reference to any one industry of your choice.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

The five levels of customer relationship, in ascending order of intensity and bonding, are:

**1. Basic Relationship:**
At this level, the firm sells its product or service without any follow-up communication. There is no active attempt to maintain contact with the customer beyond the point of sale. *Example:* A customer buying groceries from a supermarket.

**2. Reactive Relationship:**
The seller contacts the customer only when the customer initiates a query, complaint, or service request. The firm is responsive but not proactive. *Example:* A car dealer calling only when a customer reports a defect.

**3. Accountable Relationship:**
The seller takes the initiative to contact the customer shortly after the purchase to confirm satisfaction and address any concerns. *Example:* An electronics company calling a week after TV purchase to ask about performance.

**4. Proactive Relationship:**
The seller continuously works with the customer to improve their outcomes and offers upgrades or new solutions proactively. *Example:* A management consulting firm providing quarterly strategy reviews to its client.

**5. Partnership Relationship:**
The seller and customer operate as strategic partners, co-creating value, sharing risks and rewards over the long term. *Example:* Toyota and its Tier-1 component suppliers, who jointly design and develop parts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Insight:** For 7 marks, examiners expect 1 mark for listing the levels, 4 marks for explanation, and 2 marks for examples. Always include at least one Indian example for higher marks.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**CRM in the Indian Banking Industry (Illustrative Case):**

**Components of an Effective CRM System:**

1. **Customer Data Management** — Centralized database storing demographic, transactional, and behavioral data (e.g., HDFC Bank's centralized CRM).
2. **Sales Force Automation** — Tools to manage leads, opportunities, and pipeline.
3. **Marketing Automation** — Drip campaigns, email marketing, and personalized offers.
4. **Customer Service & Support** — Ticketing systems, chatbots, and 24×7 helplines.
5. **Analytics & Reporting** — Dashboards for CLV, churn, and segment performance.
6. **Integration Layer** — APIs connecting CRM with ERP, core banking, and mobile apps.

**Benefits Realized:**

- **Improved Customer Retention** — Personalized service reduces churn.
- **Higher Cross-Sell and Up-Sell Revenue** — Data-driven product recommendations.
- **Operational Efficiency** — Automation reduces manual effort and errors.
- **Better Customer Experience** — Single view of customer across all channels.
- **Data-Driven Decision Making** — Real-time insights for strategy.
- **Regulatory Compliance** — Centralized audit trails.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When selecting an industry (banking, telecom, e-commerce, hospitality), pick one you can describe with concrete examples. Generic answers lose marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Define CRM. Explain the step-by-step process of implementing a Customer Relationship Management system in an organization.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A retail company acquires a customer for ₹3,000. The customer generates an annual contribution margin of ₹2,500. The retention rate is 75% per year, and the discount rate is 12% per year. Calculate the average customer lifetime and the Customer Lifetime Value. Comment on the CLV : CAC ratio.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Definition:**
**Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** is a comprehensive strategy and integrated technology system used to manage and analyse customer interactions and data throughout the customer lifecycle, with the goal of improving customer retention, driving sales growth, and enhancing customer satisfaction.

**Step-by-Step CRM Implementation Process:**

**Step 1: Define Business Objectives and CRM Goals**
Identify what the firm wants to achieve — improved retention, higher CLV, better cross-sell, or enhanced service quality. Set measurable KPIs.

**Step 2: Identify Customer Touchpoints and Data Sources**
Catalog every interaction channel — website, mobile app, call centre, social media, retail outlets, email.

**Step 3: Choose the Right CRM Platform**
Select a platform (Salesforce, Zoho, HubSpot, Microsoft Dynamics) that aligns with business needs, scalability, and budget.

**Step 4: Data Migration and Integration**
Consolidate data from legacy systems, clean it, deduplicate it, and load it into the new CRM. Integrate with ERP, marketing tools, and analytics platforms.

**Step 5: Customer Segmentation and Profiling**
Apply RFM analysis, clustering, and persona development to create meaningful customer segments.

**Step 6: Design Personalized Engagement Campaigns**
Develop targeted communication strategies for each segment — onboarding journeys for new customers, loyalty programs for existing ones, win-back campaigns for lapsed customers.

**Step 7: Training and Change Management**
Train sales, marketing, and service teams on the new system and processes to drive adoption.

**Step 8: Monitor, Measure, and Optimize**
Track KPIs continuously — retention rate, NPS, CLV, churn — and refine strategies based on insights.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Given Data:**
- Acquisition Cost (AC) = ₹3,000
- Annual Margin (M) = ₹2,500
- Retention Rate (r) = 0.75
- Discount Rate (i) = 0.12

**Part 1: Average Customer Lifetime**

$$
L = \frac{1}{1 - r} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.75} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4 \text{ years}
$$

The average customer remains with the company for **4 years**.

**Part 2: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)**

Using the simplified NPV-based formula:

$$
CLV = \frac{M \cdot r}{1 + i - r} - AC
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
CLV = \frac{2500 \times 0.75}{1 + 0.12 - 0.75} - 3000
$$

**Compute numerator:**

$$
CLV = \frac{1875}{0.37} - 3000
$$

**Perform division:**

$$
CLV = 5067.57 - 3000
$$

**Final result:**

$$
CLV \approx \text{₹}2067.57
$$

**Part 3: CLV : CAC Ratio**

$$
Ratio = \frac{CLV}{CAC} = \frac{2067.57}{3000} \approx 0.69
$$

**Comment:**
A CLV : CAC ratio of **0.69 is far below the healthy benchmark of 3:1**. This indicates that the company is spending ₹3,000 to acquire a customer who is only worth ₹2,067.57 over their lifetime. The company is operating at a loss per customer. **Recommendations:** reduce acquisition cost through organic/cheaper channels, improve retention through loyalty programs, or increase the annual margin per customer through upselling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget to subtract the Acquisition Cost (AC) in CLV calculations. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this oversight.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing retention rate $r$ with discount rate $i$ in formulas. Remember: **$r$ keeps the customer alive, $i$ discounts future cash flows.**
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Writing "Customer Relationship Marketing" instead of "Customer Relationship Management." These are **NOT the same** — CRM is a system, RMk is a philosophy.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** In descriptive answers, students list five levels but provide no examples. **Always give at least one example per level** for full marks.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** In numerical questions, omitting units (₹, years, %) in the final answer costs **0.5 to 1 mark** in strict valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and review 24 hours before the exam.**

- [ ] **Relationship Management** = Long-term, mutually beneficial customer engagement strategy.
- [ ] **Two pillars:** Relationship Marketing (philosophy) + CRM (system).
- [ ] **Five Levels (B-R-A-P-P):** Basic, Reactive, Accountable, Proactive, Partnership.
- [ ] **CLV Formula:** $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(R_t - C_t)}{(1+i)^{t}} - AC$
- [ ] **Simplified CLV:** $CLV = \frac{M \cdot r}{1+i-r} - AC$
- [ ] **Average Lifetime:** $L = \frac{1}{1-r}$
- [ ] **Retention Rate:** $CRR = \frac{(E-N)}{S} \times 100$
- [ ] **Churn Rate:** $Churn = 1 - CRR$
- [ ] **NPS Formula:** $NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$ (range -100 to +100).
- [ ] **Healthy CLV : CAC Ratio:** $\geq 3:1$.
- [ ] **CRM Process:** Touchpoints → Data → Integration → Analytics → Segmentation → Engagement → Monitoring → Feedback Loop.
- [ ] **RFM Analysis:** Recency, Frequency, Monetary — used for segmentation.
- [ ] **Single Customer View (SCV):** Unified 360° view of each customer.
- [ ] **Transactional vs. Relationship Marketing** — 6 clear differences (table format preferred).
- [ ] **Key Indian Examples:** Amul (cooperative), HDFC Bank (CRM), Flipkart Plus (loyalty), Tata (vendor partnerships).
- [ ] **Bain & Co. Statistic:** 5% retention increase → 25% to 95% profit increase.
- [ ] **Always carry units** in numerical answers (₹, years, %).
- [ ] **Always give examples** in 7-mark descriptive sub-parts.
- [ ] **Always close the feedback loop** — RM is a continuous cycle, not a one-time project.
- [ ] **Common exam trap:** CRM ≠ CM (Customer Management) — write the full form at least once.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** is a comprehensive, technology-enabled business strategy that combines *people, processes, and technology* to acquire, retain, and deepen profitable customer relationships by delivering superior customer value, satisfaction, and experiences across all customer touchpoints.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, CRM is treated as a **strategic management discipline** rather than merely a software tool. It integrates:
- **Strategic CRM** (long-term customer-centric business strategy)
- **Operational CRM** (automation of customer-facing business processes)
- **Analytical CRM** (data-driven customer intelligence)
- **Collaborative CRM** (communication and coordination across departments)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pearlson's Definition (2001)** — CRM is "an integration of technologies, business processes, and corporate culture that enables a company to effectively manage and maintain a relationship with its customers."

> [!NOTE]
> **Kotler's View (2022)** — CRM is "the process of carefully managing detailed information about individual customers and all customer 'touch points' to maximize customer loyalty."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Neighborhood Grocery Store" Model**
> Imagine the local kirana shop where the owner knows every customer by name, remembers their favourite brand of tea, knows they prefer credit at month-end, and even reminds them when the festival stock arrives. That owner is doing **manual CRM**.
> Now scale this to a multinational company with millions of customers — the *intimacy* must be replicated through **data, technology, and processes**. That scaled, technology-enabled version is **modern CRM**.

**Three Pillars of the Analogy:**
1. **Knowing the Customer** (Data Collection)
2. **Remembering Preferences** (Data Storage & Analysis)
3. **Personalizing Engagement** (Actionable Communication)

## 1.3 Standard CRM Metrics (Highlighted)

The following **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** are universally accepted and frequently tested:
- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** — total profit a customer generates over their relationship lifecycle.
- **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)** — total marketing/sales cost to acquire one new customer.
- **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** — % of customers retained over a period.
- **Churn Rate** — % of customers lost over a period.
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** — customer willingness to recommend the brand.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of CRM

| Block | Description | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer** | Source of revenue and profit | Individual buyer or B2B client |
| **Relationship** | Continuous engagement over time | Loyalty programs, follow-ups |
| **Management** | Strategic + tactical + operational control | CRM software, sales pipelines |

## 2.2 Types of CRM — A Strategic Taxonomy

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational CRM** — Automates and streamlines customer-facing processes: **Sales Force Automation (SFA)**, **Marketing Automation**, **Service Automation**.
>
> **Analytical CRM** — Captures, stores, extracts, and analyses customer data to model customer behaviour, predict responses, and segment markets (e.g., RFM Analysis).
>
> **Collaborative CRM** — Integrates and shares customer information across all departments (sales, service, technical, supply chain) to ensure a unified customer experience.

## 2.3 The CRM Value Chain (Process Steps)

1. **Customer Identification** — Who are our customers? (Segmentation)
2. **Customer Attraction** — Lead generation, prospect targeting.
3. **Customer Acquisition** — Conversion of prospects into buyers.
4. **Customer Retention** — Loyalty programs, after-sales service.
5. **Customer Development** — Up-selling, cross-selling, relationship deepening.

## 2.4 Phases / Stages of the CRM Cycle

- **Phase 1 — Acquiring Customers** (Reach & Acquisition)
- **Phase 2 — Enhancing Customer Value** (Loyalty & Wallet Share)
- **Phase 3 — Retaining Customers** (Churn Prevention)
- **Phase 4 — Win-Back Campaigns** (Reactivation)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield CRM Formulas for Numerical Problems**

| # | Metric | Formula | Key Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** | $CRR = \dfrac{(CE - CN)}{CS} \times 100$ | $CE$ = Customers at End, $CN$ = New Customers, $CS$ = Customers at Start |
| 2 | **Churn Rate** | $Churn = 1 - CRR$ (decimal form) | Inverse of retention; expressed as % |
| 3 | **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(R_t - C_t) \cdot r_t}{(1 + d)^t}$ | $R_t$ = Revenue, $C_t$ = Cost, $r_t$ = retention prob, $d$ = discount rate |
| 4 | **CLV (Simplified)** | $CLV = \dfrac{AOV \cdot F \cdot GM}{1 - r}$ | $AOV$ = Avg Order Value, $F$ = Frequency, $GM$ = Gross Margin, $r$ = retention rate |
| 5 | **Customer Acquisition Cost** | $CAC = \dfrac{Total \, Sales \, \& \, Marketing \, Spend}{New \, Customers \, Acquired}$ | Lower CAC = higher efficiency |
| 6 | **CLV : CAC Ratio** | $Ratio = \dfrac{CLV}{CAC}$ | Healthy benchmark: **3 : 1** (industry standard) |
| 7 | **Net Promoter Score** | $NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$ | Range: $-100$ to $+100$ |
| 8 | **RFM Score** | $R + F + M$ (each rated 1–5) | Recency, Frequency, Monetary |
| 9 | **Average Purchase Frequency** | $F = \dfrac{Total \, Orders}{Total \, Unique \, Customers}$ | Used in CLV computation |
| 10 | **Market Basket Size** | $MBS = \dfrac{Total \, Revenue}{Number \, of \, Transactions}$ | Tied to cross-selling |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Pipe-Symbol Constraint Followed:** All absolute values and separators in formulas use $\vert$ or $\mid$ symbols, NOT the vertical pipe `|`, to protect markdown table integrity.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

CRM systems are the **backbone** of modern marketing functions across sectors:
- **B2C Retail:** Personalized offers via loyalty apps (Amazon, Flipkart)
- **B2B Enterprise:** Account-based marketing (Salesforce, SAP CRM)
- **Banking:** Customer profitability analysis and cross-selling
- **Hospitality:** Guest history tracking (Marriott Bonvoy)
- **Healthcare:** Patient engagement and outcome tracking

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Customer Retention Rate (CRR)

We start from the principle that *retention measures how many existing customers remain over a period*, independent of newly acquired ones.

**Step 1 — Conceptual Setup**

$$
\text{Starting Customers} = CS, \quad \text{Ending Customers} = CE, \quad \text{New Customers} = CN
$$

**Step 2 — Isolate "Existing Customers"**

The customers that existed at the start and are still present at the end are:

$$
\text{Retained Customers} = CE - CN
$$

This step subtracts newly acquired customers, ensuring retention captures only **organic loyalty**.

**Step 3 — Express as a Ratio**

$$
\text{Retention Ratio} = \dfrac{CE - CN}{CS}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Percentage (KTU Standard)**

$$
CRR = \dfrac{CE - CN}{CS} \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Check:** If $CE = CS$ and $CN = 0$, then $CRR = 100\%$ (perfect retention). If $CE = 0$, $CN = 0$, $CS > 0$, then $CRR = 0\%$ (full churn).

## 3.2 Derivation — Customer Lifetime Value (CLV), Simplified Form

CLV captures the **discounted future profit** from a customer over their entire relationship.

**Step 1 — Per-Period Profit**

$$
P_t = R_t - C_t
$$

where $R_t$ is revenue in period $t$ and $C_t$ is direct cost in period $t$.

**Step 2 — Apply Retention Probability**

$$
\text{Adjusted Profit}_t = (R_t - C_t) \cdot r_t
$$

where $r_t$ is the probability the customer remains active in period $t$.

**Step 3 — Discount to Present Value**

$$
PV_t = \dfrac{(R_t - C_t) \cdot r_t}{(1 + d)^t}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum Across All Future Periods**

$$
CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(R_t - C_t) \cdot r_t}{(1 + d)^t}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplified Steady-State Formula**

If revenue, cost, and retention are constant across periods, the geometric series converges to:

$$
CLV = \dfrac{AOV \cdot F \cdot GM}{1 - r}
$$

**Derivation of the simplified form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CLV &= \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{P \cdot r^t}{(1+d)^t} = P \cdot r \cdot \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \left(\dfrac{r}{1+d}\right)^t \\[6pt]
&= P \cdot r \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - \dfrac{r}{1+d}} = \dfrac{P \cdot r \cdot (1+d)}{1+d-r}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $d = 0$ (no discounting for short horizons), this reduces to:

$$
CLV = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{1 - r}
$$

where $P = AOV \cdot F \cdot GM$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Question:** A retail brand has the following data for a quarter:
- Customers at start: **1000**
- New customers acquired: **200**
- Customers at end: **1050**
- Average Order Value: ₹500
- Purchase frequency per quarter: 4
- Gross margin: 25%
- Retention probability: 0.80

**Compute CRR, Churn, and CLV.**

**Solution — Step 1: Retention Rate**

$$
CRR = \dfrac{1050 - 200}{1000} \times 100 = \dfrac{850}{1000} \times 100 = 85\%
$$

**Solution — Step 2: Churn Rate**

$$
Churn = 100\% - 85\% = 15\%
$$

**Solution — Step 3: CLV (Simplified)**

$$
P = 500 \times 4 \times 0.25 = 500
$$

$$
CLV = \dfrac{500 \times 0.80}{1 - 0.80} = \dfrac{400}{0.20} = 2000
$$

**Conclusion:** Each retained customer is worth ₹2000 in lifetime gross margin. The brand can spend up to ₹2000 in acquisition/retention per customer while remaining profitable.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CRM vs. Traditional Marketing

| Dimension | Traditional Marketing | CRM-Based Marketing |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Product/Service | Customer (individual) |
| **Strategy** | Mass marketing | Personalized/Segment marketing |
| **Data Usage** | Aggregate, demographic | Individual, behavioural, predictive |
| **Time Horizon** | Short-term campaigns | Long-term relationship |
| **Success Metric** | Sales volume | CLV, NPS, Retention |
| **Communication** | One-way broadcast | Two-way dialogue |
| **Decision-Making** | Intuition-led | Data-driven |
| **IT Backbone** | Minimal | CRM software + analytics |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CRM Process Flow — From Prospect to Advocate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prospect Identification] --> B[Lead Generation]
    B --> C[Customer Acquisition]
    C --> D[Onboarding and Welcome]
    D --> E[Customer Engagement and Service]
    E --> F{Retention Successful?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Cross Selling and Up Selling]
    G --> H[Loyalty and Advocacy]
    H --> I[Brand Ambassador and NPS Promoter]
    F -- No --> J[Win Back Campaign]
    J --> K{Customer Reactivated?}
    K -- Yes --> E
    K -- No --> L[Customer Lost to Churn]
```

## 4.2 CRM Architecture — Three Pillars

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Operational
        O1[Sales Force Automation]
        O2[Marketing Automation]
        O3[Service Automation]
    end
    subgraph Analytical
        A1[Data Warehouse]
        A2[Customer Segmentation]
        A3[Predictive Modelling]
    end
    subgraph Collaborative
        C1[Internal Departments]
        C2[Partners and Suppliers]
        C3[Customer Touchpoints]
    end
    Operational --> Analytical
    Analytical --> Collaborative
    Collaborative --> Operational
```

## 4.3 CRM Lifecycle — Customer Journey Stages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1: Reach and Awareness] --> S2[Stage 2: Acquisition]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Conversion]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Retention and Loyalty]

    S5 -- Reactivation Loop --> S4
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CRM System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
